Dr’s of required line α (5a –ab), – (b2 + 5), (a + ab)
Also Dr’s of required line α –2, d, –4
\[\therefore \;\frac{{5a - ab}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{ - \left( {{b^2} + 5} \right)}}{d} = \frac{{a + ab}}{{ - 4}}\;\;\;\;....\left( 1 \right)\]
\[Also\;{\text{point}}\;\left( {0,\;\frac{{ - 1}}{2},0} \right)\;will\;lie\;on\;\frac{{x - 1}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{y + 4}}{d} = \frac{{z - c}}{{ - 4}}\]
\[\frac{{0 - 1}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{\frac{{ - 1}}{2} + 4}}{d} = \frac{{0 - c}}{{ - 4}} \Rightarrow \;d = 7,\;c = 2\]
\[\begin{gathered}
  From\;\left( 1 \right)\;\frac{{5a - ab}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{ - {b^2} - 5}}{7} = \frac{{a + ab}}{{ - 4}} \\
  \frac{{5a - ab}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{a + ab}}{{ - 4}};\;\frac{{ - {b^2} - 5}}{7} = \frac{{a + ab}}{{ - 4}} \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[\begin{gathered}
   - 20a + 4ab = - 2a - 2ab\;\;\;\;\;4{b^2} + 20 = 7a + 7ab \\
  18a = 6ab\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;36 + 20 = 7a + 21a \\
  b = 3\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;56 = 28a \Rightarrow \;a = 2 \\
\end{gathered} \]
a + b + c + d = 2 + 3 + 2 + 7 = 14

Question:
If the locus of z ∈ C, such that
\[\operatorname{Re} \left( {\frac{{z - 1}}{{2z + i}}} \right) + \operatorname{Re} \left( {\frac{{\bar z - 1}}{{2\bar z - i}}} \right) = 2,\]
is a circle of radius r and center (a, b) then \[\frac{{15ab}}{{{r^2}}}\]is equal to :
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  \operatorname{Re} \left( {\frac{{z - 1}}{{2z + i}}} \right) + Re\left( {\frac{{\bar z - 1}}{{2\bar z - i}}} \right) = 2 \\
  Here,\;\frac{{z - 1}}{{2z + i}} = \left( {\frac{{\overline {\bar z - 1} }}{{2\bar z - i}}} \right) = 2 \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[\begin{gathered}
   = \operatorname{Re} \left( {\frac{{z - 1}}{{2z + i}}} \right) + \operatorname{Re} \left( {\frac{{\overline {z - 1} }}{{2z + i}}} \right) = 2 \\
   = 2\;\operatorname{Re} \left( {\frac{{z - 1}}{{2z + 1}}} \right) = 2 \Rightarrow \;\operatorname{Re} \left( {\frac{{z - 1}}{{2z + i}}} \right) = 1 \\
\end{gathered} \]
Let z = x + iy
\[\operatorname{Re} \left( {\frac{{\left( {x - 1} \right) + iy}}{{2x + i\left( {2y + 1} \right)}}} \right) = 1 \Rightarrow \operatorname{Re} \left[ {\frac{{\left( {\left( {x - 1} \right) + iy} \right)\;\left( {2x - i\left( {y + 1} \right)} \right)}}{{\left( {2x + i\left( {2y + 1} \right)\;\left( {2x - i(2y + 1} \right)} \right)}}} \right] = 1\]
\[\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow \;\frac{{2x\left( {x - 1} \right) + y\left( {2y + 1} \right)}}{{4{x^2} + {{\left( {2y + 1} \right)}^2}}} = 1 \\
   \Rightarrow \;2{x^2} - 2x + 2{y^2} + y = 4{x^2} + 4{y^2} + 1 + 4y \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow \;2{x^2} + 2{y^2} + 3y + 2x + 1 = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow \;{x^2} + {y^2} + x + \frac{3}{2}y + \frac{1}{2} = 0 \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[centre = \left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{2},\;\frac{{ - 3}}{4}} \right),\;r = \sqrt {\frac{1}{4} + \frac{9}{{16}} - \frac{1}{2}} = \frac{{\sqrt 5 }}{4}\]
\[\begin{gathered}
  a = \frac{{ - 1}}{2},\;b = \frac{{ - 3}}{4},\;{r^2} = \frac{5}{{16}} \\
  15\frac{{ab}}{{{r^2}}} = 15 \times \left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{2}} \right) \times \left( {\frac{{ - 3}}{4}} \right) \times \frac{{16}}{5} = 18 \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: For \[n \geqslant 2\], let S<sub>n</sub> denote the set of all subsets of {1, 2.......,n} with no two consecutive numbers. For example {1, 3, 5}\[ \in {S_6},\]but {1, 2, 4} \[ \notin {S_6}.\] Then n (S<sub>5</sub>) is equal to ______
Solution:
A = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5......n}
No. of subsets having r elements such that no two
are consecutive is = \[^{n - r + 1}{C_r}\]
for n = 5, no. of ways = \[^{6 - r}{C_r}\]
Subsets having no element = 1
Subsets having exactly 1 element \[ = {{\text{ }}^5}{C_1} = 5\]
Subsets having exactly 2 element \[ = {{\text{ }}^4}{C_2} = 6\]
Subsets having exactly 3 element \[ = {{\text{ }}^3}{C_3} = 1\]
\[ \Rightarrow 5 + 6 + 1 + 1 = 13\]

Question: Let the length of a latus rectum of an ellipse \[\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{a^2}}} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{b^2}}} = 1\] be 10. If its eccentricity is the minimum value of the function \[f(t) = {t^2} + t + \frac{{11}}{{12}},\]t ∈ R, then a<sup>2</sup> + b<sup>2</sup> is equal to :
Solution:
\[Length\;of\;LR = \frac{{2{b^2}}}{a} = 10 \Rightarrow \;5a = {b^2}\;\;....\left( 1 \right)\]
\[\begin{gathered}
  f(t) = {t^2} + t + \frac{{11}}{{12}} \\
  \frac{{df(t)}}{{dt}} = 2t + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow t = \frac{{ - 1}}{2} \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[\begin{gathered}
  Min\;value\;of\;f(t) = {\left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{2}} \right)^2} + \left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{2}} \right) + \frac{{11}}{{12}} \\
   = \frac{1}{4}\frac{{ - 1}}{2} + \frac{{11}}{{12}} = \frac{{3 - 6 + 11}}{{12}} = \frac{8}{{12}} = \frac{2}{3} = e \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[\begin{gathered}
  {e^2} = \frac{{1 - {b^2}}}{{{a^2}}} \Rightarrow \frac{4}{9} = \frac{{1 - {b^2}}}{{{a^2}}} \\
   \Rightarrow \;\frac{{{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}} = \frac{{1 - 4}}{a} = \frac{5}{a} \Rightarrow \;{b^2} = \frac{{5{a^2}}}{a}\;\;\;....\;\left( 2 \right) \\
\end{gathered} \]
From (1) & (2)
\[5a = \frac{{5{a^2}}}{a} \Rightarrow \;a = 9,\;b = \sqrt {45} = 3\sqrt 5 \]
∴ a2 + b2 = 81 +45 = 126

Question: Let the set of all values of \[p \in \mathbb{R},\] for which both the roots of the equation x<sup>2</sup> – (p + 2)x + (2p + 9) = 0  are negative real numbers, be the interval \[\left( {\alpha ,\beta } \right]\]. Then \[\beta  - 2\alpha \] is equal to
Solution:
Using location of roots :

(i) \[D \geqslant 0\]
(ii) \[\frac{{ - b}}{{2a}} < 0\]
(iii) \[a.f\left( 0 \right) > 0\]
\[{(p + 2)^2} - 4(2p + 9) \geqslant 0\]
\[(p + 4)(p - 8) \geqslant 0\]    ;     \[p + 2 < 0\]       ;     \[2p + 9 > 0\]
Intersection \[p \in \left( { - \frac{9}{2}, - 4} \right]\]
\[\therefore \beta - 2\alpha = - 4 + 9 = 5\]

Question:
For t > –1, let αt and βt be the roots of the equation
\[\left( {{{\left( {t + 2} \right)}^{\frac{1}{7}}} - 1} \right){x^2} + \left( {{{\left( {t + 2} \right)}^{\frac{1}{6}}} - 1} \right)x + \left( {{{\left( {t + 2} \right)}^{\frac{1}{{21}}}} - 1} \right) = 0.\]
\[If\;\mathop {\lim }\limits_{t \to - {1^ + }} {\alpha _t} = a\;and\;\mathop {\lim }\limits_{t \to {1^ + }} \;{\beta _t} = b,\]then 72(a+b)2 is equal to _____.
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  a + b = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{t \to - {1^ + }} \left( {\alpha + \beta } \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{t \to - {1^ + }} - \frac{{{{\left( {t + 2} \right)}^{\frac{1}{6}}} - 1}}{{{{\left( {t + 2} \right)}^{\frac{1}{7}}} - 1}} \\
  let\;t + 2 = y \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[\begin{gathered}
  a + b = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{y \to {1^ + }} \;\frac{{{y^{1/6}} - 1}}{{{y^{1/7}} - 1}} = \frac{7}{6} \\
  72{\left( {a + b} \right)^2} = 72\;\frac{{49}}{{36}} = 98 \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: If the function \[f(x) = \frac{{\tan \left( {\tan x} \right) - \sin \left( {\sin x} \right)}}{{\tan x - \sin x}}\]is continuous at x = 0, then f(0) is equal to _____.

\[If\;\int {\left( {\frac{1}{x} + \frac{1}{{{x^3}}}} \right)\left( {\sqrt[{23}]{{3{x^{ - 24}} + {x^{ - 26}}}}} \right)\;dx =  - \frac{\alpha }{{3\left( {\alpha  + 1} \right)}}{{\left( {3{x^\beta } + {x^\gamma }} \right)}^{\frac{{\alpha  + 1}}{\alpha }}} + C,\;x > 0,} \]\[\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma  \in Z} \right),\]where C is the constant of integration, then α + β + γ is equal to_____.
Solution:
\[\int {\left( {\frac{1}{{{x^2}}} + \frac{1}{{{x^4}}}} \right){{\left( {\frac{3}{x} + \frac{1}{{{x^3}}}} \right)}^{\frac{1}{{23}}}}dx} \]
\[{\text{using}}\;t = \frac{3}{x} + \frac{1}{{{x^3}}} \Rightarrow \;dt = - 3\left( {\frac{1}{{{x^2}}} + \frac{1}{{{x^4}}}} \right)dx\]
\[\int {\frac{{{t^{1/23}}dt}}{{ - 3}} = \frac{{{t^{24/23}}}}{{\left( {\frac{{24}}{{23}}} \right)\left( { - 3} \right)}} + C} \]
\[\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow \;\alpha = 23\;\beta = - 1\;\gamma = - 3 \\
  \alpha + \beta + \gamma = 19 \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: The integral \[\int\limits_0^\pi  {\frac{{\left( {x + 3} \right)\sin x}}{{1 + 3{{\cos }^2}x}}} \]dx is equal to :
Solution:
\[I = \int\limits_0^\pi {\frac{{\left( {x + 3} \right)\sin x}}{{1 + 3{{\cos }^2}x}}dx} \]
\[I = \int\limits_0^\pi {\frac{{\left( {\pi - x + 3} \right)\sin x}}{{\left( {1 + 3{{\cos }^2}x} \right)}}} \]
\[2I = \int\limits_0^\pi {\frac{{\left( {\pi + 6} \right)\sin x.dx}}{{\left( {1 + 3{{\cos }^2}x} \right)}}} = 2\int\limits_0^{\pi /2} {\frac{{\left( {\pi + 6} \right)\sin x}}{{\left( {1 + 3{{\cos }^2}x} \right)}}} \]
\[I = \int\limits_0^{\pi /2} {\frac{{\left( {\pi + 6} \right)\sin x.dx}}{{\left( {1 + 3{{\cos }^2}x} \right)}}} = \frac{\pi }{{3\sqrt 3 }}\left( {\pi + 6} \right)\]
\[\sqrt 3 \cos x = t\]
\[\sqrt 3 \sin x = dt\]

Question: Let x<sub>1</sub>, x<sub>2</sub>, x<sub>3</sub>, x<sub>4</sub> be in a geometric progression. If 2, 7, 9, 5 are subtracted respectively from x<sub>1</sub>, x<sub>2</sub>, x<sub>3</sub>, x<sub>4</sub> then the resulting numbers are in an arithmetic progression. Then the value of\[\frac{1}{{24}}\](x<sub>1</sub> x<sub>2</sub> x<sub>3</sub> x<sub>4</sub>) is :
Solution:
x1, x2, x3, x4 \[ \to \] G.P.
Let a, ar, ar2, ar3 \[ \to \] G.P.
Now a – 2, ar – 7, ar2 – 9, ar3 – 5 \[ \to \] A.P.
2(ar – 7) = a – 2 + ar2 – 9 …. (i)
2(ar2 – 9) = ar – 7 + ar3 – 5 … (ii)
Solving r = 2, a = – 3
\[\therefore \] Product = x1, x2, x3, x4 =a4r6 = 81 × 64

Question: The number of relations on the set A = {1, 2, 3} containing at most 6 elements including (l, 2), which are reflexive and transitive but not symmetric, is______
Solution:
\[A = \left\{ {1,2,3} \right\}\]
(1,1), (2,2), (3,3), (1, 2) \[ \in R\]
Remaining elements are
(2,1), (2,3), (1,3), (3,1), (3,2)
(1) If relation contains exactly 4 elements = 1 way
(2) if relation contains exactly 5 elements
It can be (1, 3), (3, 2) \[ \Rightarrow \] 2 ways
(3) If relation contain exactly 6 elements It can be
((2, 3), (1,3)), ((1,3), (3, 2)), ((3,1), (3,2))
\[ \Rightarrow \] 3 ways.
Total = 6 ways

Question: Let the line L pass through (1, 1, 1) and intersect the lines \[\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y + 1}}{3} = \frac{{z - 1}}{4}\] and \[\frac{{x - 3}}{1} = \frac{{y - 4}}{2} = \frac{z}{1}\] Then, which of the following points lies on the line L?
Solution:
\[Dr's{\text{ of AC}} \Rightarrow {\text{2}}\lambda {\text{, 3}}\lambda - 2,4\lambda \]
\[Dr's{\text{ of BC}} \Rightarrow \mu + 2,{\text{ }}2\mu + 3,\;\mu - 1\].
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{\mu + 2}}{{2\lambda }} = \frac{{2\mu + 3}}{{3\lambda - 2}} = \frac{{\mu - 1}}{{4\lambda }}\]
\[ \Rightarrow 2(\mu + 2) = \mu - 1 \Rightarrow \mu = - 5\]
\[ \Rightarrow Dr's{\text{ of BC}} \Rightarrow {\text{3,7,6}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow equation{\text{ of L}} \Rightarrow \frac{{x - 1}}{3} = \frac{{y - 1}}{7} = \frac{{z - 1}}{6}\]
\[(7,15,13){\text{ satisfies}}{\text{.}}\]

Question:
A wheel of radius 0.2 m rotates freely about its center when a string that is wrapped over its rim is pulled by force of 10 N as shown in figure. The established torque produces an angular acceleration of 2 rad/s2. Moment of inertia of the wheel is ________ kg m2.
(Acceleration due to gravity = 10 m/s2)
Solution:
\[FR = I\alpha \]
\[ \Rightarrow I = \frac{{FR}}{\alpha } = \frac{{10 \times 0.2}}{2} = 1{\text{ kg - }}{{\text{m}}^{\text{2}}}\]

Question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: Among \[{\left[ {{\text{Cu}}{{\left( {{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_3}} \right)}_4}} \right]^{2 + }},{\left[ {{\text{Ni}}{{({\text{en}})}_3}} \right]^{2 + }}\], $\left[\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right]^{2+}$ and $\left[\mathrm{Mn}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right]^{2+},\left[\mathrm{Mn}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right]^{2+}$, has the maximum number of unpaired electrons.
Statement II: The number of pairs among \[\left\{ {{{\left[ {{\text{NiC}}{{\text{l}}_4}} \right]}^{2 - }},\left[ {{\text{Ni}}{{({\text{CO}})}_4}} \right]} \right\},\left\{ {{{\left[ {{\text{NiC}}{{\text{l}}_4}} \right]}^{2 - }},{{\left[ {{\text{Ni}}{{({\text{CN}})}_4}} \right]}^{2 - }}} \right\}\] and $\left\{\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_{4}\right], \quad\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_{4}\right]^{2-}\right\} \quad$ that contain only diamagnetic species is two.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
Options: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Solution:
$\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{4}\right]^{2+} \Rightarrow \mathrm{d}^{9}, \mathrm{dsp}^{2}$ one unpaired electron
$\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{en})_{3}\right]^{2+} \Rightarrow \mathrm{d}^{8}, \mathrm{sp}^{3} \mathrm{~d}^{2}$ two unpaired electrons
$\left[\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right]^{2+} \Rightarrow \mathrm{d}^{8}, \mathrm{sp}^{3} \mathrm{~d}^{2}$ two unpaired electrons
$\left[\mathrm{Mn}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right]^{2+} \Rightarrow \mathrm{d}^{5}, \mathrm{sp}^{3} \mathrm{~d}^{2}$ five unpaired electrons
$\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_{4}\right]$ (diamagnetic)
$\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_{4}\right]^{-2}$ (paramagnetic)
$\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_{4}\right]^{-2}$ (diamagnetic)

Question:
For the reaction, $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}$, graph is plotted as shown below. Identify correct statements.
A. Standard free energy change for the reaction is $-5.40 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.
B. As $\mathrm{\Delta G}^{\ominus}$ in graph is positive, $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ will not dissociate into $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ at all.
C. Reverse reaction will go to completion.
D. When 1 mole of $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ changes into equilibrium mixture, value of $\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\ominus}=-0.84 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
E. When 2 mole of $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$, changes into equilibrium mixture, $\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\ominus}$ for equilibrium mixture is $-6.24 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
Solution:
A) $\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=\mathrm{G}^{\circ}{ }_{\mathrm{B}}-\mathrm{G}^{\circ}{ }_{\mathrm{A}}=+\mathrm{ve}$
B) $\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=+\mathrm{ve}, \mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ will partially dissociates into $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$.
C) For reverse reaction
It is partially completed as there is equilibrium at E .
D) For 1 mole $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4} ; \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=-0.84 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
(E) For 2 mole $\mathrm{NO}_{2} ; \Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=-5.4-0.84$
$$
=-6.24 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1 .}
$$

Question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: When an electric discharge is passed through gaseous hydrogen, the hydrogen molecules dissociate and the energetically excited hydrogen atoms produce electromagnetic radiation of discrete frequencies.
Statement II: The frequency of second line of Balmer series obtained from $\mathrm{He}^{+}$is equal to that of first line of Lyman series obtained from hydrogen atom.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
Options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Solution:
$\frac{1}{\lambda}=R Z^{2}\left(\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right)$
For Ist line of lyman series in H -atom
$\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}(1)^{2}\left(\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{2^{2}}\right)$
$\frac{1}{\lambda}=\frac{3 R}{4}$
for $2^{\text {nd }}$ line of Balmer series of $\mathrm{He}^{+}$
$\frac{1}{\lambda^{\prime}}=R(2)^{2}\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{4^{2}}\right)$
$\frac{1}{\lambda^{\prime}}=\frac{3 \mathrm{R}}{4}$
As $\lambda$ and $\lambda^{\prime}$ is equal so frequency of these lines will be also equal.

Question:
The pH and conductance of a weak acid (HX) was found to be 5 and $4 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~S}$, respectively. The conductance was measured under standard condition using a cell where the electrode plates having a surface area of $1 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ were at a distance of 15 cm apart. The value of the limiting molar conductivity is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{S} \mathrm{m}^{2} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. (nearest integer)
(Given: degree of dissociation of the weak acid (a) << 1)
Solution:
$\mathrm{pH}=5$
$\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=10^{-5}=[\mathrm{HX}] . \alpha$
$=[\mathrm{HX}] \cdot \frac{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}}{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\infty}}$
$\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{\mathrm{k} \times 1000}{[\mathrm{HX}]}$
$\mathrm{K}=\mathrm{G} . \mathrm{G}^{*}=4 \times 10^{-5} \times \frac{15}{1}=6 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~S} . \mathrm{cm}^{-1}$
$\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=10^{-5}=[\mathrm{HX}] \times \frac{6 \times 10^{-4} \times 1000}{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\infty} \times[\mathrm{HX}]}$
$\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\infty}=60000 \mathrm{S.cm}^{2} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
$\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\infty}=6 \mathrm{~S} . \mathrm{m}^{2} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$

Question:
Consider the following reactions:
NaCl + K2Cr2O7 + H2SO4 → A + KHSO4 + NaHSO4 + H2O
A + NaOH → B + NaCl + H2O
B + H2SO4 + H2O2 → C + Na2SO4 + H2O
In the product ' C ', ' X ' is the number of $\mathrm{O}_{2}^{2-}$ units, ' Y ' is the total number oxygen atoms present and ' $Z$ ' is the oxidation state of Cr . The value of $\mathrm{X}+\mathrm{Y}+\mathrm{Z}$ is $\_\_\_\_$。
Solution:
$\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{CrO}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$
$\mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}$
$\mathrm{C} \rightarrow \mathrm{CrO}_{5}$

$\mathrm{X}=2, \mathrm{Y}=5$ and $\mathrm{Z}=6$

Question:
Use the following data :
Substance$\frac{\Delta_{\mathrm{f}} \mathrm{H}^{\ominus}(500 \mathrm{~K})}{\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}$$\frac{\mathrm{~S}^{\ominus}(500 \mathrm{~K})}{\mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}$
AB(g)32222
A2(g)6146
B2(g)X280

One mole each of $\mathrm{A}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ and $\mathrm{B}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ are taken in a 1L closed flask and allowed to establish the equilibrium at 500 K .
$\mathrm{A}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{B}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{AB}(\mathrm{g})$
The value of $\mathrm{x}\left(\mathrm{in} \ \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}\right)$ is $\_\_\_\_$ (Nearest integer)
(Given: \[\log {\text{K}} = 2.2\,\,{\text{R}} = 8.3{\text{J}}{{\text{K}}^{ - 1}}\;{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}\] )
Solution:
$\mathrm{A}_{2}+\mathrm{B}_{2} \xrightarrow{500 \mathrm{~K}} 2 \mathrm{AB} \log \mathrm{K}=2.2$
$\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=(2 \times 32)-(6+\mathrm{x})=(58-\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{kJ}$
$\Delta \mathrm{S}^{\circ}=(2 \times 222)-(146+280)=18$ Joule
$\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=-\mathrm{RT} \ln \mathrm{K}$
$\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=-\frac{8.314 \times 500 \times 2.2 \times 2.303}{1000}$
$\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=-21.06$
$\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}-\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}^{\circ}=-21.06$
$58-\mathrm{x}-500\left(\frac{18}{1000}\right)=-21.06$
$\mathrm{x}=70.06 \mathrm{KJ} / \mathrm{mol}$

Question:
A body of mass 2 kg is moving along x-direction such that its displacement as function of time is given by $\mathrm{x}(\mathrm{t})=\alpha \mathrm{t}^{2}+\beta \mathrm{t}+\gamma \mathrm{m}$, where $\alpha=1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$, $\beta=1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and $\gamma=1 \mathrm{~m}$. The work done on the body during the time interval $\mathrm{t}=2 \mathrm{~s}$ to $\mathrm{t}=3 \mathrm{~s}$, is
$\_\_\_\_$ J.
Solution:
$\mathrm{x}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{t}^{2}+\mathrm{t}+1$
$\mathrm{v}(\mathrm{t})=2 \mathrm{t}+1$
$\mathrm{a}(\mathrm{t})=2$
$\mathrm{F}=4 \mathrm{~N}$.
Displacement $=\mathrm{x}(3)-\mathrm{x}(2)$
$=13-7=6 \mathrm{M}$
$\mathrm{W}=\mathrm{F} . \mathrm{S}=4 \times 6=24 \mathrm{~J}$

Question: A large drum having radius R is spinning around its axis with angular velocity $\omega$, as shown in figure. The minimum value of $\omega$ so that a body of mass M remains stuck to the inner wall of the drum, taking the coefficient of friction between the drum surface and mass $M$ is $\mu$, is :
Solution:
$\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{m} \omega^{2} \mathrm{r}, \mathrm{mg}=\mu \mathrm{N}$
$\mu \times m \omega^{2} r=m g$
$\omega=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{g}}{\mu \mathrm{r}}}$

Question:
An electromagnetic wave of frequency 100 MHz propagates through a medium of conductivity, $\sigma=10 \mathrm{mho} / \mathrm{m}$. The ratio of maximum conducting current density to maximum displacement current density is $\_\_\_\_$。
$\left[\right.$ Take $\left.\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}=9 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{Nm}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}\right]$
Solution:
$\mathrm{j}_{\mathrm{c}}=\sigma \mathrm{E}$
$\mathrm{E} \Rightarrow \mathrm{E}_{0} \sin (\omega \mathrm{t}-\mathrm{kx})$
$\mathrm{j}_{\mathrm{c}}=\sigma \mathrm{E}_{0} \sin (\omega \mathrm{t}-\mathrm{kx})$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\Rightarrow\left(\mathrm{j}_{\mathrm{c}}\right)_{\text {max }}=\sigma \mathrm{E}_{0} \tag{i}
\end{equation*}
$$
$\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{d}} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{d}}}{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~A}} \times \varepsilon_{0} \frac{\mathrm{AdE}}{\mathrm{dt}}$
$\Rightarrow \varepsilon_{0} \times \mathrm{E}_{0} \omega \cos (\omega \mathrm{t}-\mathrm{kx})$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\left(\mathrm{j}_{\mathrm{d}}\right)_{\text {max }} \Rightarrow \varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{E}_{0} \omega \tag{ii}
\end{equation*}
$$
(i)/(ii)
$\frac{\left(\mathrm{j}_{\mathrm{c}}\right)_{\text {max }}}{\left(\mathrm{j}_{\mathrm{d}}\right)_{\text {max }}}=\frac{\sigma \mathrm{E}_{0}}{\varepsilon_{0} \omega \mathrm{E}_{0}} \Rightarrow \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0} \omega}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{10 \times 4 \pi \times 9 \times 10^{9}}{2 \pi \times 100 \times 10^{6}}$
$\Rightarrow 1800$

Question:
As shown in the diagram, when the incident ray is parallel to base of the prism, the emergent ray grazes along the second surface.

If refractive index of the material of prism is $\sqrt{2}$, the angle $\theta$ of prism is.
Solution:
For grazing emergence
$\sin \mathrm{r}_{2}=\frac{1}{\mu}$
By Snell's Law at incident surface
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 1 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=\sqrt{2} \sin \mathrm{r}_{1} \\
& \mathrm{r}_{1}=30 \\
& \mathrm{r}_{1}+\mathrm{r}_{2}=\mathrm{A} \\
& \mathrm{~A}=75 \\
& 75+45+\theta=180^{\circ} \\
& \theta=60^{\circ} \\
& \hline
\end{aligned}
$$

Question:
Consider two identical metallic spheres of radius $R$ each having charge $Q$ and mass $m$. Their centers have an initial separation of $4 R$. Both the spheres are given an initial speed of $u$ towards each other. The minimum value of $u$, so that they can just touch each other is :
(Take $k=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}$ and assume $k Q^{2}>G m^{2}$ where G is the Gravitational constant)
Options: $\sqrt{\frac{k Q^{2}}{2 m R}\left(1-\frac{G m^{2}}{2 k Q^{2}}\right)}$
Solution:
Using energy conservation
(2) $\left(\frac{1}{2} m u^{2}\right)-\frac{G m^{2}}{4 R}+\frac{K Q^{2}}{4 R}=-\frac{G m^{2}}{2 R}+\frac{K Q^{2}}{2 R}$
$u=\sqrt{\frac{1}{4 m R}\left(K Q^{2}-G m^{2}\right)}$

Question: The total length of potentiometer wire AB is 50 cm in the arrangement as shown in figure. If P is the point where the galvanometer shows zero reading then the length AP is $\_\_\_\_$ cm .
Solution:
$\frac{6}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{AP}}}=\frac{4}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{PB}}}$;
$$
\begin{equation*}
\ell_{\mathrm{AP}}+\ell_{\mathrm{PB}}=50 \tag{i}
\end{equation*}
$$
$\frac{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{AP}}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{PB}}}=\frac{\ell_{\mathrm{AP}}}{\ell_{\mathrm{PB}}}=\frac{3}{2}$
$\ell_{\mathrm{AP}}=\frac{3}{5} \times 50=30 \mathrm{~cm}$

Question: The charge stored by the capacitor C in the given circuit in the steady state is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mu \mathrm{C}$.
Solution:
in steady state
$\mathrm{i}=2.5 / 5=0.5 \mathrm{~A}$
$\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{C}}=4 \times 0.5$
$\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{c}}=2 \mathrm{~V}$
charge
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{Q} & =\mathrm{CV}_{\mathrm{C}} \\
& =5 \times 2 \\
& =10 \mu \mathrm{C}

$$

Question: The r.m.s speed of oxygen molecules at $47^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is equal to that of the hydrogen molecules kept at $\_\_\_\_$ ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Mass of oxygen molecule/mass of hydrogen molecule=32/2)
Solution:
$\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 \mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{M}}}$
$\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rmsO}_{2}}=\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rmsH}_{2}}$
$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}=273+47=320 \mathrm{~K}$
$\sqrt{\frac{3 \mathrm{RT}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 \mathrm{RT}_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}}$
$\frac{\mathrm{T}_{2}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}=\frac{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}$
$\frac{320}{32}=\frac{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}{2}$
$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}=20 \mathrm{~K}$
$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}=-253^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

Question: A capacitor C is first charged fully with potential difference of $\mathrm{V}_{0}$ and disconnected from the battery. The charged capacitor is connected across an inductor having inductance L . In $\mathrm{t}  \mathrm {sec} , 25 \%$ of the initial energy in the capacitor is transferred to the inductor. The value of $t$ is $\_\_\_\_$ s.
Solution:
$U_{c_{f}}=75 \% U_{c_{i}} \mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{F}}^{2}=\frac{3}{4} \mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{I}}^{2}$
$\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{i}} \cos \omega \mathrm{t}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{i}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=\frac{\mathrm{T}}{12}$
$\mathrm{t}=\frac{\pi}{6} \sqrt{\mathrm{LC}}$

Question:
A particle having electric charge $3 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}$ and mass $6 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}$ is accelerated by applying an electric potential of 1.21 V . Wavelength of the matter wave associated with the particle is $\alpha \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~m}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .
(Take Planck's constant $=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J} . \mathrm{s}$ )
Solution:
$\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mqV}}}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \lambda=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \times 18 \times 10^{-46} \times 1.21}} \\
& \lambda=10^{-11} \mathrm{~m}=10 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~m} \\
& \alpha=10
\end{aligned}
$$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

Question: Two cars A and B each of mass $10^{3} \mathrm{~kg}$ are moving on parallel tracks separated by a distance of 10 m , in same direction with speeds $72 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$ and $36 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$. The magnitude of angular momentum of car A with respect to car B is $\_\_\_\_$ J.s.
Solution:
$\mathrm{L}=\mathrm{m} . \mathrm{V}_{\text {rel }} \mathrm{r}_{\perp}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& =1000 \times\left(36 \times \frac{5}{18}\right) \times 10 \\
& =10^{5} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Angular momentum of a point particle

Question:
The terminal velocity of a metallic ball of radius 6 mm in a viscous fluid is $20 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$. The terminal velocity of another ball of same material and having radius 3 mm in the same fluid will be
$\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{cm} / \mathrm{s}$.
Solution:
We know :
Terminal velocity $\propto(\text { radius })^{2}$
$\frac{\left(\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{T}}\right)_{1}}{\left(\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{T}}\right)_{2}}=\left(\frac{6}{3}\right)^{2}$
$\left(\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{T}}\right)_{2}=\frac{\left(\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{T}}\right)_{1}}{4}=5 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{sec}$

Question: A diatomic gas $(\gamma=1.4)$ does 100 J of work when it is expanded isobarically. Then the heat given to the gas $\_\_\_\_$ J.
Solution:
$\mathrm{w}=100 \mathrm{~J}=\mathrm{nR} \Delta \mathrm{T}$ for isobaric process.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{nC}_{\mathrm{p}} \Delta \mathrm{~T}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{f}}{2}+1\right) \mathrm{nR} \Delta \mathrm{~T} \\
& =\frac{7}{2} \cdot(100)=350 \text { Joule. }
\end{aligned}
$$

Question: The kinetic energy of a simple harmonic oscillator is oscillating with angular frequency of $176 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$. The frequency of this simple harmonic oscillator is___ Hz. $\left[\right.$ take $\left.\pi=\frac{22}{7}\right]$
Solution:
$\boldsymbol{\omega}=176 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{sec}$
$\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{k}}=\frac{\omega}{2 \pi}=\frac{176}{2 \times 22} \times 7$
$=\frac{176}{44} \times 7$
$=4 \times 7=28 \mathrm{~Hz}$
So frequency of oscillator
$=\frac{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{k}}}{2}=14 \mathrm{~Hz}$

Question: In a Young's double slit experiment set up, the two slits are kept 0.4 mm apart and screen is placed at 1 m from slits. If a thin transparent sheet of thickness $20 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ is introduced in front of one of the slits then centre bring fringe shifts by 20 mm on the screen. The refractive index of transparent sheet is given by $\frac{\alpha}{10}$, where $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$\quad y_{\text {shift }}=\frac{(\mu-1) t . D}{d}$
$20910^{-3}=\frac{(\mu-1) \times 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 1}{0.4 \times 10^{-3}}$
$(\mu-1) \Rightarrow 0.4$
$\mu \Rightarrow 1.4$
$\frac{\alpha}{10}=1.4, \alpha=14$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Variations in Young's double slit experiment

Question: The pulley shown in figure is made using a thin rim and two rods of length equal to diameter of the rim. The rim and each rod have a mass of M . Two blocks of mass of M and m are attached to two ends of a light string passing over the pulley, which is hinged to rotate freely in vertical plane about its centre. The magnitudes of the acceleration experienced by the blocks is $\_\_\_\_$ (assume no slipping of string on pulley.)
Solution:
$$
\begin{equation*}
\mathrm{Mg}-\mathrm{T}_{2}=\mathrm{Ma} \tag{1}
\end{equation*}
$$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\mathrm{T}_{1}-\mathrm{mg}=\mathrm{ma} \tag{2}
\end{equation*}
$$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\left(\mathrm{T}_{2}-\mathrm{T}_{1}\right) \mathrm{r}=\mathrm{I} \frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{r}} \tag{3}
\end{equation*}
$$
$(1)+(2)+(3)$
$(M-m) g=\left(M+m+\frac{I}{r^{2}}\right) a$
Here $\mathrm{I}=\mathrm{Mr}^{2}+\frac{\mathrm{M} \times(2 \mathrm{r})^{2}}{12} \times 2$
$=\left(1+\frac{2}{3}\right) \mathrm{Mr}^{2}$
$=\frac{5}{3} \mathrm{Mr}^{2}$
$(M-m) g=\left[M+m+\frac{5 M}{3}\right] a$
$a=\frac{(M-m) g}{\left[M+m+\frac{5 M}{3}\right]}$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Pulley problems based on fixed axis rotation

Question: A battery with EMF E and internal resistance $r$ is connected across a resistance $R$. The power consumption in R will be maximum when :
Solution:
For maximum power drawn across load Resistance $\mathrm{R}_{\text {Load }}=\mathrm{R}_{\text {internal }}$
$\mathrm{R}=\mathrm{r}$

Question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: In a Young's double slit experiment, the angular separation of fringes will increase as the screen is moved away from the plane of the slits
Statement II : In a Young's double slit experiment, the angular separation of fringes will increase when monochromatic source is replaced by another monochromatic source of higher wavelength
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
Options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Solution: Angular fringe width $=\frac{\lambda}{\mathrm{d}}$

Question: A spherical body of radius $r$ and density $\sigma$ falls freely through a viscous liquid having density $\rho$ and viscosity $\eta$ and attains a terminal velocity $\mathrm{v}_{0}$. Estimated maximum error in the quantity $\eta$ is : (Ignore errors associated with $\sigma, \rho$ and g , gravitational acceleration)
Solution:
$\mathrm{v}_{0}=\frac{2}{9} \frac{\mathrm{r}^{2} \mathrm{~g}}{\mathrm{n}}\left(\rho_{\mathrm{B}}-\rho_{\mathrm{L}}\right)$
$\mathrm{n}=\frac{2}{9} \frac{\mathrm{r}^{2} \mathrm{~g}}{\mathrm{v}_{0}}\left(\rho_{\mathrm{B}}-\rho_{\mathrm{L}}\right)$
$\frac{\Delta \mathrm{n}}{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{2 \Delta \mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{r}}+\frac{\Delta \mathrm{v}_{0}}{\mathrm{v}_{0}}$

Question: Keeping the significant figures in view, the sum of the physical quantities $52.01 \mathrm{~m}, 153.2 \mathrm{~m}$ and 0.123 m is :

A river of width 200 m is flowing from west to east with a speed of $18 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$. A boat, moving with speed of $36 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$ in still water, is made to travel one-round trip (bank to bank of the river). Minimum time taken by the boat for this journey and also the displacement along the river bank are
$\_\_\_\_$ and $\_\_\_\_$ respectively.
Solution:
Minimum time :
$\mathrm{t}_{\text {min }}=\frac{200}{10}=20 \mathrm{sec}$
For round trip $=40 \mathrm{sec}$.
Displacement along river bank $=40 \times 5=200 \mathrm{~m}$

Question: Surface tension of two liquids (having same densities), $\mathrm{T}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{T}_{2}$, are measured using capillary rise method utilizing two tubes with inner radii of $r_{1}$ and $r_{2}$ where $r_{1}>r_{2}$. The measured liquid heights in these tubes are $\mathrm{h}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{h}_{2}$ respectively. [Ignore the weight of the liquid about the lowest point of miniscus]. The heights $\mathrm{h}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{h}_{2}$ and surface tensions $\mathrm{T}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{T}_{2}$ satisfy the relation :
Solution:
$h=\frac{2 T}{\rho g r}$
$\mathrm{h} \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{r}}$
If $\mathrm{r}_{1}>\mathrm{r}_{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{~h}_{2}>\mathrm{h}_{1}$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Jurin's law/ insufficient height of capillary

Question: Two known resistance of $\mathrm{R} \Omega$ and $2 \mathrm{R} \Omega$ and and one unknown resistance $\mathrm{X} \Omega$ are connected in a circuit as shown in the figure. If the equivalent resistance between points A and B in the circuit is $\mathrm{X} \Omega$, then the value of X is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$.
Solution:
$\frac{(2 \mathrm{R}+\mathrm{x}) \cdot(\mathrm{R})}{3 \mathrm{R}+\mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{x}$
$x^{2}+2 R x-2 R^{2}=0$
$\mathrm{x}=(\sqrt{3}-1) \mathrm{R}$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Calculation of equivalent resistance of a circuit

Question: An infinitely long straight wire carrying current I is bent in a planer shape as shown in the diagram. The radius of the circular part is r . The magnetic field at the centre O of the circular loop is :
Solution:
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_{0}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_{\mathrm{AB}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_{\mathrm{DE}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_{\mathrm{BCD}}$
$=\frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{i}}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{i}}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}} \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{i}}{2 \mathrm{r}} \hat{\mathrm{i}}$
$=\frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{i}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}} \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{i}}{2 \mathrm{r}} \hat{\mathrm{i}}$
$=\frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{i}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}(1-\pi) \hat{\mathrm{i}}$
$=-\frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{i}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}(\pi-1) \hat{\mathrm{i}}$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Magnetic field due to standard shaped objects

Question: The energy of an electron in an orbit of the Bohr's atom is $-0.04 \mathrm{E}_{0} \mathrm{eV}$ where $\mathrm{E}_{0}$ is the ground state energy. If L is the angular momentum of the electron in this orbit and h is the Planck's constant, then $\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{~L}}{\mathrm{~h}}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ :
Solution:
Angular momentum $\mathrm{L}=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi}$
$\mathrm{n}=\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{~L}}{\mathrm{~h}}$
Energy $\mathrm{E}=-\frac{13.6}{\mathrm{n}^{2}} . \mathrm{z}^{2}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{E} \Rightarrow-\frac{\mathrm{E}_{0}}{\mathrm{n}^{2}}=-0.04 \mathrm{E}_{0} \\
& \mathrm{n}^{2}=25, \mathrm{n}=5
\end{aligned}
$$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Energy, radius and speed of electron in hydrogen like elements

Question:
Suppose a long solenoid of 100 cm length, radius 2 cm having 500 turns per unit length, carries a current $I=10 \sin (\omega \mathrm{t}) \mathrm{A}$, where $\omega=1000 \mathrm{rad} . / \mathrm{s}$. A circular conducting loop (B) of radius 1 cm coaxially slided through the solenoid at a speed $\mathrm{v}=1 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$. The r.m.s. current through the loop when the coil B is inserted 10 cm inside the solenoid is $\alpha / \sqrt{2} \mu \mathrm{~A}$. The value of $\alpha$ is
$\_\_\_\_$ .
[Resistance of the loop $=10 \Omega$ ]
Solution:
EMF induced $\varepsilon=\mathrm{A} \frac{\mathrm{dB}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{A} \mu_{0} \mathrm{n} \frac{\mathrm{di}}{\mathrm{dt}} \varepsilon=\mathrm{A} \mu_{0} \mathrm{n} \mathrm{i}_{0} \omega \cos \omega \mathrm{t}$
current induced $\mathrm{i}=\frac{\varepsilon}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{\pi \mathrm{r}^{2} \mu_{0} \mathrm{ni}_{0} \omega}{\mathrm{R}} \cos \omega \mathrm{t}$
So $i=\frac{\pi r^{2} \mu_{0} n i_{0} \omega}{\sqrt{2} R}$
$=\frac{\pi \times 10^{-4} \times 4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 500 \times 10 \times 10^{3}}{\sqrt{2} \times 10}$
$=\frac{20 \pi^{2}}{\sqrt{2}} \times 10^{-6}$
$\simeq \frac{197}{\sqrt{2}} \mu \mathrm{~A}$

Question:
A circular loop of radius 7 cm is placed in uniform magnetic field of 0.2 T directed perpendicular to plane of loop. The loop is converted into a square loop in 0.5 s . The EMF induced in the loop is
$\_\_\_\_$ mV .
Solution:
induced EMF $=\frac{\mathrm{d} \phi}{\mathrm{dt}}$
circumference $\simeq 14 \pi$
side length of square loop $=\frac{14 \pi}{4}=\frac{7 \pi}{2}$
$\Delta \phi=\mathrm{B}\left(\mathrm{A}_{1}-\mathrm{A}_{2}\right)$
$=(.2)\left(\left(\frac{7 \pi}{2}\right) \times \frac{7 \pi}{2}-49 \pi\right) \times 10^{-4}$
$=.2\left(\frac{49 \pi^{2}}{4}-49 \pi\right) \times 10^{-4}$
$\Delta \phi=.2 \times 33.07 \times 10^{-4}$
$\Delta \phi=6.614 \times 10^{-4}$
$\mathrm{EMF}=\frac{6.614 \times 10^{-4}}{\frac{1}{2}} \mathrm{~V}=13.23 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~V}$
$\mathrm{EMF}=1.32 \mathrm{mV}$

Question:
The ratio of speeds of electromagnetic waves in vacuum and a medium, having dielectric constant $\mathrm{k}=3$ and permeability of $\mu=2 \mu_{0}$, is
( $\mu_{0}=$ permeability of vacuum)
Solution:
$\frac{C}{V}=\mu=\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r} \mu_{r}}$
$\frac{C}{V}=\sqrt{\frac{3 \times 2}{1}}=\frac{\sqrt{6}}{1}$

Question:
A small metallic sphere of diameter 2 mm and density $10.5 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}$ is dropped in glycerine having viscosity 10 Poise and density $1.5 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}$ respectively. The terminal velocity attained by the sphere is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{cm} / \mathrm{s}$.
( $\pi=\frac{22}{7}$ and $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$ )
Solution:
$\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{T}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{r}^{2} \mathrm{~g}}{9 \eta}\left(\rho_{\mathrm{b}}-\rho_{\ell}\right)$
$\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{T}}=\frac{2}{9} \frac{(.1)^{2} \times 10}{(10)}(10.5-1.5)$
$\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{T}}=2 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{sec}$.

Question:
When an unpolarized light falls at a particular angle on a glass plate (placed in air), it is observed that the reflected beam is linearly polarized. The angle of refracted beam with respect to the normal is $\_\_\_\_$ .
$\left(\tan ^{-1}(1.52)=57.7^{\circ}\right.$, refractive indices of air and glass are 1.00 and 1.52, respectively)
Solution:
$\tan \mathrm{i}=\frac{\mu_{2}}{\mu_{1}}=\frac{\mu_{\mathrm{g}}}{\mu_{\mathrm{a}}}$
$\tan \mathrm{i}=1.52$
$\mathrm{i}=57.7^{\circ}$
$\mathrm{r}=90^{\circ}-\mathrm{i}$
$\mathrm{r}=32.3^{\circ}$

Question: The size of the images of an object, formed by a thin lens are equal when the object is placed at two different positions 8 cm and 24 cm from the lens. The focal length of the lens is $\_\_\_\_$ cm.
Solution:
$m=\frac{f}{f+u}$
$\mathrm{m}_{1}=-\mathrm{m}_{2}$
$\frac{\mathrm{f}}{\mathrm{f}-8}=-\frac{\mathrm{f}}{\mathrm{f}-24}$
$\mathrm{f}-8=24-\mathrm{f}$
$2 \mathrm{f}=32$
$\mathrm{f}=16 \mathrm{~cm}$

Question:
The average energy released per fission for the nucleus of ${ }_{92}^{235} \mathrm{U}$ is 190 MeV . When all the atoms of 47 g pure ${ }_{92}^{235} \mathrm{U}$ undergo fission process, the energy released is $\alpha \times 10^{23} \mathrm{MeV}$. The value of $\alpha$ is
$\_\_\_\_$ .
(Avogadro Number $=6 \times 10^{23}$ per mole)
Solution:
Total numbers of U-235 atom is
$47 \mathrm{~g}=\frac{47}{235}$ moles $=\frac{1}{5}$ moles
∴ Total energy released $=\frac{1}{5} \times 6 \times 10^{23} \times 190 \mathrm{MeV} =228 \times 10^{23} \mathrm{MeV}$

Question: A ball of radius r and density $\rho$ dropped through a viscous liquid of density $\sigma$ and viscosity $\eta$ attains its terminal velocity at time t , given by $\mathrm{t}=\mathrm{A} \rho^{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{r}^{\mathrm{b}} \eta^{\mathrm{c}} \sigma^{\mathrm{d}}$, where A is a constant and $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b} \mathrm{c}$ and d are integers. The value of $\frac{b+c}{a+d}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$\mathrm{T}=\left(\mathrm{ML}^{-3}\right)^{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{L}^{\mathrm{b}}\left(\mathrm{ML}^{-1} \mathrm{~T}^{-1}\right)^{\mathrm{c}}\left(\mathrm{ML}^{-3}\right)^{\mathrm{d}}$
$\mathrm{T}=\mathrm{M}^{\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{c}+\mathrm{d}} \mathrm{L}^{-3 \mathrm{a}-\mathrm{c}-\mathrm{ad}+\mathrm{b}} \mathrm{T}^{-\mathrm{c}}$
on comparing
$\mathrm{c}=-1 ; \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{c}+\mathrm{d}=0 ;-3 \mathrm{a}-\mathrm{c}-3 \mathrm{~d}+\mathrm{b}=0$
$\mathrm{b}=2 ; \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{d}=1$
$\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}=1$

Question: The velocity of sound in air is doubled when the temperature is raised from $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $\alpha^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$\mathrm{V}=\sqrt{\frac{\gamma \mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{M}}}$
$\frac{\mathrm{V}_{1}}{\mathrm{~V}_{2}}=\frac{\sqrt{\mathrm{T}_{1}}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{~T}_{2}}}$
$\frac{\mathrm{V}_{0}}{2 \mathrm{~V}_{0}}=\sqrt{\frac{273}{\mathrm{~T}_{2}}}$
$\frac{1}{4}=\frac{273}{\mathrm{~T}_{2}}$
$\mathrm{T}_{2}=4 \times 273=\alpha+273$
$\alpha=3 \times 273$
$\alpha=819^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

Question: Suppose there is a uniform circular disc of mass M kg and radius r m shown in figure. The shaded regions are cut out from the disc. The moment of inertia of the remainder about the axis A of the disc is given by $\frac{\mathrm{X}}{256} \mathrm{Mr}^{2}$. The value of x is $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$\mathrm{M}=\sigma \pi \mathrm{R}^{2}$
$\sigma \pi \mathrm{R}^{2}=16 \mathrm{~m}$
$\mathrm{m}=\frac{\sigma \pi \mathrm{R}^{2}}{16}$
$\mathrm{I}_{\text {system }}=\frac{\mathrm{MR}^{2}}{2}-2\left(\frac{\mathrm{mR}^{2}}{2 \times 16}+\frac{9 \mathrm{mR}^{2}}{16}\right)$
$=\frac{\mathrm{MR}^{2}}{2}-2 \times \frac{19 \mathrm{mR}^{2}}{32}$
$=\frac{\mathrm{MR}^{2}}{2}-\frac{19}{16} \mathrm{mR}^{2}$
$=\frac{\mathrm{MR}^{2}}{2}-\frac{19}{256} \mathrm{MR}^{2} \quad$ becoz $\quad \mathrm{m}=\frac{\mathrm{M}}{16}$
$=\frac{(128-19)\left(\mathrm{MR}^{2}\right)}{256}$
$=\frac{109 \mathrm{MR}^{2}}{256}$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Moment of inertia of truncated bodies/combined bodies

Question:
Among the statements :

I : If $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & \cos \alpha & \cos \beta \\ \cos \alpha & 1 & \cos \gamma \\ \cos \beta & \cos \gamma & 1\end{array}\right|=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}0 & \cos \alpha & \cos \beta \\ \cos \alpha & 0 & \cos \gamma \\ \cos \beta & \cos \gamma & 0\end{array}\right|$
, then $\cos ^{2} \alpha+\cos ^{2} \beta+\cos ^{2} \gamma=\frac{3}{2}$, and
II : If $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}x^{2}+x & x+1 & x-2 \\ 2 x^{2}+3 x-1 & 3 x & 3 x-3 \\ x^{2}+2 x+3 & 2 x-1 & 2 x-1\end{array}\right|=p x+q$, then $\mathrm{p}^{2}=196 \mathrm{q}^{2}$,
Solution:
Let $\cos \alpha=\mathrm{x}$
$$
\begin{gathered}
\cos \beta=y \\
\cos \gamma=z \\
\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
0 & x & y \\
x & 0 & z \\
y & z & 0
\end{array}\right|=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1 & x & y \\
x & 1 & z \\
y & z & 1
\end{array}\right|
\end{gathered}
$$

Expending both sides, we get
$$
\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}+\mathrm{z}^{2}=1
$$
i.e. $\cos ^{2} \alpha+\cos ^{2} \beta+\cos ^{2} \gamma=1$
Statement 1 is false
Now,
$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}x^{2}+x & 1+x & x-2 \\ 2 x^{2}+3 x-1 & 3 x & 3 x-3 \\ x^{2}+2 x+3 & 2 x-1 & 2 x-1\end{array}\right|=p x+q$
Put $\mathrm{x}=0$ both sides
$q=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}0 & 1 & -2 \\ -1 & 0 & -3 \\ 3 & -1 & -1\end{array}\right|$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{q}=-12$
Now put $\mathrm{x}=1$ both sides
$p+q=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}2 & 2 & -1 \\ 4 & 3 & 3 \\ 6 & 1 & 1\end{array}\right|=42$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{p}=54$
Now $\frac{\mathrm{p}^{2}}{\mathrm{q}^{2}}=\left(\frac{54}{-12}\right)^{2}+196$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{p}^{2} \neq 196 \mathrm{q}^{2}$
Statement (2) is false
Correct option (1)

Question: The value of $\frac{{ }^{100} \mathrm{C}_{50}}{51}+\frac{{ }^{100} \mathrm{C}_{51}}{52}+\ldots .+\frac{{ }^{100} \mathrm{C}_{100}}{101}$ is :
Solution:
$\mathrm{S}=\sum_{\mathrm{r}=50}^{100} \frac{{ }^{100} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}}{\mathrm{r}+1}=\sum_{\mathrm{r}=50}^{100} \frac{1}{\mathrm{r}+1} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{r}+1}{101} \cdot{ }^{101} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}+1}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& S=\frac{1}{101} \sum_{r=50}^{100}{ }^{101} C_{r+1} \\
& =\frac{1}{101} \times \frac{2^{101}}{2}=\frac{2^{100}}{101}
\end{aligned}
$$

Option (4)

Question: Let the mean and variance of 8 numbers $-10,-7,-1, x, y, 9,2,16$ be $\frac{7}{2}$ and $\frac{293}{4}$, respectively. Then the mean of 4 numbers $\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y}, \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}+1,|\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}|$ is:
Solution:
Mean $=\frac{-18+\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}+2+9+16}{8}=\frac{7}{3}$
$$
\begin{align*}
& =\frac{x+y+9}{8}=\frac{7}{2} \Rightarrow x+y+9=28 \tag{1}\\
& \text { Variance }=\frac{\sum z_{i}^{2}}{8}-(\mu)^{2}=\frac{293}{4} \tag{2}\\
& \Rightarrow \frac{10^{2}+7^{2}+1^{2}+x^{2}+y^{2}+2^{2}+9^{2}+16^{2}}{8}-\left(\frac{7}{2}\right)^{2}=\frac{293}{4}
\end{align*}
$$

Solving (1) & (2) $\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=12, \mathrm{y}=7$
Mean of $(1+x+y), x, y,|y-x|$ is
$\Rightarrow \frac{20+12+7+5}{4}=\frac{44}{4}=11$
Option (1)

Question: The sum of all possible values of $n \in N$, so that the coefficients of $\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{x}^{2}$ and $\mathrm{x}^{3}$ in the expansion of $\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)^{2}(1+\mathrm{x})^{\mathrm{n}}$, are in arithmetic progression is :
Solution:
$\left(x^{4}+2 x^{2}+1\right)\left({ }^{n} C_{0} x^{0}+{ }^{n} C_{1} x^{1}+{ }^{n} C_{2} x^{2}+{ }^{n} C_{3} x^{3}+\ldots\right)$
Coefficient $\mathrm{x} \Rightarrow{ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_{1}$,
coeff. of $x^{2} \Rightarrow 2+{ }^{n} C_{2}$
$2+\frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}-1)}{2}$
Coeff. of $\mathrm{x}^{3}=2 .{ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_{1}+{ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_{3}$
$=2 \mathrm{n}+\frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}-1)(\mathrm{n}-2)}{6} \quad($ if $\mathrm{x} \geq 3)$
Now according to question
$\mathrm{n}+2 \mathrm{n}+\frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}-1)(\mathrm{n}-2)}{6}=2\left[2+\frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}-1)}{2}\right]$
$3 \mathrm{n}+\frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}-1)(\mathrm{n}-2)}{6}=4+\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}-1)$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}^{3}-9 \mathrm{n}^{2}+26 \mathrm{n}-24=0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=2,3,4 \quad \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=3,4$
Now checking for $\mathrm{n}=2$
$\left.\begin{array}{l}\text { Coeff. of } x=2 \\ \text { Coeff. of } x^{2}=3 \\ \text { Coeff. of } x^{3}=4\end{array}\right\} \Rightarrow$ are in A.P.
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=2$ is also the correct choice
Required sum of values of ' n '
$=2+3+4=9$
Option (4)

Question: The vertices B and C of a triangle ABC lie on the line $\frac{\mathrm{x}}{1}=\frac{1-\mathrm{y}}{-2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-2}{3}$. The coordinates of A and B are $(1,6,3)$ and $(4,9, \alpha)$ respectively and C is at a distance of 10 units from B . The area (in sq. units) of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ is:
Solution:
$\frac{4}{1}=\frac{9-1}{2}=\frac{\alpha-2}{3} \Rightarrow \alpha=14$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \overrightarrow{\mathrm{AD}} \cdot(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}})=0 \\
& (\lambda-1) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(2 \lambda-5) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(3 \lambda-1) \hat{\mathrm{k}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AD}} \\
& \Rightarrow \lambda-1+4 \lambda-10+9 \lambda-3=0 \\
& \Rightarrow 14 \lambda=14 \Rightarrow \lambda=1 \\
& \mathrm{D}=(1,3,5) \\
& \mathrm{AD}=\sqrt{3^{2}+2^{2}}=\sqrt{13} \\
& \operatorname{Ar}(\triangle \mathrm{ABC})=\frac{1}{2} \times \sqrt{13} \times 10=5 \sqrt{13}
\end{aligned}
$$

Option (A)

Question: Number of solutions of $\sqrt{3} \cos 2 \theta+8 \cos \theta+3 \sqrt{3}=0, \theta \in[-3 \pi, 2 \pi]$ is:
Solution:
$\sqrt{3}\left(2 \cos ^{2} \theta-1\right)+8 \cos \theta+3 \sqrt{3}=0$
$2 \sqrt{3} \cos ^{2} \theta+8 \cos \theta+2 \sqrt{3}=0$
$(\sqrt{3} \cos +1)(\cos \theta+\sqrt{3})=0$
$\cos \theta=-\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$
as $-\sqrt{3}$ (reject)
$\therefore \theta=$ will have 5 value in $[-3 \pi, 2 \pi]$
Ans. $=5 \Rightarrow$ option (2)

Question: Let $S=\{z: 3 \leq|2 z-3(1+i)| \leq 7\}$ be a set of complex numbers. Then $\operatorname{Min}_{z \in S}\left|\left(z+\frac{1}{2}(5+3 i)\right)\right|$ is equal to :
Solution:
$\frac{3}{2} \leq\left|z-\frac{3}{2}(1+i)\right| \leq \frac{7}{2}$

$\operatorname{Min}_{\mathrm{z} \in \mathrm{s}}\left|\mathrm{z}-\left(\frac{-5}{2}-\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{i}\right)\right|=\mathrm{PB}$
$\mathrm{PB}=\mathrm{PC}-\frac{7}{2} \Rightarrow 5-\frac{7}{2} \Rightarrow \frac{3}{2}$
Option (2)

Question:
Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ respectively be the maximum and the minimum values of the function
$$
\begin{aligned}
f(\theta)=4 & \left(\sin ^{4}\left(\frac{7 \pi}{2}-\theta\right)+\sin ^{4}(11 \pi+\theta)\right) \\
& -2\left(\sin ^{6}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{2}-\theta\right)+\sin ^{6}(9 \pi-\theta)\right), \theta \in \mathbf{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

Then $\alpha+2 \beta$ is equal to :
Solution:
$f(\theta)=4\left(\sin ^{4}\left(\frac{7 \pi}{2}-\theta\right)+\sin ^{4}(11 \pi+\theta)\right)-2\left(\sin ^{6}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{2}-\theta\right)+\sin ^{6}(9 \pi-\theta)\right)$
$f(\theta)=4\left(\cos ^{4}(\theta)+\sin ^{4}(\theta)\right)-2\left(\cos ^{6} \theta+\sin ^{6} \theta\right)$
$f(\theta)=4\left(1-2 \sin ^{2} \theta \cos ^{2} \theta\right)-2\left(1-3 \sin ^{2} \theta \cos ^{2} \theta\right)$
$f(\theta)=2-2 \sin ^{2} \theta \cos ^{2} \theta$
$f(\theta)=2-\frac{\sin ^{2}(2 \theta)}{2}$
$\alpha=\mathrm{f}(\theta)_{\text {max }}=2$
$\beta=f(\theta)_{\text {min }}=\frac{3}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \alpha+2 \beta=5$
Ans. $=5$ option ( 2 )

Question: Let the domain of the function $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\log _{3} \log _{5} \log _{7} \left(9 \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x}^{2}-13\right)$ be the interval $(\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{n})$. Let the hyperbola $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$ have eccentricity $\frac{n}{3}$ and the length of the latus rectum $\frac{8 m}{3}$. Then $b^{2}-a^{2}$ is equal to :
Solution:
$\log _{5}\left(\log _{7}\left(9 \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x}^{2}-13\right)\right)>0$
$ \Rightarrow 9x - {x^2} - 13 > 7$
${x^2} - 9x + 20 < 0 \Rightarrow 4 < x < 5$
$m = 4,n = 5$
$ \Rightarrow e = \sqrt {1 + \frac{{{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}} = \frac{5}{3} \Rightarrow \frac{{{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}} = \frac{{25}}{9} - 1 = \frac{{16}}{9}$
$\frac{b}{a} = \frac{4}{3}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{2{b^2}}}{a} = \frac{{8m}}{3} \Rightarrow \frac{{2{b^2}}}{a} = \frac{{32}}{3}$
$ \Rightarrow 2{b^2} = \frac{{32}}{3} \times \frac{{3b}}{4} \Rightarrow b = 4,a = 3$
${b^2} - {a^2} = 16 - 9 = 7$

Question: A building construction work can be completed by two masons A and B together in 22.5 days. Mason A alone can complete the construction work in 24 days less than mason B alone. Then mason A alone will complete the construction work in:
Solution:
Let time taken by mason a alone to complete the work be in x days so, mason B along take $x+24$ days
work done by A in 1 day $=\frac{1}{x}$
work done by B in 1 day $=\frac{1}{x+24}$
so work done by $\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}$ in 1 day $=\frac{1}{22.5}$
So, $\frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{x+24}=\frac{2}{45}$
$\mathrm{x}^{2}-21 \mathrm{x}-540=0$
$\mathrm{x}=36 \&$
$\mathrm{x}=-15($ rejected $)$
Ans. $=36$ option (4)

Question: Let $f(x)=\int \frac{\left(2-x^{2}\right) \cdot e^{x}}{(\sqrt{1+x})(1-x)^{3 / 2}} d x$. If $f(0)-0$, then $\mathrm{f}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)$ is equal to :
Solution:
$\int \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}\left(\frac{\left(1-\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)+1}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}} \cdot(1-\mathrm{x})^{3 / 2}}\right) \mathrm{dx}$
$\int \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}\left(\frac{\left(1-\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}} \cdot(1-\mathrm{x})^{3 / 2}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}} \cdot(1-\mathrm{x})^{3 / 2}}\right) \mathrm{dx}$
$\int \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}\left(\sqrt{\frac{1+\mathrm{x}}{1-\mathrm{x}}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}} \cdot(1-\mathrm{x})^{3 / 2}}\right) \mathrm{dx}$
$=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \sqrt{\frac{1+\mathrm{x}}{1-\mathrm{x}}}+\mathrm{C}$
$\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \sqrt{\frac{1+\mathrm{x}}{1-\mathrm{x}}}-1$
$\mathrm{f}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\sqrt{3 \mathrm{e}}-1$

Question: Let $\vec{a}=-\hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}, \vec{b}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}-3 \hat{k}, \vec{c}=\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$ and $\vec{d}=\vec{c} \times \vec{a}$. Then $(\vec{a}-\vec{b}) \cdot \vec{d}$ is equal to :
Solution:
$\vec{d}=(\vec{a} \times \vec{b}) \times \vec{a}$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}=\left(\mathrm{a}^{2}\right) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}-(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}=6 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{~b}}+8 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}$
$(\vec{a}-\vec{b}) \cdot \vec{d}=(\vec{a}-\vec{b}) \cdot(6 \vec{b}+8 \vec{a})$
$=8 \mathrm{a}^{2}-6 \mathrm{~b}^{2}-2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}$
$=48-66+16=-2$

Question: A rectangle is formed by the lines $\mathrm{x}=0, \mathrm{y}=0$, $\mathrm{x}=3$ and $\mathrm{y}=4$. Let the line L be perpendicular to $3 x+y+6=0$ and divide the area of the rectangle into two equal parts. Then the distance of the point $\left(\frac{1}{2},-5\right)$ from the line L is equal to :
Solution:
Line is $\mathrm{y}=\frac{\mathrm{X}}{3}+\mathrm{C}$
Line passes thru $\left(\frac{3}{2}, 2\right)$
$2=\frac{1}{2}+\mathrm{C} \Rightarrow \mathrm{C}=\frac{3}{2}$
$\mathrm{y}=\frac{\mathrm{x}}{3}+\frac{3}{2}$
$\Rightarrow 6 \mathrm{y}=2 \mathrm{x}+9$
Line is $2 x-6 y+9=0 \&$
Dist $=\left|\frac{1+30+9}{\sqrt{40}}\right|=\sqrt{40}=2 \sqrt{10}$

Question: Let the line $y-x=1$ intersect the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{2}+\frac{y^{2}}{1}=1$ at the points A and B . Then the angle made by the line segment AB at the center of the ellipse is :
Solution:
By solving line & equation of ellipse we get $\mathrm{x}=0$
& $\mathrm{x}=-\frac{4}{3}$
$\therefore \mathrm{B}\left(-\frac{4}{3},-\frac{1}{3}\right)$
$\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{OB}}=\tan \theta=\frac{1}{4}$
$\because \angle \mathrm{AOB}=\frac{\pi}{2}+\theta=\frac{\pi}{2}+\tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{4}$

Question: Let $\mathrm{A}=\{-2,-1,0,1,2,3,4\}$. Let R be a relation on A defined by xRy if and only if $2 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y} \leq 2$. Let $l$ be the number of elements in R . Let m and n be the minimum number of elements required to be added in R to make it reflexive and symmetric relations respectively. Then $l+\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}$ is equal to :
Solution:
$\mathrm{R}\{(-2, \mathrm{a}),(-1, \mathrm{~b}),(0, \mathrm{c}),(1, \mathrm{~d}),(2, \mathrm{e})\}$
$\mathrm{a}=\{-2,-1,0,1,2,3,4\} ; \mathrm{b}=\{-2,-1,0,1,2,3,4\}$
$\mathrm{c}=\{-2,-1,0,1,2\} ; \mathrm{d}=\{-2,-1,0\}$
$\mathrm{e}=\{-2\}$
âˆ´ No. of elements in R
$=7+7+5+3+1=23=\ell$
Minimum number of element to be added to make it reflexive $=m=4 \Rightarrow\{(1,1),(2,2),(3,3),(4,4)\}$ minimum number of element to be added to make it symmetric $=\mathrm{n}=6$
for ' n '
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{R}=\{(3,-2),(4,-2),(2,-1),(2,0),(3,-1),(4,-1)\}$
$\therefore \ell+\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}=23+4+6=33$

Question:
Let $y=y(x)$ be the solution of the differential equation
$x^{4} d y+\left(4 x^{3} y+2 \sin x\right) d x=0, x>0, y\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=0$.
Then $\pi^{4} \mathrm{y}\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)$ is equal to :
Solution:
$\left(x^{4} d y+4 x^{3} y d x\right)=-2 \sin x d x$
$\Rightarrow \int \mathrm{d}\left(\mathrm{x}^{4} \mathrm{y}\right)=\int-2 \sin \mathrm{xdx}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}^{4} \mathrm{y}=2 \cos \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{c}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}^{4} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=2 \cos \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{c}$
As $\mathrm{f}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=0$
So, $\mathrm{c}=0$
$\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)^{4} \mathrm{f}\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=2 \cos \frac{\pi}{3}$
$\pi^{4} \mathrm{f}\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=81$

Question: If $\alpha$ and $\beta(\alpha<\beta)$ are the roots of the equation $(-2+\sqrt{3})(|\sqrt{x}-3|)+(x-6 \sqrt{x})+(9-2 \sqrt{3})=0$, $x \geq 0$, then $\sqrt{\frac{\beta}{\alpha}}+\sqrt{\alpha \beta}$ is equal to :
Solution:
$\quad(x-6 \sqrt{x}+9)-(2-\sqrt{3})|\sqrt{x}-3|-2 \sqrt{3}=0$
$\Rightarrow|\sqrt{\mathrm{x}}-3|^{2}-(2-\sqrt{3})|\sqrt{\mathrm{x}}-3|-2 \sqrt{3}=0$
$\Rightarrow|\sqrt{\mathrm{x}}-3|=2$ or $|\sqrt{\mathrm{x}}-3|=-\sqrt{3}$ (not possible)
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{\mathrm{x}}=1$ or 5
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=1$ or 5
$\Rightarrow \alpha=1$ and $\beta=25$

Aliter:
Let $\mathrm{x} \geq 9$, let $\sqrt{\mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{t} \Rightarrow \mathrm{t} \geq 3$
$(\sqrt{3}-2)(\mathrm{t}-3)+(\mathrm{t}-3)^{2}-2 \sqrt{3}=0$
Let $\mathrm{t}-3=\mathrm{u}$
$\mathrm{u}^{2}+(\sqrt{3}-2) \mathrm{u}-2 \sqrt{3}=0$
$\mathrm{u}=2$, or $\mathrm{u}=-\sqrt{3}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{t}-3=2$ or $\mathrm{t}-3=-\sqrt{3}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=5$ or $\mathrm{t}=3-\sqrt{3} \quad$ (rejected)
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=25$
Now let $0<\mathrm{x}<9$,
$-(\sqrt{3}-2)(\mathrm{t}-3)+(\mathrm{t}-3)^{2}-2 \sqrt{3}=0$
let $\mathrm{t}-3=\mathrm{u}$
$\mathrm{u}^{2}-(\sqrt{3}-2) \mathrm{u}-2 \sqrt{3}=0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{u}=\sqrt{3}$ or $\mathrm{u}=-2$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=3+\sqrt{3}$ (rejected) or $\mathrm{t}-3=-2$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=1 \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=1$
$\alpha=1, \beta=25$
Now $\sqrt{\frac{\beta}{\alpha}}+\sqrt{\alpha \beta}=\sqrt{25}+\sqrt{25}=10$

Question:
Let $f(x)= \begin{cases}\frac{a x^{2}+2 a x+3}{4 x^{2}+4 x-3}, & x \neq-\frac{3}{2}, \frac{1}{2} \\ b & x=-\frac{3}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\end{cases}$
be continuous at $x=-\frac{3}{2}$. If $\operatorname{fof}(x)=\frac{7}{5}$, then $x$ is equal to :
Solution:
$f(x)=\left\{ egin{array}{cc} rac{a x^{2}+2 a x+3}{(2 x-1)(2 x+3)} & ; \quad x eq rac{-3}{2}, rac{1}{2} \ b & ; \quad x= rac{-3}{2}, rac{1}{2}\end{array} ight.$
for continuous at $\mathrm{x}= rac{-3}{2}$
$\mathrm{LHL}=\mathrm{RHL}$
$\Rightarrow \lim _{x ightarrow rac{-3}{2}} rac{\left(a x^{2}+2 a x+3 ight)}{(2 x-1)(2 x+3)}$
at $x= rac{-3}{2} \Rightarrow$ Numerator $=0$
$\mathrm{a}\left( rac{-3}{2} ight)^{2}+2 \mathrm{a}\left( rac{-3}{2} ight)+3=0$
$ rac{9}{4} a-3 a+3=0$
$ rac{3 \mathrm{a}}{4}=3 \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=4$
$ herefore f(x)=\left\{ egin{array}{ccc} rac{4 x^{2}+8 x+3}{(2 x-1)(2 x+3)} & ; & x eq rac{-3}{2}, rac{1}{2} \ b & ; & x= rac{-3}{2}, rac{1}{2}\end{array} ight.$
$f(x)=\left\{ egin{array}{ccl} rac{(2 x+1)(2 x+3)}{(2 x-1)(2 x+3)} & ; & x eq rac{-3}{2}, rac{1}{2} \ b & ; x= rac{-3}{2}, rac{1}{2}\end{array} ight.$
$f \circ f(x)=f\left( rac{2 x+1}{2 x-1} ight)= rac{2\left( rac{2 x+1}{2 x-1} ight)+1}{2\left( rac{2 x+1}{2 x-1} ight)-1}$
$= rac{6 \mathrm{x}+1}{2 \mathrm{x}+3}= rac{7}{5}$
So $\mathrm{x}=1$ Ans.

Question:
Let the direction cosines of two lines satisfy the equations: $4 \ell+m-n=0$ and $2 m n+10 n \ell+3 \ell m=0$.
Then the cosine of the acute angle between these lines is :
Solution:
Direction cosines of two lines satisfy the equation $\Rightarrow 4 \ell+\mathrm{m}-\mathrm{n}=0$
$$
\begin{equation*}
2 \mathrm{mn}+10 \mathrm{n} \ell+3 \ell \mathrm{~m}=0 \tag{2}
\end{equation*}
$$
& we know
$$
\begin{equation*}
\Rightarrow \ell^{2}+\mathrm{m}^{2}-\mathrm{n}^{2}=1 \tag{3}
\end{equation*}
$$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=4 \ell+\mathrm{m}$ putting in eqn.
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}(2 \mathrm{~m}+10 \ell)+3 \ell \mathrm{~m}=0$
$\Rightarrow(4 \ell+\mathrm{m})(2 \mathrm{~m}+10 \ell)+3 \ell \mathrm{~m}=0$
$\Rightarrow 8 \ell \mathrm{~m}+40 \ell^{2}+2 \mathrm{~m}^{2}+10 \ell \mathrm{~m}+3 \ell \mathrm{~m}=0$
$\Rightarrow 40 \ell^{2}+21 \ell \mathrm{~m}+2 \mathrm{~m}^{2}=0$
$\Rightarrow(8 \ell+m)(5 \ell+2 m)=0$

Case 1: $8 \ell+\mathrm{m}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{~m}=-8 \ell$

Case 2: $5 \ell+2 \mathrm{~m}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{~m}=\frac{-5}{2} \ell$

So direction ratio of $\mathrm{L}_{1}$ is $\ell,-8 \ell,-4 \ell$
& direction ratio of $\mathrm{L}_{2}$ is $\ell, \frac{-5 \ell}{2}, \frac{3 \ell}{2}$
$\cos \theta=\left|\frac{\ell^{2}+20 \ell^{2}-6 \ell^{2}}{\sqrt{\ell^{2}+64 \ell^{2}+16 \ell^{2}} \sqrt{\ell^{2}+\frac{25 \ell^{2}}{4}+\frac{9 \ell^{2}}{4}}}\right|$
$=\frac{15 \ell^{2}}{(9 \ell) \frac{\sqrt{38} \ell}{2}}=\frac{10}{3 \sqrt{38}}$

Ans. $=\frac{10}{3 \sqrt{38}}$

Question: Let $|\mathrm{A}|=6$, where A is a $3 \times 3$ matrix. If $\left|\operatorname{adj}\left(3 \operatorname{adj}\left(\mathrm{~A}^{2} . \operatorname{adj}(2 \mathrm{~A})\right)\right)\right|=2^{\mathrm{m}} .3^{\mathrm{n}}, \mathrm{m}, \mathrm{n} \in \mathbf{N}$, then $\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$\quad \operatorname{adj} 2 \mathrm{~A}=2^{2} \operatorname{adj} \mathrm{~A} \quad \because \operatorname{adj} \mathrm{kA}=\mathrm{k}^{\mathrm{n}-1}(\operatorname{adj} \mathrm{~A})$
$$
\text { = } 4 \text { adj A }
$$

Now $\mathrm{A}^{2}(\operatorname{adj} 2 \mathrm{~A})=4 \mathrm{~A}(\operatorname{adj} \mathrm{~A})$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& =4 \mathrm{~A}|\mathrm{~A}| \mathrm{I}_{3} \\
& =24 \mathrm{~A}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now 3 adj $\left(\mathrm{A}^{2}(\operatorname{adj} 2 \mathrm{~A})\right)=3 \operatorname{adj}(24 \mathrm{~A})$
$$
=3 .(24)^{2} \operatorname{adj} \mathrm{~A}
$$

Now $\left|\operatorname{adj}\left(3 \operatorname{adj}\left(\mathrm{~A}^{2}(\operatorname{adj} 2 \mathrm{~A})\right)\right)\right|$
$=\left|\operatorname{adj}\left(3 .(24)^{2} \operatorname{adj} \mathrm{~A}\right)\right|$
$=\mid\left(3 .(24)^{2}\right)^{2}($ adj adjA $) \mid$
$=\left|3^{6} .2^{12} \operatorname{adj} \operatorname{adj} \mathrm{~A}\right|$
$=\left(3^{6} .2^{12}\right)^{3}|\operatorname{adj} \operatorname{adj} \mathrm{~A}|$
$=3^{18} .2^{36} .(\mathrm{A})^{4}$
$3^{22} .2^{40}$
$\therefore \mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}=62$

Question: Let the area of the region bounded by the curve $y=\max \{\sin x, \cos x\}, \operatorname{lines} x=0, x=\frac{3 \pi}{2}$, and the x -axis be A . Then, $\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{2}$ is equal to
Solution:
$\mathrm{A}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \cos \mathrm{xdx}+\int_{\pi / 4}^{\pi} \sin \mathrm{xdx}+\int_{\pi}^{5 \pi / 4}-\sin \mathrm{xdx}+\int_{5 \pi / 4}^{3 \pi / 2}-\cos \mathrm{xdx}$
$\mathrm{A}=(\sin \mathrm{x})_{0}^{\pi / 4}+(\cos \mathrm{x})_{\pi}^{\pi / 4}+(\cos \mathrm{x})_{\pi}^{5 \pi / 4}+(\sin \mathrm{x})_{3 \pi / 2}^{5 \pi / 4}$
$\mathrm{A}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+1+1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=3$
$\mathrm{A}^{2}+\mathrm{A}=12$

Question:
Let f be a twice differentiable non-negative function such that
$(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}))^{2}=25+\int_{0}^{\mathrm{x}}\left((\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{t}))^{2}+\left(\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{t})\right)^{2}\right) \mathrm{dt}$. Then the mean of $f\left(\log _{e}(1)\right), f\left(\log _{e}(2)\right), \ldots . ., f\left(\log _{e}(625)\right)$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$‚
Solution:
$2 f(x) f^{\prime}(x)=f^{2}(x)+\left(f^{\prime}(x)\right)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow\left(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})-\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})\right)^{2}=0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})$
$\Rightarrow \ell \mathrm{n}(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}))=\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{c} \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{c}^{\prime} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}$
$\mathrm{f}(0)=5 \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=5 \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}$
Mean $=\frac{\mathrm{f}(\ell \mathrm{n} 1)+\mathrm{f}(\ell \mathrm{n} 2)+\ldots \ldots \ldots .+\mathrm{f}(\ell \mathrm{n} 625)}{625}$
$=\frac{5[1+2+\ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots+625]}{625}=1565$

Question: From the first 100 natural numbers, two numbers first a and then b are selected randomly without replacement. If the probability that $\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{b} \geq 10$ is $\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{n}}, \operatorname{gcd}(\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{n})=1$, then $\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$a-b \geq 10$
Total cases $=100 \times 99$
Fav. Cases $=1+2+3+\ldots 90$
Req. Prob $=\frac{1+2+\ldots+90}{100 \times 99}$
$\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{90\left(\frac{91}{2}\right)}{100(99)}=\frac{91}{220}$
$\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}=311$

Question: The number of 4-letter words, with or without meaning, which can be formed using the letters PQRPQRSTUVP, is $\_\_\_\_$‚
Solution:
$\mathrm{P} \rightarrow 3, \mathrm{Q} \rightarrow 2, \mathrm{R} \rightarrow 2, \mathrm{~S}, \mathrm{~T}, \mathrm{U}, \mathrm{V}$
Case I 3 alike, 1 different
${ }^{1} \mathrm{C}_{1} \times{ }^{6} \mathrm{C}_{1} \times \frac{4!}{3!}=24$
Case II 2 alike, 2 alike
${ }^{3} \mathrm{C}_{2} \times \frac{4!}{2!2!}=18$
Case III 2 alike, 2 different
${ }^{3} \mathrm{C}_{1} \times{ }^{6} \mathrm{C}_{2} \times \frac{4!}{2!}=540$
Case IV All 4 different
${ }^{7} \mathrm{C}_{4} \times 4!=840$
Total words $=1422$

Question:
' W ' g of a non-volatile electrolyte solid solute of molar mass ' M ' $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ when dissolved in 100 mL water, decreases vapour pressure of water from 640 mm Hg to 600 mm Hg . If aqueous solution of the electrolyte boils at 375 K and $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}$ for water is $0.52 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, then the mole fraction of the electrolyte solute ( $\mathrm{x}_{2}$ ) in the solution can be expressed as
(Given : density of water $=1 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}$ and boiling point of water $=373 \mathrm{~K}$ )
Solution:
$\quad \mathrm{P}^{\circ}=640 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg}$
$\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{s}}=600 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg}$
$\Delta \mathrm{P}=40 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg}$
moles of solute $=\frac{W}{M}$
$\frac{\Delta \mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{P}^{\mathrm{o}}}=\mathrm{i} . \mathrm{X}_{\text {solute }}$
Again :
$\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=\mathrm{i} \times \mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{b}} \times \mathrm{m}$
$2=\mathrm{i} \times 0.52 \times \frac{\mathrm{W} / \mathrm{M}}{100} \times 1000$
$\mathrm{i}=\frac{2}{5.2} \times \frac{\mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{W}}$
$\mathrm{X}_{\text {solute }}=\frac{40}{640} \times \frac{1}{\mathrm{i}}=\frac{1}{16} \times \frac{5.2}{2} \times \frac{\mathrm{W}}{\mathrm{M}}$
$\mathrm{X}_{\text {solute }}=\frac{1.3}{8} \times \frac{\mathrm{W}}{\mathrm{M}}$

Question:
Given below are two statements
Statements-I: The number of paramagnetic species among $\left[\mathrm{CoF}_{6}\right]^{3-}, \quad\left[\mathrm{TiF}_{6}\right]^{3-}, \quad \mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ and $\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}\right]^{3-}$ is 3 .
Statement-II: $\mathrm{K}_{4}\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}\right]<\mathrm{K}_{3}\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}\right]< \left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right] \mathrm{SO}_{4} \cdot \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ 
$<\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{3}$ is the correct order in terms of number of unpaired electron(s) in the complexes.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
Options: Both statement-I and statement-II are true

Question:
At $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in presence of a catalyst, activation energy of a reaction is lowered by $10 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. The logarithm ratio of $\frac{\mathrm{k} \text { (catalysed) }}{\mathrm{k} \text { (uncatalysed) }}$ is ....
(Consider that the frequency factor for both the reactions is same)
Solution:
$\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\text {catalyst }}}{\mathrm{K}_{\text {uncatalyst }}}=\mathrm{e}^{\frac{\Delta \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{\mathrm{RT}}}$
$\ln \frac{\mathrm{K}_{\text {catalyst }}}{\mathrm{K}_{\text {uncatalyst }}}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{\mathrm{RT}}$
$\log \frac{\mathrm{K}_{\text {catalyst }}}{\mathrm{K}_{\text {uncatalyst }}}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{2.303 \mathrm{RT}}$
$=\frac{10 \times 1000}{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 300}$
$\log \frac{\mathrm{K}_{\text {catalyst }}}{\mathrm{K}_{\text {uncatalyst }}}=1.741$

Question:
Match the List-I with List-II
List-I (Isothermal process for ideal gas system)List-II Work done ( $\mathbf{V}_{\mathbf{f}} \boldsymbol{>} \mathbf{V}_{\mathbf{i}}$ ) | |
A. Reversible expansionI. $\mathrm{w}=0$
B. Free expansionII. $\mathrm{w}=-\mathrm{nRT} \ln \frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{f}}}{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{i}}}$
C. Irreversible expansionIII. $\mathrm{w}=-\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{ex}}\left(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{i}}\right)$
D. Irreversible compressionIV. $\mathrm{w}=-\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{ex}}\left(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{i}}-\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{f}}\right)$

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
Solution:
(A) $\mathrm{W}_{\text {Rev. }}=-\int \mathrm{P}_{\text {gas }} \mathrm{dV}$
$\mathrm{W}_{\text {Rev. Isot. Exp. }}=-\mathrm{nRT} \ln \left[\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{f}}}{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{i}}}\right]$
A) → (II)
B) Free expansion
$\mathrm{W}_{\text {irrev. }}=-\mathrm{P}_{\text {ext }} \Delta \mathrm{V}$
$\mathrm{P}_{\text {ext }}=0$
$\mathrm{W}=0$
B) $\rightarrow$ (I)
C) Irreversible expansion
$\mathrm{W}_{\text {irrev. }}=-\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{ext}} \Delta \mathrm{V}$
$\mathrm{W}_{\text {irrev. }}=-\mathrm{P}_{\text {ext }}\left(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{i}}\right)$
C) → (III)
D) Irreversible compression
$\mathrm{W}_{\text {irrev. }}=-\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{ext}} \Delta \mathrm{V}$
$\mathrm{W}_{\text {irrev. }}=-\mathrm{P}_{\text {ext }}\left(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{i}}-\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{f}}\right)$

Question:
A student is given one compound among the following compounds that gives positive test with Tollen's reagent.

The compound is :

$\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{D}$ is an endothermic reaction occurring in three steps (elementary).
(i) $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}; \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{i}}=+\mathrm{ve}$
(ii) $\mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}; \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{ii}}=-\mathrm{ve}$
(iii) $\mathrm{C} \rightarrow \mathrm{D}; \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\text {iii }}=-\mathrm{ve}$
Which of the following graphs between potential energy ( y -axis) vs reaction coordinate ( x -axis) correctly represents the reaction profile of $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{D}$ ?
Solution:
Given :
$\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{D} ; \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}=(+) \mathrm{ve}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{D}}-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{A}} \therefore \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{D}}>\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{A}}$
Mechanism
$\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{B} ; \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}=(+) \mathrm{ve} \Rightarrow \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{B}}>\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{A}}$
$\mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{C} ; \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}=(-) \mathrm{ve} \Rightarrow \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{C}}<\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{B}}$
$\mathrm{C} \rightarrow \mathrm{D} ; \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}=(-) \mathrm{ve} \Rightarrow \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{D}}<\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{C}}$

Question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement-I: Hybridisation, shape and spin only magnetic moment of $\mathrm{K}_{3}\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{CO}_{3}\right)_{3}\right]$ is $\mathrm{sp}^{3} \mathrm{d}^{2}$, octahedral and 4.9 BM respectively.
Statement-II: Geometry, hybridisation and spin only magnetic moment values (BM) of the ions $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_{4}\right]^{2-},\left[\mathrm{MnBr}_{4}\right]^{2-}$ and $\left[\mathrm{CoF}_{6}\right]^{3-}$ respectively are square planar, tetrahedral, octahedral : $\mathrm{dsp}^{2}, \mathrm{sp}^{3}$, $\mathrm{sp}^{3} \mathrm{d}^{2}$ and 0, 5.9, 4.9.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
Options: Both statement-I and statement-II are false
Statement I is false but statement-II is true
Both statement-I and statement-II are true
Statement-I is true but statement-II is false
Solution:
In $\mathrm{K}_{3}\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{CO}_{3}\right)_{3}\right] \Rightarrow \mathrm{sp}^{3} \mathrm{d}^{2}$ hybridized, octahedral
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow 4 \text { unpaired electron } \\
& \Rightarrow 4.9 \text { B.M. }
\end{aligned}
$$
$\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_{4}\right]^{2-} \quad \Rightarrow \mathrm{dsp}^{2}$ hybridized, square planar
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow 0 \text { unpaired electron } \\
& \Rightarrow 0 \text { B.M. }
\end{aligned}
$$
$\left[\mathrm{MnBr}_{4}\right]^{2-} \quad \Rightarrow \mathrm{sp}^{3}$ hybridized , tetrahedral
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow 5 \text { unpaired electron } \\
& \Rightarrow 5.9 \text { B.M. }
\end{aligned}
$$
$\left[\mathrm{CoF}_{6}\right]^{3-} \quad \Rightarrow \mathrm{sp}^{3} \mathrm{d}^{2}$ hybridized, octahedral
⇒ 4 unpaired electron
⇒ 4.9 B.M.

Question:
Electricity is passed through an acidic solution of $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ till all the $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ was exhausted, leading to the deposition of 300 mg of Cu metal. However, a current of 600 mA was continued to pass through the same solution for another 28 minutes by keeping the total volume of the solution fixed at 200 mL . The total volume of oxygen evolved at STP during the entire process is _________ mL.
(Nearest integer)
[Given :
$\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{s}) \mathrm{E}_{\text {red }}^{0}=+0.34 \mathrm{~V}$
$\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+4 \mathrm{H}^{+}+4 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \ \mathrm{E}_{\text {red }}^{0}=+1.23 \mathrm{~V}$
Molar mass of $\mathrm{Cu}=63.54 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
Molar mass of $\mathrm{O}_{2}=32 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
Faraday Constant $=96500 \mathrm{C} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$
Molar volume at $\mathrm{STP}=22.4 \mathrm{~L}$ ]
Solution:
Eq of $\mathrm{Cu}=\mathrm{Eq}$ of $\mathrm{O}_{2} \frac{300 \times 10^{-3} \times 2}{63.54}=\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}} \times 4$
$2.36 \times 10^{-3}=\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$
When current is further passed
$\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}} \times 4=\frac{600 \times 28 \times 60}{96500 \times 1000}$
$\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}=2.611 \times 10^{-3}$
Total $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ released
$=\left[10^{-3} \times(2.36+2.611)\right] \times 22400 \mathrm{ml}$
$=111.35 \mathrm{ml}$

Question:

Identify the CORRECT statements.
A. The dipole moment value of $\mathrm{NF}_{3}$ is higher than that of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$.
B. The dipole moment value of $\mathrm{BeH}_{2}$ is zero.
C. The bond order of $\mathrm{O}_{2}^{2-}$ and $\mathrm{F}_{2}$ is same.
D. The formal charge on the central oxygen atom of ozone is -1 .
E. In $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$, all the three atoms satisfy the octet rule, hence it is very stable.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
Solution:
(A) Dipole moment : $\mathrm{NF}_{3}<\mathrm{NH}_{3}$
(B) $\mathrm{BeH}_{2}$ is 'sp' hybridized, linear molecule with zero dipole moment.
(C) $\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-2} \Rightarrow$ bond order $=1$
$\mathrm{F}_{2} \Rightarrow$ bond order $=1$
(D) Formal charge on central oxygen atom in $\mathrm{O}_{3}$ is +1 .
(E) In $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$; nitrogen does not follow octet rule.
