Question:
In Dumas method for estimation of nitrogen, 0.50 g of an organic compound gave 70 mL of nitrogen collected at 300 K and 715 mm pressure. The percentage of nitrogen in the organic compound is ______ %
(Aqueous tension at 300 K is 15 mm ).
Solution:
$\quad \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}=(715-15) \mathrm{mm}=\frac{700}{760} \mathrm{~atm}$
$\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}=70 \mathrm{ml}=\frac{70}{1000} \mathrm{l}$
$n_{N_{2}}=\frac{P V}{R T}=\frac{\left(\frac{700}{760}\right) \times\left(\frac{70}{1000}\right)}{0.0821 \times 300}$
$\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}=\frac{700}{760} \times \frac{\frac{70}{1000}}{0.0821 \times 300} \times 28$
$\% \mathrm{~N}=\frac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}}{0.5} \times 100=\frac{700}{760} \times \frac{\frac{70 / 1000}{0.0821 \times 300} \times 28}{0.5} \times 100$
$=14.65 \% \approx 15$

Question: Consider a mixture ' X ' which is made by dissolving 0.4 mol of $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{SO}_{4}\right] \mathrm{Br}$ and 0.4 mol of $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{Br}\right] \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ in water to make 4 L of solution. When 2 L of mixture ' X ' is allowed to react with excess of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$, it forms precipitate ' Y '. The rest 2 L of mixture ' X ' reacts with excess $\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$ to form precipitate ' $Z$ '. Which of the following statements is CORRECT.
Options: ' Y ' is $\mathrm{BaSO}_{4}$ and ' Z ' is AgBr
Solution:
$0.4 \mathrm{~mol}\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{SO}_{4}\right] \mathrm{Br}+0.4 \mathrm{~mol}\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{Br}\right] \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ are present in 4 lit. solution.
2 lit. of mixture will contain 0.2 mol of each complex.
2 lit. mixture on reaction with excess $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}, 0.2$ mole AgBr will be formed (Y).
2 lit. mixture on reaction with excess $\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}, 0.2$ mole $\mathrm{BaSO}_{4}$ will be formed (Z).

Question:
Consider two Group IV metal ions $\mathrm{X}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Y}^{2+}$.
A solution containing $0.01M  \ \mathrm{X}^{2+}$ and $0.01M\ \mathrm{Y}^{2+}$ is saturated with $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{S}$. The pH at which the metal sulphide YS will form as a precipitate is ______
(Nearest integer)
(Given :
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}(\mathrm{XS})=1 \times 10^{-22}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}(\mathrm{YS})=4 \times 10^{-16}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$,
$\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{S}\right]=0.1 \mathrm{M}$ in solution, $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a} 1} \times \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a} 2}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{S}\right)=1.0 \times 10^{-21}$, $\log 2=0.30, \log 3=0.48, \log 5=0.70$ )
Solution:
$\mathrm{XS}(\mathrm{s}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{X}^{+2}$ (aq.) $+\mathrm{S}^{2-}$ (aq.)
For precipitation of XS(s)
$\left[\mathrm{X}^{+2}\right]\left[\mathrm{S}^{2-}\right] \geq \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{SP}}(\mathrm{XS})$
$\left[\mathrm{S}^{2-}\right] \geq \frac{1 \times 10^{-22}}{0.01}=10^{-20}$
$\mathrm{YS}(\mathrm{s}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Y}^{+2}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{S}^{2-}(\mathrm{aq})$
For precipitation of YS(s)
$\left[\mathrm{Y}^{+2}\right]\left[\mathrm{S}^{2-}\right] \geq \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{SP}}(\mathrm{YS})$
$\left[\mathrm{S}^{2-}\right] \geq \frac{4 \times 10^{-16}}{10^{-2}}=4 \times 10^{-14}$
Now, $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{S}^{2-}(\mathrm{aq})$
$\frac{\left[\mathrm{S}^{2-}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}}{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{1}} \times \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{2}}=1 \times 10^{-21}$
$\left[\mathrm{S}^{2-}\right]=\frac{1 \times 10^{-21} \times\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}} \geq 4 \times 10^{-14}$
$\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2} \leq \frac{1}{4} \times 10^{-7} \times 10^{-1}$
$\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right] \leq \frac{1}{2} \times 10^{-4} \Rightarrow \mathrm{pH} \geq 4.3$

Question:
A solution is prepared by dissolving 0.3 g of a nonvolatile non-electrolyte solute ' A ' of molar mass $60 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and 0.9 g of a non-volatile nonelectrolyte solute ' B ' of molar mass $180 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ in $100 \mathrm{~mL} \ \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Osmotic pressure of the solution will be
[Given : $\mathrm{R}=0.082 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{~atm} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ]
Solution:
Mass of solute ' A ' $=0.3 \mathrm{~g}$
Moles of solute ${ }^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\frac{0.3 \mathrm{~g}}{60 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}}=\frac{1}{200} \mathrm{~mol}$
Mass of solute ${ }^{\prime} \mathrm{B}^{\prime}=0.9 \mathrm{~g}$
Moles of solute ${ }^{\prime} \mathrm{B}^{\prime}=\frac{0.9 \mathrm{gm}}{180 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}}=\frac{1}{200} \mathrm{~mol}$
Total molarity of all solutes
$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{2 / 200}{100} \times 1000=\frac{1}{10} \mathrm{M} \\
\therefore \pi & =\frac{1}{10} \times 0.082 \times 300 \\
\pi & =2.46 \mathrm{~atm} .
\end{aligned}
$$

Question: The hydrogen spectrum consists of several spectral lines in Lyman series $\left(\mathrm{L}_{1}, \mathrm{~L}_{2}, \mathrm{~L}_{3} \ldots . ; \mathrm{L}_{1}\right.$ has lowest energy among Lyman series). Similarly it consists of several spectral lines in Balmer series ( $\mathrm{B}_{1}, \mathrm{~B}_{2}$, $\mathrm{B}_{3} \ldots \ldots . ; \mathrm{B}_{1}$ has lowest energy among Balmer lines). The energy of $\mathrm{L}_{1}$ is x times the energy of $\mathrm{B}_{1}$. The value of $x$ is ________ $\times 10^{-1}$ (Nearest inteter)
Solution:
$\quad \Delta E\left(L_{1}\right)=13.6 \times Z^{2}\left(\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{2^{2}}\right)=13.6 Z^{2} \times \frac{3}{4}$
$\Delta \mathrm{E}\left(\mathrm{B}_{1}\right)=13.6 \times \mathrm{Z}^{2} \times\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right)=13.6 \times \mathrm{Z}^{2} \times \frac{5}{4 \times 9}$
$\frac{\Delta \mathrm{E}\left(\mathrm{L}_{1}\right)}{\Delta \mathrm{E}\left(\mathrm{B}_{1}\right)}=\frac{3}{5} \times 9=\frac{27}{5}=\mathrm{x}$
$\mathrm{x}=\left(\frac{27}{5} \times 10\right) \times 10^{-1}=54 \times 10^{-1}$

Question:
The half-life of ${ }^{65} \mathrm{Zn}$ is 245 days. After x days, $75 \%$ of original activity remained. The value of x in days is $\_\_\_\_$ . (Nearest integer)
(Given : $\log 3=0.4771$ and $\log 2=0.3010$ )
Solution:
$\quad \mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=\frac{\ln 2}{\mathrm{~K}}$
$\mathrm{K}=\frac{\ln 2}{245}$
$\mathrm{t}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~K}} \ell \mathrm{n} \frac{\mathrm{a}_{0}}{\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{t}}}$
$\mathrm{t}_{25 \%}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~K}} \ln \frac{4}{3}$
$\mathrm{t}_{25 \%}=\frac{1}{\frac{\ln 2}{245}} \ln \frac{4}{3}$
$\mathrm{t}_{25 \%}=245 \frac{\ln \frac{4}{3}}{\ln 2}=245\left[\frac{2 \log 2-\log 3}{\log 2}\right]$
$=245\left[\frac{2 \times 0.3010-0.4771}{0.3010}\right]=101.66$ day.

Question:
Molar conductivity of a weak acid HQ of concentration 0.18 M was found to be $1 / 30$ of the molar conductivity of another weak acid HZ with concentration of 0.02 of M . If $\lambda_{\mathrm{Q}^{-}}^{0}$ happened to be equal with $\lambda_{\mathrm{z}^{-}}^{0}$, then the difference of the $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$ values of the two weak acids ( $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HQ})-\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HZ})$ ) is _______ (Nearest integer).
[Given: degree of dissociation $(\alpha) \ll 1$ for both weak acids, $\lambda^{\circ}$ : limiting molar conductivity of ions]
Solution:
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HQ})=\mathrm{C}_{1} \alpha_{1}{ }^{2}$
$$
\alpha_{1}=\frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}(\mathrm{HQ})}{\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\infty}(\mathrm{HQ})}
$$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HZ})=\mathrm{C}_{2} \alpha_{2}{ }^{2}$
$\alpha_{2}=\frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}(\mathrm{HZ})}{\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\infty}(\mathrm{HZ})}$
$\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HQ})}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HZ})}=\frac{\mathrm{C}_{1}}{\mathrm{C}_{2}} \cdot\left(\frac{\alpha_{1}}{\alpha_{2}}\right)^{2}=\frac{0.18}{0.02} \cdot\left[\frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}(\mathrm{HQ})}{\lambda_{\mathrm{m}}(\mathrm{HZ})}\right]^{2}$
$\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HQ})}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HQ})}=9 \times\left(\frac{1}{30}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{100}$
$\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HQ})-\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HZ})=2$

Question:
The wavelength of light absorbed for the following complexes are in the order.
I. \[{\left[ {{\text{Co}}{{\left( {{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_3}} \right)}_6}} \right]^{3 + }}\]; II. \[{\left[ {{\text{Co}}{{\left( {{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}} \right)}_6}} \right]^{3 + }}\]; III. \[{\left[ {{\text{Co}}{{({\text{CN}})}_6}} \right]^{3 - }}\]
IV. \[{\left[ {{\text{Co}}{{\left( {{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_3}} \right)}_5}\left( {{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}} \right)} \right]^{3 + }}\]; V. \[{\left[ {{\text{Co}}{{\text{F}}_6}} \right]^{3 - }}\]
Solution:
Wavelength of light absorbed increases as C.F.S.E of complex decreases.
$\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}\right]^{3-}$ has maximum CFSE
$\left[\mathrm{CoF}_{6}\right]^{3-}$ has least CFSE
Ligand field strength ↑ ; C.F.S.E ↑
Correct wavelength order.
V > II > IV > I > III

Question:
Consider the following gaseous equilibrium in a closed container of volume "V" at T(K).
$\mathrm{P}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{Q}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{PQ}(\mathrm{g})$
2 moles each of $\mathrm{P}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}), \mathrm{Q}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ and $\mathrm{PQ}(\mathrm{g})$ are present at equilibrium. Now one mole each of ' $\mathrm{P}_{2}$ ' and ' $\mathrm{Q}_{2}$ ' are added to the equilibrium keeping the temperature at $\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K})$. The number of moles of $\mathrm{P}_{2}$, $\mathrm{Q}_{2}$ and PQ at the new equilibrium, respectively, are -
Solution:
$\quad \mathrm{P}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{Q}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{PQ}(\mathrm{g})$
$\mathrm{t}=\mathrm{t}_{\text {eq }} \quad 2$ mole $\quad 2$ mole $\quad 2$ mole
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{2^{2}}{2.2}=1$
Now 1 mole of each $\mathrm{P}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{Q}_{2}$ is added
So reaction will move in forward direction

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \quad \quad\quad P_{2}(g)+Q_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 P Q(g) \\
& t=t_{\text {eq. }}^{\prime} \quad 3-x \quad 3-x \quad 2+2x\\
& K_{C}=1=\frac{(2+2 x)^{2}}{(3-x)(3-x)} \\
& \frac{2+2 x}{3-x}=1 \\
& 2+2 x=3-x \\
& x=\frac{1}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$
At new equilibrium :
Moles of $\mathrm{P}_{2}=\frac{8}{3}=2.67$
Moles of $\mathrm{Q}_{2}=\frac{8}{3}=2.67$
Moles of $\mathrm{PQ}=\frac{8}{3}=2.67$

Question:
0.25 g of an organic compound "A" containing carbon, hydrogen and oxygen was analysed using the combustion method. There was an increase in mass of $\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}$ tube and potash tube at the end of the experiment. The amount was found to be 0.15 g and 0.1837 g , respectively. The percentage of oxygen in compound A is _________ %. (Nearest integer)
(Given : molar mass in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{H}: 1, \mathrm{C}: 12, \mathrm{O}: 16$ )
Solution:
$\quad \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{z}}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
$\begin{array}{llll}\mathrm{t}=0 & 0.25 \mathrm{gm} & & \\ \mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{eq}} & - & 0.18 \mathrm{gm} & 0.15 \mathrm{gm}\end{array}$
Mass of ' C ' $=\frac{0.18}{44} \times 12=0.049 \simeq 0.05 \mathrm{gm}$
Mass of ' H ' $=\frac{0.15}{18} \times 2=0.016 \simeq 0.017 \mathrm{gm}$.
Mass of ' O ' $=0.25-0.05-0.017=0.1833 \mathrm{gm}$
Mass % of ' O ' $=\frac{0.1833}{0.25} \times 100=73.32 \%$.

Question: Two liquids A and B form an ideal solution at temperature T K. At T K, the vapour pressures of pure A and B are 55 and $15 \ \mathrm{kNm}^{-2}$ respectively. What is the mole fraction of A in solution of A and $B$ in equilibrium with a vapour in which the mole fraction of A is 0.8 ?
Solution:
$\frac{Y_{A}}{Y_{B}}=\frac{P_{A}^{o}}{P_{B}^{0}} \cdot \frac{X_{A}}{X_{B}}$
$\frac{0.8}{0.2}=\frac{55}{15} \times \frac{\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{B}}}$
$\frac{\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{60}{55}=\frac{12}{11}$
$\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{12}{23}=0.5217$

Question: The heat of atomisation of methane and ethane are ' x ' $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ and ' y ' $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ respectively. The longest wavelength ( $\lambda$ ) of light capable of breaking the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}$ bond can be expressed in SI unit as :
Solution:
$\quad \mathrm{CH}_{4}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{C}(\mathrm{g})+4 \mathrm{H}(\mathrm{g}) ; \quad \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}=\mathrm{x} \mathrm{kJ} /$ mole $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{C}(\mathrm{g})+6 \mathrm{H}(\mathrm{g}) ; \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}=\mathrm{y} \mathrm{kJ} / \mathrm{mole} 1000 \mathrm{x}=4 \times \varepsilon_{\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}}$
$1000 \mathrm{y}=1 \times \varepsilon_{\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}}+6 \times \varepsilon_{\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}}$
$\varepsilon_{\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}}=\left[\mathrm{y}-\frac{3 \mathrm{x}}{2}\right] \times 1000=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda} . \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}$
$\left(' \lambda^{\prime}\right)$ wavelength of photon $=\frac{\mathrm{hc} \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}}{[4 \mathrm{y}-6 \mathrm{x}] \times 250}$

Question: The wavelength of spectral line obtained in the spectrum of $\mathrm{Li}^{2+}$ ion, when the transition takes place between two levels whose sum is 4 and difference is 2 , is
Solution:
$\quad \mathrm{n}_{1} \rightarrow$ lower energy level
$\mathrm{n}_{2} \rightarrow$ higher energy level
$\mathrm{n}_{1}+\mathrm{n}_{2}=4, \mathrm{n}_{2}=3$
$\mathrm{n}_{2}-\mathrm{n}_{1}=2, \mathrm{n}_{1}=1$
Rydderg's formula :
$\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}} \mathrm{Z}^{2}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_{2}^{2}}\right]$
$\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}}(3)^{2}\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right]$
$\frac{1}{\lambda}=8 \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}}$
$\lambda=\frac{1}{8 \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}}}$
$\lambda=\frac{1}{8 \times 1.1 \times 10^{5}}$
$\lambda=\frac{1000}{8.8} \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~cm}$
$\lambda=113.63 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~cm}$
$\lambda \simeq 1.14 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~cm}$

Question: The number of terms of an A.P. is even; the sum of all the odd terms is 24, the sum of all the even terms is 30 and the last term exceeds the first by \[\frac{{21}}{2}\]. Then the number of terms which are integers in the A.P. is :
Solution:
\[{a_2} + {a_4} + ..... + {a_n} = 30{\text{ }}...........\left( 1 \right)\]
\[{a_1} + {a_3} + ..... + {a_{n - 1}} = 24{\text{ }}...........\left( 2 \right)\]
(1) – (2)
(a2 – a1) + (a4 – a3) ... (an – an – 1) = 6
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{n}{2}d = 6 \Rightarrow nd = 12\]
\[{a_n} - {a_1} = \left( {n - 1} \right)d = \frac{{21}}{2}\]
\[ \Rightarrow nd - d = \frac{{21}}{2} \Rightarrow 12 - \frac{{21}}{2} = d\]
\[ \Rightarrow d = \frac{3}{2},n = 8\]
Sum of odd terms \[ = \frac{4}{2}\left[ {2a + \left( {4 - 1} \right)3} \right] = 24\]
\[ \Rightarrow a = \frac{3}{2}\]
\[A.P. \Rightarrow \frac{3}{2},3,\frac{9}{2},6,\frac{{15}}{2},9,\frac{{21}}{2},12\]
no. of integer terms = 4

Question:
The line L1 is parallel to the vector \[\vec a = - 3\hat i + 2\hat j + 4\hat k\]and passes through the point
(7, 6, 2) and the line L2 is parallel to the vector \[\vec b = 2\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k\]and passes through the point
(5, 3, 4). The shortest distance between the lines L1 and L2 is :
Solution:
\[{L_1}:\left( {7\hat i + 6\hat j + 2k} \right) + \lambda \left( { - 3\hat i + 2\hat j + 4k} \right)\]
\[{L_2}:\left( {5\hat i + 3\hat j + 4k} \right) + \lambda \left( {2\hat i + \hat j + 3k} \right)\]
Distance between skew lines
\[ = \frac{{\left( {2\hat i + 3\hat j - 2\hat k} \right).\left( {2\hat i + 17\hat j - 7\hat k} \right)}}{{\sqrt {342} }}\]
\[ = \frac{{69}}{{\sqrt {342} }} = \frac{{69}}{{3\sqrt {38} }} = \frac{{23}}{{\sqrt {38} }}\]

Question: If the image of the point P(1, 0, 3) in the line joining the points A(4, 7, 1) and B(3, 5, 3) is Q \[(\alpha ,{\text{ }}\beta ,{\text{ }}\gamma )\], then \[\alpha  + \beta  + \gamma \] is equal to
Solution:
P (1, 0, 3)
A(4, 7, 1), B(3, 5, 3)
Line AB \[ \Rightarrow \frac{{x - 3}}{1} = \frac{{y - 5}}{2} = \frac{{z - 3}}{{ - 2}} = \lambda \]
Let foot of perpendicular of P on AB be
\[R \equiv \left( {\lambda + 3,2\lambda + 5, - 2\lambda + 3} \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow \left( {\lambda + 3 - 1} \right)\left( 1 \right) + \left( {2\lambda + 5 - 0} \right)\left( 2 \right) + \left( { - 2\lambda + 3 - 3} \right)\]
\[\left( { - 2} \right) = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \lambda + 2 + 4\lambda + 10 + 4\lambda = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \lambda = - \frac{4}{3}\]
\[ \Rightarrow R \equiv \left( {\frac{5}{3},\frac{7}{3},\frac{{17}}{3}} \right)\]
\[Q \equiv \left( {\frac{{10}}{3} - 1,\frac{{14}}{3} - 0,\frac{{34}}{3} - 3} \right) \equiv \left( {\frac{7}{3},\frac{{14}}{3},\frac{{25}}{3}} \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow \alpha + \beta + \gamma = \frac{{7 + 14 + 25}}{3} = \frac{{46}}{3}\]

Question: If the length of the minor axis of an ellipse is equal to one fourth of the distance between the foci, then the eccentricity of the ellipse is :
Let ${a_1},{a_2},{a_3},....$ be in an A.P. such that  \[\sum\limits_{k = 1}^{12} {{a_{2k - 1}}}  =  - \frac{{72}}{5}{a_1},{a_1} \ne 0.\] If $\sum\limits_{k = 1}^n {{a_k} = 0,} $then $n$ is
Solution:
Let a1 = a, common difference = d
a1 + a3 + a5 + …… + a23 = − \[\frac{{72}}{5}a\]
$\frac{{12}}{2}[2{\text{a}} + 11 \times 2{\text{d}}] = - \frac{{72}}{5}{\text{a}}$
 $12{\text{a}} + 132{\text{d}} = - \frac{{72}}{5}{\text{a}}$
$132a + 132 \times 5d = 0$
a = − 5d
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\frac{{\text{n}}}{2}\left( {2{\text{a}} + ({\text{n}} - 1){\text{d}}} \right) = 0}& \Rightarrow &{ - 10{\text{d}} + {\text{nd}} - {\text{d}} = 0}
\end{array}$
n = 11

Question:
Let ƒ : \[\left[ {1,\infty } \right) \to \left[ {2,\infty } \right)\] be a differentiable function,
If \[10\int\limits_1^x {f\left( t \right)dt = 5xf(x) - {x^5} - 9} \] for all \[x \geqslant 1,\] then the value of ƒ(3) is :
Solution:
\[10\frac{d}{{dx}}\int\limits_1^x {f(t)dt = \frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {5xf\left( x \right) - {x^5} - 9} \right)} \]
\[ \Rightarrow 10f\left( x \right) = 5f\left( x \right) + 5xf'\left( x \right) - 5{x^4}\]
\[ \Rightarrow f\left( x \right) + {x^4} = xf'\left( x \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow y + {x^4} = x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + y\left( { - \frac{1}{x}} \right) = {x^3}\]
\[ \Rightarrow y{e^{\smallint \frac{1}{x}dx}} = \int {{x^3}{e^{\smallint \frac{1}{x}dx}} + c} \]
\[ \Rightarrow y{e^{\left| { - \ell n} \right|x|}} = {\int {{x^3}e} ^{ - \ell n\left| x \right|}} + c\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{y}{{\left| x \right|}} = \int {\frac{{{x^3}}}{{\left| x \right|}} + c} \]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{y}{x} = \int {{x^2}} + c\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{y}{x} = \frac{{{x^3}}}{3} + c\]
Put x = 1 in given equation
\[ \Rightarrow 0 = 5f\left( 1 \right) - 1 - 9 \Rightarrow f\left( 1 \right) = 2\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{2}{1} = \frac{1}{3} + c \Rightarrow c = \frac{5}{3}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{f\left( 3 \right)}}{3} = \frac{{27}}{3} + \frac{5}{3}\]
\[ \Rightarrow f\left( 3 \right) = 32\]

Question: Let A = {1, 2, 3,...., 100} and R be a relation on A such that R = {(a, b) : a = 2b + 1}. Let (a<sub>1</sub>, a<sub>2</sub>), (a<sub>2</sub>, a<sub>3</sub>), (a<sub>3</sub>, a<sub>4</sub>), ...., (a<sub>k</sub>, a<sub>k+1</sub>) be a sequence of k elements of R such that the second entry of an ordered pair is equal to the first entry of the next ordered pair. Then the largest integer k, for which such a sequence exists, is equal to :
Solution:
a = 2b + 1
     2b = a – 1
R = {(3, 1), (5, 2), ..., (99, 49)}
Let (2m + 1, m), \[(2\lambda - 1,\lambda )\] are such ordered pairs.
According to the condition
\[m = 2\lambda - 1 \Rightarrow m = odd\] number
\[ \Rightarrow \] 1st element of ordered pair (a, b)
a = 2\[(2\lambda - 1) + 1 = 4\lambda - 1\]
Hence a \[ \in \left\{ {3,7,.....,99} \right\}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \lambda \in \left\{ {1,2.....,25} \right\}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \] set of sequence
\[\left\{ {\left( {4\lambda - 1,2\lambda-1 } \right),\left( {2\lambda - 1\lambda - 1} \right),\left( {\lambda - 1\frac{{\lambda - 2}}{2}} \right),.....} \right\}\]
2nd element of each ordered pair = \[\frac{{\lambda - {2^{r - 2}}}}{{{2^{r - 2}}}}\]
For maximum number of ordered pairs in such sequence
\[\frac{{\lambda - {2^{r - 2}}}}{{{2^{r - 2}}}} = 1{\text{ or 2; 1}} \leqslant \lambda \leqslant {\text{25}}\]
\[\lambda = {2^{r - 1}}{\text{ or }}{3.2^{r - 2}} ; 1 \leqslant \lambda \leqslant {\text{25}}\]
Case-I : \[\lambda = 2^{r - 1}\]
\[\lambda = 2,{2^2},{2^3},{2^4}\]
\[r = 2,3,4,5\]
Hence maximum value of r is 5 when\[\lambda = 16\]
Case-II : \[\lambda = {3.2^{r - 2}}\]
\[\lambda = 3,6,12,24\]
\[r = 2,{\text{ }}3,{\text{ }}4,{\text{ }}5\]
Hence maximum value of r is 5 when \[\lambda = 24\]

Question: A line passing through the point P(a, θ ) makes an acute angle α  with the positive x-axis. Let this line be rotated about the point P through an angle \[\frac{\alpha }{2}\] in the clock-wise direction. If in the new position, the slope of the line is \[2 - \sqrt 3 \]and its distance from the origin is \[\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\], then the value of \[3{\alpha ^2}{\tan ^2}\alpha  - 2\sqrt 3 \] is
Solution:
${{\text{m}}_{{\text{PR}}}} = 2 - \sqrt 3 = \tan {15^ \circ }$
$\therefore \frac{\alpha }{2} = {15^ \circ }\quad \Rightarrow \boxed{\alpha = {{30}^ \circ }}$
Equation of PR :
y = tan 15° (x – a)
\[y = (2 - \sqrt 3 )(x - a)\]
⊥ distance from origin = $\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}$
$\left| {\frac{{\sqrt 3 {\text{a}} - 2{\text{a}}}}{{\sqrt {4 + 3 - 4\sqrt 3 + 1} }}} \right| = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}$
$\frac{{\mid {\text{a}}\mid (2 - \sqrt 3 )}}{{2\sqrt {(2 - \sqrt 3 )} }} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}$
$|{\text{a}}| = \frac{{\sqrt 2 }}{{\sqrt {2 - \sqrt 3 } }} = \sqrt 2 (\sqrt {2 + \sqrt 3 } )$
${\mathfrak{a}^2} = 2(2 + \sqrt 3 )$
$3{a^2}{\tan ^2}\alpha - 2\sqrt 3 $
$3 \times (4 + 2\sqrt 3 ).\frac{1}{3} - 2\sqrt 3 = 4$

Question: Let a be the length of a side of a square OABC with O being the origin. Its side OA makes an acute angle α  with the positive x-axis and the equations of its diagonals are$\left( {\sqrt 3  + 1} \right){\text{x}} + \left( {\sqrt 3  - 1} \right){\text{y}} = 0$ and $\left( {\sqrt 3  - 1} \right)x - \left( {\sqrt 3  + 1} \right)y + 8\sqrt 3  = 0.$Then a<sup>2</sup> is equal to
Solution:
Slope of diagonal OB = $\frac{{\sqrt 3 + 1}}{{1 - \sqrt 3 }}$ = tan 105o
∴ α = 60o
∴ A (acos60o, asin60o)
∴ ${\text{A}}\left( {\frac{{\text{a}}}{2},\frac{{\sqrt 3 {\text{a}}}}{2}} \right)$
A Lies on other diagonal
$\therefore \left( {\frac{{\sqrt 3 - 1}}{2}} \right){\text{a}} - \left( {\frac{{\sqrt 3 + 1}}{2}} \right).\sqrt 3 {\text{a}} + 8\sqrt 3 = 0$
${\text{a}}\left[ {\frac{{\sqrt 3 - 1 - 3 - \sqrt 3 }}{2}} \right] = - 8\sqrt 3 $
$\boxed{{\text{a}} = 4\sqrt 3 }$
$\boxed{\therefore {{\text{a}}^2} = 48}$

Question:

(B) Al3+ does not get reduced easily

Question: Let f(x) be a positive function and ${I_1} = \int\limits_{ - \frac{1}{2}}^1 {2xf\left( {2x\left( {1 - 2x} \right)} \right)dx} $ and ${I_2} = \int\limits_{ - 1}^2 {f\left( {x\left( {1 - x} \right)} \right)dx} $. Then the value of \[\frac{{{I_2}}}{{{I_1}}}\] is equal to __________.
Solution:
${I_1} = \int\limits_{ - \frac{1}{2}}^1 {2xf\left( {2x\left( {1 - 2x} \right)} \right)} dx$
$ \Rightarrow 2x = t \Rightarrow 2dx = dt \Rightarrow {I_1} = \frac{1}{2}\int\limits_{ - 1}^2 {tf\left( {t\left( {1 - t} \right)} \right)dt} $
$ \Rightarrow 2{I_1} = \int\limits_{ - 1}^2 {\left( {1 - t} \right)f\left( {1 - t} \right)} \left( {1 - \left( {1 - t} \right)} \right)dt$
$ \Rightarrow 2{I_1} = \int\limits_{ - 1}^2 {f\left( {t\left( {1 - t} \right)} \right)dt} - \int\limits_{ - 1}^2 {tf\left( {t\left( {1 - t} \right)} \right)dt} $
$ \Rightarrow 2{{\text{I}}_1} = {{\text{I}}_2} - 2{{\text{I}}_1}$
 $ \Rightarrow 4{{\text{I}}_1} = {{\text{I}}_2}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{{\text{I}}_2}}}{{{{\text{I}}_1}}} = 4$

Question:
Let f(x) = x – 1 and g(x) = ex for x ∈ ℝ. If
\[\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \left( {{e^{ - 2\sqrt x }}g\left( {f\left( {f\left( x \right)} \right)} \right) - \frac{y}{{\sqrt x }}} \right),y(0) = 0,\,then\,y(1)\] is
Solution:
f(x) = x – 1
f(f(x)) = f(x) – 1 = x – 1 – 1 = x – 2
g(f(f(x))) = ex–2
\[\therefore \,\,\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \frac{1}{{\sqrt x }}y = {e^{x - 2\sqrt x - 2}}\] which is L.D.E
I.F. = \[{e^{\int {\frac{{dy}}{{\sqrt x }}} }} = {e^{2\sqrt x }}\]
Its solution is
\[y \times {e^{2\sqrt x }} = \int {{e^{2\sqrt x }} \times {e^{x - 2\sqrt x - 2}}} dx + c\]
\[y \times {e^{2\sqrt x }} = {e^{x - 2}} + c\]
Given x = 0, y = 0 ⇒ 0 = e–2 + c ; c = – e–2
∴ \[y \times {e^{2\sqrt x }} = {e^{x - 2}} + {e^{ - 2}}\]
\[when\,\,x = 1,\,\,y \times {e^2} = {e^{ - 1}} - {e^{ - 2}}\]
\[y = \frac{{{e^{ - 1}} - {e^{ - 2}}}}{{{e^2}}} = \frac{{\frac{1}{e} - \frac{1}{{{e^2}}}}}{{{e^2}}} = \frac{{{e^2} - e}}{{{e^5}}} = \frac{{e - 1}}{{{e^4}}}\]
Option (1) is correct

Question:
Correct statements for an element with atomic number 9 are
A. There can be 5 electrons for which ms = +\[\frac{1}{2}\] and 4 electrons for which ms = − \[\frac{1}{2}\]
B. There is only one electron in pz orbital
C. The last electron goes to orbital with n = 2 and l = 1
D. The sum of angular nodes of all the atomic orbitals is 1.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
Solution:
Element with atomic number 9 is Fluorine
F(9) = 1s2 2s2 2p5

(A) 5 electrons can be up-spin \[\left[ {{m_s} = + \frac{1}{2}} \right]\] and 4 electrons can be down spin \[\left[ {{m_s} = - \frac{1}{2}} \right]\]
(B) Unpaired electron can be in anyone of px, py or pz orbital
(C) Last electron is in 2p subshell with n = 2, = 1
(D) Angular node for s-orbital = 0 while of each p-orbital = 1
Sum of all angular node = 3

Question:
A(g) → B(g) + C(g) is a first order reaction.

The reaction was started with reactant A only. Which of the following expression is correct for rate constant k ?
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  {P_t} = {P^0} + x \Rightarrow {P_t} - {P^0} = {P_t} - \frac{{{P_\infty }}}{2} \\
  {P_\infty } = 2{P^0} \Rightarrow {P^0} = \frac{{{P_\infty }}}{2} \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[k = \frac{1}{t}ln\frac{{{P^0}}}{{{P^0} - x}}\]
\[k = \frac{1}{t}ln\frac{{{P_\infty }}}{{2\left( {{P_\infty } - {P_t}} \right)}}\]

Question: Let the values of λ  for which the shortest distance between the lines $\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y - 2}}{3} = \frac{{z - 3}}{4}$ and  $\frac{{{\text{x}} - \lambda }}{3} = \frac{{{\text{y}} - 4}}{4} = \frac{{{\text{z}} - 5}}{5}$ is $\frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }}$ be λ <sub>1</sub> and λ <sub>2</sub>. Then the radius of the circle passing through the points (0, 0), (λ <sub>1</sub>, λ <sub>2</sub>) and (λ <sub>2</sub>, λ <sub>1</sub>) is
Solution:
$\vec p = 2\hat i + 3\hat j + 4\hat k,\,\,\vec q = 3\hat i + 4\hat j + 5\hat k$
$ \Rightarrow \vec p \times \vec q = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\

  3&4&5
\end{array}} \right| = - \hat i + 2\hat j - \hat k$
${\text{A}} \equiv (1,2,3){\text{B}} \equiv (\lambda ,4,5)$
Shortest Distance \[ = \left| {\frac{{\overrightarrow {AB} .\left( {\vec p \times \vec q} \right)}}{{\left| {\vec p \times \vec q} \right|}}} \right|\]
$ = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }} = \left| {\frac{{\left( {\left( {\lambda - 1} \right)\hat i + 2\hat j + 2\hat k} \right).\left( { - \hat i + 2\hat j - \hat k} \right)}}{{\sqrt 6 }}} \right|$
$ \Rightarrow \left| { - \lambda + 1 + 4 - 2} \right| = 1 \Rightarrow \left| {\lambda - 3} \right| = 1$
$ \Rightarrow \lambda = 3 \pm 1 = 4,2$
Radius of circle passing through points
(0, 0), (4, 2) & (2, 4)
$ = \frac{{abc}}{{4\Delta }} = \frac{{\sqrt {20} \times \sqrt {20} \times \sqrt 8 }}{{4 \times \frac{1}{2}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  1&1&1 \\
  0&4&2 \\
  0&2&4
\end{array}} \right|}} = \frac{{20 \times 2\sqrt 2 }}{{2 \times 12}}$
$ = \frac{{5\sqrt 2 }}{3}$

Question: Let the ellipse 3x<sup>2</sup> + py<sup>2</sup> = 4 pass through the centre C of the circle x<sup>2</sup> + y<sup>2</sup> – 2x – 4y – 11 =  of radius r. Let f<sub>1</sub>, f<sub>2</sub> be the focal distances of the point C on the ellipse. Then 6f<sub>1</sub>f<sub>2</sub> – r is equal to
Solution:
${\text{E}}:\frac{{{{\text{x}}^2}}}{{4/3}} + \frac{{{{\text{y}}^2}}}{{4/{\text{P}}}} = 1$
Centre of circle (1, 2), radius
${\text{r}} = \sqrt {1 + 4 + 11} $
\[\boxed{r = 4}\]
∵ E pass from centre (1, 2)
$\therefore \frac{3}{4} + {\text{P}} = 1$
\[\boxed{P = \frac{1}{4}}\therefore vertical\,ellipse\]
${\text{e}} = \sqrt {1 - \frac{{4/3}}{{16}}} = \sqrt {1 - \frac{1}{{12}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{11}}{{12}}} $
∴ focal distance of C (h, k)
$ = {\text{b}} \pm {\text{ek}}$

${{\text{F}}_2} = 4 - \sqrt {\frac{{11}}{{12}}} \times 2$
$\therefore {{\text{F}}_1}{{\text{F}}_2} = 16 - \frac{{11}}{3} = \frac{{37}}{3}$
$\therefore 6{{\text{F}}_1}{{\text{F}}_2} - {\text{r}} = 74 - 4 = 70$

Question: If A and B are two events such that P(A) = 0.7, P(B) = 0.4 and $P\left( {A \cap \bar B} \right) = 0.5$, where  $\bar B$ denotes the complement of B, then  $P\left( {B|\left( {A \cup \bar B} \right)} \right)$ is equal to :-
Solution:
${\text{P}}({\text{A}}) = \frac{7}{{10}},{\text{P}}({\text{B}}) = \frac{4}{{10}}$

$P(A \cup \bar B) = \frac{5}{{10}}$
$P\left( {\frac{B}{{A \cup \bar B}}} \right) = \frac{{P\left( {B \cap \left( {A \cup \bar B} \right)} \right)}}{{P\left( {A \cup \bar B} \right)}}$
$ = \frac{{P\left( {\left( {B \cap \bar B} \right) \cup \left( {B \cap A} \right)} \right)}}{{P\left( {A \cup \bar B} \right)}} = \frac{{P\left( {A \cap B} \right)}}{{P\left( {A \cup \bar B} \right)}}$
$ = \frac{{{\text{P}}({\text{A}}) - {\text{P}}({\text{A}} \cap \overline {\text{B}} )}}{{{\text{P}}({\text{A}}) + {\text{P}}(\overline {\text{B}} ) - {\text{P}}({\text{A}} \cap \overline {\text{B}} )}} = \frac{{\frac{7}{{10}} - \frac{5}{{10}}}}{{\frac{7}{{10}} + \left( {1 - \frac{4}{{10}}} \right) - \frac{5}{{10}}}}$
 $ = \frac{2}{8} = \frac{1}{4}$

Question:
Resonance in X2Y can be represented as

The enthalpy of formation of
\[{X_2}Y\left( {X \equiv X(g) + \frac{1}{2}Y = Y(g) \to {X_2}Y(g)} \right)\] is 80 kJ mol-1.
The magnitude of resonance energy of X2Y is ________ kJ mol–1 (nearest integer value)
Given : Bond energies of X ≡ X, X = X, Y = Y and X = Y are 940, 410, 500 and 602 kJ mol–1 respectively.
Valence X : 3, Y : 2
Solution:
∆ HR.E = ∆ Hf (exp) – ∆ Hf (Theo)
∆ Hf (exp) for X2Y(g) = 80 kJ/mole
for ∆ Hf (Theo)
\[{X_{2(g)}} + \frac{1}{2}{Y_{2(g)}} \to {X_2}{Y_{(g)}}\Delta {H_f} = ?\]
\[\Delta {H_{f(Theo)}} = \left( {B{E_{X \equiv X}} + \frac{1}{2}B{E_{Y = Y}}} \right) - \left( {B{E_{X = X}} + B{E_{X = Y}}} \right) = \left( {940 + \frac{1}{2} \times 500} \right) - (410 + 602)\]
\[ = 178\,\,kJ/mole\]
\[\Delta {H_{R.E}} = 80 - 178\]
\[ = - 98\,kJ/mole\]
\[\Delta {H_{R.E.}} = 80 - 178\]
\[ = - 98\,kJ/mol\]
\[|\Delta {H_{R.E}}| = 98\]

Question: The sum of the squares of the roots of |x + 2|<sup>2</sup> + |x – 2| – 2 = 0 and the squares of the roots of x<sup>2</sup> – 2|x – 3| – 5 = 0, is
Solution:
|x – 2|2 + 2 |x – 2| – |x – 2| – 2 = 0
⇒ (|x – 2| + 2) (|x – 2| – 1) = 0
⇒ |x – 2| = 1 ⇒ x = 2 ± 1 = 3, 1
⇒ sum of square of roots = 9 + 1 = 10
x2 – 2 |x – 3| – 5 = 0
Case-I x – 3 ≥ 0
⇒ x2 – 2x + 1 = 0
⇒ (x – 1)2 = 0
⇒ x = 1
But x ≥ 3
⇒ x ∈ φ
Case-II x – 3 < 0
x2 + 2x – 11 = 0, D > 0 ⇒ Real & distinct roots
f(x) = x2 + 2x – 11
${\text{f}}(3) > 0,\frac{{ - {\text{p}}}}{{2{\text{a}}}} = - 1 < 3$
⇒ both roots < 3, both roots acceptable
Sum of square of roots = (α + β )2 – 2 α β
= 4 + 22 = 26
⇒ Final sum = 10 + 26 = 36

Question: Which of the following binary mixture does not show the behaviour of minimum boiling azeotropes ?

Question: In Dumas' method 292 mg of an organic compound released 50 mL of nitrogen gas (N<sub>2</sub>) at 300 K temperature and 715 mm Hg pressure. The percentage composition of 'N' in the organic compound is _____ % (Nearest integer) (Aqueous tension at 300 K = 15 mm Hg)
Solution:
\[Organic\;compound\;\xrightarrow{{DUMA'S}}{N_2}\]
292 mg
V = 50 ml
P = 715 mm Hg
T = 300 k
Aq. tension = 15 mm Hg
\[{P_{{N_2}}} = 715 - 15 = 700\;mmHg\]
\[{P_{{N_2}}} = \frac{{700}}{{760}}atm\]
\[{n_{{N_2}}} = \frac{{{P_{{N_2}}}.V}}{{RT}}\]
\[{n_{{N_2}}} = \frac{{700}}{{760}} \times \frac{{50}}{{1000}} \times \frac{1}{{0.0821 \times 300}}\]
\[{n_N} = 2 \times {n_{{N_2}}}\]
\[Mass\;of\;N = 2 \times {n_N} \times 14\]
\[\% N = \frac{{mass\;of\;N}}{{{\text{mass of organic compound}}}} \times 100\]
\[\% N = \frac{{700}}{{760}} \times \frac{{50}}{{1000}} \times \frac{{2 \times 14}}{{0.0821 \times 300}} \times \frac{{1000}}{{292}} \times 100\]
\[\% N = 18\% \]

Question:
Let α be a solution of x2 + x + 1 = 0, and for some a and b in  $\mathbb{R}$
$\,               [4\quad {\text{a}}\quad {\text{b}}]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  1&{16}&{13} \\
  { - 1}&{ - 1}&2 \\
  { - 2}&{ - 14}&{ - 8}
\end{array}} \right] = [0\quad 0\quad 0].$ If $\frac{4}{{{\alpha ^4}}}$ $ + \frac{\mathfrak{m}}{{{\alpha ^{\text{a}}}}} + \frac{\mathfrak{n}}{{{\alpha ^{\text{b}}}}} = 3,$then m + n is equal to ___
Solution:
x2 + x + 1 = 0
α is root
∴ α 2 + α + 1 = 0
⇒ α = ω and ω 2 [cube root of unity]
also
[ 4 – a – 2b 64 –a –14b 52 + 2a – 8b] = [0 0 0]
∴ a + 2b = 4
a + 14 b = 64

⇒ 12 b = 60 ⇒ $b = 5$
$ \Rightarrow a = - 6$
$\therefore \frac{4}{{{\alpha ^4}}} + \frac{{\text{m}}}{{{\alpha ^{ - 6}}}} + \frac{{\text{n}}}{{{\alpha ^5}}} = 3$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{4}{\omega } + \frac{{\text{m}}}{1} + \frac{{\text{n}}}{{{\omega ^2}}} = 3$
$ \Rightarrow 4{\omega ^2} + {\text{m}} + {\text{n}}\omega = 3$
$ \Rightarrow 4\left( { - \frac{1}{2} - \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}{\text{i}}} \right) + {\text{m}} + {\text{n}}\left( { - \frac{1}{2} + \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}{\text{i}}} \right) = 3$
$\therefore - 2 + {\text{m}} - \frac{{\text{n}}}{2} = 3$ … (1)
& $\frac{{ - 4\sqrt 3 }}{2} + \frac{{{\text{n}}\sqrt 3 }}{2} = 0$
$\therefore \,\,n = 4$
$m = 7$
$\therefore \,\,m + n = 11$

Question:
Let the domain of the function
${\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = {\text{co}}{{\text{s}}^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{4{\text{x}} + 5}}{{3{\text{x}} - 7}}} \right){\text{be}}[\alpha ,\beta ]$ and the domain of
g(x) = log2(2 – 6log27(2x + 5)) be (γ , δ ).
Then |7(α + β ) + 4(γ +δ )| is equal to __________
Solution:
${\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{4{\text{x}} + 5}}{{3{\text{x}} - 7}}} \right)$
 $ \Rightarrow - 1 \leqslant \left( {\frac{{4{\text{x}} + 5}}{{3{\text{x}} - 7}}} \right) \leqslant 1$
$\left( {\frac{{4{\text{x}} + 5}}{{3{\text{x}} - 7}}} \right) \geqslant - 1$
$\frac{{4{\text{x}} + 5 + 3{\text{x}} - 7}}{{3{\text{x}} - 7}} \geqslant 0$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{7{\text{x}} - 2}}{{3{\text{x}} - 7}} \geqslant 0$

${\text{x}} \in \left( { - \infty ,\frac{2}{7}} \right] \cup \left( {\frac{7}{3},\infty } \right)$
& $\frac{{4{\text{x}} + 5}}{{3{\text{x}} - 7}} \leqslant 1 \Rightarrow \frac{{{\text{x}} + 12}}{{3{\text{x}} - 7}} \leqslant 0$
 
∴ Domain of f(x) is
$\left[ { - 12,\frac{2}{7}} \right]\,\,\boxed{\alpha = - 12,\beta = \frac{2}{7}}$
g(x) = log2(2–6 log27(2x + 5))
Domain
⇒ 6 log27(2x + 5) < 2
⇒ ${\log _{27}}(2x + 5) < \frac{1}{3}$
⇒ 2x + 5 < 3
⇒ x < − 1
& 2x + 5 > 0 ⇒ x > $ - \frac{5}{2}$
Domain is x $ \in {\left( { - \frac{5}{2}, - 1} \right)}$
$\boxed{\gamma = - \frac{5}{2},\delta = - 1}$
$|7(\alpha + \beta ) + 4(\gamma + \delta )| = |7( - 12 + \frac{2}{7} + 4( - \frac{5}{2} - 1)|$
$|-82 - 14| = 96$

Question: Let A = $\left\{ {\theta \in [0,2\pi ]:1 + 10\operatorname{Re} \left( {\frac{{2\cos \theta + {\text{i}}\sin \theta }}{{\cos \theta - 3{\text{i}}\sin \theta }}} \right) = 0} \right\}.$Then \[\sum\limits_{\theta \in A} {{\theta ^2}} \] is equal to
Solution:
$1 + 10{\text{Re}}\left( {\frac{{2\cos \theta + {\text{i}}\sin \theta }}{{\cos \theta - 3{\text{i}}\sin \theta }}} \right) = 0$
 $\therefore \,\,z + \bar z = 2\operatorname{Re} \left( z \right)$
$\frac{{2\cos \theta + {\text{i}}\sin \theta }}{{\cos \theta - 3{\text{i}}\sin \theta }} + \frac{{2\cos \theta - {\text{i}}\sin \theta }}{{\cos \theta + 3{\text{i}}\sin \theta }} = 2 \times \left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{{10}}} \right)$
$\frac{{(2{{\cos }^2}\theta - 3{{\sin }^2}\theta ) + (2{{\cos }^2}\theta ) - (3{{\sin }^2}\theta )}}{{{{\cos }^2}\theta + 9{{\sin }^2}\theta }} = \frac{{ - 2}}{{10}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{2{\text{co}}{{\text{s}}^2}\theta - 3{{\sin }^2}\theta }}{{{{\cos }^2}\theta + 9{{\sin }^2}\theta }} = \frac{{ - 1}}{{10}}$
$ \Rightarrow 20{\cos ^2}\theta - 30{\sin ^2}\theta = - {\cos ^2}\theta - 9{\sin ^2}\theta $
$21{\cos ^2}\theta - 21{\sin ^2}\theta = 0$
$ \Rightarrow \cos 2\theta = 0$
$2\theta = \frac{\pi }{2},\frac{{3\pi }}{2},\frac{{5\pi }}{2},\frac{{7\pi }}{2}$
$ \Rightarrow \sum {\theta ^2} = \frac{{{\pi ^2}}}{{16}} + \frac{{9{\pi ^2}}}{{16}} + \frac{{25{\pi ^2}}}{{16}} + \frac{{49{\pi ^2}}}{{16}} = \frac{{84{\pi ^2}}}{{16}} = \frac{{21{\pi ^2}}}{4}$

The equilibrium constant for decomposition of H2O(g)
\[{H_2}O(g) \rightleftharpoons {H_2}(g) + \frac{1}{2}{O_2}(g)(\Delta {G^o} = 92.34\,kJ\,mo{l^{ - 1}})\]
is 8.0 × 10–3 at 2300 K and total pressure at equilibrium is 1 bar. Under this condition, the degree of dissociation (α ) of water is _____ × 10–2 (nearest integer value).
[Assume α is negligible with respect to 1]

Given below are two statements
Statement I :
\[\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \left( {\frac{{{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x + {{\log }_e}\sqrt {\frac{{1 + x}}{{1 - x}}} - 2x}}{{{x^5}}}} \right) = \frac{2}{5}\]
Statement II : \[\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 1} \left( {{x^{\frac{2}{{1 - x}}}}} \right) = \frac{1}{{{e^2}}}\]
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
Options: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Solution:
\[\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x + \frac{1}{2}\left[ {\ell n(1 + x) - \ell n(1 - x)} \right] - 2x}}{{{x^5}}}\]
\[ = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{\left( {x - \frac{{{x^3}}}{3} + \frac{{{x^5}}}{5}....} \right) + \frac{1}{2}\left[ {x - \frac{{{x^2}}}{2} + \frac{{{x^3}}}{3}... - \left( { - x - \frac{{{x^2}}}{2} - \frac{{{x^3}}}{3}...} \right)} \right] - 2x}}{{{x^5}}}\]
\[ = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{2x + \frac{{2{x^5}}}{5}.... - 2x}}{{{x^5}}} = \frac{2}{5}\]
\[\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 1} {x^{\frac{2}{{(1 - x)}}}}{e^{\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 1} \left( {\frac{2}{{1 - x}}} \right)(x - 1)}} = {e^{ - 2}}\]
⇒ Both statements correct

Question: Let $\vec a = \hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k$ and $\vec b = 2\hat i + \hat j - \hat k.$ Let $\vec c$ be a unit vector in the plane of the vectors $\vec a$ and $\vec b$ and be perpendicular to \[\vec a\]. Then such a vector $\vec c$ is :
Solution:
Let vector \[\vec p\]-in plane of \[\vec a\] & \[\vec b\]= K (\[\vec a \pm \lambda \vec b\])
\[\vec p \bot \vec a = \vec p.\vec a - = 0\]
$ \Rightarrow K\left( {\vec a + \lambda \vec b} \right).\,\vec a = 0$
$ \Rightarrow \vec a.\vec a + \lambda \vec b.\vec a = 0$
$ \Rightarrow 6 + \lambda (3) = 0$
$ \Rightarrow \lambda = - 2$
$ \Rightarrow \vec p = \left( { - 3\hat i + 3\hat k} \right)$
Unit vector $ \to \pm \frac{{\left( { - \hat i + \hat k} \right)}}{{\sqrt 2 }}$

Question: Choose the correct option for structures of A and B, respectively.

Let the area of the bounded region
{(x, y) :0 ≤ 9x ≤ y2, y ≥ 3x – 6} be A. Then 6A is equal to ______

0 ≤ 9x ≤ y2 & y ≥ 3x – 6

A = Required Area = \[\left[ {\int\limits_0^1 {\left( { - 3\sqrt x } \right)dx - \int\limits_0^1 {(3x - 6)dx} } } \right]\]
\[A = - 3\left. {\left( {\frac{{{x^{\frac{3}{2}}}}}{{\frac{3}{2}}}} \right)} \right|_0^1 - \left. {\left( {\frac{{3{x^2}}}{2} - 6x} \right)} \right|_0^1\]

Question:
The number of species from the following that are involved in sp3d2 hybridization is
[Co(NH3)6]3+, SF6, [CrF6]3–, [CoF6]3–,[Mn(CN)6]3– and [MnCl6]3–

In [Co(NH3]6]3+ , Co+3 : [Ar]3d6 , NH3 is S.F.L
Hybridisation state of Co3+ is d2sp3
In SF6 , Hybridisation state of sulphur is sp3d2
In [CrF6]3– , Cr+3 : [Ar]3d3

[CoF6]3–, Co+3 : [Ar]3d6 F– is W.F.L

Total number of sp3d2 hybridized molecules is 3

Question: Let the function ${\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = \frac{{\text{x}}}{3} + \frac{3}{{\text{x}}} + 3,{\text{x}} \ne 0$be strictly increasing in (–∞ , α <sub>1</sub>) U (α <sub>2</sub>, ∞ ) and strictly decreasing in (α <sub>3</sub>, α <sub>4</sub>) U (α <sub>4</sub>,α <sub>5</sub>). Then $\sum\limits_{i = 1}^5 {{\alpha _i}^2} $ is equal to
Solution:
${\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = \frac{{\text{x}}}{3} + \frac{3}{{\text{x}}} + 3,{\text{x}} \ne 0$
${\text{f'}}({\text{x}}) = \frac{1}{3} - \frac{3}{{{{\text{x}}^2}}} = 0\quad \Rightarrow {\text{x}} = \pm 3$
${\text{f'}}({\text{x}}) = \frac{{{{\text{x}}^2} - 9}}{{3{{\text{x}}^2}}}$
${\text{f}}({\text{x}}) > 0\,\,\forall ( - \infty , - 3) \cup (3,\infty ) \to {\text{increasing}}$
f'(x) < 0 ∀ (–3, 0) ∪ (0, 3) → decreasing
$\sum\limits_{i = 1}^5 {{\alpha _i}^2} = {\left( { - 3} \right)^2} + {\left( 3 \right)^2} + {\left( { - 3} \right)^2} + {\left( 0 \right)^2} + {\left( 3 \right)^2}$
= 36

Question: The integra $\int\limits_{ - 1}^{\frac{3}{2}} {\left( {\left| {{\pi ^2}x\sin \left( {\pi x} \right)} \right|} \right)dx} $  is equal to:
Solution:
Let, $I = {\pi ^2}\int\limits_{ - 1}^{\frac{3}{2}} {\left| {x\sin \pi x} \right|dx} $
$ = {\pi ^2}\left\{ {\int\limits_{ - 1}^1 {x\sin \pi xdx} - \int\limits_1^{\frac{3}{2}} {x\sin \pi x\,dx} } \right\}$
$ = {\pi ^2}\left\{ {2\int\limits_0^1 {x\sin \pi x\,dx} - \int\limits_1^{\frac{3}{2}} {x\sin \pi x\,dx} } \right\}$
Consider
$\int {x\sin \pi x\,dx} $
$ - {\text{x}}.\frac{1}{\pi }\cos \pi {\text{x}} + \smallint 1.\frac{1}{\pi }\cos \pi {\text{xdx}}$
$ = - \frac{{\text{x}}}{\pi }\cos \pi {\text{x}} + \frac{{\sin \pi {\text{x}}}}{{{\pi ^2}}}$
${\mathbf{I}} = {\pi ^2}\left\{ {2\left( { - \frac{{\mathbf{x}}}{\pi }\cos \pi {\mathbf{x}} + \frac{{\sin \pi {\mathbf{x}}}}{{{\pi ^2}}}} \right)_0^1 - \left( { - \frac{{\mathbf{x}}}{\pi }\cos \pi {\mathbf{x}} + \frac{{\sin \pi {\mathbf{x}}}}{{{\pi ^2}}}} \right)_1^{3/2}} \right\}$
$ = {\pi ^2}\left\{ {\frac{2}{\pi } - \left( { - \frac{1}{{{\pi ^2}}} - \frac{1}{\pi }} \right)} \right\}$
$ = {\pi ^2}\left\{ {\frac{3}{\pi } + \frac{1}{{{\pi ^2}}}} \right\}$
$ = 3\pi + 1$

Question: Let r be the radius of the circle, which touches x-axis at point (a, 0), a < 0 and the parabola y<sup>2</sup> = 9x at the point (4, 6). Then r is equal to ______
Solution:
(x – a)2 + (y – r)2 = r2
(4 – a)2 + (6 – r)2 = r2
16 + a2 – 8a + 36 + r2 – 12r = r2
a2 – 8a – 12r + 52 = 0
Tangent to parabola at (4, 6) is
$6.4 = 9.\left( {\frac{{{\text{x}} + 4}}{2}} \right){\text{i}}.{\text{e}}.3{\text{x}} - 4{\text{y}} + 12 = 0$
This is also tangent to the circle
∴ CP = r
$\frac{{3{\text{a}} - 4{\text{r}} + 12}}{5} = \pm {\text{r}}$
$3{\text{a}} + 12 = 4{\text{r}} \pm 5{\text{r}}\left\{ {_{ - r}^{ar}} \right.$ ……(1)
equation of circle is
(x – a)2 + (y – r)2 = r2
satsty P(4, 6) ⇒ a2 – 8a – 12r + 52 = 0 …..(2)
From equation (1)
If a + 4 = 3r then a = +6 (rejected)
If 3a + 12 = – r then a = –14 and r = 30

Question: A cord of negligible mass is wound around the rim of a wheel supported by spokes with negligible mass. The mass of wheel is 10 kg and radius is 10 cm and it can freely rotate without any friction. Initially the wheel is at rest. If a steady pull of 20 N is applied on the cord, the angular velocity of the wheel, after the cord is unwound by 1 m, would be :
Solution:
\[{W_F} = 20 \times 1 = 20J\]
\[\therefore \,\,\Delta KE = 20J = \frac{1}{2}\,I{\omega ^2}\]
\[I = M{R^2} = 10 \times {0.1^2} = 0.1\,kg\,{m^2}\]
\[\therefore \,20 = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.1 \times {\omega ^2}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \omega = 20\,rad/\sec \]

Question: A point charge +q is placed at the origin. A second point charge +9q is placed at (d, 0, 0) in Cartesian coordinate system. The point in between them where the electric field vanishes is :

\[\therefore \,\frac{{kq}}{{{x^2}}} = \frac{{k9q}}{{{{(d - x)}^2}}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{d - x}}{x} = 3 \Rightarrow x = \frac{d}{4}\]

Question:
The relationship between the magnetic susceptibility (χ ) and the magnetic permeability (μ ) is given by :
(μ 0 is the permeability of free space and μ r is relative permeability)
Solution:
We have
\[{\mu _r} = \left( {1 + \chi } \right) \Rightarrow \chi = ({\mu _r} - 1)\]
\[{\mu _r} = {\mu _0}{\mu _r} \Rightarrow {\mu _r} = \frac{\mu }{{{\mu _0}}}\]
\[\therefore \,\,\chi = \left( {\frac{\mu }{{{\mu _0}}} - 1} \right)\]

Question: In an adiabatic process, which of the following statements is true ?

The internal energy of the gas decreases as the temperature increases

For adiabatic process, dQ = 0

∵ dQ = 0 ⇒ dU = –dW

Question:
A monochromatic light is incident on a metallic plate having work function φ . An electron, emitted normally to the plate from a point A with maximum kinetic energy, enters a constant magnetic field, perpendicular to the initial velocity of electron. The electron passes through a curve and hits back the plate at a point B. The distance between A and B is :
Given : The magnitude of charge of an electron is e and mass is m, h is Planck's constant and c is velocity of light. Take the magnetic field exists throughout the path of electron)
Solution:
\[K{E_{\max }} = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda } - \phi \]
\[p = \sqrt {2m{K_{\max }}} \]
\[p = \sqrt {2m\left( {\frac{{hc}}{\lambda } - \phi } \right)} \]
\[{d_{A - B}} = 2R\]
\[ = 2\left[ {\frac{p}{{qB}}} \right]\]
\[{d_{AB}} = \frac{{2\sqrt {2m\left( {\frac{{hc}}{\lambda } - \phi } \right)} }}{{eB}} = \frac{{\sqrt {8m\left( {\frac{{hc}}{\lambda } - \phi } \right)} }}{{eB}}\]

Question:
In a moving coil galvanometer, two moving coils M1 and M2 have the following particulars:
\[{R_1} = 5\Omega ,{{\rm N}_1} = 15,{A_1} = 3.6 \times {10^{ - 3}}{m^2},{B_1} = 0.25T\]
\[{R_2} = 7\Omega ,{{\rm N}_2} = 21,{A_2} = 1.8 \times {10^{ - 3}}{m^2},{B_2} = 0.50T\]
Assuming that torsional constant of the springs are same for both coils, what will be the ratio of voltage sensitivity M1 and M2 ?
Solution:
Voltage sensitivity = \[\frac{\theta }{V} = \frac{{NAB}}{{cR}}\]
\[Ratio = \left( {\frac{{{N_1}{A_1}{B_1}}}{{{N_2}{A_2}{B_2}}}} \right)\frac{{{R_2}}}{{{R_1}}} = \frac{{15 \times 3.6 \times 0.25}}{{21 \times 1.8 \times 0.5}} \times \frac{7}{5} = \frac{1}{1}\]

Question: Given a charge q, current I and permeability of vacuum \[{\mu _0}\]. Which of the following quantity has the dimension of momentum ?
Solution:
Q = AT
     I = A
\[{\mu _0} = ML{T^{ - 2}}{A^{ - 2}}\]
\[P = {Q^x}\mu _0^y{I^z} = {\left[ {AT} \right]^x}{\left[ {ML{T^{ - 2}}{A^{ - 2}}} \right]^y}{\left[ A \right]^z}\]
\[ML{T^{ - 1}} = {M^y}{L^y}{T^{x - 2y}}{A^{ - 2y + z + x}}\]
\[Now;{\text{ y = 1}}\]
\[x - 2y = - 1\]
\[ - 2y + z = 0\]
\[\therefore x = y = z = 1\]

Question: A light wave is propagating with plane wave fronts of the type x + y + z = constant. The angle made by the direction of wave propagation with the x-axis is :
Solution:
The direction of propagation of light is perpendicular to the wave front and is symmetric about x, y and z axis.
∴ Angle made by the light with x, y & z axis is same.
∴ cosα = cosβ = cosγ (α , β & γ are angle made by light with x, y & z axis respectively)
Also cos2α + cos2β + cos2γ = 1 [Sum of direction cosines]
∴ α = cos-1 \[\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\]

Question: A steel wire of length 2 m and Young's modulus 2.0 × 10<sup>11</sup> Nm<sup>–2</sup> is stretched by a force. If Poisson ratio and transverse strain for the wire are 0.2 and 10<sup>–3</sup> respectively, then the elastic potential energy density of the wire is ____ × 10<sup>5</sup> (in SI units)
Solution:
\[\ell = 2m;Y = 2 \times {10^{11}}\frac{N}{{{m^2}}}\]
\[\mu = \frac{{\left( {\frac{{\Delta r}}{r}} \right)}}{{\left( {\frac{{\Delta \ell }}{\ell }} \right)}} \Rightarrow \frac{{\Delta \ell }}{\ell } = \frac{1}{\mu } \times \left( {\frac{{\Delta r}}{r}} \right) = \frac{1}{{0.2}} \times \left( {{{10}^{ - 3}}} \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{\Delta \ell }}{\ell } = 5 \times {10^{ - 3}}\]
\[u = \frac{1}{2}y\varepsilon _\ell ^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times {10^{11}} \times {[5 \times {10^{ - 3}}]^2}\]
= 25

Question: The length of a light string is 1.4 m when the tension on it is 5 N. If the tension increases to 7 N, the length of the string is 1.56 m. The original length of the string is _____ m.

\[ \Rightarrow 5 = K(1.4 - {\ell _0})\]
\[ \Rightarrow 7 = K(1.56 - {\ell _0})\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{5}{{1.4 - {\ell _0}}} = \frac{7}{{1.56 - {\ell _0}}}\]
\[\therefore {\ell _0} = 1{\text{ }}m\]

If a random variable $x$ has the probability distribution
$x$01234567
$p\left( x \right)$0$2k$$k$$3k$$2{k^3}$$2k$${k^2} + k$$7{k^2}$

then $\mathrm{P}(3<\mathrm{x} \leq 6)$ is equal to
Solution:

Question:
If $\frac{\cos ^{2} 48^{\circ}-\sin ^{2} 12^{\circ}}{\sin ^{2} 24^{\circ}-\sin ^{2} 6^{\circ}}=\frac{\alpha+\beta \sqrt{5}}{2}$, where $\alpha, \beta$
$\in \mathbb{N}$, then $\alpha+\beta$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
Use $\sin (\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}) \sin (\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{B})=\sin ^{2} \mathrm{~A}-\sin ^{2} \mathrm{~B}$
$$
\begin{gathered}
\cos (\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}) \cos (\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{B})=\cos ^{2} \mathrm{~A}-\sin ^{2} \mathrm{~B} \\
\frac{\cos 60^{\circ} \cos 36^{\circ}}{\sin 30^{\circ} \sin 18^{\circ}}=\frac{\sqrt{5}+1}{\sqrt{5}-1} \times \frac{\sqrt{5}+1}{\sqrt{5}+1}=\frac{(\sqrt{5}+1)^{2}}{4} \\
=\frac{3+\sqrt{5}}{2}
\end{gathered}
$$
$\alpha=3 ; \beta=1$
So, $(\alpha+\beta)=4$

Question: Let the solution curve of the differential equation $x d y-y d x=\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}} d x, x>0, y(1)=0$, be $y= y(x)$. Then $y(3)$ is equal to
Solution:
$\frac{x d y-y d x}{x^{2}}=\frac{\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}}{x^{2}} d x$
$\mathrm{d}\left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)=\sqrt{1+\frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}} \cdot \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}$
$\int \frac{\mathrm{d}\left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)}{\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)^{2}}}=\int \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}$
$\Rightarrow \ln \left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}+\sqrt{1+\frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}}\right)=\ln \mathrm{x}+\ln \mathrm{k}=\ln \mathrm{kx}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}}=\mathrm{k} \mathrm{x}^{2}$
$0+1=\mathrm{k}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}}=\mathrm{x}^{2}$
$y+\sqrt{9+y^{2}}=9$
$y=4$

Question:
Let the relation R on the set $\mathrm{M}=\{1,2,3, \ldots \ldots .16\}$ be given by

Then the minimum number of elements required to be added in R , in order to make the relation symmetric, is equal to

Question: The number of solutions of $\tan ^{-1} 4 \mathrm{x}+\tan ^{-1} 6 \mathrm{x}=\frac{\pi}{6}$, where $ - \frac{1}{{2\sqrt 6 }} < x < \frac{1}{{2\sqrt 6 }}$ is equal to
Solution:
$\tan ^{-1} 4 \mathrm{x}+\tan ^{-1} 6 \mathrm{x}=\frac{\pi}{6}$
$\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4 x+6 x}{1+24 x^{2}}\right)=\frac{\pi}{6}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{10 \mathrm{x}}{1-24 \mathrm{x}^{2}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$
$\Rightarrow 24 \mathrm{x}^{2}+10 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{x}-1=0$
$\mathrm{x}=\frac{-10 \sqrt{3} \pm \sqrt{300+96}}{48}$
$\mathrm{x}=\frac{\sqrt{396}-10 \sqrt{3}}{48}$
Only 1 solution in $\left(-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{6}}, \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{6}}\right)$

Question: Let $\overrightarrow{A B}=2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-5 k$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AD}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\lambda \mathrm{k}, \lambda \in \mathbb{R}$. Let the projection of the vector $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}$ on the diagonal $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AC}}$ of the parallelogram ABCD be of length one unit. If $\alpha, \beta$, where $\alpha>\beta$, be the roots of the equation $\lambda^{2} \mathrm{x}^{2}- 6 \lambda \mathrm{x}+5=0$, then $2 \alpha-\beta$ is equal to
Solution:
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AC}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(\lambda-5) \mathrm{k}$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}} \cdot \mathrm{AC}=1 \Rightarrow 3+6+\lambda-5=\sqrt{9+36+(\lambda-5)^{2}}$

$\Rightarrow \lambda^{2}+8 \lambda+16=\lambda^{2}-10 \lambda+70$
$\Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{54}{18}=3$
$\therefore $ Quadratic : $9 \mathrm{x}^{2}-18 \mathrm{x}+5=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{1}{3}, \frac{5}{3}$
$\therefore 2 \alpha-\beta=\frac{10-1}{3}=3$

Question: If the chord joining the points $\mathrm{P}_{1}\left(\mathrm{x}_{1}, \mathrm{y}_{1}\right)$ and $\mathrm{P}_{2}\left(\mathrm{x}_{2}\right.$, $y_{2}$ ) on the parabola $y^{2}=12 x$ subtends a right angle at the vertex of the parabola, then $x_{1} x_{2}-y_{1} y_{2}$ is equal to
Solution:
$\left(\mathrm{x}_{1} \mathrm{y}_{1}\right)=\left(3 \mathrm{t}_{1}^{2}, 6 \mathrm{t}_{1}\right) \&\left(\mathrm{x}_{2} \mathrm{y}_{2}\right)=\left(3 \mathrm{t}_{2}^{2}, 6 \mathrm{t}_{2}\right)$
$\mathrm{t}_{1} \mathrm{t}_{2}=-4$
$x_{1} x_{2}=9\left(t_{1} t_{2}\right)^{2}, y_{1} y_{2}=36 t_{1} t_{2}$
$\mathrm{x}_{1} \mathrm{x}_{2}-\mathrm{y}_{1} \mathrm{y}_{2}=9(16)-36(-4)$
$=144+144$
$=288$

Question: The coefficient of $x^{48}$ in $(1+x)+2(1+x)^{2}+3(1 +\mathrm{x})^{3}+\ldots .+100(1+\mathrm{x})^{100}$ is equal to :
Solution:
Let $1+\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{r}$
$\therefore \mathrm{S}=1 . \mathrm{r}+2 . \mathrm{r}^{2}+3 . \mathrm{r}^{3}+\ldots \ldots+100 \mathrm{r}^{100}$
$\mathrm{rS}=1 . \mathrm{r}^{2}+2 . \mathrm{r}^{3}+\ldots . .+99 \mathrm{r}^{100}+100 \mathrm{r}^{101}$
(1) - (2) gives
$\mathrm{S}=-\frac{(1+\mathrm{x})^{101}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}+\frac{100(1+\mathrm{x})^{101}}{\mathrm{x}}$
$\therefore $ coefficient $\mathrm{x}^{48}$ in S
$=-$ coefficient of $x^{48}$ in $\frac{(1+x)^{101}}{x^{2}}+100$.
Coefficient of $\mathrm{x}^{48}$ in $\frac{(1+\mathrm{x})^{101}}{\mathrm{x}}$
$=100{ }^{101} \mathrm{C}_{49}-{ }^{101} \mathrm{C}_{50}$

Question:
If $\int(\sin \mathrm{x})^{\frac{-11}{2}}(\cos \mathrm{x})^{\frac{-5}{2}} \mathrm{dx}=$ $-\frac{\mathrm{p}_{1}}{\mathrm{q}_{1}}(\cot \mathrm{x})^{\frac{9}{2}}-\frac{\mathrm{p}_{2}}{\mathrm{q}_{2}}(\cot \mathrm{x})^{\frac{5}{2}}-\frac{\mathrm{p}_{3}}{\mathrm{q}_{3}}(\cot \mathrm{x})^{\frac{1}{2}}+\frac{\mathrm{p}_{4}}{\mathrm{q}_{4}}(\cot \mathrm{x})^{\frac{-3}{2}}+\mathrm{C}$,
where $p_{i}$ and $q_{i}$ are positive integers with $\operatorname{gcd}\left(p_{i}, q_{i}\right) =1$ for $\mathrm{i}=1,2,3,4$ and C is the constant of integration, then $\frac{15 p_{1} p_{2} p_{3} p_{4}}{q_{1} q_{2} q_{3} q_{4}}$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$\int(\tan \mathrm{x})^{-11 / 2} \cdot \sec ^{8} \mathrm{x} \mathrm{dx}$
$=\int(\tan \mathrm{x})^{-11 / 2}\left(1+\tan ^{2} \mathrm{x}\right) 3 \sec ^{2} \mathrm{x} \mathrm{dx}$
Put $\tan \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{t}$
$\Rightarrow \int \mathrm{t}^{-11 / 2}\left(1+\mathrm{t}^{2}\right)^{3} \mathrm{dx}=\int \mathrm{t}^{-11 / 2}\left(1+\mathrm{t}^{6}+3 \mathrm{t}^{2}+3(4)\right) \mathrm{dt}$
$=\int\left(\mathrm{t}^{-11 / 2}+\mathrm{t}^{1 / 2}+3 . \mathrm{t}^{-7 / 2}+3 . \mathrm{t}^{-3 / 2}\right) \mathrm{dt}$
$=-\frac{2}{9}(\cot \mathrm{x})^{9 / 2}-\frac{6}{5}(\cot )^{5 / 2}-6(\cot \mathrm{x})^{1 / 2}+\frac{2}{3}(\cot \mathrm{x})^{-3 / 2}+\mathrm{C}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{p}_{1}=2, \mathrm{p}_{2}=6, \mathrm{p}_{3}=6, \mathrm{p}_{4}=2$
$\& \mathrm{q}_{1}=9, \mathrm{q}_{2}=5, \mathrm{q}_{3}=1, \mathrm{q}_{4}=3$
$\frac{15 \mathrm{p}_{1} \mathrm{p}_{2} \mathrm{p}_{3} \mathrm{p}_{4}}{\mathrm{q}_{1} \mathrm{q}_{2} \mathrm{q}_{3} \mathrm{q}_{4}}=\frac{15 \cdot 2 \cdot 6 \cdot 6 \cdot 2}{9 \cdot 5 \cdot 1 \cdot 3}=16$

Question:
Let $\mathrm{P}(\alpha, \beta, \gamma)$ be the point on the line
$\frac{\mathrm{x}-1}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}+1}{-3}=\mathrm{z}$ at a distance $4 \sqrt{14}$ from the point $(1,-1,0)$ and nearer to the origin. Then the shortest distance, between the lines $\frac{x-\alpha}{1}=\frac{y-\beta}{2}=\frac{z-\gamma}{3}$ and $\frac{x+5}{2}=\frac{y-10}{1}=\frac{z-3}{1}$ , is equal to
Solution:
Let $\mathrm{P}(2 \lambda+1,-3 \lambda-1, \lambda)$
Then $4 \lambda^{2}+9 \lambda^{2}+\lambda^{2}=16 \cdot 14 \Rightarrow \lambda= \pm 4 \Rightarrow-4$ (nearer to origin)
$\therefore \mathrm{P}(-7,11,-4)$
$\therefore $ Shortest distance $=\frac{\left|\begin{array}{ccc}2 & -1 & 7 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 1 & 1\end{array}\right|}{\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \mathrm{k} \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 1 & 1\end{array}\right|}$
$=\frac{28}{\sqrt{1+25+9}}=\frac{4 \sqrt{7}}{\sqrt{5}}$

Question: Let A be a $3 \times 3$ matrix such that $\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{A}^{\mathrm{T}}=\mathrm{O}$. If  $\mathrm{A}\left[\begin{array}{c}1 \\ -1 \\ 0\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l}3 \\ 3 \\ 2\end{array}\right], \mathrm{A}^2\left[\begin{array}{c}1 \\ -1 \\ 0\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c}-3 \\ 19 \\ -24\end{array}\right]$ and $\operatorname{det}(\operatorname{adj}(2 \operatorname{adj} (A+I)))=(2)^\alpha \cdot(3)^\beta \cdot(11)^\gamma, \alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are non-negative integers, then $\alpha+\beta+\gamma$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$
Solution:
$\quad \mathrm{A}\left[\begin{array}{l}1 \\ -1 \\ 0\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l}3 \\ 3 \\ 2\end{array}\right]$ and $\mathrm{A}\left[\begin{array}{l}3 \\ 3 \\ 2\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l}-3 \\ 19 \\ -24\end{array}\right]$
det. $\left(\operatorname{adj} .(2 \operatorname{adj}(\mathrm{~A}+\mathrm{I}))=|2 \operatorname{adj}(\mathrm{~A}+\mathrm{I})|^{2}=64|\operatorname{adj}(\mathrm{~A}+\mathrm{I})|^{2}\right. =64|\mathrm{~A}+\mathrm{I}|^{4}$
Let $\mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}0 & \mathrm{a} & \mathrm{b} \\ -\mathrm{a} & 0 & \mathrm{c} \\ -\mathrm{b} & -\mathrm{c} & 0\end{array}\right] \Rightarrow\left\{\begin{array}{l}-\mathrm{a}=3 \\ -\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}=2 \\ 3 \mathrm{a}+2 \mathrm{~b}=-3\end{array}\right\}$
$\Rightarrow\left\{\begin{array}{l}\mathrm{a}=-3 \\ \mathrm{~b}=3 \\ \mathrm{c}=5\end{array}\right.$
$\therefore|\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{I}|=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & -3 & 3 \\ 3 & 1 & 5 \\ -3 & -5 & 1\end{array}\right|=44$

Question: Let $f(x)=x^{2025}-x^{2000}, x \in[0,1]$ and the minimum value of the function $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ in the interval $[0,1]$ be $(80)^{80}(\mathrm{n})^{-81}$. Then n is equal to
Solution:

$\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\left(\frac{2000}{2025}\right)^{1 / 25}=\alpha($ say $)$
$\therefore f(0)=0, f(1)=0, f(\alpha)=\left(\frac{80}{81}\right)^{80} \cdot \frac{-1}{81}=80^{80} \cdot(-81)^{-81}$

Question:
Let $\alpha=\frac{-1+i \sqrt{3}}{2}$ and $\beta=\frac{-1-i \sqrt{3}}{2}, i=\sqrt{-1}$. If $$
(7-7 \alpha+9 \beta)^{20}+(9+7 \alpha-7 \beta)^{20}+(-7+9 \alpha+7 \beta)^{20}
$$
$+(14+7 \alpha+7 \beta)^{20}=\mathrm{m}^{10}$, then m is $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$\left(9+7 \omega-7 \omega^2\right)+\omega^{20}\left(9+7 \omega-7 \omega^2\right)^{20}+$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \omega^{40}\left(9+7 \omega-7 \omega^2\right)^{20}+\left(14+7\left(\omega+\omega^2\right)\right)^{20} \\
& \left(9+7 \omega-7 \omega^2\right)^{20}\left(1+\omega+\omega^2\right)+(14-7)^{20}=7^{20} \\
& =(49)^{10}
\end{aligned}
$$
Hence, $\mathrm{M}=49$

Question: If the sum of the first four terms of an A.P. is 6 and the sum of its first six terms is 4 , then the sum of its first twelve terms is
Solution:
Sum of first 4 term $\mathrm{S}_4=6$
$$
\frac{4}{2}(2 a+3 d)=6 \Rightarrow 2 a+3 d=3
$$
Sum of first 6 terms $\mathrm{S}_6=4$
$$
\frac{6}{2}(2 a+5 d)=4 \Rightarrow 2 a+5 d=\frac{4}{3} \ldots . .
$$
Eq. (2) - Eq. (1)
$$
\begin{aligned}
& (2 a+5 d)-(2 a+3 d)=\frac{4}{3}-3 \\
& \Rightarrow d=-\frac{5}{6} \\
& \therefore 2 a+3\left(-\frac{5}{6}\right)=3 \Rightarrow a=\frac{11}{4} \\
& S_{12}=\frac{12}{2}\left\{2 \times \frac{11}{4}+(12-1)\left(-\frac{5}{6}\right)\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
S_{12}=6\left(-\frac{22}{6}\right)=-22
$$

Question: Let the line $\mathrm{x}=-1$ divide the area of the region $\left\{(x, y): 1+x^{2} \leq y \leq 3-x\right\}$ in the ratio $m: n, g c d (\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{n})=1$. Then $\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}$ is equal to
Solution:
$\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{\int_{-1}^{1}\left[(3-\mathrm{x})-\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)\right] \mathrm{dx}}{\int_{-2}^{1}\left[(3-\mathrm{x})-\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)\right] \mathrm{dx}}=\frac{20}{7}$
$\therefore \mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}=20+7$
$=27$

Question: The value of $\int_{-\frac{\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}\left(\frac{1}{[\mathrm{x}]+4}\right) \mathrm{dx}$, where [•] denotes the greatest integer function, is

$=\frac{1}{2}\left(-1+\frac{\pi}{2}\right)+\frac{1}{3}(1)+\frac{1}{4}(1)+\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-1\right) \frac{1}{5}$
$=\frac{7 \pi}{20}-\frac{7}{60}=\frac{7}{60}(3 \pi-1)$

Question: If the image of the point $\mathrm{P}(1,2, a)$ in the line $\frac{\mathrm{x}-6}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-7}{2}=\frac{7-\mathrm{z}}{2}$ is $\mathrm{Q}(5, \mathrm{~b}, \mathrm{c})$, then $\mathrm{a}^{2}+\mathrm{b}^{2}+ \mathrm{c}^{2}$ is equal to
Solution:
Point $\mathrm{M} \equiv\left(3, \frac{\mathrm{~b}}{2}+1, \frac{\mathrm{c}+\mathrm{a}}{2}\right)$ satisfies the line
$\frac{3-6}{3}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{b}}{2}+1-7}{2}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{c}+\mathrm{a}}{2}-7}{-2}$
$-1=\frac{\mathrm{b}-12}{4}=\frac{\mathrm{c}+\mathrm{a}-14}{-4}$

$\Rightarrow(4 \mathrm{i}+(\mathrm{b}-2) \mathrm{j}+(\mathrm{c}-\mathrm{a}) \mathrm{k}) \cdot(3 \mathrm{i}+2 \mathrm{j}-2 \mathrm{k})=0$
$\Rightarrow 12+2(\mathrm{~b}-2)-2(\mathrm{c}-\mathrm{a})=0$
$\Rightarrow 6+(\mathrm{b}-2)-(\mathrm{c}-\mathrm{a})=0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{b}-\mathrm{c}+\mathrm{a}+4=0$
$\Rightarrow 8-\mathrm{c}+\mathrm{a}+4=0$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\Rightarrow \mathrm{c}+\mathrm{a}=12 \tag{3}
\end{equation*}
$$
From (2) & (3)
$\mathrm{c}=15 \& \mathrm{a}=3$
So $\mathrm{a}^{2}+\mathrm{b}^{2}+\mathrm{c}^{2}=9+64+225=298$

Question: If the line $\alpha x+2 y=1$, where $\alpha \in \mathbb{R}$, does not meet the hyperbola $x^{2}-9 y^{2}=9$, then a possible value of $\alpha$ is :
Solution:
$\mathrm{y}=\frac{1-\alpha \mathrm{x}}{2}$
Put this in equation of hyperbola
$\therefore \mathrm{x}^{2}-9\left(\frac{1-\alpha \mathrm{x}}{2}\right)^{2}=9$
$\left(4-9 \alpha^{2}\right) x^{2}+18 \alpha x-45=0$
$\because $ line does not intersect hyperbola
$\therefore \mathrm{D}<0$
$\Rightarrow \alpha^{2}-\frac{5}{9}>0$
$\Rightarrow \alpha \in\left(-\infty,-\frac{\sqrt{5}}{3}\right) \cup\left(\frac{\sqrt{5}}{3}, \infty\right)$
Here $\frac{\sqrt{5}}{3} \simeq 0.74$

Question: Let $f:[1, \infty) \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a differentiable function, If $6 \int_{1}^{x} f(t) d t=3 x f(x)+x^{3}-4$ for all $\mathrm{x} \geq 1$, then the value of $f(2)-f(3)$ is
Solution:
$\quad 6 \int_{1}^{x} f(t) d t=3 x f(x)+x^{3}-4$
Diff. both side
$6 \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=3 \mathrm{x} \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})+3 \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})+3 \mathrm{x}^{2}$
$3 \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=3 \mathrm{x} \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})+3 \mathrm{x}^{2}$
$x \frac{d y}{d x}-y=-x^{2}$
$\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-9}{x^{2}}=-1$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)=-1$
$\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}=-\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=-\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{Cx}$
at $\mathrm{x}=1, \mathrm{y}=1 \Rightarrow \mathrm{C}=2$
$f(x)=-x^{2}+2 x$
$f(2)-f(3)=3$

Question: Let ABC be a triangle. Consider four points $\mathrm{p}_{1}, \mathrm{p}_{2}$, $\mathrm{p}_{3}, \mathrm{p}_{4}$ on the side AB , five points $\mathrm{p}_{5}, \mathrm{p}_{6}, \mathrm{p}_{7}, \mathrm{p}_{8}, \mathrm{p}_{9}$ on the side BC and four points $\mathrm{p}_{10}, \mathrm{p}_{11}, \mathrm{p}_{12}, \mathrm{p}_{13}$ on the side AC . None of these points is a vertex of the triangle ABC . Then the total number of pentagons, that can be formed by taking all the vertices from the points $\mathrm{p}_{1}, \mathrm{p}_{2}, \ldots . \mathrm{p}_{13}$, is $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
Case 1:
2 from $\mathrm{AB}, 2$ from $\mathrm{BC}, 1$ from AC
$\binom{4}{2} \cdot\binom{5}{2} \cdot\binom{4}{1}=6 \cdot 10 \cdot 4=240$

Case 2:
2 from $\mathrm{AB}, 1$ from $\mathrm{BC}, 2$ from AC
$\binom{4}{2} \cdot\binom{5}{1} \cdot\binom{4}{2}=6 \cdot 5 \cdot 6=180$

Case 3:
1 from $\mathrm{AB}, 2$ from $\mathrm{BC}, 2$ from AC
$\binom{4}{1} \cdot\binom{5}{2} \cdot\binom{4}{2}=4 \cdot 10 \cdot 6=240$

Question: The number of distinct real solutions of the equation $x|x+4|+3|x+2|+10=0$ is
Solution:
Case I: $\mathrm{x}<-4$
$x(-(x+4))+3(-(x+2))+10=0$
$\mathrm{x}^{2}+7 \mathrm{x}-4=0$
$\Rightarrow x=-\frac{7+\sqrt{65}}{2}$ or $-\frac{7-\sqrt{65}}{2}$
$[$But only  $x=-\frac{7+\sqrt{65}}{2}$ is less than $-4]$
Case II: $-4 \leq \mathrm{x}<-2$
$x(x+4)+3(-(x+2))+10=0$
$\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{x}+4=0$
$\mathrm{D}<0$ No solution
Case III: $\mathrm{x} \geq-1$
$\mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}+4)+3(\mathrm{x}+2)+10=0$
$\mathrm{x}^{2}+7 \mathrm{x}+16=0$
$\mathrm{D}<0$
No solution
⇒ No. of solution $=1$.

Question: If $\mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{ll}2 & 3 \\ 3 & 5\end{array}\right]$, then the determinant of the matrix ( $\mathrm{A}^{2025}-3 \mathrm{~A}^{2024}+\mathrm{A}^{2023}$ ) is
Solution:
$\mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{ll}2 & 3 \\ 3 & 5\end{array}\right] \Rightarrow \mathrm{A}^{2}=\left[\begin{array}{ll}13 & 21 \\ 21 & 34\end{array}\right]$
$\left|\mathrm{A}^{2025}-3 \mathrm{~A}^{2024}+\mathrm{A}^{2023}\right|$
$=\left|\mathrm{A}^{2023}\left(\mathrm{~A}^{2}-3 \mathrm{~A}+\mathrm{I}\right)\right|$
$=|\mathrm{A}|^{2023}\left|\mathrm{~A}^{2}-3 \mathrm{~A}+\mathrm{I}\right|$
$=1 .\left|\begin{array}{cc}8 & 12 \\ 12 & 20\end{array}\right|=160-144=16$

Question: Let the set of all values of r , for which the circles $(x+1)^{2}+(y+4)^{2}=r^{2}$ and $x^{2}+y^{2}-4 x-2 y-4=0$ intersect at two distinct points be the interval ( $\alpha$, $\beta$ ). Then $\alpha \beta$ is equal to
Solution:
$(x-2)^{2}+(y-1)^{2}=3^{2} \&(x+1)^{2}+(y+4)^{2}=r^{2}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \left|\mathrm{r}_{1}-\mathrm{r}_{2}\right|<\mathrm{c}_{1} \mathrm{c}_{2}<\mathrm{r}_{1}+\mathrm{r}_{2} \\
& |\mathrm{r}-3|<\sqrt{(2+1)^{2}+(1+4)^{2}}<\mathrm{r}+3 \\
& |\mathrm{r}-3|<\sqrt{34} \& \mathrm{r}+3>\sqrt{34} \\
& -\sqrt{34}<\mathrm{r}-3<\sqrt{34} \& \mathrm{r}>\sqrt{34}-3
\end{aligned}
$$
i.e. $r=(3-\sqrt{34}, 3+\sqrt{34}) \cap(\sqrt{34}-3, \infty)$
i.e. $r \in(\sqrt{34}-3, \sqrt{34}+3)$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \alpha \beta=(\sqrt{34}-3)(\sqrt{34}+3) \\
& =34-9 \\
& =25
\end{aligned}
$$

Question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement-I: The correct order in terms of atomic/ionic radii is $\mathrm{Al}>\mathrm{Mg}>\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}>\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$.
Statement-II: The correct order in terms of the magnitude of electron gain enthalpy is $\mathrm{Cl}>\mathrm{Br}>\mathrm{S}>\mathrm{O}$.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
Options: Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Solution:
Correct order of size is $\mathrm{Mg}>\mathrm{Al}>\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}>\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$
Atomic size depends mainly upon $\mathrm{Z}_{\text {effective }}$ and shell number.
Generally on moving down the group electron affinity decreases and on moving across the period electron affinity increase.
In the periodic table Cl has maximum electron affinity. Halogen has higher electron affinity than Chalcogen.
Cl > Br > S > O

Question:
A substance ' X ' ( 1.5 g ) dissolved in 150 g of a solvent ' Y ' (molar mass $=300 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ) led to an elevation of the boiling point by 0.5 K . The relative lowering in the vapour pressure of the solvent ' Y ' is $\_\_\_\_$ $\times 10^{-2}$. (Nearest integer)
[Given : $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}$ of the solvent $=5.0 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ]
Assume the solution to be dilute and no association or dissociation of X takes place in solution.
Solution:
$\quad \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=\mathrm{i} \times \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}} \times \mathrm{m}$
$0.5=\mathrm{i} \times \mathrm{m} \times 5$
$\mathrm{i} \times \mathrm{m}=\frac{0.5}{5}=0.1$
$\mathrm{i} \times \mathrm{a}=\frac{15}{1000}$
(where $\mathrm{a}=$ moles of solute)
Now,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{P_{o}-P_{S}}{P^{o}}=i X_{\text {solute }}=i \times \frac{a}{a+\frac{150}{300}} \\
& =i \times \frac{a}{1 / 2}=\frac{15 / 1000}{1 / 2}=\frac{30}{1000}=3 \times 10^{-2}=3
\end{aligned}
$$

Question:
The osmotic pressure of a living cell in 12 atm at 300 K . The strength of sodium chloride solution that is isotonic with the living cell at this temperature is _______ $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{L}^{-1}$. (Nearest integer)
Given : $\mathrm{R}=0.08 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{~atm} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
Assume complete dissociation of NaCl
(Given : Molar mass of Na and Cl are 23 and 35.5 $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ respectively.)
Solution:
$\pi=\mathrm{iCRT}$
$12=2 \times \mathrm{C} \times 0.08 \times 300$
$12=2 \times \mathrm{C} \times 24$
$\mathrm{C}=\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{~mole} / \mathrm{L}$
then strength of NaCl solution
$=\frac{1}{4} \times 58.5 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{L}$
$=14.625 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{L}$
$=15 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{L}$

Question: The first and second ionization constants of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{X}$ are $2.5 \times 10^{-8}$ and $1.0 \times 10^{-13}$ respectively. The concentration of $\mathrm{X}^{2-}$ in $0.1 \mathrm{M} \ \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{X}$ solution is ________ $\times 10^{-15} \mathrm{M}$. (Nearest Integer)
Solution:
$1 \times 10^{-13}=\frac{(\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y})(\mathrm{y})}{\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}}$
Approximate : $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}_{1}} \gg \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{a}_{2}} \Rightarrow$ So $\mathrm{x} \gg \mathrm{y}$.
$\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y} \approx \mathrm{x}, \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y} \approx \mathrm{x}$
$10^{-13}=\frac{\mathrm{x} . \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}$
$\mathrm{y}=10^{-13}$
$\left[\mathrm{X}^{2-}\right]=10^{-13}$
$\left[\mathrm{X}^{2-}\right]=100 \times 10^{-15}$

Question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I: The correct order in terms of bond dissociation enthalpy is $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}>\mathrm{Br}_{2}>\mathrm{F}_{2}>\mathrm{I}_{2}$
Statement II: The correct trend in the covalent character of the metal halides is $\left[\mathrm{SnCl}_{4}>\mathrm{SnCl}_{2}\right]$, $\left[\mathrm{PbCl}_{4}>\mathrm{PbCl}_{2}\right]$ and $\left[\mathrm{UF}_{4}>\mathrm{UF}_{6}\right]$
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
Options: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Solution:
Statement-I :
Bond energy order is $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}>\mathrm{Br}_{2}>\mathrm{F}_{2}>\mathrm{I}_{2}$
Bond energy increases with increase in bond order.
Statement-II
Correct order of covalent character
According to the Fajan's rule, higher the charge on cation, greater is the covalent character.
$\mathrm{PbCl}_{2}<\mathrm{PbCl}_{4}$,
$\mathrm{UF}_{6}>\mathrm{UF}_{4}$,
$\mathrm{SnCl}_{4}>\mathrm{SnCl}_{2}$

Question:
Identify the characteristics of an adiabatic process in a monoatomic gas.
(A) Internal energy is constant.
(B) Work done in the process is equal to the charge in internal energy.
(C) The product of temperature and volume is a constant.
(D) The product of pressure and volume is a constant.
(E) The work done to change the temperature from Tl to T2 is proportional to (T2 – Tl)
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
Solution:
\[Q = \Delta U + W = 0 \Rightarrow - \Delta U = W\]
\[WD = - n{C_v}\Delta T \Rightarrow \left| {WD} \right| = n{C_v}\Delta T \propto {T_2} - {T_1}\]
\[\therefore \] B & E [Only possibility]

Question:
Energy released when two deuterons (1H2) fuse to form a helium nucleus (2He4) is :
(Given : Binding energy per nucleon of 1H2 = 1.1 MeV
and binding energy per nucleon of 2He4 = 7.0 MeV)
Solution:
\[\mathop {_1{H^2}}\limits_{1.1{\text{ }}MeV} + {\,_1}{H^2} \to {\text{ }}\mathop {_2H{e^4}}\limits_{7.0{\text{ }}MeV} \]
\[{E_B} = B{E_{{\text{reactant}}}} - B{E_{product}}\]
\[ = 1.1 \times 2 + 1.1 \times 2 - 7 \times 4 = - 23.6{\text{ }}MeV\]
\[ = Q = 23.6{\text{ MeV}}\]

Question:
A vessel with square cross–section and height of 6 m is vertically partitioned. A small window of 100 cm2 with hinged door is fitted at a depth of 3 m in the partition wall. One part of the vessel is filled completely with water and the other side is filled with the liquid having density 1.5 × 103 kg/m3. What force one needs to apply on the hinged door so that it does not get opened?
(Acceleration due to gravity = 10 m/s2)
Solution:
in equilibrium
\[{F_{ext}} + {F_w} = {F_\ell }\]
\[ \Rightarrow {F_{ext}} = {F_\ell } - {F_w}\]
\[ = \left( {{P_0} + {\rho _\ell }gh} \right)A - ({P_0} + {\rho _w}gh)A\]
\[ = ({\rho _\ell } - {p_w})ghA\]
\[ = (1500 - 1000) \times 10 \times 3 \times (100 \times {10^{ - 4}})\]
\[ = 150\,m\]

Question: Let A(4, –2), B(1, 1) and C(9, –3) be the vertices of a triangle ABC. Then the maximum area of the parallelogram AFDE, formed with vertices D, E and F on the sides BC, CA and AB of the triangle ABC respectively, is __________.
Solution:
Area of \[\Delta ABC = \frac{1}{2}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  4&{ - 2}&1 \\
  1&1&1 \\
  9&{ - 3}&1
\end{array}} \right|\]
= 6 square units
Maximum area of AFDE =\[\frac{1}{2} \times 6 = 3\]sq. units

Question: For the given logic gate circuit, which of the following is the correct truth table?
Solution:
Gate 1 : At bottom there is an OR gate with inputs n & m
out put $=n+m$
Gate 2 : A NAND gate, its Input are direct n & the output of OR gate ( $n+m$ )
out put $\mathrm{z}=\overline{\mathrm{n} \cdot(\mathrm{n}+\mathrm{m})}$
since $\mathrm{n} .(\mathrm{n}+\mathrm{m})=(\mathrm{n} . \mathrm{n})+(\mathrm{n} . \mathrm{m})$
$=\mathrm{n}+\mathrm{n} . \mathrm{m}=\mathrm{n}(1+\mathrm{m})=\mathrm{n}$
∴ output $\mathrm{z}=\overline{\mathrm{n} \cdot(\mathrm{n}+\mathrm{m})}=\overline{\mathrm{n}}$
nm\[z = \bar n\]
001
011
110
100

Question:
A prism of angle $75^{\circ}$ and refractive index $\sqrt{3}$ is coated with thin film of refractive index 1.5 only at the back exit surface. To have total internal reflection at the back exit surface the incident angle must be $\_\_\_\_$ .
( $\sin 15^{\circ}=0.25$ and $\sin 25^{\circ}=0.43$ )
Solution:
$\mathrm{r}_{1}+\mathrm{r}_{2}=75^{\circ}$
For TIR at back surface
$\sqrt{3} \sin \mathrm{r}_{2}=\frac{3}{2} \sin 90^{\circ}$
$\mathrm{r}_{2} \geq 60^{\circ}$
$\mathrm{r}_{1} \leq 15^{\circ}$
$1 \sin \mathrm{i}=\sqrt{3} \sin 15^{\circ}$
$\sin \mathrm{i}=1.73 \times .25$
$\sin \mathrm{i}=.433$
$\mathrm{i}=25^{\circ} \Rightarrow \mathrm{i}<25^{\circ}$

NTA gave option a, but a, b, and d can be correct.

Question: One mole of an ideal diatomic gas expands from volume V to 2 V isothermally at a temperature $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and does W joule of work. If the gas undergoes same magnitude of expansion adiabatically from $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ doing the same amount of work W , then its final temperature will be (close to) $\_\_\_\_$ ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.
Solution:
For Isothermal process
$$
\begin{align*}
\mathrm{W}_{\text {isothermal }} & =\mathrm{nRT} \ell \mathrm{n}\left(\frac{\mathrm{~V}_{2}}{\mathrm{~V}_{1}}\right) \\
= & 1 . \mathrm{R} 300 . \ell \mathrm{n}(2) \\
= & 300 \mathrm{R}(0.693) \ldots . . \tag{1}
\end{align*}
$$
Now for adiabatic process,
It is given work done in Isothermal = work done in adiabatic
$$
\begin{equation*}
\mathrm{W}_{\text {adiabatic }}=\frac{\mathrm{nR}\left(\mathrm{~T}_{1}-\mathrm{T}_{2}\right)}{\gamma-1} \tag{2}
\end{equation*}
$$
(1) = (2)
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{\mathrm{nR}\left(300-\mathrm{T}_{\text {final }}\right)}{1.4-1}=300 \mathrm{R}(0.693) \\
& \mathrm{T}_{\text {Final }}=216.84 \mathrm{~K} \\
& =-56.3^{\circ} \mathrm{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

Question:
The internal energy of a monoatomic gas is 3 nRT . One mole of helium is kept in a cylinder having internal cross section area of $17 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ and fitted with a light movable frictionless piston. The gas is heated slowly by suppling 126 J heat. If the temperature rises by $4^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, then the piston will move $\_\_\_\_$ cm.
(atmospheric pressure $=10^{5} \mathrm{~Pa}$ )
Solution:
$\Delta \mathrm{U}=3 \mathrm{nR} \Delta \mathrm{T}$
$\Delta \mathrm{U}=3 \times 1 \times \frac{25}{3} \times 4=100$ Joule
$\Delta \mathrm{Q}=126$
$\mathrm{W}=26=\mathrm{P} \Delta \mathrm{V}$
$26=10^{5} \times 17 \times 10^{-4} \Delta \mathrm{x}$
$\Delta \mathrm{x}=\frac{26}{170}=15.3$

Question:
A block is sliding down on an inclined plane of slope $\theta$ and at an instant $t=0$ this block is given an upward momentum so that it starts moving up on the inclined surface with velocity $u$. The distance $(S)$ travelled by the block before its velocity become zero, is $\_\_\_\_$ .
(g = gravitational acceleration)
Solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& a=-g \sin \theta \\
& V^{2}=U^{2}+2 a s \\
& 0=u^{2}-2 g \sin \theta . s \\
& s=\frac{u^{2}}{2 g \sin \theta}
\end{aligned}
$$

NTA gave option d, not matching with solution

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Motion of a body on a smooth inclined plane

Question:
A body of mass 14 kg initially at rest explodes and breaks into three fragments of masses in the ratio
$2: 2: 3$. The two pieces of equal masses fly off perpendicular to each other with a speed of $18 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ each. The velocity of the heavier fragment is
$\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$.
Solution:
Apply C.O.M
$\mathrm{M}_{1} \mathrm{~V}_{1}+\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{~V}_{2}+\mathrm{M}_{3} \mathrm{~V}_{3}=0$
$2(-18 \mathrm{i})+2(-18 \mathrm{j})+3 \mathrm{~V}_{3}=0$
$\mathrm{V}_{3}=12 \mathrm{i}+12 \mathrm{j}$
$\left|\mathrm{V}_{3}\right|=12 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
