Tags.Chapter: Centre of mass, Momentum and Collision

Question:
A paratrooper jumps from an aeroplane and opens a parachute after 2 s of free fall and starts deaccelerating with $3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$. At 10 m height from ground, while descending with the help of parachute, the speed of paratrooper is $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The initial height of the aeroplane is $\_\_\_\_$ m.
$\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right)$
Solution:
A to B
$\mathrm{x}_{1}=\frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 2^{2}=20 \mathrm{~m}$
$\mathrm{V}=0+10 \times 2$
B to C
$5^{2}=20^{2}-2(3) \mathrm{x}_{2}$
$\mathrm{x}_{2}=\frac{375}{6}$
$\mathrm{x}_{2}=62.5$
C to D
$\mathrm{x}_{3}=10 \mathrm{~m}$
$\mathrm{H}=\mathrm{x}_{1}+\mathrm{x}_{2}+\mathrm{x}_{3}=92.5$

Question:
The current passing through a conducting loop in the form of equilateral triangle of side $4 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}$ is 2A. The magnetic field at its centroid is $\alpha \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~T}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .
(Given : $\mu_{\mathrm{o}}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7}$ SI units)
Solution:
$B=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \times \frac{I}{d}\left[\sin 60^{\circ}+\sin 60^{\circ}\right] \times 3$
$\mathrm{B}=10^{-7} \times \frac{2}{2 \times 10^{-2}}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) \times 3$
$=\sqrt{3} \times 10^{-5} \times 3=3 \sqrt{3} \times 10^{-5}$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Magnetic field due to standard shaped objects

Question:
Two charges $7 \mu \mathrm{C}$ and $-2 \mu \mathrm{C}$ are placed at $(-9,0,0) \mathrm{cm}$ and $(9,0,0) \mathrm{cm}$ respectively in an external field $E=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{r}^{2}} \hat{\mathrm{r}}$, where $A=9 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C} . \mathrm{m}^{2}$.
Considering the potential at infinity is 0 , the electrostatic energy of the configuration is $\_\_\_\_$ J.
Solution:
$\mathrm{dV}=-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dr}}$
$\int_{0}^{\mathrm{v}} \mathrm{dV}=-\int_{\infty}^{\mathrm{r}} \frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{r}^{2}} \mathrm{dr}$
$\mathrm{V}=-\left[\frac{-\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{r}^{2}}\right]_{\infty}^{\mathrm{r}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{V}=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{r}}$
$\mathrm{U}=\mathrm{U}_{\text {self }}+\mathrm{U}_{\text {interaction }}$
$=\mathrm{q}_{1} \mathrm{v}_{1}=\mathrm{q}_{2} \mathrm{v}_{2}+\frac{\mathrm{kq}_{1} \mathrm{q}_{2}}{2 \mathrm{r}}$
$=7 \times 10^{-6} \frac{\mathrm{~A}}{9 \times 10^{-2}}-2 \times 10^{-6} \frac{\mathrm{~A}}{9 \times 10^{-2}}$
$-\frac{9 \times 10^{9} \times 14 \times 10^{-12}}{2 \times 9 \times 10^{-2}}$
$=\frac{5 \times 10^{-6} \times 9 \times 10^{5}}{9 \times 10^{-2}}-7 \times 10^{-1}$
$=50-0.7$
$=49.3 \mathrm{~J}$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Electric potential energy of a system of charges

Question: Two shorts dipoles $(A, B), A$ having charges $\pm 2 \mu \mathrm{C}$ and length 1 cm and $B$ having charges $\pm 4 \mu \mathrm{C}$ and length 1 cm are placed with their centres 80 cm apart as shown in the figure. The electric field at a point $P$, equi-distant from the centres of both dipoles is $\_\_\_\_$ N/C.
Solution:
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}_{2}=-\frac{\mathrm{KP}_{2}}{\mathrm{r}^{3}} ; \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}_{1}=-\frac{2 \mathrm{KP}_{1}}{\mathrm{r}^{3}}$
$\mathrm{P}_{1}=2 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-2}=2 \times 10^{-8}$
$\mathrm{P}_{2}=4 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-2}=4 \times 10^{-8}$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}_{\text {net }} \frac{2 \times 9 \times 10^{9} \times 2 \times 10^{-8}}{(0.4)^{3}} \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\frac{9 \times 10^{9} \times 4 \times 10^{-8}}{(0.4)^{3}} \hat{\mathrm{j}}$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}_{\text {net }}=\frac{9 \times 10^{9} \times 4 \times 10^{-8}}{(0.4)^{3}}[\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}]$
$\left|\vec{E}_{\text {net }}\right|=\frac{9 \times 10^{4}}{16}(\sqrt{2})$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Electric field due to a dipole

Question: A parallel plate capacitor with plate separation 5 mm is charged by a battery. On introducing a mica sheet of 2 mm and maintaining the connections of the plates with the terminals of the battery, it is found that it draws $25 \%$ more charge from the battery. The dielectric constant of mica is $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$\mathrm{C}=\frac{\in_{0} \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d}}$
$\mathrm{Q}_{1}=\mathrm{CV}$

$\mathrm{Q}_{2}=\left(\mathrm{c}_{\mathrm{eq}}\right) \mathrm{v}$
$\mathrm{Q}_{2}=1.25 \mathrm{cv}$

$c_{\text {eq }}=\frac{C_{1} C_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}=\frac{\frac{\epsilon_{0} A}{3} \times \frac{K \epsilon_{0} A}{2}}{\frac{\epsilon_{0} A}{3}+\frac{K \epsilon_{0} A}{2}}$
$c_{e q}=\frac{\left(\epsilon_{0} A\right)^{2}\left(\frac{K}{6}\right)}{\epsilon_{0} A\left(\frac{2+3 K}{6}\right)} \Rightarrow C_{e q}=\frac{K \epsilon_{0} A}{2+3 K}$
$1.25 \times \frac{\in_{0} \mathrm{~A}}{5}=\frac{\mathrm{K} \in_{0} \mathrm{~A}}{2+3 \mathrm{~K}} \Rightarrow 0.25(2+3 \mathrm{~K})=\mathrm{K}$
$$
2+3 \mathrm{~K}=4 \mathrm{~K} \Rightarrow \mathrm{~K}=2
$$

Question: A bead $P$ sliding on a frictionless semi-circular string $(A C B)$ and it is at point $S$ at $\mathrm{t}=0$ and at this instant the horizontal component of its velocity is $v$. Another bead $Q$ of the same mass as $P$ is ejected from point $A$ at $\mathrm{t}=0$ along the horizontal string $A B$, with the speed $v$, friction between the beads and the respective strings may be neglected in both cases. Let $t_{p}$ and $t_{Q}$ be the respective times taken by beads $P$ and $Q$ to reach the point $B$, then the relation between $t_{p}$ and $t_{Q}$ is
Solution:
Horizontal displacement of Q is more then P .
$$
X_{Q}>X_{P}
$$
Horizontal component of velocity is same
So $\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{p}}=\frac{\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{v}}$
$$
\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{Q}}=\frac{\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{Q}}}{\mathrm{v}}
$$
$\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{Q}}>\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{p}}$

Question: An air bubble of volume $2.9 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$ rises from the bottom of a swimming pool of 5 m deep. At the bottom of the pool water temperature is $17^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The volume of the bubble when it reaches the surface, where the water temperature is $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{cm}^{3}$. ( $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$, density of water $=10^{3} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$, and 1 atm pressure is $10^{5} \mathrm{~Pa}$ )
Solution:
For an air bubble rising in water, the no. of moles remain constant
$\frac{\mathrm{P}_{1} \mathrm{~V}_{1}}{\mathrm{~T}_{1}}=\frac{\mathrm{P}_{2} \mathrm{~V}_{2}}{\mathrm{~T}_{2}}$
$\frac{\left(\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{atm}}+\rho \mathrm{gh}\right) 2.9 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}}{290 \mathrm{~K}}=\frac{\left(\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{atm}}\right) \mathrm{V}_{2}}{300}$
$\mathrm{V}_{2}=4.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$

Question: To compare EMF of two cells using potentiometer the balancing lengths obtained are 200 cm and 150 cm . The least count of scale is 1 cm . The percentage error in the ratio of EMFs is $\_\_\_\_$
Solution:
$\in=\lambda \ell$
Potential gradient
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \epsilon_{1}=\lambda \ell_{1} \\
& \epsilon_{2}=\lambda \ell_{2} \\
& \mathrm{y}=\frac{\epsilon_{1}}{\epsilon_{2}}=\frac{\ell_{1}}{\ell_{2}} \\
& \frac{\Delta \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{\Delta \ell_{1}}{\ell_{1}}+\frac{\Delta \ell_{2}}{\ell_{2}} \\
& \begin{aligned}
\frac{\Delta \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{y}} & =\frac{1}{200}+\frac{1}{150} \\
& \frac{\Delta \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{y}} \times 100\left(\frac{1}{200}+\frac{1}{150}\right) \times 100 \\
& =\left(\frac{3+4}{600}\right) \times 100 \Rightarrow \frac{7}{6}=1.16 \%
\end{aligned}
\end{aligned}
$$
No options matching

Question: In the resonance experiment, two air columns (closed at one end) of 100 cm and 120 cm long, give 15 beats per second when each one is sounding in the respective fundamental modes. The velocity of sound in the air column is :
Solution:
Fundamental frequency in close/organ pipe
\[\begin{gathered}
  \left( f \right) = \frac{V}{{4l}} \\
  {f_1} = \frac{V}{{4{l_1}}}\& {f_2} = \frac{V}{{4{l_2}}} \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[\begin{gathered}
  Beat = \left( {{f_1} - {f_2}} \right) = \frac{V}{4}\left( {\frac{1}{{{l_1}}} - \frac{1}{{{l_2}}}} \right) \\
  15 = \frac{V}{4}\left( {\frac{1}{1} - \frac{1}{{1.2}}} \right) \\
  V = \left( {\frac{{15 \times 4 \times 1.2}}{{0.2}}} \right) = 60 \times 6 = 360\;m/s \\
\end{gathered} \]

MB = 160

U = –160 × 1/2 = – 80 J

Question:
Two blocks of masses m and M, (M > m), are placed on a frictionless table as shown in figure. A massless spring with spring constant k is attached with the lower block. If the system is slightly displaced and released then (μ = coefficient of friction between the two blocks)

(A) The time period of small oscillation of the two blocks is \[T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{\left( {m + M} \right)}}{k}} \]
(B) The acceleration of the blocks is \[{\text{a}} = \frac{{{\text{kx}}}}{{{\text{M + m}}}}\] (x = displacement of the blocks from the mean position)
(C) The magnitude of the frictional force on the upper block is \[\frac{{m\mu \left| x \right|}}{{M + m}}\]
(D) The maximum amplitude of the upper block, if it does not slip, is \[\frac{{\mu \left( {M + m} \right)g}}{k}\]
(E) Maximum frictional force can be μ (M+m)g.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
Solution:
As both blocks moving together so
Time period \[ = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{m'}{K}} ;\] where m' = M + m
\[T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{M + m}}{K}} \]
(B) Let block is displaced by x in (+ve) direction so force on block will be in(–ve) direction
F = – Kx
(M + m)a = –Kx
\[{\text{a}} = - \frac{{{\text{Kx}}}}{{\left( {{\text{M}} + {\text{m}}} \right)}}\]
(C) As upper block is moving due to friction thus \[{\text{f}} = {\text{ma}} = \frac{{{\text{mKx}}}}{{\left( {{\text{M}} + {\text{m}}} \right)}}\]
(D) This option is like two block problem in friction for maximum amplitude, force on block is also maximum, for which both blocks are moving together.

KA = (M + m)a
\[{\text{a}} = \frac{{{\text{KA}}}}{{\left( {{\text{M}} + {\text{m}}} \right)}}\]
\[{\text{f}} = {\text{ma}} = \frac{{{\text{mKA}}}}{{\left( {{\text{M}} + {\text{m}}} \right)}}\]
\[{f_{\max }} = {f_L} = \mu mg\]
f = µmg
\[\frac{{mKA}}{{\left( {M + m} \right)}} = \mu mg\]
\[A = \frac{{\mu \left( {M + m} \right)g}}{K}\]
(E) Maximum friction can be µmg as force is acting between blocks & normal force here is mg.

Question:
A particle is projected with velocity u so that its horizontal range is three times the maximum height attained by it. The horizontal range of the projectile is given as \[\frac{{n{u^2}}}{{25g}},\]where value of n is :
(Given 'g' is the acceleration due to gravity).
Solution:
Range = 3Hmax
\[\begin{gathered}
  \frac{{{u^2}\sin 2\theta }}{g} = \frac{{3{u^2}{{\sin }^2}\theta }}{{2g}} \\
  2\sin \theta \cos \theta = \frac{3}{2}{\sin ^2}\theta \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[\begin{gathered}
  \tan \theta = \frac{4}{3} \Rightarrow \theta = 53^\circ \\
  R = \frac{{{u^2}\left( {2 \times \frac{3}{5} \times \frac{4}{5}} \right)}}{g} \Rightarrow \frac{{24{u^2}}}{{25g}} \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: Three identical spheres of mass m, are placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle of length a. When released, they interact only through gravitational force and collide after a time T = 4 seconds. If the sides of the triangle are increased to length 2a and also the masses of the spheres are made 2m, then they will collide after _____
Solution:
\[{\text{T}} \propto {{\text{m}}^{\text{x}}}{{\text{G}}^{\text{y}}}{{\text{a}}^{\text{z}}}\]
\[{\text{T}} \propto {{\text{M}}^{\text{x}}}{\left[ {{{\text{M}}^{ - 1}}{{\text{L}}^3}{{\text{T}}^{ - 2}}} \right]^{\text{y}}}{\left[ {\text{L}} \right]^{\text{z}}}\]
\[{\text{T}} \propto {{\text{M}}^{{\text{x}} - {\text{y}}}}{{\text{L}}^{3{\text{y}} + {\text{z}}}}{{\text{T}}^{ - 2{\text{y}}}}\]
x – y = 0 ⇒ x = y
\[ - 2{\text{y}} = 1 \Rightarrow y = - \frac{1}{2},{\text{x = }} - \frac{1}{2}\]
⇒ 3y + z = 0
\[{\text{z}} = - 3{\text{y}} = \frac{3}{2}\]
Hence
\[{\text{T}} \propto {{\text{m}}^{ - 1/2}}{{\text{G}}^{ - 1/2}}{{\text{a}}^{3/2}}\]
\[{\text{T}} \propto {\left( {\frac{{{{\text{a}}^3}}}{{\text{m}}}} \right)^{1/2}}\]
\[{\text{T}} = 4 \times {\left( {\frac{{{{\text{2}}^3}}}{{\text{2}}}} \right)^{1/2}} = 8{\text{s}}\]

Question:
In Dumas’ method for estimation of nitrogen 0.4 g of an organic compound gave 60 mL of nitrogen collected at 300 K temperature and 715 mm Hg pressure. The percentage composition of nitrogen in the compound is
(Given : Aqueous tension at 300 K = 15 mm Hg)
Solution:
Pressure of N2 gas evolved = 715 – 15
= 700 mm Hg
\[ = \frac{{700}}{{760}}atm.\]
\[\therefore \;Mole\;of\;{N_2}evolved = \frac{{PV}}{{RT}}\]
\[ = \frac{{700 \times 60 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{760 \times 0.082 \times 300}}\]
= 0.0022 mole
∴ wt. of N2 evolved = 0.0022 × 28 = 0.063 gm
∴ wt. % of nitrogen in compound
\[ = \frac{{wt.\;of\;nitrogen}}{{wt.\;of\;compound}} \times 100 = \frac{{0.063}}{{0.4}} \times 100\]
= 15.71%

Question: Width of one of the two slits in a Young's double slit interference experiment is half of the other slit. The ratio of the maximum to the minimum intensity in the interference pattern is :
Solution:
\[I \propto width\;\;{I_{\max }} = {\left( {\sqrt {{I_1}} + \sqrt {{I_2}} } \right)^2}\]
\[\therefore \;{I_1} = {I_0},\;{I_2} = 2{I_0}\;\;\;\;{I_{\min }} = {\left( {\sqrt {{I_1}} - \sqrt {{I_2}} } \right)^2}\]
\[\frac{{{I_{\max }}}}{{{I_{\min }}}} = \frac{{{{\left( {\sqrt 2 + 1} \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {\sqrt 2 - 1} \right)}^2}}} \Rightarrow \;\frac{{3 + 2\sqrt 2 }}{{3 - 2\sqrt 2 }}\]

Question:
Given :
\[\Delta {\text{H}}_{{\text{sub}}}^\Theta \left[ {{\text{C}}\left( {{\text{graphite}}} \right)} \right] = 710{\text{kJmo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}\]
\[{\Delta _{{\text{C}} - {\text{H}}}}{\text{H}}_{}^\Theta = 414{\text{kJ mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}\]
\[{\Delta _{{\text{H}} - {\text{H}}}}{\text{H}}_{}^\Theta = 436{\text{kJ mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}\]
\[{\Delta _{{\text{C}} = {\text{C}}}}{\text{H}}_{}^\Theta = 611\,{\text{kJ mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - {\text{1}}}}\]
The \[\Delta {\text{H}}_{\text{f}}^\Theta \,{\text{forC}}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{ = C}}{{\text{H}}_{{\text{2 }}}}\]is _________ kJ mol–1 (nearest integer value)
Solution:
\[{\left[ {\Delta {\text{H}}_{\text{f}}^0} \right]_{{{\text{C}}_2}{{\text{H}}_{4\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}}} = 2 \times {\left[ {\Delta H_{{\text{sub}}}^o} \right]_{{\text{C}}\left( {\text{s}} \right)}} + 2 \times \Delta {\text{H}}_{{\text{H - H}}}^o - 1 \times \Delta {\text{H}}_{C = C}^o - 4 \times \Delta {\text{H}}_{C - H}^o\]
\[ \Rightarrow \] \[{\left[ {\Delta {\text{H}}_{\text{f}}^0} \right]_{{{\text{C}}_2}{{\text{H}}_{4\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}}} = \left( {2 \times 710} \right) + \left( {2 \times 436} \right) - 611 - 4 \times 414\]
\[ \Rightarrow \] \[{\left[ {\Delta {\text{H}}_{\text{f}}^0} \right]_{{{\text{C}}_2}{{\text{H}}_{4\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}}} = 25{\text{kJ /mol}}\]

Question: A piston of mass M is hung from a massless spring whose restoring force law goes as F = –kx3, where k is the spring constant of appropriate dimension. The piston separates the vertical chamber into two parts, where the bottom part is filled with 'n' moles of an ideal gas. An external work is done on the gas isothermally (at a constant temperature T) with the help of a heating filament (with negligible volume) mounted in lower part of the chamber, so that the piston goes up from a height L0 to L1, the total energy delivered by the filament is (Assume spring to be in its natural length before heating)
Solution:
Using WET Total energy supplied = gravitational potential energy + spring potential energy + work done by gas
\[{\text{Mg}}\left( {{{\text{L}}_1} - {{\text{L}}_0}} \right) + \int_{{{\text{L}}_0}}^{{{\text{L}}_1}} {{\text{k}}{{\text{x}}^3}{\text{dx}}} + {\text{nRT}}\ell {\text{n}}\]
\[\left[ {\frac{{{{\text{L}}_1}{\text{A}}}}{{{{\text{L}}_0}{\text{A}}}}} \right] + {{\text{W}}_{{\text{ext}}}} = 0\]
\[\frac{{\text{K}}}{4}\left[ {{{\text{X}}^4}} \right]_{{{\text{L}}_0}}^{{{\text{L}}_1}} + {\text{Mg}}\left( {{{\text{L}}_1} - {{\text{L}}_0}} \right) + \int_{{{\text{L}}_0}}^{{{\text{L}}_1}} {{\text{k}}{{\text{x}}^3}{\text{dx}}} + {\text{nRT}}\ell {\text{n}}\]
\[\left[ {\frac{{{{\text{L}}_1}}}{{{{\text{L}}_0}}}} \right] + {{\text{W}}_{{\text{ext}}}} = 0\]
\[\frac{{\text{k}}}{4}\left( {{\text{L}}_1^4 - {\text{L}}_0^4} \right) + {\text{Mg }}\left( {{{\text{L}}_1} - {{\text{L}}_0}} \right) + {\text{nRT}}\ell {\text{n}}\]
\[\left[ {\frac{{{{\text{L}}_1}}}{{{{\text{L}}_0}}}} \right] + {{\text{W}}_{{\text{ext}}}} = 0\]
\[{{\text{W}}_{{\text{ext}}}} = \frac{{\text{k}}}{4}\left( {{\text{L}}_1^4 - {\text{L}}_0^4} \right) + {\text{Mg}}\left( {{{\text{L}}_1} - {{\text{L}}_0}} \right) + {\text{nRT}}\ell {\text{n}}\left[ {\frac{{{{\text{L}}_1}}}{{{{\text{L}}_0}}}} \right]\]

Question: Using a battery, a 100 pF capacitor is charged to 60 V and then the battery is removed. After that, a second uncharged capacitor is connected to the first capacitor in parallel. If the final voltage across the second capacitor is 20 V, its capacitance is : (in pF)
Solution:
\[New\;potential = \frac{{{C_0}{V_0}}}{{{C_0} + C}} = \frac{{{V_0}}}{3}\]
\[\begin{gathered}
  3{C_0}{V_0} = {C_0}{V_0} + C{V_0} \\
  2{C_0}{V_0} = C{V_0} \\
  C \Rightarrow 2{C_0} \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: Consider two blocks A and B of masses m1 = 10 kg and m2 = 5 kg that are placed on a frictionless table. The block A moves with a constant speed v = 3 m/s towards the block B kept at rest. A spring with spring constant k = 3000 N/m is attached with the block B as shown in the figure. After the collision, suppose that the blocks A and B, along with the spring in constant compression state, move together, then the compression in the spring is, (Neglect the mass of the spring)
Solution:
m1v1 + m2v2 = (m1 + m2)vcm
\[\begin{gathered}
  {v_{cm}} \Rightarrow \frac{{10 \times 3}}{{10 + 5}} \Rightarrow \frac{{30}}{{15}} = 2\;m/s \\
  \frac{1}{2}k{x^2} = \frac{1}{2}\left( {10} \right){\left( 3 \right)^2} - \left[ {\frac{1}{2}\left( {15} \right){{\left( 2 \right)}^2}} \right] \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow \;90 - 60 = 30 = 3000\;{x^2} \\
  {x^2} \Rightarrow \frac{{30}}{{3000}} = \frac{1}{{100}} \\
  x \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{10}}m. \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: 10 mL of 2 M NaOH solution is added to 20 mL of 1 M HCl solution kept in a beaker. Now, 10 mL of this mixture is poured into a volumetric flask of 100 mL containing 2 moles of HCl and made the volume upto the mark with distilled water. The solution in this flask is :

NaOH + HCl → NaCl + H2O

= 0.2 mole, = 0.2 mole
Final: 0, 0
∴ Resulting solution becomes neutral.
Now when 10 ml of above solution is poured into a flask containing 2 mole HCl and made solution 100 ml will distilled water.
\[Molarity\;of\;HCl = \frac{2}{{100}} \times 1000 = 20\]

Question: 40 mL of a mixture of CH3COOH and HCl (aqueous solution) is titrated against 0.1 M NaOH solution conductometrically. Which of the following statement is correct?

The concentration of HCl in the original mixture is 0.005 M
CH<sub>3</sub>COOH is neutralised first followed by neutralisation of HCl
Point 'C' indicates the complete neutralisation HCl
Solution:
From the given graph 2 ml NaOH solution is used for neutralisation of HCl and 3 ml NaOH solution is used for neutralisation of CH3COOH.

M × 40 = 0.1 × 2
M = 0.005
∴ Mole of CH3COOH = Mole of NaOaH used
M × 40 = 0.1 × 3
M = 0.0075
HCl is strong acid and will be neutralised first.

Question: The radii of curvature for a thin convex lens are 10 cm and 15 cm respectively. The focal length of the lens is 12 cm. The refractive index of the lens material is
The radiation pressure exerted by a 450 W light source on a perfectly reflecting surface placed at 2m away from it, is :
Solution:
\[{\operatorname{P} _{{\text{rad}}}} = \frac{{2{\text{I}}}}{{\text{C}}}\]
Where I = intensity at surface
C = Speed of light
\[I = \frac{{Power}}{{Area}} = \frac{{450}}{{4\pi {r^2}}}\]
\[ = \frac{{450}}{{4\pi \times 4}} = \frac{{450}}{{16\pi }}\]
\[{\operatorname{P} _{{\text{rad}}}} = \frac{{2 \times 450}}{{16\pi \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}} = \frac{{150}}{{8\pi \times {{10}^8}}}\]
= 5.97 × 10–8 ≈ 6 × 10–8 Pascals

Question:
Consider the following reactions
A + NaCl + H2SO4 → CrO2Cl2 + Side Products
Little amount
CrO2 Cl2(Vapour) + NaOH → B + NaCl + H2O
B + H+ → C + H2O
The number of terminal 'O' present in the compound 'C' is _________.

\[{\text{C}}{{\text{r}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}_7^{2 - } + {\text{NaCl + }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_4} \to {\text{Cr}}{{\text{O}}_2}{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_2}\]
\[{\text{Cr}}{{\text{O}}_2}{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_2}\left( {Vapour} \right){\text{ }} + {\text{ NaOH }} \to {\text{N}}{{\text{a}}_2}{\text{Cr}}{{\text{O}}_4} + {\text{NaCl}} + {{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}\]
\[{\text{N}}{{\text{a}}_2}{\text{Cr}}{{\text{O}}_4} + {{\text{H}}^ \oplus } \to \mathop {{\text{N}}{{\text{a}}_2}{\text{C}}{{\text{r}}_2}{{\text{O}}_7}}\limits_{\left( {\text{C}} \right)} + {{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}\]

Question: In the figure shown below, a resistance of 150.4 Ω is connected in series to an ammeter A of resistance 240 Ω . A shunt resistance of 10 Ω is connected in parallel with the ammeter. The reading of the ammeter is ______ mA.

A parallel plate capacitor is filled equally (half) with two dielectrics of dielectric constant ε 1 and ε 2, as shown in figures. The distance between the plates is d and area of each plate is A. If capacitance in first configuration and second configuration are C1 and C2 respectively, then \[\frac{{{{\text{C}}_1}}}{{{{\text{C}}_2}}}\]is :
Solution:
Area of plate is A. then \[C = \frac{{{\varepsilon _2}{\varepsilon _0}A}}{{d/2}} = \frac{{2{\varepsilon _2}{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d}\]
\[C' = \frac{{{\varepsilon _1}{\varepsilon _0}A}}{{d/2}} = \frac{{2{\varepsilon _1}{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d}\]
Let \[{C_0} = \frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d}\]
\[{\text{C = 2}}{\varepsilon _{\text{2}}}{{\text{C}}_0}\]
\[{\text{C' = 2}}{\varepsilon _{\text{1}}}{{\text{C}}_0}\]
C & C' are in series
\[{C_1} = \frac{{CC'}}{{C + C'}} = \frac{{4{\varepsilon _2}{\varepsilon _1}C_0^2}}{{2{C_0}\left( {{\varepsilon _2} + {\varepsilon _1}} \right)}}\]
\[ = \frac{{2{\varepsilon _2}{\varepsilon _1}{C_0}}}{{\left( {{\varepsilon _2} + {\varepsilon _1}} \right)}}\]

Here \[C = \frac{{{\varepsilon _1}{\varepsilon _0}A}}{{2d}} = \frac{{{\varepsilon _1}{C_0}}}{2}\]
\[C' = \frac{{{\varepsilon _2}{C_0}}}{2}\]
C & C' are in parallel
\[{C_2} = C' + C = \left( {{\varepsilon _1} + {\varepsilon _2}} \right)\frac{{{{\text{C}}_0}}}{2}\]
Thus, \[\frac{{{C_1}}}{{{C_2}}} = \frac{{2{\varepsilon _2}{\varepsilon _1}{C_0}}}{{\left( {{\varepsilon _0} + {\varepsilon _1}} \right)}} \times \frac{2}{{\left( {{\varepsilon _1} + {\varepsilon _2}} \right){C_0}}}\]
\[ = \frac{{4{\varepsilon _2}{\varepsilon _1}}}{{{{\left( {{\varepsilon _2} + {\varepsilon _1}} \right)}^2}}}\]

Question: The electrostatic potential on the surface of uniformly charged spherical shell of radius R = 10 cm is 120 V. The potential at the centre of shell, at a distance r = 5 cm from centre, and at a distance r = 15 cm from the centre of the shell respectively, are :
Solution:
Potential inside shell is equal to potential on surface
\[{{\text{V}}_{{\text{in}}}} = {{\text{V}}_{{\text{surface}}}} = \frac{{{\text{kQ}}}}{{\text{R}}} = 120{\text{V}}\]
at r = 15 cm
\[{\text{V}} = \frac{{{\text{kQ}}}}{{\text{r}}} = \frac{{120 \times 10}}{{15}} = 80{\text{V}}\]

Question:
A loop ABCDA, carrying current I = 12 A, is placed in a plane, consists of two semi-circular segments of radius R1 = 6π m and R2 = 4 π m. The magnitude of the resultant magnetic field at center O is k × 10–7 T. The value of k is _______ .
(Given μ 0 = 4π × 10–7 Tm A–1)
Solution:
Magnetic field due to AB & CD = 0
\[{{\text{B}}_{\text{0}}} = \left| {{{\text{B}}_{{{\text{R}}_{\text{1}}}}} - {{\text{B}}_{{{\text{R}}_{\text{2}}}}}} \right|\]
\[ = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{4{R_2}}} - \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{4{R_1}}}\]
\[ = \frac{{4\pi \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times 12}}{4}\left( {\frac{1}{{4\pi }} - \frac{1}{{6\pi }}} \right)\]
\[ = 12\pi \times {10^{ - 7}}\left( {\frac{1}{{12\pi }}} \right) = 1 \times {10^{ - 7}}\]
K = 1

Question: The sequence from the following that would result in giving predominantly 3, 4, 5 –Tribromoaniline is :

Consider a completely full cylindrical water tank of height 1.6 m and cross-sectional area 0.5 m2. It has a small hole in its side at a height 90 cm from the bottom. Assume, the cross-sectional area of the hole to be negligibly small as compared to that of the water tank. If a load 50 kg is applied at the top surface of the water in the tank then the velocity of the water coming out at the instant when the hole is opened is : (g = 10 m/s2)
Solution:
Apply Bernouli equation between points 1 & 2
\[{P_1} + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 + \rho gh{\text{ }} = {\text{ }}{P_2} + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2 + 0\]
\[{P_0} + \frac{{mg}}{A} + \rho g{\text{ }}\frac{{70}}{{100}} = {P_0} + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2\]
\[\frac{{5000}}{{0.5}} + {10^3} \times 10\frac{{70}}{{100}} = \frac{1}{2} \times {10^3}v_2^2\]
\[{10^3} + {10^3} \times 7 = \frac{{{{10}^3}}}{2}v_2^2\]
\[{\text{v}}_2^2 = 16\]
\[{{\text{v}}_2} = 4{\text{m/s}}\]
As the tank area is large v1 is negligible compared to v2

Question: An ideal gas exists in a state with pressure P<sub>0</sub>, volume V<sub>0</sub>. It is isothermally expanded to 4 times of its initial volume (V<sub>0</sub>), then isobarically compressed to its original volume. \[\begin{gathered}
  {\omega _1} = {P_0}{v_0}ln4 \\
  {\omega _2} = \frac{{{P_0}}}{4}\left( { - 3{v_0}} \right) = - \frac{{3{P_0}{v_0}}}{4} \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[\begin{gathered}
  {\omega _3} = 0 \\
  {Q_T} = \Delta {U_{cyclic}} + \omega \\
  {Q_T} = \omega \;\;\left( {\Delta {U_{cyclic}} = 0} \right) \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[\begin{gathered}
  {Q_T} = {P_0}{v_0}\left( {ln4 - \frac{3}{4}} \right) \\
   = {P_0}{v_0}\left( {2ln2 - 0.75} \right) \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: Let $I(x)=\int \frac{3 d x}{(4 x+6)\left(\sqrt{4 x^{2}+8 x+3}\right)}$ and $\mathrm{I}(0)=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}+20$. If $\mathrm{I}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{a} \sqrt{2}}{\mathrm{~b}}+\mathrm{c}$, where $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c} \in \mathrm{N}, \operatorname{gcd}(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b})=1$, then $\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}$ is equal to :
Solution:
Let $4 \mathrm{x}+6=\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}}-6}{4}$
$4 \mathrm{dx}=-\frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{t}^{2}} \quad\left\{\frac{\mathrm{x}+1=\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}}-2}{4}\right.$
$\int \frac{3 d x}{(4 x+6) \sqrt{4(x+1)^{2}-1}}$
$\int \frac{3(-\mathrm{dt})}{4 \mathrm{t}^{2} \times \frac{1}{\mathrm{t}} \sqrt{4\left(\frac{\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}}-2}{4}\right)^{2}-1}}$
$-\frac{3}{4} \int \frac{d t}{t \sqrt{\frac{(1-2 t)^{2}}{4 t^{2}}-1}}$
$-\frac{3}{4} \int \frac{\mathrm{dt}(2 \mathrm{t})}{\mathrm{t} \sqrt{1-4 \mathrm{t}}}$
$-\frac{3}{2} \int \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\sqrt{1-4 \mathrm{t}}}=-\frac{3}{2}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-4 \mathrm{t}}}{\frac{1}{2} \times-4}\right)+\mathrm{c}$
$=\frac{3}{4} \sqrt{1-4 \mathrm{t}}+\mathrm{c} \quad \because \mathrm{t}=\frac{1}{4 \mathrm{x}+6}$
$=\frac{3}{4} \sqrt{1-4\left(\frac{1}{4 x+6}\right)}+c$
$=\frac{3}{4} \sqrt{\frac{4 \mathrm{x}+6-4}{4 \mathrm{x}+6}}+\mathrm{c}$
$\mathrm{I}(\mathrm{x})=\frac{3}{4} \sqrt{\frac{4 \mathrm{x}+2}{4 \mathrm{x}+6}}+\mathrm{c}$
$\mathrm{I}(0)=\frac{3}{4} \sqrt{\frac{2}{6}}+\mathrm{c}$
$\mathrm{I}(0)=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}+\mathrm{c} \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=20$
Hence $I(x)=\frac{3}{4} \sqrt{\frac{4 x+2}{4 x+6}}+20$
$\mathrm{I}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\frac{3}{4} \sqrt{\frac{4}{8}}+20$
$=\frac{3}{4 \sqrt{2}}+20$
$=\frac{3 \sqrt{2}}{8}+20$
$\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}=3+8+20=31$

Question: The area of the region enclosed between the circles $x^{2}+y^{2}=4$ and $x^{2}+(y-2)^{2}=4$ is :
Solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& A=2 \int_{0}^{\sqrt{3}}\left[\sqrt{4-x^{2}}-\left(2-\sqrt{4-x^{2}}\right)\right] d x \\
& =2 \int_{0}^{\sqrt{3}}\left(2 \sqrt{4-x^{2}}-2\right) d x \\
& =4 \int_{0}^{\sqrt{3}}\left(\sqrt{4-x^{2}}-1\right) d x \\
& =\left[4\left[\frac{1}{2}\left(x \sqrt{4-x^{2}}+4 \sin ^{-1} \frac{x}{2}\right)-x\right]\right]_{0}^{\sqrt{3}} \\
& =4\left[\frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{3}+4 \times \frac{\pi}{3}\right)-\sqrt{3}\right]=4\left[\frac{2 \pi}{3}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right] \\
& =\frac{8 \pi}{3}-2 \sqrt{3} \text { (Sq. units) }
\end{aligned}
$$

$$

$$
$\therefore \mathrm{p}+\mathrm{q}=23$

Question: Let $\frac{\pi}{2}<\theta<\pi$ and $\cot \theta=-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}$. Then the value of $\sin \left(\frac{15 \theta}{2}\right)(\cos 8 \theta+\sin 8 \theta)+\cos \left(\frac{15 \theta}{2}\right)(\cos 8 \theta-\sin 8 \theta)$ is equal to :
Solution:
$\frac{\pi}{2}<\theta<\pi$ and $\cot \theta=-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \sin \left(\frac{15 \theta}{2}\right)(\cos 8 \theta+\sin 8 \theta)$
$+\cos \left(\frac{15 \theta}{2}\right)(\cos 8 \theta-\sin 8 \theta)$
$\Rightarrow \sin \left(\frac{15 \theta}{2}\right) \cos 8 \theta-\cos \left(\frac{15 \theta}{2}\right) \sin 8 \theta$
$+\sin \frac{15 \theta}{2} \sin 8 \theta+\cos \frac{15 \theta}{2} \cos 8 \theta$
$\Rightarrow \sin \left(\frac{15 \theta}{2}-8 \theta\right)+\cos \left(\frac{15 \theta}{2}-8 \theta\right)$
$\Rightarrow \cos \frac{\theta}{2}-\sin \frac{\theta}{2}=-\sqrt{1-\sin \theta} \quad\left(\frac{\pi}{4}<\frac{\theta}{2}<\frac{\pi}{2}\right)$
$ \Rightarrow $given $\cot \theta=-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}, \sin \theta=\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{3}$
$\Rightarrow-\sqrt{1-\sin \theta}=\sqrt{\frac{3-2 \sqrt{2}}{3}}=-\frac{(\sqrt{2}-1)}{3}$
$=\frac{1-\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}}$

Class4-88-1212-1616-20
Frequency3$\lambda $47

are $\mu$ and 19 respectively, then the value of $\lambda+\mu$ is

$=\frac{200+10 \lambda}{\lambda+14}=10+\left(\frac{60}{\lambda+14}\right)$

Question:
If $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl}\frac{a|x|+x^{2}-2(\sin |x|)(\cos |x|)}{x} & , x \neq 0 \\ b & , x=0\end{array}\right.$
is continuous at $\mathrm{x}=0$, then $\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}$ is equal to :
Solution:
$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}\frac{a|x|+x^{2}-2 \sin |x| \cos |x|}{x} & ; x \neq 0 \\ b & ; x=0\end{array}\right.$
for continuity
$\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x)=f(0)$
$\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{\mathrm{ah}+\mathrm{h}^{2}-2(\sinh ) \cosh }{-\mathrm{h}}$
$=\lim _{\mathrm{x} \rightarrow 0^{+}} \frac{\mathrm{ah}+\mathrm{h}^{2}-2(\sinh ) \cosh }{\mathrm{h}}$
or $-\mathrm{a}+2=\mathrm{a}-2=\mathrm{b}$
$2 \mathrm{a}=4$
$\mathrm{a}=2, \mathrm{~b}=0$
$\therefore \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}=2$

Question: If $\mathrm{z}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{\mathrm{i}}{2}, \mathrm{i}=\sqrt{-1}$, then $\left(\mathrm{z}^{201}-\mathrm{i}\right)^{8}$ is equal to
Solution:
$z=\cos \frac{\pi}{6}+i \sin \frac{\pi}{6}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& z^{201}=\cos \left(201 \frac{\pi}{6}\right)+i \sin \left(201 \frac{\pi}{6}\right)=-i \\
& \left(z^{201}-i\right)^{8}=(-2 i)^{8}=256
\end{aligned}
$$

Question:

Question:
Consider two sets $\mathrm{A}=\{\mathrm{x} \in \mathrm{z}:|(|\mathrm{x}-3|-3)| \leq 1\}$ and $B=\left\{x \in \mathbb{R}-\{1,2\}: \frac{(x-2)(x-4)}{x-1} \log _{e}(|x-2|)=0\right\}$.
Then the number of onto functions $f: \mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}$ is equal to :
Solution:
A : $||x-3|-3| \leq 1$
$\Rightarrow-1 \leq|\mathrm{x}-3|-3 \leq 1$
$2 \leq|x-3| \leq 4$
$2 \leq(\mathrm{x}-3) \leq 4$ or $-4 \leq(\mathrm{x}-3) \leq-2$
$5 \leq \mathrm{x} \leq 7 \quad$ or $-1 \leq \mathrm{x} \leq 1$
$\mathrm{A}=\{-1,0,1,5,6,7\}$
$\mathrm{B} \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=4,|\mathrm{x}-2|=1 \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=3$ or 1 (reject) +
$\mathrm{B}=\{3,4\}$
Number of onto functions from A to $\mathrm{B}=2^{6}-2=62$

Question: If the solution curve $y=f(x)$ of the differential equation $\left(x^{2}-4\right) y^{\prime}-2 x y+2 x\left(4-x^{2}\right)^{2}=0, x>2$, passes through the point $(3,15)$, then the local maximum value of $f$ is
Solution:
$\left(x^{2}-4\right) y^{1}-2 x y=-2 x\left(x^{2}-4\right)^{2}$
$\mathrm{d}\left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}-4}\right)=-2 \mathrm{x}$
$\mathrm{y}=\left(-\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{C}\right)\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}-4\right)$
for $x=3 y=15 \Rightarrow C=12$
$\mathrm{y}=\left(-\mathrm{x}^{2}+12\right)\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}-4\right)$
$y^{1}=0 \Rightarrow x=2 \sqrt{2}$
$y_{\text {local maxi . }}=\left((2 \sqrt{2})^{2}-4\right)\left(-(2 \sqrt{2})^{2}+12\right)$
$=16$

Question: Let $\mathrm{A}(1,2)$ and $\mathrm{C}(-3,-6)$ be two diagonally opposite vertices of a rhombus, whose sides AD and BC are parallel to the line $7 \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}=14$. If $\mathrm{B}(\alpha, \beta)$ and $\mathrm{D}(\gamma, \delta)$ are the other two vertices, then $|\alpha+\beta+\gamma+\delta|$ is equal to :
Solution:
Given the points of B and D are $(\alpha, \beta)$ and $(\gamma, \delta)$ and mid point of A and C is $(-1,-2)$

So $\frac{\alpha+\gamma}{2}=-1$ and $\frac{\beta+\delta}{2}=-2$
$|\alpha+\gamma+\beta+\delta|=6$

Question: Let $\sum_{\mathrm{k}=1}^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{k}}=\alpha \mathrm{n}^{2}+\beta \mathrm{n}$. If $\mathrm{a}_{10}=59$ and $\mathrm{a}_{6}=7 \mathrm{a}_{1}$ then $\alpha+\beta$ is equal to
Solution:
$\quad a_{n}=S_{n}-S_{n-1}$
$=\left(\alpha \mathrm{n}^{2}+\beta \mathrm{n}\right)-\left(\alpha(\mathrm{n}-1)^{2}+\beta(\mathrm{n}-1)\right)$
(a) $\mathrm{a}_{59} \Rightarrow 19 \alpha+\beta=59$
(b) $a_{6}=7 a_{1} \Rightarrow 11 \alpha+\beta=7(\alpha+\beta)$
$\Rightarrow 2 \alpha=3 \beta$
$\mathrm{a}=3, \beta=2$
$\alpha+\beta=5$

Question: Let $\vec{a}=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}, \vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}, \vec{c}=\lambda \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}$ and $\vec{v}=\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$. If $\vec{v} \cdot \vec{c}=11$ and the length of the projection of $\vec{b}$ on $\vec{c}$ is $p$, then $9 p^{2}$ is equal to :
Solution:
$\vec{a}=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}, \vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}, \vec{c}=\lambda \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}$, and $\vec{v}=\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$. If $\vec{v} \cdot \vec{c}=11$
$\vec{v}=(\vec{a} \times \vec{b})=(-\hat{i}+7 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k})$
$\vec{v} \cdot \vec{c}=11=(-\hat{i}+7 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}) \cdot(\lambda \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})=11$
$\Rightarrow-\lambda+7+5=11$
$\Rightarrow \lambda=1$
Length of projection of $\vec{b}$ on $\vec{c}=\vec{b} \cdot \hat{c}$
$\Rightarrow\left|(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}) \cdot \frac{(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})}{\sqrt{3}}\right|=\frac{2+1-1}{\sqrt{3}}=P=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}$
$\Rightarrow 9 \mathrm{p}^{2}=9\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)=12$

Question:
The number of elements in the
set $S=\left\{x: x \in[0,100]\right.$ and $\left.\int_{0}^{x} t^{2} \sin (x-t) d t=x^{2}\right\}$ is.....
Solution:
$\quad \int_{0}^{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{t}^{2} \sin (\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{t}) \mathrm{dt}=\mathrm{x}^{2}$
Use by parts
$-\mathrm{x}^{2} \operatorname{cox}+\mathrm{x}^{2}+2 \mathrm{x}^{2} \cos \mathrm{x}-2 \mathrm{x} \sin \mathrm{x}$
$-\mathrm{x}^{2} \operatorname{cox}+2 \mathrm{x} \sin \mathrm{x}+2 \cos \mathrm{x}-2=\mathrm{x}$
$\cos \mathrm{x}=1$
$\mathrm{x}=0,2 \pi, 4 \pi, \ldots \ldots, 30 \pi$
Total Elements $=16$

Question:
The system of linear equations
$x+y+z=6$
$2 \mathrm{x}+5 \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{az}=36$
$x+2 y+3 z=b$
has
Solution:
If $\mathrm{D}=\left|\begin{array}{lll}1 & 1 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 & \mathrm{a} \\ 1 & 2 & 3\end{array}\right|=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=8$
If $\quad \mathrm{D}_{1}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}6 & 1 & 1 \\ 36 & 5 & \mathrm{a} \\ \mathrm{b} & 2 & 3\end{array}\right|=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{ab}-5 \mathrm{~b}-12 \mathrm{a}+54=0$
If $D_{2}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 6 & 1 \\ 2 & 36 & a \\ 1 & b & 3\end{array}\right|=0 \Rightarrow a b-6 a-2 b-36=0$
If $\mathrm{D}_{3}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 1 & 6 \\ 2 & 5 & 36 \\ 1 & 2 & \mathrm{~b}\end{array}\right|=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{~b}=14$
For $\mathrm{a}=8 \& \mathrm{~b}=14 \Rightarrow \mathrm{D}_{1} \& \mathrm{D}_{2}$ are also zero
For $\mathrm{a}=8 \& \mathrm{~b}=14 \Rightarrow \mathrm{D}=\mathrm{D}_{1}=\mathrm{D}_{2}=\mathrm{D}_{3}=0$
$ \Rightarrow $ infinitely many solutions.

Solution:

and $\frac{\mathrm{a}+4 \lambda-\mathrm{a}}{2}=2 \mu+2 \mathrm{~b}$
and $\frac{2 \lambda-2 \mathrm{a}-2+2}{2}=\mu+\mathrm{a}$

$\lambda-\mu=2 \mathrm{a}$

Question: Let $\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}$ be three vectors such that $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=2(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}})$.If $|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}|=1,|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{~b}}|=4,|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}|=2$, and the angle between $\vec{b}$ and $\vec{c}$ is $60^{\circ}$, then $|\vec{a} . \vec{c}|$ is :
Solution:
$\vec{a} \times \vec{b}-2(\vec{a} \times \vec{c})=0$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}-2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}})=0 \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{~b}}-2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=\lambda \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \tag{1}
\end{equation*}
$$
$|\lambda \mathrm{a}|^{2}=|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}-2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}|^{2} \Rightarrow \lambda^{2}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}|^{2}=\mathrm{b}^{2}+4 \mathrm{c}^{2}-4 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{~b}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}$
$\lambda^{2}=16+16-4.4 .2 \cdot \frac{1}{2}$
$\lambda^{2}=16$
$\lambda= \pm 4$
$\because \vec{b}-2 \vec{c}= \pm 4 \vec{a}$
Dot with $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}-2|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}|^{2}= \pm 4(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}})$
4.2. $\frac{1}{2}-2.4= \pm 4(\vec{a} . \vec{c})$
$|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} . \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}|=1$

Question: Let PQ be a chord of the hyperbola $\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{4}-\frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}}{\mathrm{~b}^{2}}=1$, perpendicular to the x -axis such that OPQ is an equilateral triangle, $O$ being the centre of the hyperbola. If the eccentricity of the hyperbola is $\sqrt{3}$, then the area of the triangle OPQ is :
Solution:
$\mathrm{e}=\sqrt{1+\frac{\mathrm{b}}{4}}=\sqrt{3} \quad \Rightarrow \mathrm{~b}=8$
$\therefore $ Hyperbola $\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{4}-\frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}}{8}=1$

$\frac{\mathrm{PM}}{\mathrm{OM}}=\tan 30^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{2 \sqrt{2} \tan \theta}{2 \sec \theta}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow \sin \theta=\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}$
Area $=2 \times \frac{1}{2} \times \mathrm{OM} \times \mathrm{MP}$
$=2 \sec \theta \times 2 \sqrt{2} \tan \theta$
$=4 \sqrt{2} \frac{\sin \theta}{\cos ^{2} \theta}=4 \sqrt{2} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{6} \times\left(1-\frac{1}{6}\right)}=\frac{8 \sqrt{3}}{5}$

Question: If the points of intersection of the ellipses $x^{2}+2 y^{2}-6 x-12 y+23=0$ and $4 x+2 y^{2}-20 x-12 y+35=0$ lie on a circle of radius r and centre $(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b})$, then the value of $\mathrm{ab}+18 \mathrm{r}^{2}$ is
Solution:
By family of curve equation of circle will be
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{S}_{1}+\lambda \mathrm{S}_{2}=0$
$\Rightarrow\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}+2 \mathrm{y}^{2}-6 \mathrm{x}-12 \mathrm{y}+23\right)$
$+\lambda\left(4 \mathrm{x}^{2}+2 \mathrm{y}^{2}-20 \mathrm{x}-12 \mathrm{y}+35\right)=0$
$ \Rightarrow $ for circle coeff of $x^{2}=$ coeff. of $y^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{1}{2}$
So equation of circle is
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}-\frac{16}{3} \mathrm{x}-6 \mathrm{y}+\frac{27}{2}=0$
Centre $\left(\frac{8}{3}, 3\right):$ Radius $\mathrm{r}=\sqrt{\frac{47}{18}}=\mathrm{r}$
$\therefore \mathrm{ab}+18 \mathrm{r}^{2}=8+47=55$

Question: The sum of all the real solutions of the equation $\log _{(x+3)}\left(6 x^{2}+28 x+30\right)=5-2 \log _{(6 x+10)}\left(x^{2}+6 x+9\right)$ is equal to :
Solution:
$\log _{x+3}[(x+3)(6 x+10)]=5-2 \log _{6 x+10}(x+3)^{2}$
$1+\log _{\mathrm{x}+3}(6 \mathrm{x}+10)=5-4 \log _{6 \mathrm{x}+10}(\mathrm{x}+3)$
Let $\log _{(x+3)}(6 \mathrm{x}+10)=\mathrm{A}$
$\Rightarrow A+\frac{4}{A}=4$ or $A=2$
$\Rightarrow \log _{(\mathrm{x}+3)}(6 \mathrm{x}+10)=2$
$\Rightarrow 6 \mathrm{x}+10=(\mathrm{x}+3)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow 6 \mathrm{x}+10=\mathrm{x}^{2}+9+6 \mathrm{x}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}^{2}=1, \mathrm{x}= \pm 1$
So sum of roots $=0$

Question:

A. The complex exhibits geometrical isomerism.
B. The complex is white in colour.
C. The calculated spin-only magnetic moment of the complex is 2.84 BM .
D. The calculated CFSE (Crystal Field Stabilization Energy) of Ni in this complex is $-0.8 \Delta_{0}$.
E. The geometrical arrangement of ligands in this complex is similar to that in $\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_{4}$.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
Solution:
$\left[\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{PPh}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]$
Given : Paramagnetic complex hence it must be tetrahedral so

Question: The energy of first (lowest) Balmer line of H atom is x J . The energy (in J) of second Balmer line of H atom is :
Solution:
Transition of first Balmer line
$\mathrm{n}_{1}=2 ; \mathrm{n}_{2}=3$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\Delta \mathrm{E}=\mathrm{x}=13.6(1)^{2}\left[\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right] \tag{i}
\end{equation*}
$$
Transition of $2{ }^{\text {nd }}$ Balmer line
$\mathrm{n}_{1}=2 ; \mathrm{n}_{2}=4$

$$
\begin{equation*}
\Delta \mathrm{E}=13.6(1)^{2}\left[\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{4^{2}}\right] \tag{ii}
\end{equation*}
$$

Divide Eq. (ii) by eq. (i)
$\frac{\Delta \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{x}}=\frac{\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{16}}{\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{9}}$
$\frac{\Delta \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{x}}=\frac{\frac{3}{16}}{\frac{5}{36}}$
$\frac{\Delta \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{x}}=\frac{27}{20}$
$\Delta \mathrm{E}=1.35 \mathrm{x}$.

Question: Consider $\mathrm{A} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{k}_{1}} \mathrm{~B}$ and $\mathrm{C} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{k}_{2}} \mathrm{D}$ are two reactions. If the rate constant $\left(k_{1}\right)$ of the $A \rightarrow B$ reaction can be expressed by the following equation $\log _{10} \mathrm{k}=14.34-\frac{1.5 \times 10^{4}}{\mathrm{~T} / \mathrm{K}}$ and activation energy of $\mathrm{C} \rightarrow \mathrm{D}$ reaction $\left(\mathrm{Ea}_{2}\right)$ is $\frac{1}{5}$ th of the $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}$ reaction $\left(\mathrm{Ea}_{1}\right)$, then the value of $\left(\mathrm{Ea}_{2}\right)$ is _________ $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$. (Nearest Integer)
Solution:
$\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}_{1}}}{2.303 \mathrm{R}}=1.5 \times 10^{4}$
$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}_{1}}=1.5 \times 10^{4} \times 2.303 \times 8.314$
$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}_{1}}=28.7207 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~J}$
$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}_{1}}=287.207 \mathrm{~kJ}$
$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}_{1}}}{5}=\frac{287.207}{5}=57.44 \mathrm{~kJ}$

Question: The crystal field splitting energy of $\left[\mathrm{Co}(\text { oxalate })_{3}\right]^{3-}$ complex is ' n ' times that of the $\left.[\mathrm{Cr} \text { (oxalate) })_{3}\right]^{3-}$ complex. Here ' n ' is ________.
Solution:
Pairing energy neglected w.r.t. $\Delta_{0}$
$\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{ox})_{3}\right]^{3-}: \mathrm{Co}^{+3}: \mathrm{d}^{6} ; \mathrm{t}_{2} \mathrm{~g}^{2,2,2} \mathrm{eg}^{0,0}$
$\mathrm{CFSE}=6 \times\left(-0.4 \Delta_{0}\right)=-2.4 \Delta_{0}$
$\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{ox}_{3}\right)\right]^{3-}: \mathrm{Cr}^{+3}: \mathrm{d}^{3} ; \mathrm{t}_{2} \mathrm{~g}^{1,1,1} \mathrm{eg}^{0,0}$
CFSE $=3 \times\left(-0.4 \Delta_{0}\right)=-1.2 \Delta_{0}$
$\frac{(\mathrm{CFSE})_{\mathrm{Co}^{+3}}}{(\mathrm{CFSE})_{\mathrm{Cr}^{+3}}}=2$

Question:
Given,
(A) $n=5, m_{l}=-1$
(B) $\mathrm{n}=3,1=2, \mathrm{~m}_{l}=-1, \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{s}}=+\frac{1}{2}$
The maximum number of electron(s) in an atom that can have the quantum numbers as given in (A) and (B) respectively are :
Solution:
(A) $n=5$
$l=0 \quad \mathrm{~m}_{l}=0$
$l=1 \quad \mathrm{~m}_{l}=\mathbf{- 1}, 0,1 \Rightarrow 2$ electrons
$l=2 \quad \mathrm{~m}_{l}=-2,-\mathbf{1}, 0,1,2 \Rightarrow 2$ electrons
$l=3 \quad \mathrm{~m}_{l}=-3,-2,-\mathbf{1}, 0,1,2,3 \Rightarrow 2$ electrons
$l=4 \quad \mathrm{~m}_{l}=-4,-3,-2,-\mathbf{1}, 0,1,2,3,4 \Rightarrow 2$ electrons
Total number of electrons $=8$
(B) $\mathrm{n}=3, \mathrm{l}=2, \mathrm{~m}_{l}=-1, \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{s}}=+1 / 2 \Rightarrow$ only 1 electron is possible

Question:
For the following gas phase equilibrium reaction at constant temperature,
$\mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
If the total pressure is $\sqrt{3} \mathrm{~atm}$ and the pressure equilibrium constant $\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}\right)$ is 9 atm , then the degree of dissociation is given as $\left(\mathrm{x} \times 10^{-2}\right)^{-1 / 2}$.
The value of $x$ is ________ (Nearest integer)
Solution:
$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)^{1 / 2}\left(\frac{3 \alpha}{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{(1-\alpha)}\left[\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{T}}}{1+\alpha}\right]^{1} \quad\left[\because \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{T}}=\sqrt{3} \mathrm{~atm}\right] \\
& 9=\frac{\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)^{1 / 2}\left(\frac{3 \alpha}{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{(1-\alpha)} \times \frac{(3)^{1 / 2}}{1+\alpha} \\
& 9=\frac{9\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)^{2}}{1-\alpha^{2}} \\
& 1-\alpha^{2}=\frac{\alpha^{2}}{4} \\
& \frac{5 \alpha^{2}}{4}=1 \\
& \alpha^{2}=0.8 \\
& \alpha=(0.8)^{1 / 2} \\
& \alpha=\left[\frac{1}{0.8}\right]^{-1 / 2} \\
& \alpha=\left[125 \times 10^{-2}\right]^{-1 / 2} \\
& \mathrm{x}=125 .
\end{aligned}
$$

A. Sodium hypochlorite reacts with KI to give KOI.
B. KOI is a reducing agent.
C. $\alpha, \beta$-unsaturated methylketone
will give iodoform reaction.
D. Isopropyl alcohol will not give iodoform test.
E. Methanoic acid will give positive iodoform test.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
Solution:
(A) $\mathrm{NaOCl}+\mathrm{KI} \rightarrow \mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{KOI}$
(B) Incorrect statement
(C) gives iodoform reaction.
(D) Incorrect statement
(E) Incorrect statement

Question:
Observe the following reactions at $\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K})$
I. A → products.
II. $5 \mathrm{Br}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{BrO}_{3}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})+6 \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow 3 \mathrm{Br}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})+ 3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$
Both the reactions are started at 10.00 am . The rates of these reactions at 10.10 am are same. The value of $-\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{Br}^{-}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}$ at 10.10 am is $2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{Min}^{-1}$. The concentration of A at 10.10 am is $10^{-2} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$. What is the first order rate constant (in $\mathrm{min}^{-1}$ ) of reaction I?
Solution:
At $\mathrm{t}=10$ minutes
Rate of reaction $=-\frac{1}{5} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{Br}^{-}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=\frac{1}{5} \times\left(2 \times 10^{-4}\right) =4 \times 10^{-5}$
For reaction $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{P}$
at $\mathrm{t}=10$ minutes
Rate of reaction $=4 \times 10^{-5}=\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{A}]$
$\mathrm{k}=4 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~min}^{-1}$.

Question: The work functions of two metals ( $\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}}$ ) are in the $1: 2$ ratio. When these metals are exposed to photons of energy 6 eV , the kinetic energy of liberated electrons of $M_{A}: M_{B}$ is in the ratio of 2.642 : 1. The work functions (in eV ) of $\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}}$ are respectively.
Solution:
$\mathrm{KE}_{\text {max }}=\mathrm{E}-\phi$
\[{\left( {{\text{K}}{{\text{E}}_{\max }}} \right)_1} = 6 - {\phi _1}\] …. (1)
\[{\left( {{\text{K}}{{\text{E}}_{\max }}} \right)_2} = 6 - {\phi _2}\] …. (2)
By eq. (1) divide eq. (2)
$\frac{\left(\mathrm{KE}_{\text {max }}\right)_{1}}{\left(\mathrm{KE}_{\text {max }}\right)_{2}}=\frac{2.642}{1}=\frac{6-\phi_{1}}{6-\phi_{2}}$
$\frac{2.642}{1}=\frac{6-\phi_{1}}{6-2 \phi_{1}}$
$\phi_{1}=2.3 \mathrm{eV}$
$\phi_{2}=4.6 \mathrm{eV}$.

Solution:
A → nB
$0.05 \quad 0$

Question: A mixed ether ( P ), when heated with excess of hot concentrated hydrogen iodide produces two different alkyl iodides which when treated with aq. NaOH give compounds (Q) and (R). Both (Q) and (R) give yellow precipitate with NaOI. Identify the mixed ether (P):
In Carius method 0.2425 g of an organic compounds gave 0.5253 g silver chloride. The percentage of chlorine in the organic compound is
Solution:
Organic compound : 0.2425 gm
AgCl obtained : 0.5253 gm
In carius method for estimation of halogen amount of AgCl obtained is 0.5253 gm from 0.2425 gm of organic compound.
Hence percentage of Cl.
Percentage of $\mathrm{Cl}=\frac{143.5}{35.5} \times \frac{0.5253}{0.2425} \times 100$
$=53.58 \%$

Question:
Iodoform test can differentiate between
A. Methanol and Ethanol
B. $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$ and $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{COOH}$
C. Cyclohexene and cyclohexanone
D. Diethyl ether and Pentan-3-one
E. Anisole and acetone
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:

Total number of unpaired electrons present in the central metal atoms/ions of
$\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_{4}\right],\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_{4}\right]^{2-},\left[\mathrm{PtCl}_{2}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right],\left[\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{CN}_{4}\right)\right]^{2-}$ and $\left[\mathrm{Pt}\left(\mathrm{CN}_{4}\right)\right]^{2-}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
In $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_{4}\right], \mathrm{Ni}^{0}: 3 \mathrm{~d}^{8} 4 \mathrm{~s}^{2}$
Hybridisation state: $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$
Unpaired electron $=0$
In $\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_{4}\right]^{2-}, \mathrm{Ni}^{2+}: 3 \mathrm{~d}^{8}$
Hybridisation state: $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$
Unpaired electron $=2$
In $\left[\mathrm{PtCl}_{4}\right]^{2-}, \mathrm{Pt}^{2+}: 5 \mathrm{~d}^{8}$
Hybridisation state: $\mathrm{dsp}^{2}$
Unpaired electron $=0$
In $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_{4}\right]^{2-}, \mathrm{Ni}^{2+}: 3 \mathrm{~d}^{8}$
Hybridisation state: $\mathrm{dsp}^{2}$
Unpaired electron $=0$
In $\left[\mathrm{Pt}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right], \mathrm{Pt}^{2+}: 5 \mathrm{~d}^{8}$
Hybridisation state: $\mathrm{dsp}^{2}$
Unpaired electron $=0$

Question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3}\stackrel{\oplus}{\mathrm{C}}$ is more stable than $\stackrel{\oplus}{\mathrm{C}}\mathrm{H}_{3}$ as nine hyperconjugation interactions are possible in $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3}\stackrel{\oplus}{\mathrm{C}}$.
Statement II: $\stackrel{\oplus}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}_{3}$ is less stable than $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \stackrel{\oplus}{\mathrm{C}}$ as only three hyperconjugation interactions are possible in $\stackrel{\oplus}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}_{3}$.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
Options: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Solution:
S-1: $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{C}^{+}>\stackrel{+}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}_{3}$; due to hyperconjugation interaction
S-2 : False

Question:
Two liquids A and B form an ideal solution. At 320 K , the vapour pressure of the solution, containing 3 mol of A and 1 mol of B is 500 mm Hg. At the same temperature, if 1 mol of A is further added to this solution, vapour pressure of the solution increases by 20 mm Hg . Vapour pressure (in mm Hg ) of B in the pure state is
________. (Nearest integer)
Solution:
$\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{3}{4}, \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{1}{4}$
$P_{S}=P_{A}^{o} X_{A}+P_{B}^{o} X_{B}$
$500=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}^{\mathrm{o}} \times \frac{3}{4}+\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}^{\mathrm{o}} \times \frac{1}{4}$

$$
\begin{equation*}
3 \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}^{\mathrm{o}}+\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}^{\mathrm{o}}=2000 \tag{1}
\end{equation*}
$$

Now 1 moles of A is further added so $\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{A}}=4$ mole, $\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{B}}=1$ mole
$\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{A}}^{\prime}=\frac{4}{5}, \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{B}}^{\prime}=\frac{1}{5}$
$\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{s}}=520=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}^{\mathrm{o}} \times \frac{4}{5}+\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}^{\mathrm{o}} \times \frac{1}{5}$

$$
\begin{equation*}
4 \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}^{\mathrm{o}}+\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}^{\mathrm{o}}=2600 \tag{2}
\end{equation*}
$$

By equation (2) - equation (1)
$\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}^{\mathrm{o}}=600 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg}$
$\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}^{\mathrm{o}}=200 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg}$

Question:
Identify the CORRECT set of details from the following :
A. $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right]^{3+}$ : Inner orbital complex ; d${ }^{2}$ sp$^{3}$ hybridized
B. $\left[\mathrm{MnCl}_{6}\right]^{3-}$ Outer orbital complex; sp${ }^{3}d^{2}$ hybridized
C. $\left[\mathrm{CoF}_{6}\right]^{3-}$ : Outer orbital complex ; $\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{sp}^{3}$ hybridized
D. $\left[\mathrm{FeF}_{6}\right]^{3-}$ : Outer orbital complex: $\mathrm{sp}^{3}\mathrm{d}^{2}$ hybridized
E. $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_{4}\right]^{2-}$ : Inner orbital complex ; sp${ }^{3}$ hybridized
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
Solution:
(A) $\mathrm{Co}^{3+}:-3 \mathrm{d}^{6}$
$\mathrm{NH}_{3} \Rightarrow$ S.F.L
Hybridisation $\Rightarrow \mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{sp}^{3}$, Inner orbital complex
(B) $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}:-3 \mathrm{d}^{4}$
$\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \Rightarrow$ W.F.L
Hybridisation $\Rightarrow \mathrm{sp}^{3} \mathrm{d}^{2}$, Outer orbital complex
(C) $\mathrm{Co}^{3+}:-3 \mathrm{~d}^{6}$
$\mathrm{F}^{-} \Rightarrow$ W.F.L
Hybridisation $\Rightarrow \mathrm{sp}^{3} \mathrm{d}^{2}$, Outer orbital complex
(D) $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}:-3 \mathrm{d}^{5}$
$\mathrm{F}^{-} \Rightarrow$ W.F.L
Hybridisation $\Rightarrow \mathrm{sp}^{3} \mathrm{d}^{2}$, Outer orbital complex
(E) $\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}:-3 \mathrm{d}^{8}$
$\mathrm{CN}^{-} \Rightarrow$ S.F.L
Hybridisation ⇒ dsp ${ }^{2}$, Inner orbital complex

Question:
$\mathrm{X}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{Y}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{Z}(\mathrm{g})$
$\mathrm{X}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ and $\mathrm{Y}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ are added to a 1 L flask and it is found that the system attains the above equilibrium at $\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K})$ with the number of moles of $\mathrm{X}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}), \mathrm{Y}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ and $\mathrm{Z}(\mathrm{g})$ being 3, 3 and 9 mol respectively (equilibrium moles). Under this conditions of equilibrium, 10 mol of $\mathrm{Z}(\mathrm{g})$ is added to the flask and the temperature is maintained at $\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K})$. Then the number of moles of $\mathrm{Z}(\mathrm{g})$ in the flask when the new equilibrium is established is $\_\_\_\_$ . (Nearest integer).
Solution:
$\mathrm{X}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{Y}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{Z}(\mathrm{g})$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{(9)^{2}}{3 \times 3}=9$
Now 10 moles of $Z$ are added then reaction will move in backward direction.
$\mathrm{X}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{Y}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{Z}(\mathrm{g})$
$3+\mathrm{X} \quad 3+\mathrm{X} \quad 19-2 \mathrm{X}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{(19-2 \mathrm{X})^{2}}{(3+\mathrm{X})(3+\mathrm{X})}=9$
$\frac{19-2 X}{3+X}=3$
$19-2 X=9+3 X$
$10=5 \mathrm{X}$
$\mathrm{X}=2$
At equilibrium ⇒ moles of $\mathrm{Z}=19-2 \times 2$

$$
=15 \text { moles }
$$

Question: Both human DNA and RNA are chiral molecules. The chirality in DNA and RNA arises due to the presence of
Solution: DNA & RNA are chiral molecules due to presence of chiral 2-deoxy-Ribose & Ribose sugar unit respectively.

Question: Consider the above electrochemical cell where a metal electrode ( M ) is undergoing redox reaction by forming $\mathrm{M}^{+}\left(\mathrm{M} \rightarrow \mathrm{M}^{+}+\mathrm{e}\right)$. The cation $\mathrm{M}^{+}$is present in two different concentrations $\mathrm{c}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{c}_{2}$ as shown above. Which of the following statement is correct for generating a positive cell potential?
Options: If $\mathrm{c}_{1}$ is present at anode, then $\mathrm{c}_{1}=\mathrm{c}_{2}$
If $\mathrm{c}_{1}$ is present at cathode, then $\mathrm{c}_{1}<\mathrm{c}_{2}$
If $\mathrm{c}_{1}$ is present at cathode, then $\mathrm{c}_{1}>\mathrm{c}_{2}$
If $\mathrm{c}_{1}$ is present at anode, then $\mathrm{c}_{1}>\mathrm{c}_{2}$
Solution:
(1) If $\mathrm{C}_{1}$ is at anode ⇒ cell reaction
$\mathrm{M}^{+}\left(\mathrm{C}_{2}\right) \rightarrow \mathrm{M}^{+}\left(\mathrm{C}_{1}\right)$
$\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=-0.059 \log \frac{\mathrm{C}_{1}}{\mathrm{C}_{2}}$
$\therefore \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{cell}}>0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{1}<\mathrm{C}_{2}$
(2) If $\mathrm{C}_{1}$ is at cathode
$\mathrm{M}^{+}\left(\mathrm{C}_{1}\right) \rightarrow \mathrm{M}^{+}\left(\mathrm{C}_{2}\right)$
$\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=-0.059 \log \frac{\mathrm{C}_{2}}{\mathrm{C}_{1}}>0$
$\mathrm{C}_{2}<\mathrm{C}_{1}$

Question:
It is noticed that $\mathrm{Pb}^{2+}$ is more stable than $\mathrm{Pb}^{4+}$ but $\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}$ is less stable than $\mathrm{Sn}^{4+}$. Observe the following reactions
$$
\begin{equation*}
\mathrm{PbO}_{2}+\mathrm{Pb} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{PbO} ; \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ} \tag{1}
\end{equation*}
$$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\mathrm{SnO}_{2}+\mathrm{Sn} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{SnO} ; \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ} \tag{2}
\end{equation*}
$$
Identify the correct set from the following.
Solution:
$\mathrm{PbO}_{2}+\mathrm{Pb} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{PbO}$
$\mathrm{Pb}^{2+}$ is more stable hence reaction will be spontaneous. So $\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}{ }_{\mathrm{r}}(1)$ is negative.
$\mathrm{SnO}_{2}+\mathrm{Sn} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{SnO}$
$\mathrm{Sn}^{+2}$ is less stable, so reaction will be nonspontaneous hance $\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}{ }_{\mathrm{r}}(2)$ is positive.

$$

Question:
Water flows through a horizontal tube as shown in the figure. The difference in height between the water columns in vertical tubes is 5 cm and the area of cross-sections at $A$ and $B$ are $6 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ and $3 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ respectively.
The rate of flow will be $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{cm}^{3} / \mathrm{s}$. (take $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$ )
Solution:
From continuity equation
$A_{A} V_{A}=A_{B} V_{B} \Rightarrow 6 V_{A}=3 V_{B} \Rightarrow V_{B}=2 V_{A}$
Applying Bernoullis equation between $\mathrm{A} \& \mathrm{~B}$,
$$
\begin{aligned}
& P_{A}+\frac{1}{2} \rho V_{A}^{2}=P_{B}+\frac{1}{2} \rho V_{B}^{2} \\
\Rightarrow \quad & \rho g \times 0.05=\frac{1}{2} \rho\left[V_{B}^{2}-V_{A}^{2}\right]=\frac{1}{2} \rho\left(3 V_{A}^{2}\right) \\
\Rightarrow \quad & V_{A}=\sqrt{\frac{2 g \times 0.05}{3}} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}=\frac{100}{\sqrt{3}} \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Volume flow rate= A.v= 200(3)1/2

Question:
A point charge of $10^{-8} \mathrm{C}$ is placed at origin. The work done in moving a point charge $2 \mu \mathrm{C}$ from point $\mathrm{A}(4,4,2) \mathrm{m}$ to $\mathrm{B}(2,2,1) \mathrm{m}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ J.
( $\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}=9 \times 10^{9}$ in SI units)
Solution:
Work done by external agent :
$\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{ext}}=\Delta \mathrm{U} ;$
$\Delta \mathrm{U} \rightarrow$ Change in potential energy in taking the charge from initial to final configuration
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{W}_{\text {ext }}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}} \frac{\mathrm{q}_{1} \mathrm{q}_{2}}{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{f}}}-\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}} \frac{\mathrm{q}_{1} \mathrm{q}_{2}}{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{i}}}$
Now, $r_{f}=\sqrt{(2-0)^{2}+(2-0)^{2}+(1-0)^{2}}=3 \mathrm{~m}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{i}} & =\sqrt{(4-0)^{2}+(4-0)^{2}+(2-0)^{2}}=6 \mathrm{~m} \\
\therefore \quad \mathrm{~W}_{\mathrm{ext}} & =\left(9 \times 10^{9}\right) \times\left(10^{-8} \times 2 \times 10^{-6}\right)\left[\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{6}\right] \\
& =3 \times 10^{-5} \\
& =30 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~J}
\end{aligned}
$$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Electric potential energy of a system of charges

Question: A conducting circular loop of area $1.0 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$ is placed perpendicular to a magnetic field which varies as $\mathrm{B}=\sin (100 \mathrm{t})$ Tesla. If the resistance of the loop is $100 \Omega$, then the average thermal energy dissipated in the loop in one period is $\_\_\_\_$ J.
Solution:
Area of the loop $=1 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
$\mathrm{B}=\sin (100 \mathrm{t})$
$\therefore \quad \phi=\mathrm{BA}=\sin (100 \mathrm{t})$
$\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} \phi}{\mathrm{dt}}=100 \cos (100 \mathrm{t})$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{P}=\frac{\mathrm{V}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{10^{4} \cos ^{2}(100 \mathrm{t})}{100}$
$\therefore \quad$ Thermal energy dissipated in 1 time period
$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\int_{0}^{\mathrm{T}} \mathrm{Pdt}=\int_{0}^{\mathrm{T}} 100 \cos ^{2}(100 \mathrm{t}) \mathrm{dt} \\
& \mathrm{~T}=\frac{2 \pi}{100}=\frac{\pi}{50} \mathrm{sec} \\
& \therefore \quad \mathrm{Q}=100 \int_{0}^{\pi / 50} \cos ^{2}(100 \mathrm{t}) \mathrm{dt} \\
& \quad=100 \int_{0}^{\pi / 50} \frac{1+\cos 200 \mathrm{t}}{2} \mathrm{dt} \\
& =100\left[\frac{\pi}{100}\right]=\pi
\end{aligned}
$$
Correct option (c)

Question:
Initially a satellite of 100 kg is in a circular orbit of radius $1.5 \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{E}}$. This satellite can be moved to a circular orbit of radius $3 \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{E}}$ by supplying $\alpha \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~J}$ of energy. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .
(Take Radius of Earth $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{E}}=6 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~m}$ and $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$ )
Solution:
Energy of a satellite in a circular orbit is given as $\mathrm{E}=\frac{-\mathrm{GM}_{\mathrm{E}} \mathrm{m}}{2 \mathrm{r}} ; \mathrm{r}=$ radius of circular orbit
Required energy to be supplied $=\Delta \mathrm{E}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{i}}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta \mathrm{E} & =\left(\frac{-\mathrm{GM}_{\mathrm{E}} \mathrm{~m}}{2\left(3 \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{E}}\right)}\right)-\left(\frac{-\mathrm{GM}_{\mathrm{E}} \mathrm{~m}}{2\left(1.5 \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{E}}\right)}\right) \\
& =\frac{\mathrm{GM}_{\mathrm{E}} \mathrm{~m}}{6 \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{E}}}
\end{aligned}
$$
Now, $\mathrm{g}=\frac{\mathrm{GM}_{\mathrm{E}}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{E}}^{2}} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{GM}_{\mathrm{E}}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{E}}}=\mathrm{gR}_{\mathrm{E}}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\therefore \quad \Delta \mathrm{E} & =\frac{1}{6} \mathrm{gmR}_{\mathrm{E}} \\
& =\frac{1}{6} \times 10 \times 100 \times 6 \times 10^{6} \\
& =1000 \times 10^{6}
\end{aligned}
$$
$\alpha=1000$

Question:
A gas based geyser heats water flowing at the rate of 5.0 litres per minute from $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $87^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.
The rate of consumption of the gas is $\_\_\_\_$ g/s.
(Take heat of combustion of gas $=5.0 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{g}$ ,specific heat capacity of water $=4200 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg} .{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$)
Solution:
Water flow rate $=5 \ell / \min =\frac{5}{60} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{s}$
∴ Power of heater $=\frac{\mathrm{dm}}{\mathrm{dt}} \mathrm{S} \Delta \mathrm{T}=\frac{1}{12} \times 4200 \times 60 \mathrm{~W}$
∴ Let rate of consumption of gas be $\mathrm{xg} / \mathrm{s}$.
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{x} \times 5.0 \times 10^{4}=\frac{1}{12} \times 4200 \times 60$
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{x}=4200 \times 10^{-4}=0.42 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{s}$
Correct option (3)

Tags.Topic: Other types of problems involving temperature change

Question: Two strings (A, B) having linear densities $\mu_{\mathrm{A}}=2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}$ and $\mu_{\mathrm{B}}=4 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}$ and lengths $\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}=2.5 \mathrm{~m}$ and $\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}=1.5 \mathrm{~m}$ respectively are joined. Free ends of A and B are tied to two rigid supports C and D , respectively creating a tension of 500 N in the wire. Two identical pulses, sent from C and D ends, take time $\mathrm{t}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{t}_{2}$, respectively, to reach the joint. The ratio $\mathrm{t}_{1} / \mathrm{t}_{2}$ is :
Solution:
Given $\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}=2.5 \mathrm{~m}$,
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{B}}=1.5 \mathrm{~m}, \\
& \mathrm{~T}=500 \mathrm{~N} \\
& \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{A}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{T}}{\mu_{\mathrm{A}}}}=\sqrt{\frac{500}{2 \times 10^{-4}}}=5 \sqrt{10} \times 10^{2} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\
& \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{B}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{T}}{\mu_{\mathrm{B}}}}=\sqrt{\frac{500}{4 \times 10^{-4}}}=5 \sqrt{5} \times 10^{2} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\
& \mathrm{t}_{1}=\frac{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{A}}}=\frac{2.5}{5 \sqrt{10}} \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~s} \\
& \mathrm{t}_{2}=\frac{\mathrm{L}_{B}}{\mathrm{v}_{B}}=\frac{1.5}{5 \sqrt{5}} \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~s} \\
& \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{t}_{1}}{\mathrm{t}_{2}}=\frac{2.5}{5 \sqrt{10}} \times \frac{5 \sqrt{5}}{1.5}=\frac{5}{3} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{1.66}{1.41}=1.18
\end{aligned}
$$

Slope of potential energy $\mathrm{v} / \mathrm{s}$ position curve gives negative of force.

Question: A 1 m long metal rod AB completes the circuit as shown in figure. The area of circuit is perpendicular to the magnetic field of 0.10 T . If the resistance of the total circuit is $2 \Omega$ then the force needed to move the rod towards right with constant speed (v) of $1.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ N.
Solution:
To maintain constant speed
 
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{ext}}=\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{B}} \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{~F}_{\mathrm{ext}}=\mathrm{ilB} \\
& =\left(\frac{\mathrm{vBl}}{\mathrm{R}}\right) \mathrm{lB} \\
& =\frac{\mathrm{B}^{2} l^{2} \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{R}} \\
& =\frac{(0.1)^{2} \times(1)^{2} \times 1.5}{2} \\
& =7.5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~N}
\end{aligned}
$$

Question: An aluminium and steel rods having same lengths and cross-sections are joined to make total length of 120 cm at $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The coefficient of linear expansion of aluminium and steel are $24 \times 10^{-6} /{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $1.2 \times 10^{-5} /{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, respectively. The length of this composite rod when its temperature is raised to $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, is $\_\_\_\_$ cm.
Solution:
$\ell_{\text {final }}=\ell_{0}\left(1+\alpha_{\mathrm{A}} \Delta \mathrm{T}\right)+\ell_{0}\left(1+\alpha_{\mathrm{B}} \Delta \mathrm{T}\right)$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\ell_{0}\left[2+\left(\alpha_{\mathrm{A}}+\alpha_{\mathrm{B}}\right) \Delta \mathrm{T}\right] \\
& =60\left[2+\left(36 \times 10^{-6}\right) \times 70\right] \\
& =60[2+0.0025] \\
& =120.15 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Question:
A uniform rod of mass m and length $l$ suspended by means of two identical inextensible light strings as shown in figure. Tension in one string immediately after the other string is cut, is
$\_\_\_\_$ . (g acceleration due to gravity)
Solution:
$$
\begin{align*}
& \operatorname{mg} \frac{l}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{m} l^{2}}{3} \alpha \\
& \alpha=\frac{3 \mathrm{~g}}{2 l} \ldots(1) \tag{1}
\end{align*}
$$
$\mathrm{mg}-\mathrm{T}=\mathrm{ma}_{\mathrm{c}}$
$\mathrm{T}=\mathrm{mg}-\mathrm{ma}_{\mathrm{c}}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\mathrm{mg}-\mathrm{m}\left(\frac{l}{2} \alpha\right) \\
& =\mathrm{mg}-\mathrm{m}\left(\frac{l}{2} \cdot \frac{3 \mathrm{~g}}{2 l}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
\mathrm{T}=\frac{\mathrm{mg}}{4}
$$

Question:
If an alpha particle with energy 7.7 MeV is bombarded on a thin gold foil, the closest distance from nucleus it can reach is $\_\_\_\_$ m .
(Atomic number of gold $=79$ and $\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}=9 \times 10^{9}$ in SI units)
Solution:
Energy conservation
$$
\begin{aligned}
& K_{i}+U_{i}=K_{f}+U_{f} \\
& 7.7 \times 10^{6} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}+0 \\
& =0+\frac{9 \times 10^{9}\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)\left(79 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)}{r} \\
& r=2.95 \times 10^{-14}
\end{aligned}
$$

Question:
A light wave described by $\mathrm{E}=[60 \sin \left(3 \times 10^{15}\right) \mathrm{t}+ \sin \left(12 \times 10^{15}\right)$ t] (in SI units) falls on a metal surface of work function 2.8 eV . The maximum kinetic energy of ejected photoelectron is (approximately)
$\_\_\_\_$ eV. $\quad\left(\mathrm{h}=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}-\mathrm{s} . \quad\right.$ and $\mathrm{e}=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}$ )
Solution:
$\quad \omega_{1}=3 \times 10^{15} \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{sec}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \omega_{2}=12 \times 10^{15} \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{sec} \\
& \because \quad v=\frac{\omega}{2 \pi} \\
& \begin{aligned}
\mathrm{E}_{\text {photon }} & =\mathrm{h} v=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 1.91 \times 10^{15} \\
& =1.26 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{~J} \\
\mathrm{E}_{\max } & =\frac{1.26 \times 10^{-18}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \simeq 7.9 \mathrm{eV} \\
\mathrm{~K}_{\max } & =\mathrm{E}_{\max }-\phi_{0} \\
& =7.9-2.8 \\
\mathrm{~K}_{\max } & =5.1 \mathrm{eV}
\end{aligned}
\end{aligned}
$$
