Tags.Chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
Tags.Topic: Photoelectric effect and Einstein's photoelectric equation

Question: In a double slit experiment the distance between the slits is 0.1 cm and the screen is placed at 50 cm from the slits plane. When one slit is covered with a transparent sheet having thickness $t$ and refractive index $\mathrm{n}(=1.5)$, the central fringe shifts by 0.2 cm . The value of t is $\_\_\_\_$ cm .
Solution:
$\mathrm{d} \sin \theta=(\mu-1) \mathrm{t}$
$\mathrm{d}\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{D}}\right]=(\mu-1) \mathrm{t}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{t} & =\frac{\mathrm{xd}}{\mathrm{D}(\mu-1)} \\
& =\frac{(0.2)(0.1)}{50(1.5-1)} \\
\mathrm{t} & =8 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Variations in Young's double slit experiment

Question:
The electric field a plane electromagnetic wave is given by :
$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{y}}=69 \sin \left[0.6 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{x}-1.8 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{t}\right] \mathrm{V} / \mathrm{m}$.
The expression for magnetic field associated with this electromagnetic wave is $\_\_\_\_$ T.
Solution:
$\hat{B}=\hat{c} \times \hat{E}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \hat{\mathrm{c}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}$ because phase of electric field is function of x .
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Rightarrow \quad & \hat{\mathrm{E}}=\hat{\mathrm{j}} \text { (given) } \\
\Rightarrow \quad & \hat{\mathrm{B}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}} \times \hat{\mathrm{j}}=\hat{\mathrm{k}} \\
& |\mathrm{~B}|=\frac{|\mathrm{E}|}{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{69 \times 0.6 \times 10^{3}}{1.8 \times 10^{11}}=\frac{69}{3 \times 10^{8}} \\
& |\mathrm{~B}|=2.3 \times 10^{-7} \\
& \overrightarrow{\mathrm{~B}}_{2}=2.3 \times 10^{-7} \sin \left(0.6 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{x}-1.8 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{t}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$
(phase is same as that of electric field)

Question:
A parallel plate capacitor has capacitance C , when there is vacuum within the parallel plates.
A sheet having thickness $\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{\text {rd }}$ of the separation between the plates and relative permittivity K is introduced between the plates. The new capacitance of the system is :
Solution:
\[C = \frac{{A{ \in _0}}}{d}\]
 
$\mathrm{C}_{1}=\frac{3 \mathrm{~A} \epsilon_{0}}{2 \mathrm{~d}}$
$\mathrm{C}_{2}=\frac{3 \mathrm{~A} \in_{0} \times \mathrm{K}}{\mathrm{d}}$
$\mathrm{C}_{1}=\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{C}$
$\mathrm{C}_{2}=3 \mathrm{KC}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{Ceq}=\frac{\mathrm{C}_{1} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{\mathrm{C}_{1}+\mathrm{C}_{2}}=\frac{\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{C} \times 3 \mathrm{KC}}{\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{C}+3 \mathrm{KC}} \\
& \mathrm{Ceq}=\frac{\frac{9}{2} \mathrm{KC}^{2}}{\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{C}(2 \mathrm{~K}+1)}=\frac{3 \mathrm{KC}}{2 \mathrm{~K}+1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Question: In a microscope the objective is having focal length $f_{0}=2 \mathrm{~cm}$ and eye-piece is having focal length $f_{\mathrm{e}}=4 \mathrm{~cm}$. The tube length is 32 cm . The magnification produced by this microscope for normal adjustment is $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$\mathrm{m} \simeq \frac{\mathrm{lD}}{\mathrm{f}_{0} \mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{e}}}$
$$
=\frac{32}{2} \times \frac{25}{4}
$$
$\mathrm{m}=100$
Correct Answer : 100

Question: A current carrying is placed vertically and a particle of mass m with charge Q is released from rest. The particle moves along the axis of solenoid. If g is acceleration due to gravity then the acceleration (a) of the charged particle will satisfy :
Solution:
Since the solenoid is placed vertically, the magnetic field inside the solenoid will be either along - y or +y axis.
⇒ Particle will gain velocity along -y axis.
$\Rightarrow \quad \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\mathrm{B}}=\mathrm{q}(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}})$
$\Rightarrow \quad \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\mathrm{B}}=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\text {net }}=\mathrm{mg}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{a}_{\text {net }}=\mathrm{g}$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Force on a moving charge in magnetic field

Question: 10 mole of oxygen is heated at constant volume from $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The change in the internal energy of the gas is $\_\_\_\_$ cal. (The molecular specific heat of oxygen at constant pressure, $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{p}}=7 \mathrm{cal} . / \mathrm{mol}^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $\mathrm{R}=2 \mathrm{cal} . / \mathrm{mol}^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.)
Solution:
$\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{nC}_{\mathrm{v}} \Delta \mathrm{T}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& =n\left(C_{p}-R\right) \Delta T \\
& =10(7-2)(40-30) \\
\Delta U & =500
\end{aligned}
$$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Change in internal energy of a gas

Question:
Consider a modified Bernoulli equation.
$$
\left(\mathrm{P}+\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{Bt}^{2}}\right)+\rho \mathrm{g}(\mathrm{~h}+\mathrm{Bt})+\frac{1}{2} \rho \mathrm{~V}^{2}=\text { constant }
$$
If t has the dimension of time then the dimensions of A and B are $\_\_\_\_$ , $\_\_\_\_$ respectively.
Solution:
⇒ $[P]=\left[\frac{A}{B t^{2}}\right]$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\Rightarrow[\mathrm{h}]=[\mathrm{Bt}] \tag{2}
\end{equation*}
$$
$$
\Rightarrow[\mathrm{B}]=\left[\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{t}}\right]=\left[\frac{\mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{~T}}\right]=\left[\mathrm{LT}^{-1}\right]
$$
Putting B is equation (1)
$$
\left[\mathrm{ML}^{-1} \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]=\left[\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{LT}^{-1} \times \mathrm{T}^{2}}\right]
$$
$$
[\mathrm{A}]=\left[\mathrm{ML}^{0} \mathrm{~T}^{-1}\right]
$$

Question:
Two identical thin rods of mass M kg and length L m are connected as shown in figure. Moment of inertia of the combined rod system about an axis passing through point P and perpendicular to the plane of the rods is $\frac{\mathrm{X}}{12} \mathrm{ML}^{2} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{2}$. The value of x is
$\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$\mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{ML}^{2}}{3}+\left(\frac{\mathrm{ML}^{2}}{12}+\mathrm{ML}^{2}\right)$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{4 \mathrm{ML}^{2}+\mathrm{ML}^{2}+12 \mathrm{ML}^{2}}{12} \\
& \mathrm{I}=\frac{17}{12} \mathrm{ML}^{2} \\
& \therefore \quad \mathrm{x}=17
\end{aligned}
$$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Moment of inertia of truncated bodies/combined bodies

Question:
A 4 kg mass moves under the influence of a force $\vec{F}=\left(4 t^{3} \hat{i}-3 t \hat{j}\right) N$ where $t$ is the time in second.
If mass starts from origin at $\mathrm{t}=0$, the velocity and position after $\mathrm{t}=2 \mathrm{~s}$ will be :
Solution:
$\vec{F}=4 t^{3} \hat{i}-3 t \hat{j}$
$\vec{a}=\frac{\vec{F}}{m}=t^{3} \hat{i}-\frac{3}{4} \hat{j}$
$\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{t}^{3}$
$a_{y}=\frac{-3}{4} t$
$\frac{\mathrm{dv}_{\mathrm{x}}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{t}^{3}$
$\frac{\mathrm{dv}_{\mathrm{y}}}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\frac{3}{4}$
$\int_{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{x}}=0}^{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{x}_{2}}} \mathrm{dv}_{\mathrm{x}}=\int_{\mathrm{t}=0}^{\mathrm{t}=2} \mathrm{t}^{3} \mathrm{dt}$
$\int_{0}^{\mathrm{v}_{y_{2}}} \mathrm{dv}_{\mathrm{y}}=\int_{0}^{2} \frac{-3}{4} \mathrm{t} d \mathrm{t}$
$\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{x}_{2}}-0=\left[\frac{\mathrm{t}^{4}}{4}\right]_{0}^{2}$
$\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{y}_{2}}=\frac{-3}{4}\left[\frac{\mathrm{t}^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{2}$
$\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{x}_{2}}=4$
$$
v_{y_{2}}=\frac{-3}{2}
$$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{2}=4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\frac{3}{2} \hat{\mathrm{j}}$
$\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{x}}=\frac{\mathrm{t}^{4}}{4}$
$\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{-3}{8} \mathrm{t}^{2}$
$\int_{0}^{x_{2}} d x=\int_{0}^{2} \frac{t^{4}}{4} d t$
$\int_{0}^{y_{2}} d x=\frac{-3}{8} t^{2} d t$
$\mathrm{x}_{2}-0=\left[\frac{\mathrm{t}^{5}}{20}\right]_{0}^{2}$
$\mathrm{y}_{2}-0=\frac{-3}{8}\left[\frac{\mathrm{t}^{3}}{3}\right]_{0}^{2}$
$\mathrm{x}_{2}=\frac{8}{5}$
$\mathrm{y}_{2}=-1$
$$
\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{8}{5} \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}
$$

Question: The heat generated in 1 minute between points A and B in the given circuit, when a battery of 9 V with internal resistance of $1 \Omega$ is connected across these points is $\_\_\_\_$ J.
Solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{i}=\frac{9}{3} & =3 \mathrm{~A} \\
\therefore \quad \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{AB}} & =\mathrm{i}^{2} \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{AB}} \mathrm{t} \\
& =(3)^{2} \times 2 \times 60=1080 \mathrm{~J}
\end{aligned}
$$

Question: In an experiment the values of two spring constants were measured as $\mathrm{k}_{1}=(10 \pm 0.2) \mathrm{N} / \mathrm{m}$ and $\mathrm{k}_{2}=(20 \pm 0.3) \mathrm{N} / \mathrm{m}$. If these springs are connected in parallel, then the percentage error in equivalent spring constant is :
Solution:
For parallel combination of spring,
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\mathrm{K}_{1}+\mathrm{K}_{2}=30 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$
$\Delta \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\Delta \mathrm{K}_{1}+\Delta \mathrm{K}_{2}=0.2+0.3=0.5 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$
$\therefore \quad$ % Error in $\mathrm{K}=\frac{0.5}{30} \times 100=1.67 \%$

Question:
Consider the following reactions.
$\mathrm{PbCl}_{2}+\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4} \rightarrow \mathrm{~A}+2 \mathrm{KCl}$
(Hot solution)
$\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{NaOH} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{B}+\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}$
$\mathrm{PbSO}_{4}+4 \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONH}_{4} \rightarrow\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+\mathrm{X}$
In the above reactions, $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$ and X are respectively.
Solution:
$\underset{\text { (Hot solution) }}{\mathrm{PbCl}_{2}}+\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4} \rightarrow \underset{\text { (A) }}{\mathrm{PbCrO}_{4}}+2 \mathrm{KCl}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{PbCrO}_{4}+4 \mathrm{NaOH} & \text { (excess) } \\
& \rightarrow \mathrm{Na}_{2}\left[\mathrm{~Pb}(\mathrm{OH})_{4}\right]+\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{PbSO}_{4} & +4 \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONH}_{4} \\
& \rightarrow\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2}\left[\mathrm{~Pb}\left(\underset{(\mathrm{X})}{\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}\right)_{4}}\right]+\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}\right.
\end{aligned}
$$

Question:
Which of the following represents the correct trend for the mentioned property?
A. $\mathrm{F}>\mathrm{P}>\mathrm{S}>\mathrm{B}-$ Frist Ionization Energy
B. $\mathrm{Cl}>\mathrm{F}>\mathrm{S}>\mathrm{P}-$ Electron Affinity
C. $\mathrm{K}>\mathrm{Al}>\mathrm{Mg}>\mathrm{B}-$ Metallic character
D. $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{O}>\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}>\mathrm{MgO}>\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ - Basic character
Choose the correct answer from the option given below.
Solution:
⇒ On moving left to right in a period IE increases and from top to bottom in a group IE decreases.
$\mathrm{F}>\mathrm{P}>\mathrm{S}>\mathrm{B}$ (IE order)
⇒ On moving left to right in a period metallic and basic character decreases.
$\mathrm{K}>\mathrm{Mg}>\mathrm{Al}>\mathrm{B}$ (Metallic character order)
⇒ On moving top to bottom in a group metallic and basic character increases.
$\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{O}>\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}>\mathrm{MgO}>\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$
⇒ EA : Group $17>$ Group $16>$ Group 15
$\mathrm{Cl}>\mathrm{F}>\mathrm{S}>\mathrm{P}$

Question: Identify A in the following reaction.

A hydrocarbon ' P ' $\left(\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{8}\right)$ on reaction with HCl gives an optically active compound ' Q ' $\left(\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{9} \mathrm{Cl}\right)$ which on reaction with one mole of ammonia gives compound ' R ' $\left(\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{11} \mathrm{~N}\right)$. ' R ' on diazotization followed by hydrolysis gives ' S '. Identify $\mathrm{P}, \mathrm{Q}, \mathrm{R}$ and S .

Given below are two statements :
Statement I: The number of pairs among [ $\mathrm{SiO}_{2}, \mathrm{CO}_{2}$ ], $\left[\mathrm{SnO}, \mathrm{SnO}_{2}\right],\left[\mathrm{PbO}, \mathrm{PbO}_{2}\right]$ and $\left[\mathrm{GeO}, \mathrm{GeO}_{2}\right]$, which contain oxides that are both amphoteric is 2 .
Statement II: $\mathrm{BF}_{3}$ is an electron deficient molecule can act as a lewis acid, forms adduct with $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ and has a trigonal planar geometry.
In the light of the above statement, choose the correct answer from the option given below.
Options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true.
Both Statement I and Statement II are false.
Statement I is true but Statement II is false.
Statement I is false Statement II is true.
Solution:
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{SiO}_{2}, \mathrm{CO}_{2}, \mathrm{GeO}, \mathrm{GeO}_{2}$ are acidic in nature. $\mathrm{SnO}, \mathrm{SnO}_{2}, \mathrm{PbO}, \mathrm{PbO}_{2}$ are amphoteric in nature.
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{BF}_{3}$ is lewis acid acoording to lewic octet theory and has $\mathrm{sp}^{2}$ hybridization with trigonal planar geometry and it can accept lone pair form ammonia to form adduct.

Question: 80 mL of a hydrocarbon on mixing with 264 mL of oxygen in a closed U-tube undergoes complete combustion. The residual gases after cooling to 273 K occupy 224 mL . When the system is treated with KOH solution, the volume decreases to 64 mL . The formula of the hydrocarbon is :
Solution:
$$
\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{y}(\mathrm{~g})}+\left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{\mathrm{y}}{4}\right) \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow \mathrm{xCO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\frac{\mathrm{y}}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(\ell)}
$$
$\begin{array}{llccl}\mathrm{t}=0 & 80 & 264 & 0 & - \\ \mathrm{t}=\mathrm{t}_{\text {final }} & - & 264-80\left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{\mathrm{y}}{4}\right) & 80 \mathrm{x} & -\end{array}$
$264-80\left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{\mathrm{y}}{4}\right)+80 \mathrm{x}=224$
$264-\frac{80 \mathrm{y}}{4}=224$
$40=\frac{80 \mathrm{y}}{4} \Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=2$
$264-80\left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{\mathrm{y}}{4}\right)=64$
$264-80\left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{2}\right)=64$
$264-80 \mathrm{x}-40=64$
$\mathrm{x}=2$

Question:
14.0 g of calcium metal is allowed to react with excess HCl at 1.0 atm pressure and 273 K .
Which of the following statements is incorrect?
[Given : Molar mass in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ of $\mathrm{Ca}-40, \mathrm{Cl}-35.5, \mathrm{H}-1$ ]
Options: The limiting reagent is calcium metal.
Solution:
$\underset{\frac{14}{40}}{\mathrm{Ca}_{(\mathrm{s})}}+\underset{\text { excess }}{2 \mathrm{HCl}_{(\mathrm{g})}} \longrightarrow \underset{-}{\mathrm{CaCl}_{2(\mathrm{~s})}}+\underset{-}{\mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}}$
$=0.35$ mole $\quad 0.35$ mole 0.35 mole
Volume of $\mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$ evolved $=0.35 \times 22.4=7.84 \mathrm{~L}$
c) is wrong because weight of $\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}=0.35 \times 111=$ 38.85 gm

Question: In Carius method, 0.75 g of an organic compound gave 1.2 g of barium sulphate, find percentage of sulphur (molar mass $32 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ). Molar mass of barium sulphate is $233 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.
Solution:
$\frac{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{BaSO}_{4}} \times 32}{\mathrm{~W}_{\text {(unknown comp.) }}} \times 100$
$=\frac{1.2 \times 32}{233} \times \frac{100}{0.75}=21.97 \%$

Question: Elements P and Q form two types of non-volatile, non-ionizable compounds PQ and $\mathrm{PQ}_{2}$. When 1 g of PQ is dissolved in 50 g of solvent ' A '. $\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}$ was 1.176 K while when 1 g of $\mathrm{PQ}_{2}$ is dissolved in 50 g of solvent ' A ', $\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}$ was 0.689 K . ( $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}}$ of ' A ' $=5 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ ). The molar masses of elements P and Q (in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ ) respectively, are :
Solution:
$\left(\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}\right)_{\mathrm{PQ}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}} \mathrm{m}$
$1.176=5 \times \frac{1}{\mathrm{M}_{1}} \times \frac{1000}{50}$
$\mathrm{M}_{1}=85.03$
$\left(\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}\right)_{\mathrm{PQ}_{2}}=5 \times \frac{1}{\mathrm{M}_{2}} \times \frac{1000}{50}=0.689$
$\mathrm{M}_{2}=145.13$
Let molar mass of $\mathrm{P} \& \mathrm{Q}$ are $\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{P}}$ and $\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{Q}}$ respectively
$\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{P}}+\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{Q}}=85.03$
$\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{P}}+2 \mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{Q}}=145.13$
$\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{p}}=24.93 \approx 25$
$\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{Q}}=60.1 \approx 60$

Question: An organic compound $(\mathrm{P})$ on treatment with aqueous ammonia under hot condition forms compound $(\mathrm{Q})$ which on heating with $\mathrm{Br}_{2}$ and KOH forms compound (R) having molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{7}\mathrm{N}$. Names of $\mathrm{P}, \mathrm{Q}$ and R respectively are.

Match the LIST-I with LIST-II
List – IList – II
A.Spring ConstantI.\[M{L^2}{T^{ - 2}}{K^{ - 1}}\]
B.Thermal ConductivityII.\[M{L^0}{T^{ - 2}}\]
C.Boltzmann constantIII.\[M{L^2}{T^{ - 3}}{A^{ - 2}}\]
D.Inductive reactanceIV.\[ML{T^{ - 3}}{K^{ - 1}}\]

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
Solution:
(A) $F=K x$
$\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-2}\right]=[\mathrm{K}][\mathrm{L}]$
$[\mathrm{K}]=\mathrm{ML}^{0} \mathrm{~T}^{-2}$
(B) Thermal conductivity
$\frac{\mathrm{dQ}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{\mathrm{kA}}{\ell} \Delta \mathrm{T}$
$\mathrm{ML}^{2} \mathrm{~T}^{-3}=\frac{[\mathrm{k}] \mathrm{L}^{2} \mathrm{~K}}{\mathrm{~L}}$
$\left[\mathrm{K}^{1}\right]=\mathrm{MLT}^{-3} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$
(C) Boltzman constant
$[\mathrm{K}]=\mathrm{ML}^{2} \mathrm{~T}^{-2} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$
(D) Inductive reactance
$\frac{[\mathrm{V}]}{[\mathrm{I}]}=\frac{\mathrm{ML}^{2} \mathrm{~T}^{-3} \mathrm{~A}^{-1}}{\mathrm{~A}}$
$=\mathrm{ML}^{2} \mathrm{~T}^{-3} \mathrm{~A}^{-2}$

Question: A circular disc has radius $R_{1}$ and thickness $T_{1}$. Another circular disc made of the same material has radius $R_{2}$ and thickness $T_{2}$. If the moment of inertia of both discs are same and $\frac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}=2$ then $\frac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}=\frac{1}{\alpha}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Moment of inertia of standard bodies

Question: A projectile is thrown upward at an angle $60^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The speed of the projectile is $20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ when its direction of motion is $45^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The initial speed of the projectile is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$.
Solution:
$\mathrm{u} \cos 60^{\circ}=\frac{20}{\sqrt{2}}$
$\frac{\mathrm{u}}{2}=\frac{20}{\sqrt{2}}$
$\mathrm{u}=\frac{40}{\sqrt{2}}$
$\mathrm{u}=20 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

Question: Inductance of a coil with $10^{4}$ turns is 10 mH and it is connected to a dc source of 10 V with internal resistance of $10 \Omega$. The energy density in the inductor when the current reaches $\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{e}}\right)$ of its maximum value is $\alpha \pi \times \frac{1}{\mathrm{e}^{2}} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$ . $\left(\mu_{0}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{Tm} / \mathrm{A}\right)$.
Solution:
$\mathrm{L}=10 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{H}$
$\mathrm{N}=10^{4}$

$\mathrm{I}_{0}=\frac{10}{10}=1 \mathrm{~A}$ (max current)
$\mathrm{I}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{e}}$
$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{d}}=\frac{\mathrm{B}^{2}}{2 \mu_{0}}$
$\mathrm{B}=\mu_{0} \mathrm{nI}$
$\mathrm{L}=\mu_{0} \mathrm{n}^{2} \pi \mathrm{R}^{2} \ell$
$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{d}}=\frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{n}^{2} \mathrm{I}^{2}}{2}$
$=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10^{8} \times \frac{1}{\mathrm{e}^{2}}}{2}$
$=\frac{20 \pi}{\mathrm{e}^{2}}$

Question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: Pressure of fluid is exerted only on a solid surface in contact as the fluid-pressure does not exist everywhere in a still fluid.
Statement II: Excess potential energy of the molecules on the surface of a liquid, when compared to interior, results in surface tension.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
Options: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Solution:
According to pascal's law pressure at any point in liquid at rest is same in all direction.
It exist at every point in the liquid not just at boundaries. So statement (1) is false.
For interior molecule net cohesive forces are zero statement (2) is correct.

Question: Find the correct combination of A, B, C and D inputs which can cause the LED to glow.
Solution:
LED will glow in forward biasing :
P higher potential - 1
Q lower potential - 0

Question: Net gravitational force at the centre of a square is found to be $F_{1}$ when four particles having mass $M, 2 M, 3 M$ and $4 M$ are placed at the four corners of the square as shown in figure and it is $F_{2}$ when the positions of $3 M$ and $4 M$ are interchanged. The ratio $\frac{F_{1}}{F_{2}}$ is $\frac{\alpha}{\sqrt{5}}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
Initial configuration

$\mathrm{F}=2 \sqrt{2} \frac{\mathrm{Gmm}_{0}}{\mathrm{r}^{2}}$
New configuration

$\mathrm{F}^{\prime}=\sqrt{10} \frac{\mathrm{Gmm}_{0}}{\mathrm{r}^{2}} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~F}}{\mathrm{~F}^{\prime}}=2 \sqrt{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{10}}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}$
$\therefore \alpha=2$

Question: Three identical coils $\mathrm{C}_{1}, \mathrm{C}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{3}$ are closely placed such that they share a common axis. $\mathrm{C}_{2}$ is exactly midway. $\mathrm{C}_{1}$ carries current $I$ in anti-clockwise direction while $\mathrm{C}_{3}$ carries current $I$ in clockwise direction. An induced current flows through $\mathrm{C}_{2}$ will be in clockwise direction when
Options: $\mathrm{C}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{3}$ move with equal speeds away from $\mathrm{C}_{2}$
$\mathrm{C}_{1}$ moves towards $\mathrm{C}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{3}$ moves away from $\mathrm{C}_{2}$
$\mathrm{C}_{1}$ moves away from $\mathrm{C}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{3}$ moves towards $\mathrm{C}_{2}$
$C_{1}$ and $C_{3}$ move with equal speeds towards $C_{2}$
Solution:
Magnetic field through the coil is
$$
\begin{aligned}
\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} & =\left(\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{C}_{2}}-\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{C}_{1}}\right) \hat{\mathrm{i}} \\
\phi & =\left(\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{C}_{2}}-\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{C}_{1}}\right) \mathrm{A} \\
\varepsilon & =\frac{-\mathrm{d} \phi}{\mathrm{dt}}
\end{aligned}
$$
Find the direction according to Lenz's law
If coil move away then magnetic field decreases & vice versa
Correct Ans. (2)

Question:
Six point charges are kept $60^{\circ}$ apart from each other on the circumference of a circle of radius R as shown in figure. The net electric field at the centre of the circle is $\_\_\_\_$ .
( $\epsilon_{\mathrm{o}}$ is permittivity of free space)
Solution:
Let $\frac{\mathrm{KQ}}{\mathrm{r}^{2}}=\mathrm{E}_{0}$

$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}_{\text {net }}=2 \mathrm{E}_{0} \cos 30^{\circ}(-\hat{\mathrm{i}})+2 \mathrm{E}_{0} \sin 30^{\circ}(\hat{\mathrm{j}})$
$=\frac{2 \mathrm{kQ}}{\mathrm{r}^{2}}\left[\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}(-\hat{\mathrm{i}})+\frac{1}{2} \hat{\mathrm{j}}\right]$
$=\frac{-1 Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{2}}(\sqrt{3} \hat{i}-\hat{j})$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Electric field due to a point charge or combination of point charges

Question:
Two loudspeakers ( $L_{1}$ and $L_{2}$ ) are placed with a separation of 10 m , as shown in figure. Both speakers are fed with an audio input signal of same frequency with constant volume. A voice recorder, initially at point $A$, at equidistance to both loud speakers, is moved by 25 m along the line $A B$ while monitoring the audio signal. The measured signal was found to undergo 10 cycles of minima and maxima during the movement. The frequency of the input signal is $\_\_\_\_$ Hz
(Speed of sound in air is $324 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and $\sqrt{5}=2.23$ )
Solution:
Point B will $10{ }^{\text {th }}$ maxima
$\Delta \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{L}_{2} \mathrm{~B}-\mathrm{L}_{1} \mathrm{~B}$
$L_{1} B=\sqrt{20^{2}+40^{2}}=20 \sqrt{5} \mathrm{~m}=44.6 \mathrm{~m}$
$\mathrm{L}_{2} \mathrm{~B}=\sqrt{40^{2}+30^{2}}=50 \mathrm{~m}$
$\Delta \mathrm{x}=50-44.6=5.4 \mathrm{~m}$
$\Delta \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{n} \lambda$
$5.4=10 \times \lambda$
$\lambda=0.54 \mathrm{~m}$
$\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{f} \lambda$
$\mathrm{f}=\frac{324}{0.54}=600 \mathrm{~Hz}$

Question:
XPQY is a vertical smooth long loop having a total resistance R where PX is parallel to QY and separation between them is $l$. A constant magnetic field B perpendicular to the plane of the loop exists in the entire space. A rod CD of length L ( $\mathrm{L}>l$ ) and mass $m$ is made to slide down from rest under the gravity as shown in figure. The terminal speed acquired by the rod is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$.
( $\mathrm{g}=$ acceleration due to gravity)
Solution:
at equilibrium (Or for terminal velocity)
$\mathrm{mg}=\mathrm{iB} \ell \Rightarrow \mathrm{mg}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{Bv} \ell}{\mathrm{R}}\right) \mathrm{B} \ell$
$\mathrm{V}=\frac{\mathrm{mgR}}{\mathrm{B}^{2} \ell^{2}}$

Question:
The electric field of a plane electromagnetic wave, travelling in an unknown non-magnetic medium is given by,
$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{y}}=20 \sin \left(3 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{x}-4.5 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{t}\right) \mathrm{V} / \mathrm{m}$
(where $\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{t}$ and other values have S.I. units). The dielectric constant of the medium is
(speed of light in free space is $3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ )
Solution:
$n=\frac{C}{V}$
$\mathrm{V}=\frac{\omega}{\mathrm{k}}=\frac{4.5 \times 10^{14}}{3 \times 10^{6}}=\frac{3}{2} \times 10^{8}$
$\mathrm{n}=2$
$n=\sqrt{\mu_{\mathrm{r}} \varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}} \quad\left(\mu_{\mathrm{r}}=1\right)$
$2=\sqrt{\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}}$
$\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}=4$

Question: The escape velocity from a spherical planet A is 10 $\mathrm{km} / \mathrm{s}$. The escape velocity from another planet B whose density and radius are $10 \%$ of those of planet A, is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$.
Solution:
$\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{e}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{GM}}{\mathrm{R}}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{G} \times \rho \times \frac{4 \pi \mathrm{R}^{3}}{3}}{\mathrm{R}}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{e}} \propto \sqrt{\rho} \times \mathrm{R}$
$\frac{\left(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{e}}\right)_{\mathrm{B}}}{\left(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{e}}\right)_{\mathrm{A}}}=\sqrt{\frac{\rho_{\mathrm{B}}}{\rho_{\mathrm{A}}}} \times \frac{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{A}}}=\sqrt{\frac{0.1 \rho_{\mathrm{A}}}{\rho_{\mathrm{A}}}} \times\left(\frac{0.1 \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{A}}}\right)$
$\frac{\left(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{e}}\right)_{\mathrm{B}}}{\left(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{e}}\right)_{\mathrm{A}}}=\frac{1}{10} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{10}}$
$\left(V_{e}\right)_{B}=\frac{10 \times 1000}{10 \sqrt{10}}=100 \sqrt{10} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}$

Question: A parallel beam of light travelling in air (refractive index 1.0) is incident on a convex spherical glass surface of radius of curvature 50 cm . Refractive index of glass is 1.5 . The rays converge to a point at a distance x cm from the centre of the curvature of the spherical surface. The value of x is $\_\_\_\_$ cm .
Solution:
$\frac{\mu_{2}}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{\mu_{1}}{\mathrm{u}}=\frac{\mu_{2}-\mu_{1}}{\mathrm{R}} \Rightarrow \frac{1.5}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{1}{\infty}=\frac{1.5-1}{50}$
$\mathrm{V}=150 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\mathrm{x} \rightarrow$ measure from center
$\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{V}-\mathrm{R}$
$=150-50=100 \mathrm{~cm}$

Question: A meter bridge with two resistances $\mathrm{R}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{R}_{2}$ as shown in figure was balanced (null point) at 40 cm from the point P . The null point changed to 50 cm from the point P , when $16 \Omega$ resistance is connected in parallel to $R_{2}$. The values of resistances $R_{1}$ and $\mathrm{R}_{2}$ are $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$$
\begin{equation*}
\frac{\mathrm{R}_{1}}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}=\frac{40}{60}=\frac{2}{3} \tag{1}
\end{equation*}
$$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\frac{\mathrm{R}_{1}}{\left(\frac{\mathrm{R}_{2} \times 16}{\mathrm{R}_{2}+16}\right)}=\frac{50}{50} \Rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{1}=\frac{16 \mathrm{R}_{2}}{16+\mathrm{R}_{2}} \tag{2}
\end{equation*}
$$
$\frac{2}{3} \mathrm{R}_{2}=\frac{16 \mathrm{R}_{2}}{16+\mathrm{R}_{2}}$
$\frac{32}{3}+\frac{2 \mathrm{R}_{2}}{3}=16$
$$
\frac{2 \mathrm{R}_{2}}{3}=16-\frac{32}{3}=\frac{16}{3}
$$
$\mathrm{R}_{2}=8 \Omega$
By equation (1)
$$
\mathrm{R}_{1}=\frac{2}{3} \mathrm{R}_{2}=\frac{16}{3} \Omega
$$

Question:
The minimum frequency of photon required to break a particle of mass 15.348 amu into $4 \alpha$ particles is $\_\_\_\_$ kHz .
[mass of He nucleus = 4.002 amu,
$1 \mathrm{amu}=1.66 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}, \mathrm{~h}=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J} . \mathrm{s}$ and $\mathrm{c}= 3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ ]
Solution: $h v=(4 \times 4.002-15.348) \times 1.66 \times 10^{-27} \times\left(3 \times 10^{8}\right)^{2} v=14.94 \times 10^{19} \mathrm{kHz}$

Question: A cylindrical tube $A B$ of length $l$, closed at both ends contains an ideal gas of 1 mol having molecular weight $M$. The tube is rotated in a horizontal plane with constant angular velocity $\omega$ about an axis perpendicular to $A B$ and passing through the edge at end A , as shown in the figure. If $P_{A}$ and $P_{B}$ are the pressures at $A$ and $B$ respectively, then (Consider the temperature is same at all points in the tube)
Solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& P \rightarrow \square<-(P+d P) \\
& A[(P+d P)-P]=(d m)\left(\omega^{2} x\right) \\
& d P=\frac{(d m)}{A} \omega^{2} x \\
& d P=\frac{(\rho)(A)(d x) \omega^{2} x}{A} \\
& \text { also }[P M=\rho R T] \\
& \rho=\frac{P M}{R T} \\
& d P=\left(\frac{P M}{R T}\right) \omega^{2} x d x \\
& \int_{P_{A}}^{P_{B}} \frac{d P}{P}=\frac{\omega^{2} M}{R T} \int_{0}^{\ell} x d x \\
& \ell n\left(\frac{P_{B}}{P_{A}}\right)=\frac{\omega^{2} \ell^{2} M}{2 R T} \\
& P_{B}=P_{A} e^{\frac{M \omega^{2} \ell^{2}}{2 R T}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Question: The volume of an ideal gas increases 8 times and temperature becomes $(1 / 4)^{\text {th }}$ of initial temperature during a reversible change. If there is no exchange of heat in this process ( $\Delta \mathrm{Q}=0$ ) then identify the gas from the following options (Assuming the gases given in the options are ideal gases) :
Solution:
$\quad \mathrm{PV}^{\gamma}=$ constant
$\mathrm{TV}^{\gamma-1}=$ constant
$\mathrm{TV}^{\gamma-1}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{T}}{4}\right)(8 \mathrm{~V})^{(\gamma-1)}$
$4=8^{(\gamma-1)}$
$2^{2}=2^{3 \gamma-3}$
$2=3(\gamma-1)$
$\gamma=\frac{5}{3}$
Gas is a monoatomic gas
Answer is He.

Question: Electric field in a region is given by $\vec{E}=A x \hat{i}+B y \hat{j}$, where $A=10 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$ and $B=5 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$. If the electric potential at a point $(10,20)$ is 500 V , then the electric potential at origin is $\_\_\_\_$ V.
Solution:
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=10 x \hat{\mathrm{i}}+5 y \hat{\mathrm{j}}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{at}(10,20)}=500 \mathrm{~V} \\
& \Delta \mathrm{~V}-\int \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \mathrm{~d} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}
\end{aligned}
$$
$500-V_{0}=-\int_{(0,0)}^{(10,20)}(10 x \hat{i}+5 y \hat{j}) \cdot(d x \hat{i}+d y \hat{j})$
$500-V_{0}=-\left[5 x^{2}+\frac{5 y^{2}}{2}\right]_{(0,0)}^{(10,20)}$
$\mathrm{V}_{0}-500=\left(500+5 \times \frac{400}{2}\right)-(0-0)$
$\mathrm{V}_{0}-500=500+1000$
$\mathrm{V}_{0}=2000 \mathrm{~V}$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Relation between electric field and electric potential

Question:
A simple pendulum has a bob with mass $m$ and charge q . The pendulum string has negligible mass. When a uniform and horizontal electric field $\vec{E}$ is applied, the tension in the string changes. The final tension in the string, when pendulum attains an equilibrium position is $\_\_\_\_$ .
(g : acceleration due to gravity)
Solution: $\mathrm{T}=\sqrt{(\mathrm{qE})^{2}+(\mathrm{mg})^{2}}$

Question: A solid sphere of mass 5 kg and radius 10 cm is kept in contact with another solid sphere of mass 10 kg and radius 20 cm . The moment of inertia of this pair of spheres about the tangent passing through the point of contact is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{kg} . \mathrm{m}^{2}$.
Solution:
$I=\frac{7}{5}\left[m_{1} R_{1}^{2}+m_{2} R_{2}^{2}\right]$
$=\frac{7}{5}\left[5(10)^{2}+10 \times(20)^{2}\right] \times 10^{-4}$
$\mathrm{I}=63 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{2}$
$\mathrm{I}=0.63 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{2}$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Moment of inertia of truncated bodies/combined bodies

Question:
$7.9 \mathrm{MeV} \alpha$-particle scatters from a target material of atomic number 79. From the given data the estimated diameter of nuclei of the target material is (approximately) $\_\_\_\_$ m.
$\left[\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{\mathrm{o}}}=9 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{Nm}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}\right.$ and electron charge $\left.=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right]$
Solution:
By mechanical energy conservation

$(\mathrm{Me})_{\mathrm{i}}=(\mathrm{Me})_{\mathrm{f}}$
$\mathrm{PE}_{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{KE}_{\mathrm{i}}=\mathrm{PE}_{\mathrm{f}}+\mathrm{KE}_{\mathrm{f}}$
$0+7.9 \times 10^{6} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}=\frac{\mathrm{k}(2 \mathrm{e})(\mathrm{Ze})}{\mathrm{r}}+0$
$\mathrm{r}=\frac{9 \times 10^{9} \times 2 \times\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^{2} \times 79}{7.9 \times 10^{6} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}=2.88 \times 10^{-14} \mathrm{~m}$
For diameter ⇒ D = $2 \mathrm{r}=5.76 \times 10^{-14} \mathrm{~m}$

Question: Rods $x$ and $y$ of equal dimensions but of different materials are joined as shown in figure. Temperatures of end points $A$ and $F$ are maintained at $100{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $40{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ respectively. Given the thermal conductivity of rod x is three times of that of rod y , the temperature at junction points $B$ and $E$ are (close to) :
Solution:
Let $\left[\mathrm{R}=\frac{\ell}{3 \mathrm{KA}}\right]$

$$
\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{A}}=100 \bullet \stackrel{11 \mathrm{R} / 2}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{F}}=40
$$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\left[\mathrm{H}=\frac{100-40}{\frac{11 \mathrm{R}}{2}}\right] \ldots \tag{1}
\end{equation*}
$$
$$
\begin{equation*}
H=\frac{100-T_{B}}{R} \ldots \ldots \tag{2}
\end{equation*}
$$
$$
\begin{equation*}
H=\frac{T_{E}-40}{3 R} \ldots . \tag{3}
\end{equation*}
$$
using (1) and (2)
$120=1100-11 \mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{A}}$
$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{B}}=89^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
using (1) and (3)
$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{E}}=73^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

Question: A thin convex lens of focal length 5 cm and a thin concave lens of focal length 4 cm are combined together (without any gap) and this combination has magnification $\mathrm{m}_{1}$ when an object is placed 10 cm before the convex lens. Keeping the positions of convex lens and object undisturbed a gap of 1 cm is introduced between the lenses by moving the concave lens away, which lead to a change in magnification of total lens system to $\mathrm{m}_{2}$. The value of $\left|\frac{m_{1}}{m_{2}}\right|$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .

Consider an equilateral prism (refractive index $\sqrt{2}$ ). A ray of light is incident on its one surface at a certain angle $i$. If the emergent ray is found to graze along the other surface then the angle of refraction at the incident surface is close to $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
Equilateral prism.
$\mathrm{A}=60^{\circ}$

$\mu \operatorname{Sinr}_{2}=1 . \operatorname{sine}=1$
$\operatorname{Sinr}_{2}=\frac{1}{\mu}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$
$\mathrm{r}_{2}=45^{\circ}$
$\therefore \mathrm{r}_{1}=\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{r}_{2}=15^{\circ}$

Question: A 'p'-block element (E) and hydrogen form a binary cation $\left(\mathrm{EH}_{\mathrm{x}}\right)^{+}$, while $\mathrm{EH}_{3}$ on treatment with $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{HgI}_{4}$ in alkaline medium gives a precipitate of basic mercury(II)amido-iodine. Given below are first ionisation enthalpy values ( $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ ) for first element each from group 13, 14, 15 and 16. Identify the correct first ionisation enthalpy value for element E .
Solution: Element E is N , the species is $\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}$, among $\mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$, N and $\mathrm{O}, \mathrm{N}$ has highest first ionization energy.

Question:
Consider a solution of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ dissolved in water in a closed container.
Which one of the following plots correctly represents variation of $\log$ (partial pressure of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ in vapour phase above water) [y-axis] with log (mole fraction of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ in water) [ x -axis] at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ?
Solution:
From Henry's law :
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{~g})=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{H}} \cdot \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{~g}) \\
& \operatorname{logP}(\mathrm{g})=\log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{H}}+\log \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{~g})
\end{aligned}

Question: A first row transition metal (M) does not liberate $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ gas from dilute HCl .1 mol of aqueous solution of $\mathrm{MSO}_{4}$ is treated with excess of aqueous KCN and then $\mathrm{H}_{2}S(\mathrm{~g})$ is passed through the solution. $\mathrm{Cu} \xrightarrow{\text { dil. } \mathrm{HCl}}$ no reaction

Question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I: Benzene is nitrated to give nitrobenzene, which on further treatment with
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCl} / \mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ will give
Statement II: $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ group is a $m$-directing, and deactivating group.

Both Statement I and Statement II are correct.
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct.
Both Statement I and Statement II is are incorrect.
Solution: Nitrobenzene does not give Fridel-Craft acylation since it is highly deactivated ring.

Question:
A → products (First order reaction).
Three sets of experiment were performed for a reaction under similar experimental conditions.
Run $1 \Rightarrow 100 \mathrm{~mL}$ of 10 M solution of reactant A
Run $2 \Rightarrow 200 \mathrm{~mL}$ of 10 M solution of reactant A
Run $3 \Rightarrow 100 \mathrm{~mL}$ of 10 M solution of reactant $\mathrm{A}+$ 100 mL of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ added.
The correct variation of rate of reaction is

Question: The correct order of reactivity of $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{Br}$ in methanol with the following nucleophiles is $\mathrm{F}^{-}, \mathrm{I}^{-}, \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{O}^{-}$and $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{O}^{-}$
Solution:
Order of nucleophilicity :
$\mathrm{I}^{\ominus}>\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{O}^{\ominus}>\mathrm{PhO}^{\ominus}>\mathrm{F}^{\ominus}$

Question:
Match the LIST-I with LIST-II
List-I (Thermodynamic Process)List-II (Magnitude in kJ)
A. Work done in reversible, isothermal expansion of 2 mol of ideal gas from 2 dm3 to 20 dm3 at 300 K.I. 4
B. Work done in irreversible isothermal expansion of 1 mol ideal gas from 1 m3 to 3 m3 at 300 K against A constant pressure of 3 kPa.II. 11.5
C. Change in internal energy for adiabatic expansion of a 1 mol ideal gas with change of temperature $=320 \mathrm{~K}$ and $\overline{\mathrm{C}}_{\mathrm{V}}=\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{R}$. III. 6
D. Change in enthalpy at constant pressure of 1 mole ideal gas with change of temperature $=$ 337 K and $\overline{\mathrm{C}}_{\mathrm{p}}=\frac{5}{2} \mathrm{R}$.IV. 7

Choose the correct answer from the option given below :
Solution:
Option A)
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{W}=-\mathrm{nRT} \ln \frac{\mathrm{~V}_{2}}{\mathrm{~V}_{1}} \\
& =\frac{-2 \times 8.314 \times 300}{1000} \times \ln \left(\frac{20}{2}\right) \mathrm{kJ} \\
& =-11.5 \mathrm{~kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$
Option B)
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{W}=-\mathrm{P}_{\text {ext }}\left[\mathrm{V}_{2}-\mathrm{V}_{1}\right] \\
& =-3[3-1] \\
& =-6 \mathrm{~kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$
Option C)
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{nC}_{\mathrm{v}} \Delta \mathrm{~T} \\
& =1 \times \frac{3}{2} \times \frac{8.314 \times 320}{1000} 100 \mathrm{~kJ} \\
& =3.99
\end{aligned}
$$
Option D)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta \mathrm{H} & =\mathrm{nC}_{\mathrm{p}} \Delta \mathrm{~T} \\
& =1 \times \frac{5}{2} \times \frac{8.314 \times 337}{1000} \mathrm{~kJ} \\
& =7 \mathrm{~kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

Question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: Sucrose is dextrorotatory. However sucrose upon hydrolysis gives a solution having mixture of products. This solution shows laevorotation.
Statement II: Hydrolysis of sucrose gives glucose and fructose. Since the laevorotation of glucose is more than the dextrorotation of fructose the resulting solution becomes laevorotatory.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
Options: Statement I is false but Statement II is true.
Both Statement I and Statement II are false.
Both Statement I and Statement II are true.
Statement I is true but Statement II is false.
Solution:
$\mathrm{C}_{12} \mathrm{H}_{22} \mathrm{O}_{11}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{HCl}} \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}+\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}$
D- Glucose + D fructose
$[\alpha]_{\text {D-sucrose }}=+66.5^{\circ},[\alpha]_{\text {D-Glucose }}=+52.5^{\circ}$,
$[\alpha]_{\text {D-Fructose }}=-92.4^{\circ}$
⇒ Sucrose is dextrorotatory and hydrolysed product is laevorotatory.

Question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I: The halogen that makes longest bond with hydrogen in HX, has the smallest covalent radius in its group.
Statement II: A group 15 element's hydride $\mathrm{EH}_{3}$ has the lowest boiling point among corresponding hydrides of other group 15 elements. The maximum covalency of that element E is 4 .
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
Options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true.
Statement I is false but Statement II is true.
Both Statement I and Statement II are false.
Statement I is true but Statement II is false.
Solution:
$\mathrm{HF}<\mathrm{HCl}<\mathrm{HBr}<\mathrm{HI}$ (bond length order)
$\mathrm{F}<\mathrm{Cl}<\mathrm{Br}<\mathrm{I}$ (radius order)
$\mathrm{PH}_{3}<\mathrm{AsH}_{3}<\mathrm{NH}_{3}<\mathrm{SbH}_{3}<\mathrm{BiH}_{3}$ (Boiling point order)
Maximum possible covalency of phosphorous is 6

Question: 'A' is a neutral organic compound (M. F : $\mathrm{C}_{8} \mathrm{H}_{9} \mathrm{ON}$ ). On treatment with aqueous $\mathrm{Br}_{2} / \mathrm{OH}^{(-)}$, 'A' forms a compound ' B ' which is soluble in dilute acid. ' B ' on treatment with aqueous $\mathrm{NaNO}_{2} / \mathrm{HCl}\left(0-5^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ produces a compound ' C ' which on treatment with $\mathrm{CuCN} / \mathrm{NaCN}$ produces 'D' Hydrolysis of 'D' produces ' $E$ ' which is also obtainable from the hydrolysis of 'A'. 'E' on treatment with acidified $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ produces 'F'. 'F' contains two different types of hydrogen atoms. The structure of ' A ' is

The correct order of the rate of reaction of the following reactants with nucleophile by $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$ mechanism is :
(Given : Structure I and II are rigid)
Solution:
Rate of $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1 \propto$ Stability of $\mathrm{C}^{\oplus}$ formed
(I) and (II) are unstable due to Bredt's rule, (I) has more +I effect.
(II) < (I) < (III) < (IV)

Solution:
$\mathrm{XF}_{3}=\mathrm{BF}_{3} ; \mathrm{sp}^{2}$

Question: Consider the transition metal ions $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}, \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}, \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{Co}^{3+}$ and all form low spin octahedral complexes. The correct decreasing order of unpaired electrons in their respective d-orbitals of the complexes is
Solution:
$\quad \mathrm{Co}^{3+} \longrightarrow 3 \mathrm{~d}^{6} \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}^{2,2,2} \mathrm{e}_{\mathrm{g}}^{0,0}$, unpaired electron $=0$
$\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} \rightarrow 3 \mathrm{~d}^{5} \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}^{2,2,1} \mathrm{e}_{\mathrm{g}}^{0,0}$ unpaired electron $=1$
$\mathrm{Cr}^{3+} \rightarrow 3 \mathrm{~d}^{3} \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}^{1,1,1} \mathrm{e}_{\mathrm{g}}^{0,0}$ unpaired electron $=3$
$\mathrm{Mn}^{3+} \rightarrow 3 \mathrm{~d}^{4} \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}^{2,1,1} \mathrm{e}_{\mathrm{g}}^{0,0}$ unpaired electron $=2$

Question:
As compared with chlorocyclohexane, which of the following statements correctly apply to chlorobenzene?
A. The magnitude of negative charge is more on chlorine atoms
B. The $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl}$ bond has partial double bond character
C. $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl}$ bond is less polar
D. $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl}$ bond is longer due to repulsion between delocalised electrons of the aromatic ring and lone pairs of electrons of chlorine.
E. The $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl}$ bond is formed using $\mathrm{sp}^{2}$ hybridised orbital of carbon.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
Solution:
Chlorocylohexane is more polar due to -I effect of -Cl,
Whereas chlorobenzene has $-\mathrm{I}>+\mathrm{M}$, so it is less polar & also has partial double bond character.

Question:
Sodium fusion extract of an organic compound (Y) with $\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}$ and chlorine water gives violet color to the $\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}$ layer. 0.15 g of $(\mathrm{Y})$ gave 0.12 g of the silver halide precipitate in Carius method. Percentage of halogen in the compound $(\mathrm{Y})$ is _______. (Nearest integer).
(Given : molar mass $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{C}: 12, \mathrm{H}: 1, \mathrm{Cl}: 35.5$, Br: 80, I : 127)
Solution:
Iodine gives violet colour
$\%$ of $\mathrm{I}=\frac{\text { Atomic weight of } \mathrm{I}}{\text { Molecular weight of } \mathrm{AgI}} \times \frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{W}} \times 100$
$=\frac{127}{235} \times \frac{0.12}{0.15} \times 100$
% of $\mathrm{I}=43.23 \% \approx 43 \%$

Question:
Dissociation of a gas $\mathrm{A}_{2}$ takes place according to the following chemical reactions. At equilibrium, the total pressure is 1 bar at 300 K .
$\mathrm{A}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{~A}(\mathrm{~g})$
The standard Gibbs energy of formation of the involved substances has been provided below:
Substance$\Delta \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{t}}^{\circ} / \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$
$\mathrm{~A}_{2}$–100.00
A–50.832

The degree of dissociation of $\mathrm{A}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ is given by $\left(\mathrm{x} \times 10^{-2}\right)^{1 / 2}$ where $\mathrm{x}=$ $\_\_\_\_$ .
(Nearest integer).
[Given : $\mathrm{R}=8 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}, \log 2$ = 0.3010, $\log 3=0.48]$
Solution:
$-1.664 \times 10^{3}=-8.3 \times 300 \ln \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{p}}$
$\operatorname{lnK}_{\mathrm{P}}=0.693$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=2$
$2=\frac{4 \alpha^{2} \mathrm{P}_{0}}{1-\alpha^{2}}$
$\alpha=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$
$\alpha=\left(\frac{100}{3} \times 10^{-2}\right)^{1 / 2}$
$=\left(33.33 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{1 / 2}$

Question:
Consider the following electrochemical cell at 298 K
$\mathrm{Pt}\left|\mathrm{HSnO}_{2}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})\right| \mathrm{Sn}(\mathrm{OH})_{6}{ }^{2-}(\mathrm{aq})\left|\mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})\right| \mathrm{Bi}(\mathrm{s})$.
If the reaction quotient at a given time is $10^{6}$, then the cell EMF ( $\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}$ ) is $\_\_\_\_$ $\times 10^{-1} \mathrm{~V}$ (Nearest integer).
Given the standard half-cell reduction potential as
$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} / \mathrm{Bi}{, \mathrm{OH}^{-}}}^{0}=-0.44 \mathrm{~V}$ and
$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Sn}(\mathrm{OH})_{6}^{2-} / \mathrm{HSnO}_{2}^{-}, \mathrm{OH}}^{0}=-0.90 \mathrm{~V}$
Solution:
$\quad \mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=-0.44-(-0.90)$
$$
=+0.46 \mathrm{~V}
$$
Applying Nernst equation :-
$\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.06}{\mathrm{n}} \log \mathrm{Q}$
$\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=0.46-\frac{0.06}{6} \log 10^{6}$
$\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=4 \times 10^{-1}$
$\mathrm{x}=4$

Question:
The temperature at which the rate constants of the given below two gaseous reactions become equal is _________ K. (Nearest integer).
$\mathrm{X} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Y} \quad \mathrm{k}_{1}=10^{6} \mathrm{e}^{\frac{-30000}{\mathrm{~T}}}$
$\mathrm{P} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Q} \quad \mathrm{k}_{2}=10^{4} \mathrm{e}^{\frac{-24000}{\mathrm{~T}}}$
Given : $\ln 10=2.303$
Solution:
$10^{4} \mathrm{e}^{\frac{-24000}{\mathrm{~T}}}=10^{6} \mathrm{e}^{\frac{-30000}{\mathrm{~T}}}$
$\mathrm{e}^{\frac{6000}{\mathrm{~T}}}=100$
$\frac{6000}{T}=2 \ln 10$
$\mathrm{T}=\frac{6000}{2 \times 2.303}$
$\mathrm{T}=1302.64 \mathrm{~K}$
$\mathrm{T} \approx 1303 \mathrm{~K}$

Question:
The cycloalkane ( X ) on bromination consumes one mole of bromine per mole of ( X ) and gives the product ( Y ) in which $\mathrm{C}: \mathrm{Br}$ ratio is $3: 1$. The percentage of bromine in the product $(\mathrm{Y})$ is________. (Nearest integer)
(Given : Molar mass in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{H}: 1, \mathrm{C}: 12$, $\mathrm{O}: 16, \mathrm{Br}: 80$ )
Solution:
$\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{10} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{Br}_{2}} \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{10} \mathrm{Br}_{2}$
Molecular mass of $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{10} \mathrm{Br}_{2}$ is :
$12 \times 6+10+160$
$72+10+160=242$
% of $\mathrm{Br}=\frac{160}{242} \times 100$
% of $\mathrm{Br}=66.11 \% \approx 66 \%$

Question:
The correct increasing order of spin-only magnetic moment values of the complex ions
$\left[\mathrm{MnBr}_{4}\right]^{2-}(\mathrm{A}),\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right]^{2+}(\mathrm{B}),\left[\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{CN}\right)_{4}\right]^{2-}(\mathrm{C})$ and $\left[\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right]^{2+}(\mathrm{D})$ is:

Question:
For the given reaction;
$\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}+2 \mathrm{HCl} \rightarrow \mathrm{CaCl}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
If $90 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ is added to 300 mL of HCl which contains $38.55 \% \mathrm{HCl}$ by mass and has density $1.13 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}$, then which of the following option is correct?
Given molar mass of $\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{Cl}, \mathrm{Ca}$ and O are 1, 35.5, 40 and $16 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively.
Solution:
Density of HCl solution (d) $=1.13 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{ml}$
$\mathrm{V}=300 \mathrm{ml}$
Wt. of HCl solution $=339 \mathrm{~g}$
Wt. of $\mathrm{HCl}=339 \times \frac{38.55}{100}=130.68 \mathrm{~g}$
(LR)
$\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}+2 \mathrm{HCl} \rightarrow \mathrm{CaCl}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
$\frac{90}{100} \quad \frac{130.68}{36.5}$
$=0.90 \mathrm{~mole}=3.58 \mathrm{~mole}$
Moles of HCl remained $=1.78$ mole.
Mass of HCl remained $=64.97 \mathrm{~g}$.

Question:
Consider the following reactions.
$\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~B}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{7} \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \mathrm{X}+\mathrm{Y}$
$\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}+\mathrm{Y} \xrightarrow{\text { Non-Luminous flame }} \mathrm{Z}+\mathrm{SO}_{3}$
$2 \mathrm{Z}+2 \mathrm{X}+$ Carbon $\xrightarrow{\text { Luminous flame }} 2 \mathrm{Q}+\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~B}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{7}+\mathrm{CO}$
The oxidation states of Cu in Z and Q , respectively are :
Solution:
\[{\text{N}}{{\text{a}}_2}{{\text{B}}_4}{{\text{O}}_7}\xrightarrow{\Delta }\mathop {\left( {2{\text{NaB}}{{\text{O}}_2}} \right)}\limits_{\left( {\text{X}} \right)} + {{\text{B}}_2}{{\text{O}}_3}\]
\[{\text{CuS}}{{\text{O}}_4} + {{\text{B}}_2}{{\text{O}}_3}\xrightarrow[{{\text{ Non - Luminous flame }}}]{\Delta }\mathop {\mathop {{\text{Cu}}}\limits^{ + 2} {{\left( {{\text{B}}{{\text{O}}_2}} \right)}_2}}\limits_{\left( {{\text{Blue bead}}} \right)\left( Z \right)} + {\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_3}\]
\[2{\text{Cu}}{\left( {{\text{B}}{{\text{O}}_2}} \right)_2} + 2{\text{NaB}}{{\text{O}}_2} + {\text{C}}\xrightarrow[{{\text{ Luminous Flame }}}]{\Delta }\]
\[\mathop {2\mathop {{\text{Cu}}}\limits^{ + 1} {\text{B}}{{\text{O}}_2}}\limits_{{\text{Colorless}}\left( Q \right)} + {\text{N}}{{\text{a}}_2}{{\text{B}}_4}{{\text{O}}_7} + {\text{CO}}\]

Question:
A volume of x mL of $5 \mathrm{M}\ \mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}$ solution was mixed with 10 mL of $2 \mathrm{M} \ \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$ solution to make an electrolytic buffer. If the same buffer was used in the following electrochemical cell to record a cell potential of 235.3 mV , then the value of $\mathrm{x}=$ ________ mL (nearest integer).
\[{\text{Sn}}({\text{s}})\left| {{\text{Sn}}({\text{OH}})_6^{2 - }(0.5{\text{M}})} \right|\]\[{\text{HSnO}}_2^ - (0.05{\text{M}})\mid \]\[{\text{O}}{{\text{H}}^ - }\left| {{\text{B}}{{\text{i}}_2}{{\text{O}}_3}(\;{\text{s}})} \right|\operatorname{Bi} ({\text{s}})\]
Consider upto one place of decimal for intermediate calculations
[Given: \[{\text{E}}_{{\text{HSnO}}_2^ - \mid {\text{Sn}}({\text{OH}})_6^{2 - }}^{\text{o}} = - 0.9\;{\text{V}}\], \[{\text{E}}_{{\text{B}}{{\text{i}}_2}{{\text{O}}_3}\mid {\text{Bi}}}^{\text{o}} = - 0.44\;{\text{V}}\], \[{\text{pK}}{{\text{a}}_{\left( {{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_3}} \right)}} = 6.11\], \[\frac{{2.303{\text{RT}}}}{{\text{F}}} = 0.059\;{\text{V}}\], \[{\text{Anti }}\log (1.29) = 19.5\]]
Solution:
We have considered
$$
\begin{gathered}
\mathrm{E}_{\left[\mathrm{Sn}(\mathrm{OH})_{6}\right]^{2-} / \mathrm{HSnO}_{2}^{-}}^{\mathrm{o}}=-0.9 \mathrm{~V} \\
\mathrm{Pt}\left|\mathrm{HSnO}_{2}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}),\left[\mathrm{Sn}(\mathrm{OH})_{6}\right]^{2-}(\mathrm{aq}), \mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})\right| \mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})|\mathrm{Bi}(\mathrm{~s})| \\
0.5 \mathrm{M} \quad 0.05 \mathrm{M} \\
\mathrm{E}_{{ }_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}}^{\circ}=+0.9-0.44=0.46 \mathrm{~V}
\end{gathered}
$$

Oxidation Half :
$\mathrm{HSnO}_{2}^{-}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+3 \mathrm{OH}^{-} \rightarrow\left[\mathrm{Sn}(\mathrm{OH})_{6}\right]^{2-}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$
Reduction Half :
$\mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+6 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Bi}+6 \mathrm{OH}^{-}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \begin{array}{l}
3 \mathrm{HSnO}_{2}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~s})+6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+3 \mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}) \\
\rightarrow 3\left[\mathrm{Sn}(\mathrm{OH})_{6}\right]^{2-}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{Bi}(\mathrm{~s})
\end{array} \\
& \mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\mathrm{o}}-\frac{0.059}{6} \log \frac{(0.5)^{3}}{(0.05)^{3} \times\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]^{3}} \\
& 0.2353=0.46-\frac{0.059}{6} \times 3 \log \left[\frac{10}{\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}\right] \\
& \log \left[\frac{10}{\mathrm{OH}^{-}}\right]=\frac{2 \times 0.2247}{0.059}=7.6 \\
& 1+\mathrm{pOH}=7.6 \\
& \mathrm{pOH}=6.6 \\
& \mathrm{pH}=14-6.6=7.4 \\
& \mathrm{pH}=\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}_{1}}+\log \frac{\left[\mathrm{HCO}_{3}^{-}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}\right]} \\
& 7.4=6.11+\log \frac{5 \mathrm{x}}{20} \\
& 1.29=\log \frac{\mathrm{x}}{4} \\
& \frac{\mathrm{x}}{4}=19.5 \\
& \mathrm{x}=78
\end{aligned}
$$

Note : In question paper, $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{HSnO}_{2}^{-} /\left[\mathrm{Sn}(\mathrm{OH})_{6}\right]^{2-}}^{\mathrm{o}}=-0.9 \mathrm{~V}$
data is given, but NTA has given answer by considering $\mathrm{E}_{\left[\mathrm{Sn}(\mathrm{OH})_{6}\right]^{2-} / \mathrm{HSnO}_{2}^{-}}^{\mathrm{o}}=-0.9 \mathrm{~V}$ therefore this question should be BONUS.

Question:
Consider the following statements about manganate and permanganate ions. Identify the correct statements :
A. The geometry of both manganate and permanganate ions is tetrahedral.
B. The oxidation states of Mn in manganate and permanganate are +7 and +6 , respectively.
C. Oxidation of Mn (II) salt by peroxodisulphate gives manganate ion as the final product.
D. Manganate ion is paramagnetic and permanganate ions is diamagnetic.
E. Acidified permanganate ion reduces oxalate, nitrite and iodide ions.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
Solution:
Manganate ion $\rightarrow \mathrm{MnO}_{4}{ }^{-2}$
Permanganate ion $\rightarrow \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}$
(A) Both are tetrahedral ( $\mathrm{d}^{3} \mathrm{~s}$ Hybridisation)
(B) $\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}$( +7 oxidation state)
$\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-2}$ ( +6 oxidation state)
(C) $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}+\mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{8}^{2-} \rightarrow \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}$(Permanganate ion)
(D) $\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-} \rightarrow$ Diamagnetic
$\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-2} \rightarrow$ Paramagnetic
(E) It is oxidising agent

Question:
For strong electrolyte $\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}$ increases slowly with dilution and can be represented by the equation $\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}=\Lambda^{\circ}{ }_{\mathrm{m}}-\mathrm{Ac}^{1 / 2}$
Molar conductivity values of the solutions of strong electrolyte AB at $18^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ are given below :
$\mathrm{c}\left[\mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1}\right]$0.040.090.160.25
$\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}\left[\mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right]$96.195.795.394.9

The value of constant A based on the above data [ in $\mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} /(\mathrm{mol} / \mathrm{L})^{1 / 2}$ ] unit is ________.
Solution:
Using equation : $\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}=\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{0}-\mathrm{A} \sqrt{\mathrm{c}}$
$96.1=\Lambda^{0}{ }_{\mathrm{m}}-\mathrm{A} \sqrt{0.04}$
$$
\begin{equation*}
96.1=\Lambda^{0}{ }_{\mathrm{m}}-\mathrm{A} \times 0.2 \tag{1}
\end{equation*}
$$

$95.7=\Lambda^{0}{ }_{\mathrm{m}}-\mathrm{A} \times \sqrt{0.09}$

$$
\begin{equation*}
95.7=\Lambda^{0}{ }_{\mathrm{m}}-\mathrm{A} \times 0.3 \tag{2}
\end{equation*}
$$

From eq. (1) and eq. (2)
$\mathrm{A}=4$

(E)
And. → (3) C, D

Question:
$\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}$ (first reaction)
$\mathrm{C} \rightarrow \mathrm{D}$ (second reaction)
Consider the above two first-order reactions. The rate constant for first reaction at 500 K is double of the same at 300 K . At $500 \mathrm{~K}, 50 \%$ of the reaction becomes complete in 2 hour. The activation energy of the second reaction is half of that of first reaction. If the rate constant at 500 K of the second reaction becomes double of the rate constant of first reaction at the same temperature; then rate constant for the second reaction at 300 K is _______ $\times 10^{-1}$ hour $^{-1}$ (nearest integer).
Solution:
For $\mathrm{A} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{K}_{1}} \mathrm{~B}$
$\ln (2)=\frac{E_{a_{1}}}{R}\left[\frac{1}{300}-\frac{1}{500}\right]$
$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}_{1}}=\frac{\ln 2 \times \mathrm{R} \times 1500}{2}$
$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}_{1}}}{2}=\frac{\ln 2 \times \mathrm{R} \times 1500}{4}$
$\left(\mathrm{K}_{1}\right)_{\text {at } 500 \mathrm{~K}}=\frac{\ln 2}{2}$
$\left(\mathrm{K}_{2}\right)_{\text {at } 500 \mathrm{~K}}=\ln 2$
Now for $\mathrm{C} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{K}_{2}} \mathrm{D}$
$\ln \left[\frac{\left(\mathrm{K}_{2}\right)_{\text {at } 500 \mathrm{~K}}}{\left(\mathrm{~K}_{2}\right)_{\text {at } 300 \mathrm{~K}}}\right]=\left(\frac{\ln 2 \times \mathrm{R} \times 1500}{4}\right) \times \frac{1}{\mathrm{R}} \times\left[\frac{1}{300}-\frac{1}{500}\right]$
$\left(\mathrm{K}_{2}\right)_{\mathrm{at} 300 \mathrm{~K}}=\frac{\ln 2}{\sqrt{2}}=0.49$
$\left(\mathrm{K}_{2}\right)_{\mathrm{at} 300 \mathrm{~K}}=4.9 \times 10^{-1}$
Ans is 5.

Question: The plot of $\log _{10} \mathrm{~K}$ vs $\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}}$ gives a straight line. The intercept and slope respectively are (where K is equilibrium constant).
Solution:
$\log _{10} \mathrm{~K}=-\frac{\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\mathrm{o}}}{2.303 \mathrm{RT}}+\frac{\Delta \mathrm{S}^{\mathrm{o}}}{2.303 \mathrm{R}}$
y -intercept $=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{S}^{\circ}}{2.303 \mathrm{R}}$
Slope $=-\frac{\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\mathrm{o}}}{2.303 \mathrm{R}}$
Ans. (2) is correct.

Question:
Two positively charged particles $\mathrm{m}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{m}_{2}$ have been accelerated across the same potential difference of 200 keV as shown below.

[Given mass of $\mathrm{m}_{1}=1$ amu and $\mathrm{m}_{2}=4$ amu]
The deBroglie wavelength of $\mathrm{m}_{1}$ will be x times of $\mathrm{m}_{2}$. The value of x is _______. (nearest integer)
Solution:
$\quad \lambda_{\mathrm{d}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mK} . \mathrm{E} .}}$
Here KE is same i.e. 200 k eV
So $\lambda_{\mathrm{d}} \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{~m}}}$
$\frac{\left(\lambda_{\mathrm{d}}\right)_{\mathrm{m}_{1}}}{\left(\lambda_{\mathrm{d}}\right)_{\mathrm{m}_{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{m}_{2}}{\mathrm{~m}_{1}}}=\sqrt{4}=2$
$\left(\lambda_{\mathrm{d}}\right)_{\mathrm{m}_{1}}=2\left(\lambda_{\mathrm{d}}\right)_{\mathrm{m}_{2}}$
So $\mathrm{x}=2$.

Question:
Observe the following equilibrium in a 1 L flask.
$$
A(g) \rightleftharpoons B(g)
$$
At $\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K})$, the equilibrium concentrations of A and B are 0.5 M and 0.375 M respectively. 0.1 moles of A is added into the flask and heated to $\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K})$ to establish the equilibrium again. The new equilibrium concentrations (in M ) of A and B are respectively.
Solution:
$\mathrm{A} ⇌ \mathrm{B}$
$$
0.5 \mathrm{M} \quad 0.375 \mathrm{M} \quad \text { (At equilibrium) }
$$

$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{[\mathrm{B}]_{\mathrm{eq}}}{[\mathrm{A}]_{\mathrm{eq}}}=\frac{0.375}{0.5}=0.75$
Now 0.1 mole of A is added so reaction will move in forward direction.
$\mathrm{A} ⇌ \mathrm{B}$
$0.6-\mathrm{x} \quad 0.375+\mathrm{x}$
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}=0.75=\frac{0.375+\mathrm{x}}{0.6-\mathrm{x}}$
$0.45-0.75 \mathrm{x}=0.375+\mathrm{x}$
$1.75 \mathrm{x}=0.075$
$\mathrm{X}=\frac{0.075}{1.75}=\frac{3}{70}=0.043$
Moles of $\mathrm{A}=0.557$
Moles of $\mathrm{B}=0.418$
Ans. (4) is correct.

A. activates the ring towards electrophilic substitutions.
B. deactivates the ring towards electrophilic substitutions.
C. activates the ring towards nucleophilic substitutions.

Solution:
Presence of $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ group in Benzene ring deactivate ring towards electrophilic substitution reaction due to -M effective & activate ring towards nucleophilic substitution.

Question:
The correct order of acidic strength of the major products formed in the given reactions, is :

A. $\mathrm{PhNH}_{2} \xrightarrow[\substack{\text { (2) } \mathrm{CuCN} \\ \text { (3) } \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+} / \Delta}]{\text { (1) } \mathrm{NaNO}_{2}+\mathrm{HCl}\left(<5^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)}[\mathrm{A}]$

B. $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CHO} \xrightarrow[\Delta]{\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{+}, \mathrm{OH}^{-}}[\mathrm{B}]$

C. $\mathrm{CH}_{4}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \xrightarrow[\text { (ii) } \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7} / \mathrm{H}^{+}]{\text {(i) } \mathrm{Mo}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}}[\mathrm{C}]$

D. $\mathrm{PhCH}_{2} \mathrm{MgBr}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \xrightarrow[\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}]{\text {Dryether }}[\mathrm{D}]$

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
Solution:
Correct order of acidic strength of major product formed in the given reaction is
(A)
(B) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CHO} \xrightarrow[\Delta]{\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{OH}} \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{COOH}$
(C) $\mathrm{CH}_{4}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \xrightarrow[\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7} / \mathrm{H}^{+}]{\mathrm{MO}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}} \mathrm{HCOOH}$
(D) $\mathrm{Ph}-\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{MgBr}+\mathrm{CO}_{2} \xrightarrow[\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{\oplus}]{\text { Dryether }} \mathrm{Ph}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{COOH}$
Ans. (4) $\mathrm{C}>\mathrm{A}>\mathrm{D}>\mathrm{B}$

Question:
Consider the following aqueous solutions.
I. 2.2 g Glucose in 125 mL of solution.
II. 1.9 g Calcium chloride in 250 mL of solution.
III. 9.0 g Urea in 500 mL of solution.
IV. 20.5 g Aluminium sulphate in 750 mL of solution.
The correct increasing order of boiling point of these solutions will be:
[Given: Molar mass in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}: \mathrm{H}=1, \mathrm{C}=12, \mathrm{~N}=14$, $\mathrm{O}=16, \mathrm{Cl}=35.5, \mathrm{Ca}=40, \mathrm{Al}=27$ and $\mathrm{S}=32$ ]
Solution:
$\quad \Delta T_{b}=i \cdot k_{b} \cdot m$
For dilute solution ( $\mathrm{M}=\mathrm{m}$ )
Molarity$\mathbf{i} \boldsymbol{\times} \mathbf{m}$
(I) $\mathrm{M}_{\text {glucose }}=\frac{2.2}{180} \times \frac{1000}{125}=0.098$$0.098 \times 1$
(II) $\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}}=\frac{1.9}{111} \times \frac{1000}{250}=0.068$$0.068 \times 3$
(III) $\mathrm{M}_{\text {urea }}=\frac{9}{60} \times \frac{1000}{500}=0.3$$0.3 \times 1$
(IV) $\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{Al}_{2}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)_{2}}=\frac{20.5}{342} \times \frac{1000}{750} \simeq 0.08$$0.08 \times 5$

Order of $\Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=\mathrm{Al}_{2}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)_{3}>$ Urea $>\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}>$ Glucose
So order of $\mathrm{BP}=\mathrm{Al}_{2}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)_{3}>$ Urea $>\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}>$ Glucose
So Answer will be I < II < III < IV

Question:
Let $729,81,9,1, \ldots$ be a sequence and $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{n}}$ denote the product of the first $n$ terms of this sequence.
If $2 \sum_{n=1}^{40}\left(P_{n}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}}=\frac{3^{\alpha}-1}{3^{\beta}}$ and $\operatorname{gcd}(\alpha, \beta)=1$, then $\alpha+\beta$ is equal to
Solution:
$\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{n}}=729.81 .9 \ldots \ldots$. ( n terms)
$$
\begin{aligned}
& =3^{6} \cdot 3^{4} \cdot 3^{2} \ldots \ldots \cdot 3^{-2 n+8} \\
& P_{n}=3^{6+4+2+\ldots \ldots+(-2 n+8)}=3^{n(7-n)} \\
& P_{n}^{1 / n}=3^{7-n}
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \sum_{n=1}^{40}\left(P_{n}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}}=3^{6}+3^{5}+\ldots . .+(40 \text { terms }) \\
& =3^{6}\left[\frac{1-\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{40}}{1-\frac{1}{3}}\right] \\
& =\frac{3^{6}\left[3^{40}-1\right] \times 3^{1}}{3^{40} \times 2} \\
& \sum\left(P_{n}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}}=\frac{\left(3^{40}-1\right)}{2 \times 3^{33}}, \quad \alpha=40 \\
& \alpha+\beta=73
\end{aligned}
$$

Question: The number of the real solutions of the equation : $\mathrm{x}|\mathrm{x}+3|+|\mathrm{x}-1|-2=0$ is
Solution:
(I)
$$
\begin{aligned}
& x^{2}+3 x+x-1-2=0 \\
& x^{2}+4 x-3=0 \\
& x=-2+\sqrt{7} \text { (rejected), }-2-\sqrt{7} \text { (rejected) } \\
& \text { (II) }
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& x^{2}+3 x+1-x-2=0 \\
& x^{2}+2 x-1=0 \\
& x=-1+\sqrt{2},-1-\sqrt{2}
\end{aligned}
$$
(III)
$$
\begin{aligned}
& -x^{2}-3 x+1-x-2=0 \\
& x^{2}+4 x+1=0 \\
& x=-2-\sqrt{3},-2+\sqrt{3}(\text { rejected })
\end{aligned}
$$

Question: Let the lines $L_{1}: \vec{r}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k})$, $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$ and $L_{2}: \vec{r}=(4 \hat{i}+\hat{j})+\mu(5 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}), \mu \in \mathbb{R}$, intersect at the point R . Let P and Q be the points lying on lines $\mathrm{L}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{L}_{2}$, respectively, such that $|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PR}}|=\sqrt{29}$ and $|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PQ}}|=\sqrt{\frac{47}{3}}$. If the point P lies in the first octant, then $27(\mathrm{QR})^{2}$ is equal to
Solution:
For POI
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 2 \lambda+1=5 \mu+4 ; 3 \lambda+2=2 \mu+1 ; 4 \lambda+3=\mu \\
& \Rightarrow \lambda=\mu=-1 \\
& \mathrm{R}(-1,-1,-1) \quad \mathrm{P}(2 \lambda+1,3 \lambda+2,4 \lambda+3) \\
& \mathrm{PR}^{2}=29 \Rightarrow(2 \lambda+2)^{2}+(3 \lambda+3)^{2}+(4 \lambda+4)^{2}=29 \\
& \Rightarrow \lambda=0 \text { or } \lambda=-2 \text { (Reject) } \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{P}(1,2,3) \\
& \mathrm{Q}(5 \mu+4,2 \mu+1, \mu) \\
& |\mathrm{PQ}|=\sqrt{\frac{47}{3}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{PQ}^{2}=\frac{47}{3} \\
& \Rightarrow(5 \mu+3)^{2}+(2 \mu-1)^{2}+(\mu-3)^{2}=\frac{47}{3} \\
& \Rightarrow \mu=-\frac{1}{3} \\
& \mathrm{Q}=\left(\frac{7}{3}, \frac{1}{3},-\frac{1}{3}\right) \\
& (\mathrm{QR})^{2}=\left(\frac{7}{3}+1\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{1}{3}+1\right)^{2}+\left(-\frac{1}{3}+1\right)^{2} \\
& =\frac{100+16+4}{9}=\frac{120}{9} \\
& \Rightarrow 27 \times(\mathrm{QR})^{2}=27 \times \frac{120}{9}=360
\end{aligned}
$$

Question: From a lot containing 10 defective and 90 non-defective bulbs, 8 bulbs are selected one by one with replacement. Then the probability of getting at least 7 defective bulbs is :-
Solution:
10 defective & 90 non-defective
Req. probability $=(7$ def 1 fair $)$ or ( 8 defective)
Req. probability $=\frac{\left(10^{7} \times 90\right) \times 8+10^{8}}{100^{8}}$
$=\frac{72 \times 10^{8}+10^{8}}{100^{8}}=\frac{73}{10^{8}}$

Question:
Let $\alpha, \beta \in \mathbb{R}$ be such that the function
$f(\mathrm{x})= \begin{cases}2 \alpha\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}-2\right)+2 \beta \mathrm{x} & , \mathrm{x}<1 \\ (\alpha+3) \mathrm{x}+(\alpha-\beta) & , \mathrm{x} \geq 1\end{cases}$
be differentiable at all $\mathrm{x} \in \mathbb{R}$. Then $34(\alpha+\beta)$ is equal to
Solution:
$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}2 \alpha x^{2}+2 \beta x-4 \alpha ; & x<1 \\ (\alpha+3) x+\alpha-\beta ; & x \geq 1\end{array}\right.$
$\mathrm{f}\left(1^{\dagger}\right)=2 \alpha-\beta+3, \mathrm{f}\left(1^{-}\right)=-2 \alpha+2 \beta$
$$
\begin{equation*}
2 \alpha-\beta+3=2 \beta-2 \alpha \Rightarrow 4 \alpha-3 \beta+3=0 \tag{1}
\end{equation*}
$$
$\mathrm{f}^{\prime}\left(1^{+}\right)=4 \alpha+2 \beta, \mathrm{f}^{\prime}\left(1^{-}\right)=\alpha+3$
$$
\begin{equation*}
4 \alpha+2 \beta=\alpha+3 \Rightarrow 3 \alpha+2 \beta-3=0 \tag{2}
\end{equation*}
$$
Solving (1) & (2)
We get $\alpha=\frac{3}{17}, \beta=\frac{21}{17}$
$\Rightarrow 34(\alpha+\beta)=34 \times \frac{27}{17}=48$

Question: Let $\mathrm{A}_{1}$ be the bounded area enclosed by the curves $y=x^{2}+2, x+y=8$ and $y$-axis that lies in the first quadrant. Let $\mathrm{A}_{2}$ be the bounded area enclosed by the curves $y=x^{2}+2, y^{2}=x, x=2$, and $y$-axis that lies in the first quadrant. Then $\mathrm{A}_{1}-\mathrm{A}_{2}$ is equal to
Solution:
$A_{1}=\int_{0}^{2}\left((8-x)-\left(x^{2}+2\right)\right) d x$
$=A_{1}=\int_{0}^{2}\left(6-x-x^{2}\right) d x$
$\mathrm{A}_{1}\left(6 \mathrm{x}-\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{2}-\frac{\mathrm{x}^{3}}{3}\right)_{0}^{2}=12-2-\frac{8}{3}=10-\frac{8}{3}=\frac{22}{3}$

$\mathrm{A}_{2}=\int_{0}^{2}\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}+2\right) \mathrm{dx}-\frac{2}{3}(2 \sqrt{2})$
$\mathrm{A}_{2}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}^{3}}{3}+2 \mathrm{x}\right)_{0}^{2}-\frac{4 \sqrt{2}}{3}$
$\mathrm{A}_{2}=\frac{8}{3}+4-\frac{4 \sqrt{2}}{3}=\frac{20}{3}-\frac{4 \sqrt{2}}{3}$
$\mathrm{A}_{1}-\mathrm{A}_{2}=\frac{2}{3}+\frac{4 \sqrt{2}}{3}$

Question: Let a circle of radius 4 pass through the origin O , the points $\mathrm{A}(-\sqrt{3} \mathrm{a}, 0)$ and $\mathrm{B}(0,-\sqrt{2} \mathrm{~b})$, where a and b are real parameters and $\mathrm{ab} \neq 0$. Then the locus of the centroid of $\triangle \mathrm{OAB}$ is a circle of radius
Solution:
$\mathrm{AB}=8$
$3 \mathrm{a}^{2}+2 \mathrm{~b}^{2}=64$
Centroid $\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{h}, \mathrm{k})$
$h=-\frac{\sqrt{3} a}{3}, k=-\frac{\sqrt{2} b}{3}$
$\mathrm{a}=-\sqrt{3} \mathrm{~h}, \mathrm{~b}=\frac{-3}{\sqrt{2}} \mathrm{k}$
$9 h^{2}+9 k^{2}=64$
$\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}=\frac{64}{9}$
$\mathrm{r}=\frac{8}{3}$

Question: The mean and variance of a data of 10 observations are 10 and 2 , respectively. If an observations $\alpha$ in this data is replaced by $\beta$, then the mean and variance become 10.1 and 1.99, respectively. Then $\alpha+\beta$ equals.
Solution:
Let first 10 numbers are $\mathrm{x}_{1}, \mathrm{x}_{2}, \ldots \ldots . \mathrm{x}_{9}, \alpha$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \alpha+\sum_{\mathrm{i}=1}^{9} \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}=100 \Rightarrow \sum_{\mathrm{i}=1}^{9} \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}=100-\alpha \\
& \text { Variance }=\left(\frac{\sum \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}^{2}}{\mathrm{n}}\right)-\left(\frac{\sum \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}}{\mathrm{n}}\right)^{2} \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{\sum \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}^{2}}{\mathrm{n}}=98 \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}_{1}^{2}+\mathrm{x}_{2}^{2}+\ldots \ldots . \mathrm{x}_{9}^{2}+\alpha^{2}=1020 \Rightarrow \sum \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}^{2}=1020-\alpha^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

In second case, let number are
$$
\begin{aligned}
& x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots \ldots . x_{9}, \beta \\
& 100-\alpha+\beta=101 \alpha-\beta+1=0 \\
& \frac{\sum x_{i}^{2}+\beta^{2}}{10}-(10.1)^{2}=1.99 \\
& \beta^{2}-\alpha^{2}=20 \\
& \alpha=\frac{19}{2} \\
& \beta=\frac{21}{2} \\
& \alpha+\beta=\frac{19+21}{2}=20
\end{aligned}
$$

Question:
If the function $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$
$=\frac{e^{x}\left(e^{\tan x-x}-1\right)+\log _{e}(\sec x+\tan x)-x}{\tan x-x}$ is
Continuous at $\mathrm{x}=0$, then the value of $\mathrm{f}(0)$ is equal to
Solution:
$f(0)=\operatorname{Lim}_{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{e^{\tan x}-e^{x}+\ell n(\sec x+\tan x)-x}{\tan x-x}$
Applying L'hospital rule
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(0)=\operatorname{Lim}_{\mathrm{x} \rightarrow 0} \frac{\mathrm{e}^{\tan \mathrm{x}} \cdot \sec ^{2} \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}+\sec \mathrm{x}-1}{\sec ^{2} \mathrm{x}-1}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(0)=\operatorname{Lim}_{\mathrm{x} \rightarrow 0} \frac{\mathrm{e}^{\tan \mathrm{x}}\left(\sec ^{2} \mathrm{x}-1\right)+\left(\mathrm{e}^{\tan \mathrm{x}}-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}\right)+\sec \mathrm{x}-1}{\tan ^{2} \mathrm{x}}$
$\Rightarrow f(0)=\operatorname{Lim}_{x \rightarrow 0}\left(e^{\tan x}+\frac{e^{x}\left(e^{\tan x-x}-1\right)}{\tan ^{2} x}+\frac{1}{\sec x+1}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(0)=1+0+\frac{1}{2}=\frac{3}{2}$

Question: Let a line L passing through the point $\mathrm{P}(1,1,1)$ be perpendicular to the lines $\frac{x-4}{4}=\frac{y-1}{1}=\frac{z-1}{1}$ and $\frac{\mathrm{x}-17}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-71}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}}{0}$. Let the line L intersect the yz -plane at the point Q . Another line parallel to L and passing through the point $\mathrm{S}(1,0,-1)$ intersects the yz -plane at the point R . Then the square of the area of the parallelogram PQRS is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$\quad \mathrm{d}_{1}=\overline{<4,1,1>}$ and $\mathrm{d}_{2}=<1,1,0>$
$\mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{L}}=\mathrm{d}_{1} \times \mathrm{d}_{2}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\mathrm{i} & \mathrm{j} & \mathrm{k} \\ 4 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 0\end{array}\right|=\langle-1,1,3\rangle$
Line L passes through $\mathrm{P}<1,1,1>$ with $\mathrm{d}_{2}=<-1,1,3>$
$r(\mathrm{t})=<1,1,1>+\mathrm{t}<-1,1,3>$
$=<1-\mathrm{t}, 1+\mathrm{t}, 1+3 \mathrm{t}>$
$=<1-\mathrm{t}, 1+\mathrm{t}, 1+3 \mathrm{t}>$
For point $\mathrm{Q} ; \mathrm{x}=0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=1$
$\mathrm{Q}=<0,2,4>$
Another
Line parallel to L passes through $\mathrm{S}<1,0,-1>$
with $\mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{L}}=<-1,1,3>$
$\mathrm{r}^{\prime}(\mathrm{u})=<1,0,-1>+\mu<-1,1,3>$
$=<1-\mu, \mu,-1+3 \mu>$
for point $\mathrm{R}, \mathrm{x}=0$
$\Rightarrow \mu=1$
$\mathrm{R}<0,1,2>$
Area of parallelogram with adjacent vectors $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PQ}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PS}}$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PQ}}=\langle-1,1,3\rangle$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PS}}=<0,-1,-2>$
Area of parallelogram
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PQ}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{PS}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\mathrm{i} & \mathrm{j} & \mathrm{k} \\ -1 & 1 & 3 \\ 0 & -1 & -2\end{array}\right|=<1,-2,1>$
Area $=\sqrt{1^{2}+(-2)^{2}+1^{2}}=\sqrt{6}$

Question: Let $\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}, \vec{b}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}$ and $\vec{c}=\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$. Let $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}$ be a vector such that $|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}|=\sqrt{11},|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}|=3$ and the angle between $\vec{c}$ and $\vec{d}$ is $\frac{\pi}{4}$. Then $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{d}$ is equal to
Solution:
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 2 & 1 & -2 \\ 1 & 1 & 0\end{array}\right|$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}},|\mathrm{c}|=3$
$|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}|=3$
$|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}||\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}| \sin \frac{\pi}{4}=3 \Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{~d}}|=\sqrt{2}$
$|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}|=\sqrt{11}$
$\Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}|^{2}+|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}|^{2}-2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}=11$
$9+2-2 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{d}=11$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}=0$

Question:
Let a differentiable function $f$ satisfy the equation $\int_{0}^{36} f\left(\frac{\mathrm{tx}}{36}\right) \mathrm{dt}=4 \alpha f(\mathrm{x})$.
If $\mathrm{y}=f(\mathrm{x})$ is a standard parabola passing through the points $(2,1)$ and $(-4, \beta)$,
Then $\beta^{\alpha}$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$\quad \int_{0}^{36} f\left(\frac{\mathrm{tx}}{36}\right) \mathrm{dt}=4 \alpha f(\mathrm{x}), \quad$ Put $\frac{\mathrm{tx}}{36}=\mathrm{y}$
$$
\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{\mathrm{x}}{36}
$$
$\int_{0}^{\mathrm{x}} \frac{f(\mathrm{y}) 36 \mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{x}}=4 \alpha f(\mathrm{x})$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \int_{0}^{\mathrm{x}} f(\mathrm{y}) \mathrm{dy}=\frac{\alpha f(\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{x}}{9} \\
& f(\mathrm{x})=\frac{\alpha}{9}\left(f(\mathrm{x})+\mathrm{x} f^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})\right) \\
& \left(1-\frac{\alpha}{9}\right) f(\mathrm{x})=\frac{\alpha \mathrm{x}}{9} f^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) \Rightarrow(9-\alpha) f(\mathrm{x}) \\
& \frac{f^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})}{f(\mathrm{x})}=\left(\frac{9}{\alpha}-1\right) \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}} \\
& \log _{\mathrm{e}} f(\mathrm{x})=\left(\frac{9}{\alpha}-1\right) \log _{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{x}+\log _{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{c} \\
& f(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{cx}^\left(\frac{9}{\alpha}-1\right) \text { for standard parabola } \\
& \frac{9}{\alpha}-1=2 \\
& \alpha=3 \\
& f(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{cx}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$
passing through $(2,1)$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 1=4 c \Rightarrow c=1 / 4 \\
& y=\frac{x^{2}}{4} \text { passing through }(-4, \beta) \\
& \beta=4 \\
& \beta^{x}=4^{3}=64
\end{aligned}
$$

Question:
Let $(2 \alpha, \alpha)$ be the largest interval in which the function $f(\mathrm{t})=\frac{|\mathrm{t}+1|}{\mathrm{t}^{2}}, \mathrm{t}<0$, is strictly decreasing. Then the local maximum value of the function
$\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x})=2 \log _{\mathrm{e}}(\mathrm{x}-2)+\alpha \mathrm{x}^{2}+4 \mathrm{x}-\alpha, \mathrm{x}>2$, is $\_\_\_\_$
Solution:
Drawing graph of $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{t})$ for $\mathrm{t}<0$

$\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x})=\log _{\mathrm{e}}(\mathrm{x}-2)-\mathrm{x}^{2}+4 \mathrm{x}+1 ; \mathrm{x}>2$
$\mathrm{g}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=\frac{2}{\mathrm{x}-2}-(2(\mathrm{x}-2) ; \mathrm{x}>2$
$\mathrm{g}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=\frac{1-(\mathrm{x}-2)^{2}}{(\mathrm{x}-2)}=\frac{-(\mathrm{x}-3)(\mathrm{x}-1)}{(\mathrm{x}-2)}$

maxima occur at $\mathrm{x}=3$
$\mathrm{g}(3)=2 \log _{\mathrm{e}} 1-9+12+1=4$
