Question: Let each of the two ellipses $\mathrm{E}_{1}: \frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{\mathrm{a}^{2}}+\frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}}{\mathrm{~b}^{2}}=1,(\mathrm{a}>\mathrm{b})$ and $\mathrm{E}_{2}: \frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{\mathrm{~A}^{2}}+\frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}}{\mathrm{~B}^{2}}=1,(\mathrm{~A}<\mathrm{B})$ have eccentricity $\frac{4}{5}$. Let the lengths of the latus recta of $\mathrm{E}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{E}_{2}$ be $\ell_{1}$ and $\ell_{2}$, respectively, such that $2 \ell_{1}^{2}=9 \ell_{2}$. If the distance between the foci of $\mathrm{E}_{1}$ is 8 , then the distance between the foci of $\mathrm{E}_{2}$ is
Solution:
$2 \mathrm{ae}=8 \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=5$
$b^{2}=a^{2}\left(1-e^{2}\right)$
$\mathrm{b}^{2}=\mathrm{a}^{2} \times \frac{9}{25} \quad \mathrm{~b}^{2}=9$
$\mathrm{E}_{1}: \frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{25}+\frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}}{9}=1$
$\ell_{1}: \frac{2 \mathrm{~b}^{2}}{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{2 \times 9}{5}=\frac{18}{5}$
$A^{2}=B^{2}\left(1-e^{2}\right) \Rightarrow A^{2}=\frac{9}{25} B^{2} \Rightarrow A=\frac{3}{5} B$
$2 \ell^{2}=9 \ell_{2} \Rightarrow 2\left(\frac{18}{5}\right)^{2}=9 \ell_{2} \Rightarrow \ell_{2}=\frac{4 \times 18}{25}$
$\frac{2 \mathrm{~A}^{2}}{\mathrm{~B}}=\frac{72}{25} \Rightarrow \mathrm{~A}^{2} \frac{36}{25} \mathrm{~B}$
$\frac{9}{25} \mathrm{~B}^{2}=\frac{36 \mathrm{~B}}{25} \Rightarrow \mathrm{~B}=4$,
Distance between focii $2 \mathrm{Be}=2 \times \frac{4}{5} \times 4=\frac{32}{5}$

Question: The number of numbers greater than 5000 , less than 9000 and divisible by 3 , that can be formed using the digits $0,1,2,5,9$, if the repetition of the digits is allowed, is $\_\_\_\_$
Solution:
(1) all different
$5,0,1,9 \Rightarrow\lfloor 3=6$ ways
(b) 2 alike, 2 different
$5,0,0,1 \Rightarrow 3$ ways
$5,1,1,2 \Rightarrow 3$ ways
$5,2,2,0 \Rightarrow 3$ ways
$5,2,2,9 \Rightarrow 3$ ways
$5,5,0,2 \Rightarrow 6$ ways
$5,5,2,9 \Rightarrow 6$ ways
$5,1,9,9 \Rightarrow 3$ ways
(c) 3 alike, 1 different
$5,5,5,0 \Rightarrow 3$ ways
$5,5,5,9 \Rightarrow 3$ ways
(d) 2 alike, 2 other alike
$5,5,1,1 \Rightarrow 3$ ways
Total ways $=42$

Question:
If the domain of the function
$$
f(x)=\log _{\left(10 x^{2}-17 x+7\right)}\left(18 x^{2}-11 x+1\right)
$$
is $(-\infty, a) \cup(b, c) \cup(d, \infty)-\{e\}$, then
$90(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}+\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{e})$ equals:
Solution:
$18 \mathrm{x}^{2}-11 \mathrm{x}+1>0$
$(2 \mathrm{x}-1)(9 \mathrm{x}-1)>0$
$\mathrm{x}<\frac{1}{9}$ or $\frac{1}{2}<\mathrm{x}$
Also $10 \mathrm{x}^{2}-17 \mathrm{x}+7>0$
$(\mathrm{x}-1)(10 \mathrm{x}-7)>0$
$\mathrm{x}<\frac{7}{10}$ or $1<\mathrm{x}$
$\& 10 x^{2}-17 x+7 \neq 1$
$x \in\left(-\infty, \frac{1}{9}\right) \cup\left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{7}{10}\right) \cup(1, \infty)-\left\{\frac{6}{5}\right\}$
$90(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}+\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{e})=90\left(\frac{1}{9}+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{7}{10}+1+\frac{6}{5}\right)$
$=10+45+63+90+108=316$

Question:
Let $\mathrm{S}=\left\{\mathrm{z} \in \mathbb{C}:\left|\frac{\mathrm{z}-6 \mathrm{i}}{\mathrm{z}-2 \mathrm{i}}\right|=1\right.$ and $\left.\left|\frac{\mathrm{z}-8+2 \mathrm{i}}{\mathrm{z}+2 \mathrm{i}}\right|=\frac{3}{5}\right\}$.
Then $\sum_{\mathrm{z} \in \mathrm{s}}|\mathrm{z}|^{2}$ is equal to
Solution:
Solving $\left|\frac{z-6 i}{z-2 i}\right|=1 \Rightarrow y=4 \ldots \ldots$ (1)
(where $\mathrm{z}=\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{iy}$ )
Now solving $\left|\frac{z-8+2 i}{z+2 i}\right|=\frac{3}{5}$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}-25 \mathrm{x}+4 \mathrm{y}+104=0 \tag{2}
\end{equation*}
$$
Solving (1) & (2) $ \Rightarrow $ $\mathrm{z}=17+4 \mathrm{i} \& 8+4 \mathrm{i}$
$\Rightarrow \Sigma|\mathrm{z}|^{2}=(17)^{2}+(4)^{2}+(8)^{2}+(4)^{2}=385$

Question: The number of $3 \times 2$ matrices A , which can be formed using the elements of the set $\{-2,-1,0,1,2\}$ such that the sum of all the diagonal elements of $\mathrm{A}^{\mathrm{T}} \mathrm{A}$ is 5, is $\_\_\_\_$
The value of $\frac{\sqrt{3} \operatorname{cosec} 20^{\circ}-\sec 20^{\circ}}{\cos 20^{\circ} \cos 40^{\circ} \cos 60^{\circ} \cos 80^{\circ}}$ is equal to
Solution:
$\mathrm{E}=\frac{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sin 20^{\circ}}-\frac{1}{\cos 20^{\circ}}}{\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{4} \cdot \cos 60^{\circ}}$
$=\frac{\left(\sqrt{3} \cos 20^{\circ}-\sin 20^{\circ}\right)}{\cos 20^{\circ} \cdot \sin 20^{\circ}} 16$
$=\frac{\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cos 20^{\circ}-\frac{1}{2} \sin 20^{\circ}\right) 32 \times 2}{2 \cos 20^{\circ} \cdot \sin 20^{\circ}}$
$=\frac{\sin 40^{\circ}}{\sin 40^{\circ}} \times 64=64$

Question:
If $\cot x=\frac{5}{12}$ for some $x \in\left(\pi, \frac{3 \pi}{2}\right)$, then
$\sin 7 x\left(\cos \frac{13 x}{2}+\sin \frac{13 x}{2}\right)+$
$\cos 7 x\left(\cos \frac{13 x}{2}-\sin \frac{13 x}{2}\right)$ is equal to
Solution:
$\cot x=\frac{5}{12} \Rightarrow \cos x=\frac{-5}{13}=2 \cos ^{2} \frac{x}{2}-1$
$\cos \left(\frac{x}{2}\right)=-\frac{2}{\sqrt{13}}$ or $\frac{2}{\sqrt{13}}($ rejected $)$
$\left\{\because \frac{\mathrm{x}}{2} \in\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right)\right\}$
$\left(\sin 7 \mathrm{x} \frac{\sin 13 \mathrm{x}}{2}+\cos 7 \mathrm{x} \frac{\cos 13 \mathrm{x}}{2}\right)+\left(\sin 7 \mathrm{x} \frac{\cos 13 \mathrm{x}}{2}-\cos 7 \mathrm{x} \frac{\sin 13 \mathrm{x}}{2}\right)$
$\cos \left(7 \mathrm{x}-\frac{13 \mathrm{x}}{2}\right)+\sin \left(7 \mathrm{x}-\frac{13 \mathrm{x}}{2}\right)$
$\cos \frac{\mathrm{x}}{2}+\sin \left(\frac{\mathrm{x}}{2}\right)$
$\frac{3}{\sqrt{13}}-\frac{2}{\sqrt{13}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{13}}$

Solution:

Slope of $\mathrm{AD}=\frac{-3 / 2}{\frac{1}{2}}=-3$
$3 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}=5$
$\alpha=3, \beta=1 ; \alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}=10$

Question: Consider an A.P.: $\mathrm{a}_{1}, \mathrm{a}_{2}, \ldots, \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}} ; \mathrm{a}_{1}>0$. If $\mathrm{a}_{2}-\mathrm{a}_{1} =\frac{-3}{4}, \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{a}_{1}$, and $\sum_{\mathrm{i}=1}^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{i}}=\frac{525}{2}$, then $\sum_{\mathrm{i}=1}^{17} \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{i}}$ is equal to
Solution:
$\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}}{2}\left[\mathrm{a}_{1}+\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}}\right]=\frac{525}{2}, \mathrm{~d}=\frac{-3}{4}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{n}{2}\left[a_{1}+\frac{a_{1}}{4}\right]=\frac{525}{2} \\
& \frac{5 a_{1} n}{4}=525 \\
& a_{1} n=420 \\
& a_{n}=a_{1}+(n-1)\left(\frac{-3}{4}\right) \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{-3}{4} a_{1}=\left(\frac{-3}{4}\right)(n-1) \Rightarrow a_{1}=n-1 \\
& n(n-1)=420 \\
& n^{2}-n-420=0 \\
& (n-21)(n+20)=0 \\
& n=21, a_{1}=20 \\
& \sum_{i=1}^{17} a_{i}=\frac{17}{2}\left[2 a_{1}+16 d\right] \\
& =\frac{17}{2}\left[40+16\left(\frac{-3}{4}\right)\right] \\
& =\frac{17}{2}[40-12] \\
& =17 \times 14=238
\end{aligned}
$$

Question:
Let $\mathrm{S}=\frac{1}{25!}+\frac{1}{3!23!}+\frac{1}{5!21!}+\ldots$ up to 13 terms.
If $13 \mathrm{~S}=\frac{2^{\mathrm{k}}}{\mathrm{n}!}, \mathrm{k} \in \mathrm{N}$, then
$\mathrm{n}+\mathrm{k}$ is equal to
Solution:
$\frac{1}{26!}\left(\frac{26!}{25!1!}+\frac{26!}{3!23!}+\frac{26!}{5!21!}+\ldots . .+13\right.$ terms $)$
$\frac{1}{26!}\left({ }^{26} \mathrm{C}_{1}+{ }^{26} \mathrm{C}_{3}+{ }^{26} \mathrm{C}_{5}+\ldots .+13\right.$ terms $)$
$\frac{1}{26!}\left({ }^{26} \mathrm{C}_{1}+{ }^{26} \mathrm{C}_{5}+\ldots .+{ }^{26} \mathrm{C}_{25}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{S}=\frac{1}{26!} \times 2^{25}$
$\Rightarrow 13 \mathrm{~S}=\frac{2^{24}}{25!}$
so $\mathrm{n}+\mathrm{k}=25+24=49$

Question:
Let $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{t})=\int\left(\frac{1-\sin \left(\log _{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{t}\right)}{1-\cos \left(\log _{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{t}\right)}\right) \mathrm{dt}, \mathrm{t}>1$.
If $\mathrm{f}\left(\mathrm{e}^{\pi / 2}\right)=-\mathrm{e}^{\pi / 2}$ and $\mathrm{f}\left(\mathrm{e}^{\pi / 4}\right)=\alpha \mathrm{e}^{\pi / 4}$, then $\alpha$ equals
Solution:
$f(t)=\int \frac{1-\sin (\ln t)}{1-\cos (\ln t)} d t$
Let $\ln \mathrm{t}=\mathrm{x} \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{dt}=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}$
$=\frac{1}{2} \int\left(\operatorname{cosec}^{2} \frac{x}{2}-2 \cot \frac{x}{2}\right) e^{x} d x-t \cot \left(\frac{\ln t}{2}\right)+C \left(\therefore \int\left(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})+\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})\right) \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}=\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}) \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{c}\right)$

Now $\mathrm{f}\left(\mathrm{e}^{\pi / 2}\right)=-\mathrm{e}^{\pi / 2} \cot \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)+\mathrm{C}=-\mathrm{e}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}$ (given) $\mathrm{C}=0$
Now $\mathrm{f}\left(\mathrm{e}^{\pi / 4}\right)=-\mathrm{e}^{\pi / 4} \cot \left(\frac{\pi}{8}\right)+\mathrm{C}=-\mathrm{e}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}(\sqrt{2}+1)$

Question: Let S be a set of 5 elements and $\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{S})$ denote the power set of S . Let E be an event of choosing an ordered pair (A, B) from the set $\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{S}) \times \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{S})$ such that $\mathrm{A} \cap \mathrm{B}=\varnothing$. If the probability of the event E is $\frac{3^{\mathrm{p}}}{2^{\mathrm{q}}}$, where $\mathrm{p}, \mathrm{q} \in \mathbb{N}$, then $\mathrm{p}+\mathrm{q}$ is equal to
Solution:
$\mathrm{S}=\{\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}, \mathrm{d}, \mathrm{e}\}$
$\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{S})$ contains 32 elements
both set A and set B are subsets of $\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{S})$
Every element has 4 choices


Favourable cases $=3^{5}$
Total cases $=4^{5}$
$\mathrm{P}=\frac{3^{5}}{4^{5}}=\frac{3^{5}}{2^{10}}$
$\mathrm{m}=5, \mathrm{n}=10$
$\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}=15$

Question:
$\left(\frac{1}{3}+\frac{4}{7}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{3^{2}}+\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{4}{7}+\frac{4^{2}}{7^{2}}\right)+$
$\left(\frac{1}{3^{3}}+\frac{1}{3^{2}} \times \frac{4}{7}+\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{4^{2}}{7^{2}}+\frac{4^{3}}{7^{3}}\right)+\ldots .$. upto infinite terms is equal to –
Solution:
Let $\mathrm{a}=\frac{4}{7}, \mathrm{~b}=\frac{1}{3}$
Multiply $\mathrm{N}^{\mathrm{r}}$ and $\mathrm{D}^{\mathrm{r}}$ by $(\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{b})=\frac{4}{7}-\frac{1}{3}=\frac{5}{21}$
$\frac{1}{a-b}\left[\left(a^{2}-b^{2}\right)+\left(a^{3}-b^{3}\right)+\left(a^{4}-b^{4}\right)+\ldots . \infty\right]$
$\frac{1}{a-b}\left[\frac{a^{2}}{1-a}-\frac{b^{2}}{1-b}\right]=\frac{21}{5}\left[\frac{\frac{16}{49}}{1-\frac{4}{7}}-\frac{\frac{1}{9}}{1-\frac{1}{3}}\right]$
$=\frac{21}{5}\left[\frac{16}{21}-\frac{1}{6}\right]=\frac{21}{5}\left[\frac{96-21}{21.6}\right]$
$=\frac{75}{5.6}=\frac{15}{6}=\frac{5}{2}$

Question: Let $\mathrm{P}=\left[\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{ij}}\right]$ and $\mathrm{Q}=\left[\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{ij}}\right]$ be two square matrices of order 3 such that $\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{ij}}=2^{(\mathrm{i}+\mathrm{j}-1)} \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{ij}}$ and $\operatorname{det}(\mathrm{Q})= 2^{10}$. Then the value of $\operatorname{det}(\operatorname{adj}(\operatorname{adj} \mathrm{P}))$ is :
Solution:
$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}2 \mathrm{p}_{11} & 2^{2} \mathrm{p}_{12} & 2^{3} \mathrm{p}_{13} \\ 2^{2} \mathrm{p}_{21} & 2^{3} \mathrm{p}_{22} & 2^{4} \mathrm{p}_{23} \\ 2^{3} \mathrm{p}_{31} & 2^{4} \mathrm{p}_{32} & 2^{5} \mathrm{p}_{33}\end{array}\right|=2^{10}$
$2^{2} \cdot 2 \cdot 2^{3}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\mathrm{p}_{11} & \mathrm{p}_{12} & \mathrm{p}_{13} \\ 2 \mathrm{p}_{21} & 2 \mathrm{p}_{22} & 2 \mathrm{p}_{23} \\ 2^{2} \mathrm{p}_{31} & 2^{2} \mathrm{p}_{32} & 2^{2} \mathrm{p}_{33}\end{array}\right|=2^{10}$
$2^{9}\left|\begin{array}{lll}\mathrm{p}_{11} & \mathrm{p}_{12} & \mathrm{p}_{13} \\ \mathrm{p}_{21} & \mathrm{p}_{22} & \mathrm{p}_{23} \\ \mathrm{p}_{31} & \mathrm{p}_{32} & \mathrm{p}_{33}\end{array}\right|=2^{10} \Rightarrow|\mathrm{P}|=2$
$|\operatorname{adj}(\operatorname{adj}(\mathrm{P}))|=|\mathrm{P}|^{(\mathrm{n}-1)^{2}}=|\mathrm{P}|^{4}=2^{4}=16$

Solution:

\mathrm{t}^{2}-\mathrm{at}+9= \tag{2}

for roots of equation (1) to be real & distinct roots of equation (2) must be positive & distinct.

Question:
Let $f(x)=\int \frac{7 x^{10}+9 x^{8}}{\left(1+x^{2}+2 x^{9}\right)^{2}} d x, \quad x \quad>0$,
$\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} f(x)=0 \quad$ and $\quad f(1)=\frac{1}{4}$.
$A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}0 & 0 & 1 \\ \frac{1}{4} & f^{\prime}(1) & 1 \\ \alpha^{2} & 4 & 1\end{array}\right]$ and $B=\operatorname{adj}(\operatorname{adj} A)$ be such
that $|\mathrm{B}|=81$, then $\alpha^{2}$ is equal to
Solution:
$f(x)=\frac{\int\left(\frac{7}{x^{8}}+\frac{9}{x^{10}}\right)}{\left(\frac{1}{x^{9}}+\frac{1}{x^{7}}+2\right)^{2}} d x$
Put $t=\frac{1}{x^{9}}+\frac{1}{x^{7}}+2 \Rightarrow \frac{d t}{d x}=\frac{-9}{x^{10}}-\frac{7}{x^{8}}$
$\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\int \frac{-\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{t}^{2}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}}+\mathrm{C}$
$f(x)=\frac{1}{\frac{1}{x^{9}}+\frac{1}{x^{7}}+2}+C$
$=\frac{x^{9}}{1+x^{2}+2 x^{9}}+C$
Given $\mathrm{f}(1)=\frac{1}{4}=\frac{1}{4}+\mathrm{C} \Rightarrow \mathrm{C}=0$
$f(x)=\frac{x^{9}}{1+x^{2}+2 x^{9}}$
$f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{\left(1+x^{2}+2 x^{9}\right)-9 x^{8}-x^{9}\left(2 x+18 x^{8}\right)}{\left(1+x^{2}+2 x^{9}\right)^{2}}$
$f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{36-20}{16}=1$
$\mathrm{A}=\left(\begin{array}{ccc}0 & 0 & 1 \\ 4 & 1 & 1 \\ \alpha^{2} & \frac{1}{4} & 1\end{array}\right)$
$|\mathrm{A}|=\left|1-\alpha^{2}\right|=3$
$1-\alpha^{2}=3,-3 \Rightarrow \alpha^{2}=-2,4$
Value of $\alpha^{2}=4$
$\mathrm{B}=\operatorname{adj}(\operatorname{adj} \mathrm{A})$
$|\mathrm{B}|=81=|\mathrm{A}|^{4} \Rightarrow|\mathrm{~A}|=3$

Question:
Let $\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+5 k$ and $\vec{b}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 k$. If $\vec{c}$ is a vector such that
$2(\vec{a} \times \vec{c})+3(\vec{b} \times \vec{c})=\overrightarrow{0}$ and $(\vec{a}-\vec{b}) \cdot \vec{c}=-97$, then $|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}} \times \mathrm{k}|^{2}$ is equal to
Solution:
$2(\vec{a} \times \vec{c})+3(\vec{b} \times \vec{c})=0$
$\Rightarrow(2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}+3 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{~d}}) \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=0 \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=\lambda(2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}+3 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{~d}})$
$\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=\lambda(7 \mathrm{i}-13 \mathrm{j}+19 \mathrm{k})$
Now $(\vec{a}-\vec{b}) \cdot \vec{c}-=\lambda(7+52+38)+97 \lambda=-97$
$\Rightarrow \lambda=-1$
Now $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=-7 \mathrm{i}+13 \mathrm{j}-19 \mathrm{k}$
$\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{k}}-=7 \mathrm{j}+13 \mathrm{i} \Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}} \times \hat{\mathrm{k}}|^{2}=7^{2}+13^{2}=218$

Question: Let the length of the latus rectum of an ellipse $\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{\mathrm{a}^{2}}+\frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}}{\mathrm{~b}^{2}}=1,(\mathrm{a}>\mathrm{b})$, be 30 . If its eccentricity is the maximum value of the function $f(t)=-\frac{3}{4}+2 t-t^{2}$, then $\left(\mathrm{a}^{2}+\mathrm{b}^{2}\right)$ is equal to –
Solution:
$f(t)=\frac{-3}{4}+2 t-t^{2}$
$\left.f(t)\right|_{\text {maximum }}=\frac{1}{4}=e \Rightarrow e^{2}=\frac{1}{16} \Rightarrow \frac{a^{2}-b^{2}}{a^{2}}=\frac{1}{16} .$....(1)
$$
\begin{equation*}
\because \frac{2 b^{2}}{a}=30 \Rightarrow b^{2}=15 a \tag{2}
\end{equation*}
$$
By (1) & (2)
$16\left(a^{2}-15 a\right)=a^{2} \Rightarrow 15 a^{2}-16 \times 15 a=0$
$\mathrm{a}=16$
$\mathrm{b}^{2}=240$
$\mathrm{a}^{2}+\mathrm{b}^{2}=256+240$
$=496$

Question:
Let $\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{y}(\mathrm{x})$ be a differentiable function in the interval $(0, \infty)$ such that $y(1)=2$.
and $\lim _{\mathrm{t} \rightarrow \mathrm{x}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{t}^{2} \mathrm{y}(\mathrm{x})-\mathrm{x}^{2} \mathrm{y}(\mathrm{t})}{\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{t}}\right)=3$ for each $\mathrm{x}>0$.
Then $2 \mathrm{y}(2)$ is equal to
Solution:
$\lim _{\mathrm{t} \rightarrow \mathrm{x}} \frac{2 \mathrm{t} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})-\mathrm{x}^{2} \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{t})}{-1}=3$
$x^{2} f^{\prime}(x)-2 x f(x)=3$
$\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}-\frac{2 \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}=\frac{3}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}$
I.F. $=\mathrm{e}^{-\int \frac{2}{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{e}^{-2 \log _{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{x}}=1 / \mathrm{x}^{2}$
$y \cdot \frac{1}{x^{2}}=\int \frac{3}{x^{4}} d x$
$\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}=-\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}^{3}}+\mathrm{c} \Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\mathrm{cx}^{2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$
$\mathrm{f}(1)=2=\mathrm{c}-1 \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=3$
$f(x)=3 x^{2}-\frac{1}{x}$
$f(2)=12-\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow 2 f(2)=23$

Question: Let f be a function such that $3 \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})+2 \mathrm{f}\left(\frac{\mathrm{m}}{19 \mathrm{x}}\right)=5 \mathrm{x}$, $x \neq 0$, where $m=\sum_{i=1}^{9}(i)^{2}$. Then $f(5)-f(2)$ is equal to
Solution:
$\mathrm{m}=\frac{9 \times 10 \times 19}{6}=15 \times 19$
$3 f(x)+2 f\left(\frac{15}{x}\right)=5 x$
Replace x by $\frac{15}{\mathrm{x}}$
$3 f\left(\frac{15}{x}\right)+2 f(x)=\frac{75}{x}$
$9 f(x)-4 f(x)=15 x-\frac{150}{x}$
$5 f(x)=15 x-\frac{150}{x}$
$f(x)=3 x-\frac{30}{x}$
$f(5)=15-\frac{30}{5}=9$
$f(2)=6-15=-9$
$f(5)-f(2)=18$

Question:
Let $(\mathrm{h}, \mathrm{k})$ lie on the circle $\mathrm{C}: \mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}=4$ and the point ( $2 \mathrm{~h}+1,3 \mathrm{k}+2$ ) lie on an ellipse with eccentricity $e$. Then the value of $\frac{5}{e^{2}}$ is equal to
______ .
Solution:
Let $\mathrm{P} \equiv(2 \cos \theta, 2 \sin \theta)$
$\therefore $ coordinates of $\mathrm{Q}=(4 \cos \theta+1,6 \sin \theta+3)$
$\therefore $ locus of $Q$ is $\left(\frac{x-1}{4}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{y-3}{6}\right)^{2}=1$
$\therefore \mathrm{e}^{2}=1-\frac{16}{36}=\frac{5}{9}$
$\therefore \frac{5}{\mathrm{e}^{2}}=9$

Question: Let $\alpha_{1}, \alpha_{2}, \alpha_{3}, \alpha_{4}$ be an A.P. of four terms such that each term of the A. P. and its common difference $l$ are integers. If $\alpha_{1}+\alpha_{2}+\alpha_{3}+\alpha_{4}=48$ and $\alpha_{1}\alpha_{2}\alpha_{3} \alpha_{4}+l^{4}=361$ then the largest term of the A.P. is equal to
Solution:
$\mathrm{a}_{1}, \mathrm{a}_{2}, \mathrm{a}_{3}, \mathrm{a}_{4}$ as $\mathrm{a}-3 \mathrm{~d}, \mathrm{a}-\mathrm{d}, \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{d}, \mathrm{a}+3 \mathrm{~d}$
where $\mathrm{d}=\frac{\ell}{2}$
$\because a_{1}+a_{2}+a_{3}+a_{4}=48 \Rightarrow 4 a=48 \Rightarrow a=12$
$\& a_{1} a_{2} a_{3} a_{4}+\ell^{4}=361 \Rightarrow\left(a^{2}-9 d^{2}\right)\left(a^{2}-d^{2}\right)+16 d^{4}$
$=361$
$\Rightarrow\left(144-9 \mathrm{~d}^{2}\right)\left(144-\mathrm{d}^{2}\right)+16 \mathrm{~d}^{4}=361$
$\Rightarrow 25 \mathrm{~d}^{4}-1440 \mathrm{~d}^{2}+(144)^{2}=361$
$\left(5 \mathrm{~d}^{2}-144\right)^{2}=19^{2}$
$\therefore 5 \mathrm{~d}^{2}-144=19$ or -19
$\mathrm{d}^{2}=\frac{163}{5}$ or $\mathrm{d}^{2}=\frac{125}{5}=25$
$\mathrm{d}=\sqrt{\frac{163}{5}}$ or $\mathrm{d}=5$
$\therefore \ell=2 \sqrt{\frac{163}{5}}$ or $\ell=10$
(rejected)
$\because $ common difference is an integer
$\therefore $ largest term $=12+15=27$

Question: Let $\mathrm{z}=(1+\mathrm{i})(1+2 \mathrm{i})(1+3 \mathrm{i}) \ldots(1+\mathrm{ni})$, where i $=\sqrt{-1}$. If $|z|^{2}=44200$, then $n$ is equal to -
Solution:
$|z|^{2}=2^{3} .5^{2} .13 .17$
$\prod_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{\mathrm{n}}\left(1+\mathrm{r}^{2}\right)=2^{3} \cdot 5^{2} \cdot 13 \cdot 17=(2) \cdot(5) \cdot(2 \cdot 5) \cdot(17) \cdot(2 \cdot 13)=2 \cdot 5 \cdot 10 \cdot 17 \cdot 26$
so $n=5$

Question: Let the image of parabola $x^{2}=4 y$, in the line $x-y =1$ be $(\mathrm{y}+\alpha)^{2}=\mathrm{b}(\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{c}), \mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c} \in \mathbb{N}$. Then $\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}$ +c is equal to
Solution:
Parametric point P on $\mathrm{x}^{2}=4 \mathrm{y}$ is $\mathrm{P}\left(2 \mathrm{t}, \mathrm{t}^{2}\right)$
$\therefore $ mirror image of P in $\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}=1$ is
$\mathrm{Q} \equiv\left(2 \mathrm{t}-\frac{2 \cdot 1 \cdot\left(2 \mathrm{t}-\mathrm{t}^{2}-1\right)}{2}, \mathrm{t}^{2}+\frac{2 \cdot 2(1) \cdot\left(2 \mathrm{t}-\mathrm{t}^{2}-1\right)}{2}\right)$
$\mathrm{Q} \equiv\left(\mathrm{t}^{2}+1,2 \mathrm{t}-1\right) \equiv(\mathrm{h}, \mathrm{k})$
$\therefore $ locus of Q is $\mathrm{x}=\frac{(\mathrm{y}+1)^{2}}{4}+1$ which is the required parabola.
$\therefore(\mathrm{y}+1)^{2}=4(\mathrm{x}-1)$
$\therefore \mathrm{a}=1, \mathrm{~b}=4, \mathrm{c}=1$
$\therefore \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}=6$

Question:
Let $[t]$ denote the greatest integer less than or equal to $t$. If the function
$f(x)=\left\{\begin{aligned} b^{2} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}\left[\frac{\pi}{2}(\cos x+\sin x) \cos x\right]\right) & , x<0 \\ \frac{\sin x-\frac{1}{2} \sin 2 x}{x^{3}} & , x>0 \\ a & , x=0\end{aligned}\right.$
is continuous at $\mathrm{x}=0$, then $\mathrm{a}^{2}+\mathrm{b}^{2}$ is equal to
Solution:
$\mathrm{f}(0)=\mathrm{a}$
$\mathrm{RHL}=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} \frac{\sin x(1-\cos x)}{x^{3}}=\frac{1}{2}$
$\mathrm{LHL}=\lim _{\mathrm{x} \rightarrow 0^{-}}\left(\mathrm{b}^{2} \sin \frac{\pi}{2}\left[\frac{\pi}{2}(\sin \mathrm{x}+\cos \mathrm{x}) \cos \mathrm{x}\right]\right)=\mathrm{b}^{2}$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{a}=\frac{1}{2} \& \mathrm{~b}^{2}=\frac{1}{2}$
so $\left(\mathrm{a}^{2}+\mathrm{b}^{2}\right)=\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{2}=\frac{3}{4}$

Question: Let the angles made with the positive x -axis by two straight lines drawn from the point $\mathrm{P}(2,3)$ and meeting the line $\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}=6$ at a distance $\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}$ from the point P be $\theta_{1}$ and $\theta_{2}$. Then the value of $\left(\theta_{1}+\theta_{2}\right)$ is :
Solution:
Let Q is $\left(\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \cos \theta+2, \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \sin \theta+3\right)$
so, $x+y=6$
$\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}(\cos \theta+\sin \theta)+5=6$
$\sin \theta+\cos \theta=\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}}$
$1+\sin 2 \theta=\frac{3}{2}$
$\sin 2 \theta=\frac{1}{2}$
$2 \theta=\frac{\pi}{6}, \frac{5 \pi}{6}$
$\theta=\frac{\pi}{12} \& \frac{5 \pi}{6}$
So $\theta_{1}+\theta_{2}=\frac{\pi}{2}$

Question: If $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ satisfies the relation $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}+\int_{0}^{1}\left(\mathrm{y}+\mathrm{xe}^{\mathrm{x}}\right) f(y) d y$, then $e+f(0)$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$f(x)=e^{x}+\int_{0}^{1} y f(y) d y+x e^{x} \int_{0}^{1} f(y) d y$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{Bxe}^{\mathrm{x}} \\
& \mathrm{~A}=\int_{0}^{1} \mathrm{yf}(\mathrm{y}) \mathrm{dy}=\int_{0}^{1} \mathrm{y}\left(\mathrm{~A}+\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}+\mathrm{By} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}\right) \mathrm{dy} \\
& \mathrm{~A}=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{2}+(0-(-1))+\mathrm{B}(\mathrm{e}-1) \\
& \frac{\mathrm{A}}{2}+\mathrm{B}(1-\mathrm{e})=1 \\
& \mathrm{~B}=\int_{0}^{1} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{y}) \mathrm{dy} \\
& \mathrm{~B}=\int_{0}^{1}\left(\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}+\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{By} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{y}}\right) \mathrm{dy} \\
& \mathrm{~B}=(\mathrm{e}-1)+\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}(0-(-1)) \\
& \mathrm{B}=\mathrm{e}^{-} 1+\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B} \Rightarrow \mathrm{~A}=1-\mathrm{e} \\
& \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{Bx} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \\
& \mathrm{f}(0)=1+\mathrm{A}=1-\mathrm{e}+1=2-\mathrm{e} \\
& \mathrm{e}+\mathrm{f}(0)=2
\end{aligned}
$$

Question: Let $\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k}, \vec{b}=\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k} \quad$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$. Let $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}$ be the vector in the plane of the vectors $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$, such that the length of its projection on the vector $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}$ is $\frac{1}{\sqrt{14}}$. Then $|\vec{v}|$ is equal to
Solution:
$\vec{v}=x \vec{a}+y \vec{b}=x(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k})+y(\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k})$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=(2 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(3 \mathrm{y}-\mathrm{x}) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(-\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}) \hat{\mathrm{k}}$
$\left|\frac{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{C}}}{|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{C}}|}\right|=\frac{1}{\sqrt{14}}$
$\vec{v} \cdot \vec{c}=2(2 x+y)+3 y-x-3 x-3 y$
$=2 \mathrm{y}$
$\left|\frac{2 \mathrm{y}}{\sqrt{14}}\right|=\frac{1}{\sqrt{14}} \Rightarrow|2 \mathrm{y}|=1$
$|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}|=\sqrt{(2 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y})^{2}+(3 \mathrm{y}-\mathrm{x})^{2}+(\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y})^{2}}$
$=\sqrt{6 x^{2}+11 y^{2}+4 x y-6 x y+2 x y}$
$=\sqrt{6 \mathrm{x}^{2}+\frac{11}{4}}=\frac{\sqrt{24 \mathrm{x}^{2}+11}}{2}$
Now if we take $x^{2}=1$ then option $\frac{\sqrt{35}}{2}$ matches most probably NTA thought could been this.

Question:
Consider the following three statements for the function $\mathrm{f}:(0, \infty) \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ defined by
$\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\left|\log _{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{x}\right|-|\mathrm{x}-1|$ :
(I) f is differentiable at all $\mathrm{x}>0$.
(II) f is increasing in $(0,1)$.
(III) f is decreasing in ( $1, \infty$ ).
Then.
Solution:
$\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=|\ell \mathrm{nx}|-|\mathrm{x}-1|$

$=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}\ln \mathrm{x}-(\mathrm{x}-1) & \mathrm{x} \geq 1 \\ -\ln \mathrm{x}+(\mathrm{x}-1) & 0<\mathrm{x}<1\end{array}\right.$

$=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}\ln \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x}+1 & \mathrm{x} \geq 1 \\ -\ln \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{x}-1 & 0<\mathrm{x}<1\end{array}\right.$

$\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=\left(\begin{array}{ll}\frac{1}{x}-1 & x \geq 1 \\ -\frac{1}{x}+1 & 0<x<1\end{array}\right.$

$\mathrm{f}^{\prime}\left(1^{+}\right)=\mathrm{f}^{\prime}\left(1^{-}\right)=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ is differentiable $\forall \mathrm{x}>0$

$\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})<0 \quad \forall \mathrm{x}>1$

$\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})<0 \quad \forall 0<\mathrm{x}<1$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ is decreasing $\forall \mathrm{x} \in(0, \infty)$

Option (d)

Question: The largest value of n , for which $40^{\mathrm{n}}$ divides 60 !, is
Solution:
$40^{\mathrm{n}}=2^{3 \mathrm{n}} \times 5^{\mathrm{n}}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{E}_{2}(60!)=\left[\frac{60}{2}\right]+\left[\frac{60}{2^{2}}\right]+\left[\frac{60}{2^{3}}\right]+\left[\frac{60}{2^{4}}\right]+\left[\frac{60}{2^{5}}\right] \\
& =30+15+7+3+1=56 \\
& \mathrm{E}_{5}(60!)=\left[\frac{60}{5}\right]+\left[\frac{60}{5^{2}}\right] \\
& =12+2=14 \\
& 40^{\mathrm{n}}=\left(2^{3}\right)^{\mathrm{n}} \times 5^{\mathrm{n}}=\left(2^{3} \times 5\right)^{\mathrm{n}} \\
& 60!=2^{56} \times 5^{14} \ldots=2^{14} \cdot\left(2^{3} \cdot 5\right)^{14}
\end{aligned}
$$
$\therefore $ Maximum value of n is 14 .

Question:
The sum of all values of $\alpha$, for which the shortest distance between the lines
$\frac{\mathrm{x}+1}{\alpha}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-2}{-1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-4}{-\alpha}$ and $\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\alpha}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-1}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-1}{2 \alpha}$ is $\sqrt{2}$, is
Solution:
$\sqrt{2}=\frac{\left|\begin{array}{ccc}-1 & 1 & 3 \\ \alpha & -1 & -\alpha \\ \alpha & 2 & 2 \alpha\end{array}\right|}{\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & j & k \\ \alpha & -1 & -\alpha \\ \alpha & 2 & 2 \alpha\end{array}\right|}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{2}= & \frac{-1(-2 \alpha+2 \alpha)-1\left(2 \alpha^{2}+\alpha^{2}\right)+3(2 \alpha+\alpha)}{\left|\hat{\mathrm{i}}(-2 \alpha+2 \alpha)-\hat{\mathrm{j}}\left(2 \alpha^{2}+\alpha^{2}\right)+\hat{\mathrm{k}}(2 \alpha+\alpha)\right|} \\
& \sqrt{2}=\frac{-3 \alpha^{2}+9 \alpha}{\sqrt{9 \alpha^{4}+9 \alpha^{2}}} \\
& \sqrt{2}=\frac{-\alpha+3}{\sqrt{\alpha^{2}+1}} \\
& \Rightarrow 2 \alpha^{2}+2=\alpha^{2}+9-6 \alpha \\
& \alpha^{2}+6 \alpha-7=0 \\
& (\alpha+7)(\alpha-1)=0 \\
& \alpha=-7,1 \\
& \text { sum }=-7+1=-6
\end{aligned}
$$
option (b)

Question:
The number of elements in the set
$\left\{\begin{array}{r}\mathrm{x} \in\left[0,180^{\circ}\right]: \tan \left(\mathrm{x}+100^{\circ}\right)= \\ \tan \left(\mathrm{x}+50^{\circ}\right) \tan \mathrm{x} \tan \left(\mathrm{x}-50^{\circ}\right)\end{array}\right\}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$\frac{\tan \left(\mathrm{x}+100^{\circ}\right)}{\tan \mathrm{x}}=\tan \left(\mathrm{x}+50^{\circ}\right) \tan \left(\mathrm{x}-50^{\circ}\right)$
$\frac{\sin \left(\mathrm{x}+100^{\circ}\right) \cos \mathrm{x}}{\cos \left(\mathrm{x}+100^{\circ}\right) \sin \mathrm{x}}=\frac{\sin \left(\mathrm{x}+50^{\circ}\right) \sin \left(\mathrm{x}-50^{\circ}\right)}{\cos \left(\mathrm{x}+50^{\circ}\right) \cos \left(\mathrm{x}-50^{\circ}\right)}$
Apply C & D
$\frac{\sin \left(2 \mathrm{x}+100^{\circ}\right)}{\sin 100^{\circ}}=\frac{\cos 100^{\circ}}{-\cos 2 \mathrm{x}}$
$2 \sin \left(2 \mathrm{x}+100^{\circ}\right) \cos 2 \mathrm{x}+\sin 200^{\circ}=0$
$\sin \left(4 x+100^{\circ}\right)+\sin 100^{\circ}+\sin 200^{\circ}=0$
$\sin \left(4 \mathrm{x}+100^{\circ}\right)=-2 \sin 150^{\circ} \cos 50^{\circ}$
$\sin \left(4 \mathrm{x}+100^{\circ}\right)=-\cos 50^{\circ}=\sin \left(-40^{\circ}\right)$
$\therefore 4 \mathrm{x}+100^{\circ}=\mathrm{n} \pi+(-1)^{\mathrm{n}} \cdot\left(-40^{\circ}\right)$
$\mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{n} \pi+(-1)^{\mathrm{n}+1}\left(40^{\circ}\right)-100^{\circ}}{4}$
$\therefore \mathrm{x}=30^{\circ}, 55^{\circ}, 120^{\circ}, 145^{\circ}$ in ( $0, \pi$ )
$\therefore $ no. of solutions $=4$

Question: The letters of the word "UDAYPUR" are written in all possible ways with or without meaning and these words are arranged as in a dictionary. The rank of the word "UDAYPUR" is :

ADIPRUU

$$

Question: Let $\mathrm{X}=\{\mathrm{x} \in \mathbb{N}: 1 \leq \mathrm{x} \leq 19\}$ and for some $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b} \in \mathbb{R}$, $\mathrm{Y}=\{\mathrm{ax}+\mathrm{b}: \mathrm{x} \in \mathrm{X}\}$. If the mean and variance of the elements of Y are 30 and 750, respectively, then the sum of all possible values of $b$ is
Solution:
$\Sigma \mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{i}}=\mathrm{a} \Sigma \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}+\Sigma \mathrm{b}$
$=\mathrm{a} \times(1+2+\ldots . .+19)+19 \mathrm{~b}$
$\frac{\Sigma \mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{i}}}{19}=\frac{\mathrm{a} \times 19 \times 20}{2 \times 19}+\mathrm{b}$
$$
\begin{equation*}
30=10 \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b} \tag{1}
\end{equation*}
$$
Variance of $\mathrm{X}=\frac{\Sigma \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}^{2}}{19}-\left(\frac{\Sigma \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}}{19}\right)^{2}$
$=\frac{19 \times 20 \times 39}{19 \times 6}-(10)^{2}=30$
Variance of $\mathrm{Y}=\mathrm{a}^{2}$ (variance of X )
$750=\mathrm{a}^{2} \times 30$
$\mathrm{a}^{2}=25 \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}= \pm 5$
if $\mathrm{a}=+5 \Rightarrow \mathrm{~b}=30-50=-20 \quad \ldots$ from (i)
if $\mathrm{a}=-5 \Rightarrow \mathrm{~b}=30+50=80 \quad \ldots$ from (i)
sum of values of $b=80-20=60$
options (4)

Question: Let $f(\alpha)$ denote the area of the region in the first quadrant bounded by $\mathrm{x}=0, \mathrm{x}=1, \mathrm{y}^{2}=\mathrm{x}$ and $\mathrm{y}= |\alpha x-5|-|1-\alpha x|+\alpha x^{2}$. Then $(f(0)+f(1))$ is equal to
Solution:
At $\alpha=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(0)$
$\mathrm{x}=0, \mathrm{x}=1, \mathrm{y}^{2}=\mathrm{x}$
$y=|0 \cdot x-5|-|1-0 \cdot x|+0 \cdot x^{2}$
$y=4$

$A_{1}=\int_{0}^{1}(4-\sqrt{x}) d x$
$=4 x-\left.\frac{x^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\frac{3}{2}}\right|_{0} ^{1}$
$=4-\frac{2}{3}(1)=\frac{10}{3}$
at $\alpha=1 \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(1)$
$\mathrm{x}=0, \mathrm{x}=1, \mathrm{y}^{2}=\mathrm{x}$,
$\mathrm{y}=|\mathrm{x}-5|-|1-\mathrm{x}|+\mathrm{x}^{2}$ in $\mathrm{x} \in(0,1)$
$y=5-x-(1-x)+x^{2}$
$\mathrm{y}=4+\mathrm{x}^{2}$

$A_{2}=\int_{0}^{1}\left(\left(4+x^{2}\right)-(\sqrt{x})\right) d x$
$=4 x+\frac{x^{3}}{3}-\left.\frac{x^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\frac{3}{2}}\right|_{0} ^{1}$
$=4+\frac{1}{3}-\frac{2}{3}=\frac{11}{3}$
$|\mathrm{f}(0)+\mathrm{f}(1)|=\left|\mathrm{A}_{1}+\mathrm{A}_{2}\right|=\left|\frac{10}{3}+\frac{11}{3}\right|=\left|\frac{21}{3}\right|=7$
option (3)

Question: If the domain of the function $f(x)=\sin ^{-1} \left(\frac{1}{x^{2}-2 x-2}\right)$, is $(-\infty, \alpha] \cup[\beta, \gamma] \cup[\delta, \infty)$, then $\alpha+\beta+\gamma+\delta$ is equal to
Solution:
$-1 \leq \frac{2}{x^{2}-2 x-2} \leq 1$
$\frac{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}-2 \mathrm{x}-2}{\mathrm{x}^{2}-2 \mathrm{x}-2} \geq 0 \Rightarrow \frac{(\mathrm{x}-1)^{2}-2}{(\mathrm{x}-1)^{2}-3} \geq 0$
$\Rightarrow \frac{(\mathrm{x}-1-\sqrt{2})(\mathrm{x}-1+\sqrt{2})}{(\mathrm{x}-1-\sqrt{3})(\mathrm{x}-1+\sqrt{3})} \geq 0$
$$
\begin{equation*}
x \in(-\infty, 1-\sqrt{3}) \cup[1-\sqrt{2}, 1+\sqrt{2}] \cup(1+\sqrt{3}, 0) \tag{1}
\end{equation*}
$$
$1-\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}^{2}-2 \mathrm{x}-2} \geq 0 \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}-2 \mathrm{x}-3}{\mathrm{x}^{2}-2 \mathrm{x}-2} \geq 0$
$\Rightarrow \frac{(\mathrm{x}+1)(\mathrm{x}-3)}{(\mathrm{x}-1+\sqrt{3})(\mathrm{x}-1-\sqrt{3})} \geq 0$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\mathrm{x} \in(-\infty,-1] \cup(1-\sqrt{3}, \sqrt{3}+1) \cup[3, \infty) \tag{2}
\end{equation*}
$$
(1) $\cap(2)$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \in(-\infty,-1] \cup[1-\sqrt{2}, 1+\sqrt{2}] \cup[3, \infty)$
$\therefore \alpha+\beta+\gamma+\delta=4$

Question:
Consider the dissociation equilibrium of the following weak acid $\mathrm{HA} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{A}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})$ If the pKa of the acid is 4 , then the pH of 10 mM HA solution is ________. (Nearest integer)

Solution:
$\mathrm{pH}=\frac{1}{2}\left[\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}-\log \mathrm{c}\right]$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{pH}=\frac{1}{2}\left[4-\log 10^{-2}\right] \\
& \mathrm{pH}=3
\end{aligned}
$$

Question:

Statement I: Griss-Ilosvay test is used for the detection of nitrite ion, which involves the use of sulphanilic acid and $\alpha$-naphthylamine reagent.
Statement II: In the above test, sulphanilic acid is diazotized by the acidified nitrite ion, which on further coupling with $\alpha$-naphthylamine forms an azo-dye.

Both Statement I and Statement II are true

$\mathrm{NO}_{2}^{-}+\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH} \rightarrow \mathrm{HNO}_{2}+\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}$

Question:
Regarding the hydrides of group 15 elements $\mathrm{EH}_{3}$ ( $\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{N}, \mathrm{P}, \mathrm{As}, \mathrm{Sb}$ ), select the correct statement from the following:
A. The stability of hydrides decreases down the group.
B. The basicity of hydrides decreases down the group.
C. The reducing character increases down the group.
D. The boiling point increases down the group.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
Solution:
Stability: $\mathrm{NH}_{3}>\mathrm{PH}_{3}>\mathrm{AsH}_{3}>\mathrm{SbH}_{3}>\mathrm{BiH}_{3}$
Basicity : $\mathrm{NH}_{3}>\mathrm{PH}_{3}>\mathrm{AsH}_{3}>\mathrm{SbH}_{3}>\mathrm{BiH}_{3}$
Reducing character : $\mathrm{NH}_{3}<\mathrm{PH}_{3}<\mathrm{AsH}_{3}<\mathrm{SbH}_{3}< \mathrm{BiH}_{3}$
Boiling point: $\mathrm{PH}_{3}<\mathrm{AsH}_{3}<\mathrm{NH}_{3}<\mathrm{SbH}_{3}<\mathrm{BiH}_{3}$

Question:
X is the number of geometrical isomers exhibited by $\left[\mathrm{Pt}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right) \mathrm{BrCl}\right]$.
Y is the number of optically inactive isomer(s) exhibited by $\left[\mathrm{CrCl}_{2}(\mathrm{ox})_{2}\right]^{3-}$
Z is the number of geometrical isomers exhibited by $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{3}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right)_{3}\right]$
The value of $\mathrm{X}+\mathrm{Y}+\mathrm{Z}$ is _______.
Solution:
Here
$\mathrm{X}=3$ (Two cis + one trans isomers)
$\mathrm{Y}=1$ (trans isomer)
$\mathrm{Z}=2$ (Fac- mer isomer)
$X+Y+Z=3+1+2=6$

HCl + B → BH+ +Cl–
0.02 M, 0.02 M
x ml, y ml
tf, 0, 0.02y–0.02x, 0.02x

$\left[\mathrm{Sc}^{3+}, \mathrm{Ti}^{3+}\right],\left[\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}, \mathrm{Cr}^{2+}\right],\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}, \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]$ and $\left[\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}, \mathrm{Ti}^{4+}\right]$
Statement II: $\mathrm{Th}^{4+}$ is the strongest reducing agent among $\mathrm{Th}^{4+}, \mathrm{Ce}^{4+}, \mathrm{Gd}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{Eu}^{2+}$.

Question:
$20.0 \mathrm{dm}^{3}$ of an ideal gas ' X ' at 600 K and 0.5 MPa undergoes isothermal reversible expansion until pressure of the gas is 0.2 MPa . Which of the following option is correct?
(Given: $\log 2=0.3010$ and $\log 5=0.6989$ )
Solution:
For isothermal reversible process $\Delta \mathrm{U}=\Delta \mathrm{H}=0$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{W}_{\text {iso }}=-\mathrm{p}_{1} \mathrm{v}_{1} \ln \frac{\mathrm{P}_{1}}{\mathrm{P}_{2}} \\
& \mathrm{~W}_{\text {iso }}=-0.5 \times 10^{6} \times 20 \times 10^{-3} \ln \frac{0.5}{0.2} \\
& \mathrm{~W}_{\text {iso }}=-0.5 \times 10^{6} \times 20 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.303 \times(.6989-.3010) \\
& \mathrm{W} \simeq-9.1 \mathrm{~kJ} \\
& \mathrm{q}=-\mathrm{W}=9.1 \mathrm{~kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

Question:
\[{\text{Ph}} - {\text{CH}} = {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_2}\xrightarrow[{{\text{HBr}}}]{{{{({\text{PhCOO}})}_2}}}{\text{Product}}\]
Consider the above reaction
A. The reaction proceeds through a more stable radical intermediate.
B. The role of peroxide is to generate $\dot{H}$ (Hydrogen radical).
C. During this reaction, benzene is formed as a biproduct.
D. 1-Bromo-2-phenylethane is fanned as the minor product.
E. The same reaction in absence of peroxide proceeds via carbocation intermediate.
Identify the correct statements. Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
Solution:
$\mathrm{Ph}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2} \xrightarrow[\mathrm{HBr}]{(\mathrm{PhCOO})_{2}} \mathrm{Ph}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{Br}$
Anti Markovnikov addition
- Reaction follow radical addition in presence of peroxide
- In absence of peroxide follow carbocation mechanism
- Benzene also formed


\[\mathop {{\text{Ph}}}\limits^ \bullet + {\text{HBr}} \to {\text{Ph}} - {\text{H}} + \mathop {{\text{Br}}}\limits^ \bullet \]

Question:
Consider the following redox reaction taking place in acidic medium
$\mathrm{BH}_{4}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{ClO}_{3}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{BO}_{3}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})$
If the Nernst equation for the above balanced reaction is
$\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\mathrm{o}}-\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{nF}} \ell \mathrm{n} \mathrm{Q}$,
Then the value of $n$ is ________. (Nearest integer)
Solution:
$3 \mathrm{BH}_{4}^{-}+4 \mathrm{ClO}_{3}^{-} \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{BO}_{3}^{-}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
n-factor $=8$
moles $=3$
$\therefore \mathrm{n}=3 \times 8=24$

Question: 500 mL of 1.2 M KI solution is mixed with 500 mL of $0.2\mathrm{M} \ \mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ solution in basic medium. The liberated iodine was titrated with standard $0.1 \mathrm{M} \ \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ solution in the presence of starch indicator till the blue color disappeared. The volume (in L ) of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ consumed is _________. (Nearest integer)

$\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}+\mathrm{I}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{MnO}_{2}+\mathrm{I}_{2}$
$\mathrm{I}_{2}+\mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}{ }^{2-} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{6}{ }^{2-}+\mathrm{I}^{-}$
gram eq of $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}=$ gram eq of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$
$0.2 \times \frac{500}{1000} \times 3=0.1 \times \mathrm{V} \times 1$
$\mathrm{V}=3 \mathrm{~L}$

Question: The correct statement among the following is :
Options: $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_{4}\right]^{2-}$ and $\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_{4}\right]^{2-}$ are diamagnetic and $\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_{4}$ is paramagnetic.
$\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_{4}$ and $\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_{4}\right]^{2-}$ are diamagnetic and $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_{4}\right]^{2-}$ is paramagnetic.
$\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_{4}$ and $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_{4}\right]^{2-}$ are diamagnetic and $\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_{4}\right]^{2-}$ is paramagnetic.
$\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_{4}$ is diamagnetic and $\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_{4}\right]^{2-}$ and $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_{4}\right]^{2-}$ are paramagnetic.
Solution:
$\quad\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_{4}\right]^{2-} \rightarrow 3 \mathrm{~d}^{8} \rightarrow$ diamagnetic $\rightarrow \mathrm{dsp}^{2}$
$\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_{4}\right] \rightarrow 3 \mathrm{~d}^{10} \rightarrow$ diamagnetic $\rightarrow \mathrm{sp}^{3}$
$\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_{4}\right]^{2-} \rightarrow 3 \mathrm{~d}^{8} \rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{2,2} \mathrm{t}_{2}^{2,1,1} \rightarrow \mathrm{sp}^{3} \rightarrow$ paramagnetic.

Question: At $\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K}), 2$ moles of liquid A and 3 moles of liquid B are mixed. The vapour pressure of ideal solution formed is 320 mm Hg . At this stage, one mole of A and one mole of B are added to the solution. The vapour pressure is now measured as 328.6 mm Hg . The vapour pressure (in mm Hg ) of A and B are respectively:
Solution:
$P_{S}=X_{A} P_{A}^{\circ}+X_{B} P_{B}^{\circ}$
$320=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}^{\circ}\left(\frac{2}{5}\right)+\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}^{\circ}\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)$

$$
\begin{equation*}
2 \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}^{\circ}+3 \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}^{\circ}=1600 
\end{equation*}
$$  ..... (I)

Now 1 mole of $A$ & 1 mole of $B$ is added
$\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{A}}^{\prime}=\frac{3}{7}, \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{B}}^{\prime}=\frac{4}{7}$
$\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{S}}^{\prime}=328.6=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}^{\circ}\left(\frac{3}{7}\right)+\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}^{\circ}\left(\frac{4}{7}\right)$

$$
\begin{equation*}
3 \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}^{\circ}+4 \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}^{\circ}=2300.2 
\end{equation*} 
$$ ..... (II)
Now eq (I) $\times 3-$ eq (II) $\times 2$
$6 \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}^{\circ}+9 \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}^{\circ}=4800$
$6 \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}^{\circ}+8 \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}^{\circ}=4600.4$
$\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}^{\circ} \simeq 200 \mathrm{~mm}$ of Hg
$\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}^{\circ} \simeq 500 \mathrm{~mm}$ of Hg

Stability $\propto \% \mathrm{~S}$
Order of stability
$\mathrm{CH} \equiv \overline{\mathrm{C}}>\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{C} \overline{\mathrm{H}}>\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{C} \overline{\mathrm{H}}_{2}$

Question: An insulated cylinder of volume $60 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$ is filled with a gas at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and 2 atmospheric pressure. Then the gas is compressed making the final volume as $20 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$ while allowing the temperature to rise to $77^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The final pressure is $\_\_\_\_$ atmospheric pressure.
Solution:
$\mathrm{PV}=\mathrm{nRT}$
$\frac{\mathrm{P}_{1} \mathrm{~V}_{1}}{\mathrm{RT}_{1}}=\frac{\mathrm{P}_{2} \mathrm{~V}_{2}}{\mathrm{RT}_{2}}$
$\frac{2 \times 10^{5} \times 60}{300}=\frac{\mathrm{P}_{2} \times 20}{350}$
$\mathrm{P}_{2}=\frac{2 \times 10^{5} \times 7 \times 3}{6}$
$P_{2}=7 \times 10^{5}=7 \mathrm{~atm}$

Question: Two masses m and 2 m are connected by a light string going over a pulley (disc) of mass 30 m with radius $r=0.1 \mathrm{~m}$. The pulley is mounted in a vertical plane and it is free to rotate about its axis. The 2 m mass is released from rest and its speed when it has descended through a height of 3.6 m is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$. (Assume string does not slip and $\left.\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right)$
Solution:
Using energy conservation
$\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}+\frac{1}{2} 2 m v^{2}+\frac{1}{2} \frac{30 m R^{2}}{2} \times \frac{v^{2}}{R^{2}}=m g h$
$9 \mathrm{mv}^{2}=\mathrm{mgh}$
$\mathrm{v}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{gh}}{9}}=\sqrt{\frac{10 \times 3.6}{9}}$
$\mathrm{v}=\sqrt{4}=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Pulley problems based on fixed axis rotation

Question: Three small identical bubbles of water having same charge on each coalesce to form a bigger bubble. Then the ratio of the potentials on one initial bubble and that on the resultant bigger bubble is :
Solution:
Using volume conservation
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 3\left(\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}\right)=\left(\frac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}\right) \\
& R=3^{1 / 3} r
\end{aligned}
$$
$\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{i}}}{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{f}}}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{kq}}{\mathrm{r}}}{\frac{\mathrm{k} 3 \mathrm{q}}{\mathrm{R}}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{3 \mathrm{r}}=\frac{3^{1 / 3} \mathrm{r}}{3 \mathrm{r}}=\frac{1}{3^{2 / 3}}$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Potential due to a point charge or combination of charges

Question: A uniform bar of length 12 cm and mass $20 m$ lies on a smooth horizontal table. Two point masses m and $2 m$ are moving in opposite directions with same speed of v and in the same plane as the bar, as shown in figure. These masses strike the bar simultaneously and get stuck to it. After collision the entire system is rotating with angular frequency $\omega$. The ratio of v and $\omega$ is :
Solution:
Using angular momentum conservation about COM of rod :
$\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{i}}=\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{f}}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{m} \times \mathrm{V} \times 4 & +2 \mathrm{~m} \times \mathrm{V} \times 2=\left(\frac{20 \mathrm{~m}(12)^{2}}{12}+\mathrm{m} \times 4^{2}+2 \mathrm{~m} \times 2^{2}\right) \omega \\
8 \mathrm{mV} & =(240 \mathrm{~m}+24 \mathrm{~m}) \omega \\
8 \mathrm{~V} & =264 \omega \\
\frac{\mathrm{~V}}{\omega} & =33
\end{aligned}
$$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Collision based problems in translation + rotation

Question: A cylindrical conductor of length 2 m and area of cross-section $0.2 \mathrm{~mm}^{2}$ carries an electric current of 1.6 A when its ends are connected to a 2 V battery. Mobility of electrons in the conductor is $\alpha \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{V}$.s. The value of $\alpha$ is : (electron concentration $=5 \times 10^{28} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$ and electron charge $=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}$ )
Solution:
$\mathrm{V}_{\ell}=\mu \mathrm{E}=\mu \times \frac{\mathrm{V}}{\ell}$
$\mathrm{I}=\mathrm{neAV}_{\mathrm{d}}$
$\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{d}}=\frac{\mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{neA}}$
$\mu=\frac{\mathrm{I} \ell}{\text { NneA }}$
$\mu=\frac{1.6 \times 3}{2 \times 5 \times 10^{26} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 2 \times 10^{-7}}$
$\mu=1 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{v} \cdot \mathrm{s}$
$\alpha=1$

Question: A capacitor $P$ with capacitance $10 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~F}$ is fully charged with a potential difference of 6.0 V and disconnected from the battery. The charged capacitor $P$ is connected across another capacitor $Q$ with capacitance $20 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~F}$. The charge on capacitor $Q$ when equilibrium is established will be $\alpha \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{C}$ (assume capacitor $Q$ does not have any charge initially), the value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$。
Solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{C}_{2}=2 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~F} \\
& \mathrm{~V}_{2}=0
\end{aligned}
$$
$V=\frac{C_{1} V_{1}+C_{2} V_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}=\frac{10^{-5} \times 6+0}{3 \times 10^{-5}}$
$\mathrm{V}=2$ volt
$\mathrm{Q}_{2}=\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{~V}=2 \times 10^{-5} \times 2=4 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{C}$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Charge transfer and switch toggling in circuits

Question: If $\in, \mathrm{E}$ and t represent the free space permittivity, electric field and time respectively, then the unit of $\frac{\in E}{t}$ will be :
Solution:
$\frac{\varepsilon \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{t}}=\frac{\varepsilon}{\mathrm{t}} \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon} \frac{\mathrm{q}}{\mathrm{r}^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{AT}}{\mathrm{TL}^{2}}=\left(\mathrm{AL}^{-2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{A} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$

Question:
Consider two boxes containing ideal gases A and B such that their temperatures, pressures and number densities are same. The molecular size of A is half of that of B and mass of molecule A is four times that of B . If the collision frequency in gas B is $32 \times 10^{18}$ /s then collision frequency in gas A is
$\_\_\_\_$ /s.
Solution:
Collision frequency $(z)=\sqrt{2} \pi d^{2} N \sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M}}$
Temp, N are same
$\mathrm{Z} \propto \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{M}}}$
$\mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{\mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{B}}}{2}$
$\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{A}}=4 \mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}}$
$\frac{\mathrm{Z}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{Z}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{\mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{A}}^{2}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{A}}}} \times \frac{\sqrt{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}}}}{\mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{B}}^{2}}=\left(\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{A}}}}\right)\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{B}}}\right)^{2}$
$=\left(\sqrt{\frac{1}{4}}\right)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}$
$\frac{\mathrm{Z}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{Z}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{4}=\frac{1}{8}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{Z}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{32 \times 10^{8}}{8}=4 \times 10^{8} / \mathrm{s}$

Question: Using a simple pendulum experiment g is determined by measuring its time period T. Which of the following plots represent the correct relation between the pendulum length L and time period T?
Solution:
$\mathrm{T}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\ell}{\mathrm{~g}}} \quad \mathrm{~T}^{2}=\frac{4 \pi^{2} \ell}{\mathrm{~g}}$
$\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}^{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{g}}{4 \pi^{2} \ell}$

Question:
A conducting circular loop is rotated about its diameter at a constant angular speed of $100 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$ in a magnetic field of 0.5 T perpendicular to the axis of rotation. When the loop is rotated by $30^{\circ}$ from the horizontal position, the induced EMF is 15.4 mV . The radius of the loop is $\_\_\_\_$ mm .
(Take $\left.\pi=\frac{22}{7}\right)$
Solution:
$\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{B} \omega \mathrm{Asin} \omega \mathrm{t}$
$15.4 \times 10^{-3}=\frac{1}{2} \times 100 \times \frac{22}{7} \mathrm{r}^{2} \times \frac{1}{2}$
$\mathrm{r}=\sqrt{\frac{15.4 \times 28 \times 10^{-5}}{22}}$
$\mathrm{R}=14 \mathrm{~mm}$

Question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I: For a mechanical system of many particles total kinetic energy is the sum of kinetic energies of all the particles.
Statement II : The total kinetic energy can be the sum of kinetic energy of the center of mass w.r.t. to the origin and the kinetic energy of all the particles w.r.t. the center of mass as the reference.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
Options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Solution:
$\quad K E=\frac{1}{2} m_{1} v_{1}^{2}+\frac{1}{2} m_{2} v_{2}^{2}$
$\mathrm{KE}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{~m}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2}\right) \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{cm}}^{2}+\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{~m}_{1} \mathrm{~m}_{2}}{\mathrm{~m}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2}}\left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{1}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{2}\right|^{2}$

Question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I: An object moves from position $r_{1}$ to position $r_{2}$ under a conservative force field $\vec{F}$. The work done by the force is $W=-\int_{r_{1}}^{r_{2}} \vec{F} \cdot \overrightarrow{d r}$.
Statement II : Any object moving from one location to another location can follow infinite number of paths. Therefore, the amount of work done by the object changes with the path it follows for a conservative force.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
Options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Solution:
Statement-I : Incorrect
Correct equation is $W=\int_{r_{1}}^{r_{2}} \vec{F} \cdot d \vec{r}$
Statement-II : Incorrect

Question:
Light is incident on a metallic plate having work function $110 \times 10^{-20} \mathrm{~J}$. If the produced photoelectrons have zero kinetic energy then the angular frequency of the incident light is
$\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s} .\left(\mathrm{h}=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J} . \mathrm{s}\right)$
Solution:
$\phi=\mathrm{h} v$
$v=\frac{\phi}{\mathrm{h}}$
$\omega=2 \pi \nu=\frac{2 \pi \phi}{\mathrm{~h}}=\frac{2 \times 3.14 \times 110 \times 10^{-2}}{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}$
$\omega=1.04 \times 10^{16} \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{sec}$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

Question: A laser beam has intensity of $4.0 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$. The amplitude of magnetic field associated with beam is $\_\_\_\_$ T. ( Take $\epsilon_{0}=8.85 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}^{2} / \mathrm{Nm}^{2}$ and $\mathrm{c}=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ )
Solution:
$\mathrm{I}=\frac{1}{2} \in_{0} \mathrm{E}_{0}^{2} \cdot \mathrm{C}$
$\therefore \mathrm{E}_{0}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{I}}{\epsilon_{0} \mathrm{C}}}$
$\& \frac{\mathrm{E}_{0}}{\mathrm{~B}_{0}}=\mathrm{C}$
$\therefore B_{0}=\frac{E_{0}}{C}=\frac{1}{C} \sqrt{\frac{2 I}{\epsilon_{0} C}}$
$\therefore \mathrm{B}_{0}=\frac{1}{3 \times 10^{8}} \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 4 \times 10^{14}}{8.85 \times 10^{-12} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}}$
$\mathrm{B}_{0}=\frac{10}{3} \sqrt{\frac{8}{8.85 \times 3}}$
$\mathrm{B}_{0}=1.83 \mathrm{~T}$

Question: The wavelength of light, while it is passing through water is 540 nm . The refractive index of water is $\frac{4}{3}$. The wavelength of the same light when it is passing through a transparent medium having refractive index of $\frac{3}{2}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ nm .
Solution:
$\left.\frac{\mu_{1}}{\mu_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{v}_{2}}{\mathrm{v}_{1}}=\frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}} \right\rvert\, \mathrm{v}=\mathrm{f} \lambda$
$\frac{\mu_{1}}{\mu_{2}}=\frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}}$
$\frac{\mu_{\mathrm{B}}}{3 / 2}=\frac{\lambda}{540}$
$\lambda=\left(\frac{4 \times 2}{3 \times 3} \times 540\right)$
$\lambda=480 \mathrm{~nm}$

Question:
Which of the following are true for a single slit diffraction?
(A) Width of central maxima increases with increase in wavelength keeping slit width constant.
(B) Width of central maxima increases with decrease in wavelength keeping slit width constant.
(C) Width of central maxima increases with decrease in slit width at constant wavelength.
(D) Width of central maxima increases with increase in slit width at constant wavelength.
(E) Brightness of central maxima increases for decrease in wavelength at constant slit width.
Solution:
$\beta_{\mathrm{cm}}=\frac{2 \lambda \mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{a}}$
(A) Correct $\beta \propto \lambda$
(B) Incorrect
(C) Correct $\beta \propto \frac{1}{d}$
(D) Incorrect
(E) Correct
Statement A, C & E are correct.
No option matching

Question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I: A satellite is moving around earth in the orbit very close to the earth surface. The time period of revolution of satellite depends upon the density of earth.
Statement II : The time period of revolution of the satellite is $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{R_{e}}{g}}$ (for satellite very close to the earth surface), where $R_{e}$ radius of earth and $g$ acceleration due to gravity.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
Options: Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Solution:
$\mathrm{T}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{R}^{3}}{\mathrm{GM}}}$
$\because \mathrm{M}=\rho \cdot \frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{R}^{3}$
$\therefore \mathrm{T}=2 \mathrm{gp} \sqrt{\frac{1}{\mathrm{Gp} \frac{4}{3} \pi}}$
Statement I is correct.
And $\therefore \frac{\mathrm{GM}}{\mathrm{R}^{2}}=\mathrm{g}$
$\therefore \mathrm{T}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{g}}}$
Statement II is correct

$\mathrm{Y}=(\mathrm{A} \cdot \mathrm{B})+(\overline{\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{D}})$

Question: When a part of a straight capillary tube is placed vertically in a liquid, the liquid raises upto certain height h . If the inner radius of the capillary tube, density of the liquid and surface tension of the liquid decrease by $1 \%$ each, then the height of the liquid in the tube will change by $\_\_\_\_$ %.
Solution:
$\mathrm{h}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~T} \cos \theta}{\rho \mathrm{gr}}$
$\frac{\Delta \mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{h}} \%=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{T}} \%-\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} \%-\frac{\Delta \mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{r}} \%$
$\frac{\Delta \mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{h}} \%=1+1+1$
$\frac{\Delta \mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{h}}=+1 \%$

Question: An electric power line having total resistance of $2 \Omega$, delivers 1 kW of power of 250 V . The percentage efficiency of transmission line is $\_\_\_\_$。
Solution:
$\mathrm{P}_{\text {out }}=1000 \mathrm{~W}$
$\mathrm{P}=\mathrm{VI}$
$1000=250 \times \mathrm{I}$
$\mathrm{I}=4 \mathrm{~A}$
$\mathrm{P}_{\text {loss }}=\mathrm{I}^{2} \mathrm{R}=(4)^{2} \times 2=3200$
$\mathrm{P}_{\text {net }}=1000+32=103200$
$\eta=\left(\frac{P_{\text {out }}}{P_{\text {net }}}\right) \times 100=\frac{1000}{1032} \times 100=96.9 \%$

Question: In parallax method for the determination of focal length of a concave mirror, the object should always be placed :
Options: between the focus (F) and the centre of curvature (C) of the mirror ONLY
at any point beyond the focus $(\mathrm{F})$ of the mirror
beyond the centre of the curvature ( C ) of the mirror ONLY
between the pole ( P ) and the focus ( F ) of the concave mirror ONLY
Solution: Image should be real. So object should be placed beyond focus.

Question:
In an open organ pipe $v_{3}$ and $v_{6}$ are $3^{\text {rd }}$ and $6^{\text {th }}$ harmonic frequencies, respectively.

If $v_{6}-v_{3}=2200 \mathrm{~Hz}$ then length of the pipe is
$\_\_\_\_$ mm.
(Take velocity of sound in air is $330 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$.)
Solution:
$\quad \mathrm{f}=\mathrm{n}\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}_{0}}{2 \mathrm{~L}}\right)$
$\frac{6 \mathrm{~V}_{0}}{2 \mathrm{~L}}-\frac{3 \mathrm{~V}_{0}}{2 \mathrm{~L}}=2200$
$\frac{3 \mathrm{~V}_{0}}{2 \mathrm{~L}}=2200$
$\frac{3 \times 330}{2 \times L}=2200$
$\mathrm{L}=\frac{3 \times 330}{2 \times 2200}$
$\mathrm{L}=0.225 \mathrm{~m}$
$\mathrm{L}=225 \mathrm{~mm}$

Question: The smallest wavelength of Lyman series is 91 nm . The difference between the largest wavelengths of Paschen and Balmer series is nearly $\_\_\_\_$ nm .
Solution:
$\mathrm{n}=4$
$\mathrm{n}=3$ - paschen
$\mathrm{n}=2$ - Balmer
$\mathrm{n}=1$ Lyman
Smallest wavelength of lyman
$\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left(\frac{1}{12}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}\right)$
$\mathrm{R}=\frac{1}{\lambda}=\frac{1}{91} \mathrm{~nm}^{-1}$
$\lambda_{\text {max }}$ for balmer series
$\mathrm{n}_{1}=2 \rightarrow \mathrm{n}_{2}=3$
$\frac{1}{\lambda_{\mathrm{B}}}=\mathrm{R}\left(\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{9}\right)$
$\frac{1}{\lambda_{\text {B }}}=\frac{1}{91}\left(\frac{5}{36}\right)$
$\lambda_{\mathrm{B}}=\left(\frac{91 \times 36}{5}\right)=655.2 \mathrm{~nm}$
$\lambda_{\text {max }}$ paschen
$\mathrm{n}_{1}=3 \rightarrow \mathrm{n}_{2}=4$
$\frac{1}{\lambda_{p}}=\frac{1}{91}\left(\frac{1}{3^{2}}-\frac{1}{4^{2}}\right)=\frac{1}{91} \times \frac{7}{144}$
$\lambda_{\mathrm{p}}=\left(\frac{91 \times 144}{7}\right)=1872 \mathrm{~nm}$
$\Delta \lambda=\lambda_{\mathrm{P}}-\lambda_{\mathrm{B}}=1872-655.2$
$\Delta \lambda=1216.8$
$\Delta \lambda \approx 1217$

Question: Let L be the line $\frac{\mathrm{x}+1}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}+1}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{z}+3}{6}$ and let S be the set of all points $(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c})$ on L , whose distance from the line $\frac{x+1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{3}=\frac{z+9}{0}$ along the line L is 7 . Then $\sum_{(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}) \in \mathrm{S}}(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c})$ is equal to :
Solution:
$M$ is the point of intersection of $L_{1} \& L_{2}$
$\Rightarrow 2 \lambda-1=2 \mu-1,3 \lambda-1=3 \mu-1,6 \lambda-3=9$
$\Rightarrow \lambda=2=\mu$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{M}(3,5,9)$
Now let point P be $(2 \mathrm{~K}-1,3 \mathrm{~K}-1,6 \mathrm{~K}-3)$ on $\mathrm{L}_{2}$ such that $\mathrm{PM}=7$
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{(2 \mathrm{~K}-4)^{2}+(3 \mathrm{~K}-6)^{2}+(6 \mathrm{~K}-12)^{2}}=7$
$\Rightarrow 49 \mathrm{~K}^{2}+196-196 \mathrm{~K}=49$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{K}^{2}+4-4 \mathrm{~K}=1$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{K}^{2}-4 \mathrm{~K}+3=0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{K}=1,3$
So points $\mathrm{P} \& \mathrm{Q}$ are $(1,2,3) \&(5,8,15)$
So sum of all co-ordinates of $\mathrm{P} \& \mathrm{Q}=34$

Question: Let $\mathrm{P}(10,2 \sqrt{15})$ be a point on the hyperbola $\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{\mathrm{a}^{2}}-\frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}}{\mathrm{~b}^{2}}=1$, whose foci are S and $\mathrm{S}^{\prime}$. If the length of its latus rectum is 8 , then the square of the area of $\triangle \mathrm{PSS}^{\prime}$ is equal to :
Solution:
$\quad \mathrm{P}(10,2 \sqrt{15})$ lies on $\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{\mathrm{a}^{2}}-\frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}}{\mathrm{~b}^{2}}=1$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\therefore \frac{100}{\mathrm{a}^{2}}-\frac{60}{\mathrm{~b}^{2}}=1 \tag{1}
\end{equation*}
$$
$\because $ length of latus rectum $=8$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\frac{2 \cdot \mathrm{~b}^{2}}{\mathrm{a}}=8 \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~b}^{2}}{\mathrm{a}}=4 \tag{2}
\end{equation*}
$$
From (1) & (2)
$\frac{100}{\mathrm{a}^{2}}-\frac{60}{4 \mathrm{a}}=1$
$400-60 \mathrm{a}=4 \mathrm{a}^{2}$
$4 a^{2}+60 a-400=0$
$\mathrm{a}^{2}+15 \mathrm{a}-100=0$
$\mathrm{a}=5 \&-20$ (rejected)
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{b}=\sqrt{20}$
$\therefore $ Hyperbola is $\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{25}-\frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}}{20}=1$
$\therefore $ Focal length $\mathrm{S}_{1} \mathrm{~S}_{2}=2 \mathrm{ae}=2.5 \cdot\left(\sqrt{1+\frac{4}{5}}\right)=6 \sqrt{5}$
$\therefore $ Area of $\triangle \mathrm{PS}_{1} \mathrm{~S}_{2}=\frac{1}{2} \cdot 6 \sqrt{5} \cdot 2 \sqrt{15}=30 \sqrt{3}=\mathrm{A}$
$\therefore \mathrm{A}^{2}=2700$

Question:
The area of the region
$\mathrm{A}=\left\{(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y}): 4 \mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2} \leq 8\right.$ and $\left.\mathrm{y}^{2} \leq 4 \mathrm{x}\right\}$ is :
Solution:
$\mathrm{A}=\int_{0}^{2} 2 \sqrt{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}+2 \int_{1}^{\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{8-4 \mathrm{x}^{2}} \mathrm{dx}$
$=\frac{8}{3}\left(\mathrm{x}^{\frac{3}{2}}\right)|_{0} ^{{1}}+4 \int_{1}^{\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{2-\mathrm{x}^{2}} \mathrm{dx}$
$=\frac{8}{3}+4 \times\left.\frac{1}{2}\left[x \sqrt{2-x^{2}}+2 \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]\right|_{1} ^{\sqrt{2}}$
$=\frac{8}{3}+2\left[2 \times \frac{\pi}{2}-1-2 \times \frac{\pi}{4}\right]$
$=\frac{8}{3}+2 \pi-2-\pi=\pi+\frac{2}{3}$ sq. units

Question:
Let $\alpha, \beta$ be the roots of the quadratic equation
$12 \mathrm{x}^{2}-20 \mathrm{x}+3 \lambda=0, \lambda \in \mathbb{Z}$. If $\frac{1}{2} \leq|\beta-\alpha| \leq \frac{3}{2}$, then the sum of all possible values of $\lambda$ is :
Solution:
$\quad \frac{1}{2} \leq|\alpha-\beta| \leq \frac{3}{2}$
$\frac{1}{4} \leq|\alpha-\beta|^{2} \leq \frac{9}{4}$
$\frac{1}{4} \leq(\alpha+\beta)^{2}-4 \alpha \beta \leq \frac{9}{4}$
$\frac{1}{4} \leq \frac{25}{9}-4 \times \frac{\lambda}{4} \leq \frac{9}{4}$
$-\frac{91}{36} \leq-\lambda \leq \frac{-19}{36}$
$\frac{19}{36} \leq \lambda \leq \frac{91}{36}$
$\lambda=1,2$
Sum $=3$

Question: Suppose $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$ are in A.P. and $\mathrm{a}^{2}, 2 \mathrm{~b}^{2}, \mathrm{c}^{2}$ are in G.P. If $\mathrm{a}<\mathrm{b}<\mathrm{c}$ and $\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}=1$, then $9\left(\mathrm{a}^{2}+\mathrm{b}^{2}+\right. \mathrm{c}^{2}$ ) is equal to $\_\_\_\_$。
Solution:
$a = b - d,c = b + d, \Rightarrow b = \frac{1}{3}$

$ \Rightarrow 4{b^4} = {a^2}{c^2}$

$ \Rightarrow 4{b^4} = {\left[ {\left( {b - d} \right)\left( {b + d} \right)} \right]^2}$

$ \Rightarrow \frac{4}{{81}} = {\left( {\frac{1}{9} - {d^2}} \right)^2}$

$ \Rightarrow \left( {\frac{1}{9} - {d^2}} \right) =  \pm \frac{2}{9}$

${d^2} = \frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow d =  + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}$ (as $a < b < c$)

$\therefore \,\,9\left( {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} \right)$

$ = 9\left[ {{{\left( {\frac{1}{3} - \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {\frac{1}{3}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {\frac{1}{3} - \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}} \right)}^2}} \right]$

$ = 9\left[ {\frac{1}{3} + \frac{2}{3}} \right] = 3 + 6 = 9$

Question: Let S be the set of the first 11 natural numbers. Then the number of elements in $A=\{B \subseteq S: n(B)$  $ \geq 2$ and product of all elements in B is even} is _________.
Solution:
A $\{1,2,3 \ldots . .11\}$
$\therefore \mathrm{n}(\mathrm{B}) \geq 2$ & product of all elements in B is even
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Case (i) } \mathrm{n}(\mathrm{~B})=2 \Rightarrow{ }^{11} \mathrm{C}_{2}-{ }^{6} \mathrm{C}_{2} \\
& \mathrm{n}(\mathrm{~B})=3 \Rightarrow{ }^{11} \mathrm{C}_{3}-{ }^{6} \mathrm{C}_{3} \\
& \mathrm{n}(\mathrm{~B})=4 \Rightarrow{ }^{11} \mathrm{C}_{4}-{ }^{6} \mathrm{C}_{4} \\
& \mathrm{n}(\mathrm{~B})=5 \Rightarrow{ }^{11} \mathrm{C}_{5}-{ }^{6} \mathrm{C}_{5} \\
& \mathrm{n}(\mathrm{~B})=6 \Rightarrow{ }^{11} \mathrm{C}_{6}-{ }^{6} \mathrm{C}_{6} \\
& \mathrm{n}(\mathrm{~B})=7 \Rightarrow{ }^{11} \mathrm{C}_{7} \\
& : \\
& \mathrm{n}(\mathrm{~B})=11 \Rightarrow{ }^{11} \mathrm{C}_{11} \\
& \therefore \text { number of set } \mathrm{B} \Rightarrow \sum_{\mathrm{r}=2}^{11}{ }^{11} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}-\sum_{\mathrm{r}=2}^{6}{ }^{6} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}} \\
& =2^{11}-(12)-\left(2^{6}-7\right) \\
& =2048-64-5 \\
& =1979
\end{aligned}
$$

Alternate Solution :
Total subsets $=2^{11}$
No. of subsets having odd terms only $=2^{6}$
No. of subsets having one term only & also having even terms $=5$
Req. ways $=2^{11}-2^{6}-5=1979$ $\geq 2$ and the product of all elements of B is even $\}$ is

Question: Let $\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}$ and $\vec{b}=\lambda \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}, \lambda \in Z$ be two vectors, Let $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}$ be a vector of magnitude 2 in yz -plane. If $|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}|=\sqrt{53}$, then the maximum possible value of $(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}})^{2}$ is equal to :
Solution:
$\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}$
$\vec{b}=\lambda \hat{j}+2 \hat{k} ; \lambda \in Z$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=(-2-\lambda) \hat{\mathrm{i}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \lambda \hat{\mathrm{k}}$
$|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}|=\sqrt{53}$
$\Rightarrow 5 \lambda^{2}+4 \lambda-33=0$
$\lambda=2.2$ or -3
$\Rightarrow \lambda=-3$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$
let $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}=y \hat{\mathrm{j}}+z \hat{\mathrm{k}}$
$|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}|=2$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}^{2}+\mathrm{z}^{2}=4$
$(\vec{c} \cdot \vec{d})^{2}=(4 y+6 z)^{2} \leq\left(\sqrt{4^{2}+6^{2}} \times \sqrt{y^{2}+z^{2}}\right)^{2} \leq 208$

Question:
If $\mathrm{X}=\left[\begin{array}{l}\mathrm{x} \\ \mathrm{y} \\ \mathrm{z}\end{array}\right]$ is a solution of the system of equations
$A X=B$, where adj $A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}4 & 2 & 2 \\ -5 & 0 & 5 \\ 1 & -2 & 3\end{array}\right]$ and
$\mathrm{B}=\left[\begin{array}{l}4 \\ 0 \\ 2\end{array}\right]$, then $|\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}+\mathrm{z}|$ is equal to :
Solution:
$\mathrm{X}=\mathrm{A}^{-1} \mathrm{~B}=\left(\frac{\operatorname{adj} \mathrm{A}}{|\mathrm{A}|}\right) \mathrm{B}$
$= \pm \frac{1}{10}\left(\begin{array}{ccc}4 & 2 & 2 \\ -5 & 0 & 5 \\ 1 & -2 & 3\end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{l}4 \\ 0 \\ 2\end{array}\right)$
$= \pm \frac{1}{10}\left(\begin{array}{c}20 \\ -10 \\ 10\end{array}\right)= \pm\left(\begin{array}{c}2 \\ -1 \\ 1\end{array}\right)$
$\therefore|\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}+\mathrm{z}|=2$

Question: Let $f(x)=[x]^{2}-[x+3]-3, x \in \mathbb{R}$ where $[\bullet]$ is the greatest integer function. Then
Solution:
$f(x)=[x]^{2}-[x]-6=([x]+2)([x]-3)$
(a) $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})>0 \Rightarrow[\mathrm{x}] \in(-\infty,-2) \cup(3, \infty)$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \in(-\infty,-2) \cup[4, \infty)$
(b) $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})<0 \Rightarrow[\mathrm{x}] \in(-2,3)$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \in[-1,3)$
option (2) is correct

$=-6-6$
$=-12$
(d) $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=0 \Rightarrow[\mathrm{x}]=3$ or $[\mathrm{x}]=-2$
infinitely many solutions

Question:
Let n be the number obtained on rolling a fair die. If the probability that the system
$\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{ny}+\mathrm{z}=6$
$x+(n-2) y+(n+1) z=8$
$$
(n-1) y+z=1
$$
Has a unique solution is $\frac{\mathrm{k}}{6}$, then the sum of k and all possible values of $n$ is :
Solution:
$x-n y+z=6$
$x+(n-2) y+(n+1) z=8$
$(\mathrm{n}-1) \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{z}=1$
$\left| egin{array}{ccc}1 & -n & 1 \ 1 & (n-2) & n+1 \ 0 & n-1 & 1\end{array} ight| eq 0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}^{2}-3 \mathrm{n}+2 eq 0$
$\mathrm{n} eq 1,2$
for unique solution $\mathrm{n}=3,4,5,6$
Now
P (probability when system of equations has unique solution) $= rac{4}{6}$
So $\mathrm{k}=4$
Now required sum $=4+(3+4+5+6)=22$

Tags.Topic: Solution of simultaneous linear equations using determinants

Question:
Let $f$ and g be functions satisfying
$f(\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y})=f(\mathrm{x}) f(\mathrm{y}), \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{l})=7$ and $\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y})=\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{xy})$,
$\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{l})=1$, for all $\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y} \in \mathbb{N} . \sum_{\mathrm{x}=1}^{\mathrm{n}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})}{\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x})}\right)=19607$, then n is equal to :
Solution:
$\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y})=\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}) \cdot \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{y}) \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{a}^{\mathrm{x}}$
$\left(\because f(1)=7 \Rightarrow a^{1}=7\right)$
So $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=7^{\mathrm{x}}$
Now
$g(x+y)=g(x y) \quad($ put $y=1)$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x}+1)=\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x})$
so $\mathrm{g}(1)=\mathrm{g}(2)=\mathrm{g}(3)=\ldots=\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{n})=1$
Given $\sum_{\mathrm{x}=1}^{\mathrm{n}} \frac{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})}{\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x})}=19607$
$\sum_{x=1}^{n} \frac{7^{x}}{1}=19607$
$\Rightarrow 7\left(\frac{7^{\mathrm{n}}-1}{7-1}\right)=19607$
$7^{\mathrm{n}}-1=\frac{6}{7} \times 19607$
$7^{\mathrm{n}}=16807 \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=5$

Question: If the mean deviation about the median of the numbers $\mathrm{k}, 2 \mathrm{k}, 3 \mathrm{k}, \ldots ., 1000 \mathrm{k}$ is 500 , then $\mathrm{k}^{2}$ is equal to :
Solution:
$\because$ median $=\frac{1001 \mathrm{k}}{2}=\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{M}}$
$\therefore $ mean deviation about median $=\frac{\sum\left|\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{i}}-\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{M}}\right|}{\mathrm{n}}$
$=\frac{2\left(\frac{\mathrm{k}}{2}+\frac{3 \mathrm{k}}{2}+\frac{5 \mathrm{k}}{2}+\ldots 500 \text { terms }\right)}{1000}$
$=\frac{2 \cdot \frac{\mathrm{k}}{2}(500)^{2}}{1000}=\frac{500 \mathrm{k}}{2}=500$ (given)
$\therefore \mathrm{k}=2$
$\therefore \mathrm{k}^{2}=4$

Question:
Let $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}$ denote the coefficient of $\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{r}}$ in the binomial expansion of $(1+\mathrm{x})^{\mathrm{n}}, \mathrm{n} \in \mathbb{N}, 0 \leq \mathrm{r} \leq \mathrm{n}$.
If $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{C}_{0}-\mathrm{C}_{1}+\frac{2^{2}}{3} \mathrm{C}_{2}-\frac{2^{3}}{4} \mathrm{C}_{3}+\ldots . .+\frac{(-2)^{\mathrm{n}}}{\mathrm{n}+1} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{n}}$, then the value of $\sum_{n=1}^{25} \frac{1}{\mathrm{P}_{2 n}}$ equals.
Solution:
$\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{n}}=\sum_{\mathrm{r}=0}^{\mathrm{n}} \frac{{ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}(-2)^{\mathrm{r}}}{\mathrm{r}+1}=\sum_{\mathrm{r}=0}^{\mathrm{n}} \frac{1}{(\mathrm{n}+1)}{ }^{\mathrm{n}+1} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}+1}(-2)^{\mathrm{r}}$
$=\frac{-1}{2(n+1)} \sum_{r=0}^{n}{ }^{n+1} C_{r+1}(-2)^{r+1}$
$=\frac{-1}{2(\mathrm{n}+1)}\left[(1-2)^{\mathrm{n}+1}-1\right]$
$\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{1}{2(\mathrm{n}+1)}\left[1-(-1)^{\mathrm{n}+1}\right]$
$\mathrm{P}_{2 \mathrm{n}}=\frac{1}{2(2 \mathrm{n}+1)}\left[1-(-1)^{2 \mathrm{n}+1}\right]$
$P_{2 n}=\frac{1}{2 n+1}$
$\sum_{\mathrm{n}=1}^{25} \frac{1}{\mathrm{P}_{2 \mathrm{n}}}=\sum_{\mathrm{n}=1}^{25}(2 \mathrm{n}+1)$
$=3+5+\ldots+51$
$=\frac{25}{2}[51+3]$
$=25 \times 27=675$

Question: The number of elements in the relation $\mathrm{R}=\{(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y})$ : $\left.4 x^{2}+y^{2}<52, x, y \in Z\right\}$ is
Solution:
$4 x^{2}+y^{2}<52, \quad x, y \in Z$
$\quad $↓$\quad $↓
$0 \quad 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \pm 3, \pm 4, \pm 5, \pm 6, \pm 7 \quad \rightarrow \quad 1 \times 15=15$
$\pm 1 \quad 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \pm 3 \ldots \ldots \ldots . \pm 6 \quad \rightarrow \quad 2 \times 13=26$
$\pm 2 \quad 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \pm 3 \ldots \ldots \ldots . \pm 5 \quad \rightarrow \quad 2 \times 11=22$
$\pm 3 \quad 0, \pm 1, \pm 2 \pm 3 \quad \rightarrow \quad 2 \times 7=14$
Number of elements $=77$

Question: Let a vector $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=\sqrt{2} \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\lambda \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \lambda>0$, make an obtuse angle with the vector $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=-\lambda^{2} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \sqrt{2} \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \sqrt{2} \hat{\mathrm{k}}$ and an angle $\theta, \frac{\pi}{6}<\theta< \frac{\pi}{2}$, with the positive z -axis. If the set of all possible values of $\lambda$ is $(\alpha, \beta)-\{\gamma\}$, then $\alpha+\beta+\gamma$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$\frac{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}|}=\cos \theta \Rightarrow \frac{\lambda}{\sqrt{3+\lambda^{2}}}=\cos \theta$
$\Rightarrow 0<\frac{\lambda}{\sqrt{3+\lambda^{2}}}<\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \lambda>0 \& 4 \lambda^{2}<9+3 \lambda^{2} \Rightarrow \lambda^{2}<9$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\Rightarrow \lambda \in(0,3) \tag{1}
\end{equation*}
$$
$\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}<0 \Rightarrow-\sqrt{2} \lambda^{2}-4 \sqrt{2}+4 \sqrt{2} \lambda<0$
$\Rightarrow \lambda^{2}-4 \lambda+4>0 \Rightarrow(\lambda-2)^{2}>0$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\Rightarrow \lambda \neq 2 \tag{2}
\end{equation*}
$$
from (1) & (2)
$\lambda \in(0,3)-\{2\}$
$\therefore \alpha=0, \beta=3, \gamma=2$
$\Rightarrow \alpha+\beta+\gamma=5$

Question: Let $\mathrm{S}=\left\{\mathrm{z} \in \mathbb{C}: 4 \mathrm{z}^{2}+\overline{\mathrm{z}}=0\right\}$. Then $\sum_{\mathrm{z} \in \mathrm{S}}|\mathrm{z}|^{2}$ is equal to :
Solution:
$4 \mathrm{z}^{2}+\overline{\mathrm{z}}=0$
let $z=x+i y$
$4(x+i y)^{2}+x-i y=0$
$4 x^{2}-4 y^{2}+8 x y i+x-i y=0$
$4 x^{2}-4 y^{2}+x=0 \& y(8 x-1)=0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=0$ or $\mathrm{x}=\frac{1}{8}$
If $y=0,4 x^{2}+x=0$
$\mathrm{x}=0, \frac{-1}{4}$
$\therefore \mathrm{z}_{1}=0+0 . \mathrm{i} \quad\left|\mathrm{z}_{1}\right|^{2}=0$
$z_{2}=0-\frac{1}{4} i \quad\left|z_{2}\right|^{2}=\frac{1}{16}$
If $\mathrm{x}=\frac{1}{8}$,
$4 \times \frac{1}{64}-4 y^{2}+\frac{1}{8}=0$
$\Rightarrow 4 y^{2}=\frac{3}{16} \Rightarrow y= \pm \frac{\sqrt{3}}{8}$
$\therefore \mathrm{z}_{3}=\frac{1}{8}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{8}\mathrm{i}$
$\left|z_{3}\right|^{2}=\frac{1}{64}+\frac{3}{64}=\frac{1}{16}$
$\mathrm{z}_{4}=\frac{1}{8}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{8} \mathrm{i}$
$\left|z_{4}\right|^{2}=\frac{1}{64}+\frac{3}{64}=\frac{1}{16}$
$\therefore \sum_{\mathrm{i}=1}^{\mathrm{n}}\left|\mathrm{z}_{\mathrm{i}}\right|^{2}=0+\frac{1}{16}+\frac{1}{16}+\frac{1}{16}=\frac{3}{16}$

Question:
Let [•] be the greatest integer function. If $\alpha=\int_{0}^{64}\left(\mathrm{x}^{1 / 3}-\left[\mathrm{x}^{1 / 3}\right]\right) \mathrm{dx}$, then
$\frac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\alpha \pi}\left(\frac{\sin ^{2} \theta}{\sin ^{6} \theta+\cos ^{6} \theta}\right) \mathrm{d} \theta$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$\because \int_{0}^{64} \mathrm{x}^{\frac{1}{3}} \mathrm{dx}=\frac{3}{4} \cdot\left[\mathrm{x}^{\frac{4}{3}}\right]_{0}^{64}=192$ &
$\int_{0}^{64}\left[\mathrm{x}^{1 / 3}\right] \mathrm{dx}=\int_{0}^{1}\left[\mathrm{x}^{1 / 3}\right] \mathrm{dx}+\int_{1}^{8}\left[\mathrm{x}^{1 / 3}\right] \mathrm{dx}+\int_{8}^{27}\left[\mathrm{x}^{1 / 3}\right] \mathrm{dx}+\int_{27}^{64}\left[\mathrm{x}^{1 / 3}\right] \mathrm{dx}=156$
So $\alpha=192-156=36$
Now $E=\frac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{36 \pi} \frac{\sin ^{2} \theta}{\sin ^{6} \theta+\cos ^{6} \theta} d \theta$
$=\frac{36}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\sin ^{2} \theta}{\sin ^{6} \theta+\cos ^{6} \theta} d \theta$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{E}=\frac{36 \cdot 2}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin ^{2} \theta \mathrm{~d} \theta}{\sin ^{6} \theta+\cos ^{6} \theta}$
Let $\mathrm{J}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin ^{2} \theta}{\sin ^{6} \theta+\cos ^{6} \theta} \mathrm{~d} \theta$
Applying King
$$
\begin{equation*}
J=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos ^{2} \theta}{\sin ^{6} \theta+\cos ^{6} \theta} d \theta \tag{2}
\end{equation*}
$$
Now $2 \mathrm{~J}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{1}{\sin ^{6} \theta+\cos ^{6} \theta} \mathrm{~d} \theta \quad(\operatorname{add}(1) \&(2))$
$=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sec ^{6} \theta}{\tan ^{6} \theta+1} \mathrm{~d} \theta$
$=\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\left(1+\lambda^{2}\right)}{\lambda^{4}-\lambda^{2}+1} d \lambda$
$=\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{1+\frac{1}{\lambda^{2}}}{\lambda^{2}-1+\frac{1}{\lambda^{2}}} d \lambda$
$=\pi$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{J}=\frac{\pi}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{E}=\frac{36 \cdot 2}{\pi} \times \mathrm{J}=36$

Question: If $\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{e^{(a-1) x}+2 \cos b x+(c-2) e^{-x}}{x \cos x-\log _{e}(1+x)}=2$, then $a^{2}+ \mathrm{b}^{2}+\mathrm{c}^{2}$ is equal to :
Solution:
$\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left(1+(a-1) x+\frac{(a-1)^{2} x^{2}}{2!}\right)+2\left(1-\frac{b^{2} x^{2}}{2!}\right)+(c-2)\left(1-x+\frac{x^{2}}{2!}\right)}{x\left(1-\frac{x^{2}}{2!}\right)-\left(x-\frac{x^{2}}{2} \cdots\right)}=2$
$\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{(1+2+c-2)+x(a-1-c+2)+x^{2}\left(\frac{(a-1)^{2}}{2}-b^{2}+\left(\frac{c-2}{2}\right)\right)}{\frac{x^{2}}{2}-\frac{x^{3}}{2!}+\ldots}=2$
For which
$\because c+1=0 \Rightarrow c=-1$
$\because \mathrm{a}-\mathrm{c}=-1 \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=-2$
$\because \frac{(\mathrm{a}-1)^{2}}{2}-\mathrm{b}^{2}+\left(\frac{\mathrm{c}-2}{2}\right)=1$
$\frac{9}{2}-\mathrm{b}^{2}-\frac{3}{2}=1 \Rightarrow \mathrm{~b}^{2}=2$
$\mathrm{a}^{2}+\mathrm{b}^{2}+\mathrm{c}^{2}=4+2+1=7$

Question:
Let $\cos (\alpha+\beta)=-\frac{1}{10}$ and $\sin (\alpha-\beta)=\frac{3}{8}$, where $0<\alpha<\frac{\pi}{3}$ and $0<\beta<\frac{\pi}{4}$.
If $\tan 2 \alpha=\frac{3(1-r \sqrt{5})}{\sqrt{11}(s+\sqrt{5})}, r, s \in \mathbb{N}$, then $r+s$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$。
Solution:
$\tan 2 \alpha=\tan [(\alpha+\beta)+(\alpha-\beta)]$
$\tan 2 \alpha=\frac{\tan (\alpha+\beta)+\tan (\alpha-\beta)}{1-\tan (\alpha+\beta) \cdot \tan (\alpha-\beta)}$
$\tan 2 \alpha=\frac{\left(-\sqrt{99}+\frac{3}{\sqrt{55}}\right)}{1-(\sqrt{99})\left(\frac{3}{\sqrt{55}}\right)}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \tan 2 \alpha=\frac{-3 \sqrt{11}+\frac{3}{\sqrt{5} \times \sqrt{11}}}{1+\frac{9 \sqrt{11}}{\sqrt{5} \times \sqrt{11}}} \\
& \tan 2 \alpha=\frac{3(1-11 \sqrt{5})}{\sqrt{11}(9+\sqrt{5})} \\
& r=11, s=9 \\
& r+s=20
\end{aligned}
$$

Question:
If $y=y(x)$ satisfies the differential equation
$16(\sqrt{x+9 \sqrt{x}})(4+\sqrt{9+\sqrt{x}}) \cos y d y=(1+2$ siny)dx, $x>0$ and $y(256)=\frac{\pi}{2}, y(49)=\alpha$, then 2 $\sin \alpha$ is equal to :
Solution:
$\int \frac{\cos y}{1+2 \sin y} d y=\int \frac{d x}{16(\sqrt{9 \sqrt{x}+x})(4+\sqrt{9+\sqrt{x}})}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 4+\sqrt{9+\sqrt{x}}=t \\
& \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{9+\sqrt{x}}} \times \frac{d x}{2 \sqrt{x}}=1 d x
\end{aligned}
$$
$\frac{1}{2} \ell \mathrm{n}|1+2 \sin \mathrm{y}|=\int \frac{4 \mathrm{dt}}{16 \mathrm{t}}+\mathrm{C}$
$\left.\frac{1}{2} \ell \mathrm{n}|1+2 \sin \mathrm{y}|=\frac{1}{4} \ell \mathrm{n} \right\rvert\, 4+\sqrt{9+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}}}+\mathrm{C}$
$\frac{1}{2} \ell \mathrm{n}(2 \sin \mathrm{y}+1)=\frac{1}{4} \ell \mathrm{n}|4+\sqrt{9+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}}}|+\mathrm{C}$
Substituting $\left(256, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$
$\frac{1}{2} \ell \mathrm{n} 3=\frac{1}{2} \ell \mathrm{n} 3+\mathrm{C} \quad \mathrm{C}=0$
Substituting $(49, \alpha)$
$\frac{1}{2} \ell \mathrm{n}(2 \sin \alpha+1)=\frac{1}{4} \ell \mathrm{n} 8$
$\ell \mathrm{n}(2 \sin \alpha+1)=\frac{1}{2} \ell \mathrm{n} 8$
$\ell \mathrm{n}(2 \sin \alpha+1)=\ell \mathrm{n} 2 \sqrt{2}$
$2 \sin \alpha+1=2 \sqrt{2}$
$2 \sin \alpha=2 \sqrt{2}-1$

Question:
Among the statements
(S1) : If $\mathrm{A}(5,-1)$ and $\mathrm{B}(-2,3)$ are two vertices of a triangle, whose orthocentre is $(0,0)$, then its third vertex is $(-4,-7)$ and
(S2) : If positive numbers $2 \mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$ are three consecutive terms of an A.P., then the lines $\mathrm{ax}+$ by $+\mathrm{c}=0$ are concurrent at ( $2,-2$ )
Solution:
Solution of statement-1
$\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{AO}} \cdot \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{BC}}=-1$

$$
\begin{equation*}
\Rightarrow 5 \mathrm{~h}-\mathrm{k}+13=0 \tag{1}
\end{equation*}
$$
& $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{AB}} \cdot \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{OC}}=-1$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\Rightarrow 4 \mathrm{k}=7 \mathrm{~h} \tag{2}
\end{equation*}
$$
$ \Rightarrow $ third vertex is $(-4,-7)$
$\therefore $ Statement 1 is correct.
Solution of statement-2
$2 \mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c} \rightarrow$ A.P.
$\mathrm{b}=\frac{2 \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{c}}{2}$
$\Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{a}-2 \mathrm{~b}+\mathrm{c}=0$
$\because $ lines $\mathrm{ax}+\mathrm{by}+\mathrm{c}=0$ are concurrent then
$\frac{\mathrm{x}}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}}{-2}=\frac{1}{1}$
$\mathrm{x}=2$ and $\mathrm{y}=-2$
$\therefore $ Point of concurrency is $(2,-2)$
$\therefore $ Statement 2 is correct.
