Question: The exit surface of a prism with refractive index $n$ is coated with a material having refractive index $\frac{\mathrm{n}}{2}$. When this prism is set for minimum angle of deviation it exactly meets the condition of critical angle. The prism angle is
Solution:
$\quad i=e \& r=A / 2$ for minimum deviation

For TIR ; $r=\theta_{\text {c }}$

$\operatorname{Sin} \mathrm{r}=\operatorname{Sin} \theta_{\mathrm{c}}$
$\operatorname{Sin} \mathrm{r}=\frac{\mathrm{n} / 2}{\mathrm{n}}$
$\operatorname{Sin} \mathrm{r}=\frac{1}{2}$
$\operatorname{Sin} \frac{\mathrm{A}}{2}=\sin 30^{\circ}$
$\frac{\mathrm{A}}{2}=30^{\circ}$
$\mathrm{A}=60^{\circ}$

Question: A gas of certain mass filled in a closed cylinder at a pressure of 3.23 kPa has temperature $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The gas is now heated to double its temperature. The modified pressure is $\_\_\_\_$ Pa.
Solution:
As per NTA
$\mathrm{V}=$ constant
so $\mathrm{P} \propto \mathrm{T}$
$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{i}}=50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=323 \mathrm{~K}$
$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}=100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=373 \mathrm{~K}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{i}}}=\frac{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{f}}}{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{i}}}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}}}{3.23 \mathrm{kPa}}=\frac{373}{323}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}}=3730 \mathrm{~Pa}$
Other Solution:
As volume is constant
$\therefore \mathrm{P} \propto \mathrm{T}$
Since T is doubled (must be in Kelvin) so pressure must be doubled.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore P_{f}=2 P_{i} \\
& P_{f}=2 \times 3.23=6.46 \mathrm{KPa} \\
& P_{f}=6460 \mathrm{~Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

Question: A short bar magnet placed with its axis at $30^{\circ}$ with an external field of 800 Gauss, experiences a torque of 0.016 N.m. The work done in moving it from most stable to most unstable position is $\alpha \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~J}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$\tau=\mu \mathrm{B} \sin \theta \Rightarrow 0.016=\mu \times \mathrm{B} \times \frac{1}{2}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \mu=\frac{0.032}{B} \\
& W_{e x t}=U_{f}-U_{i}=\mu B-(\mu B)=2 \mu B \\
& =2 \times \frac{0.032}{B} \times B \\
& =0.064 \mathrm{~J}
\end{aligned}
$$

Question:
Three masses $200 \mathrm{~kg}, 300 \mathrm{~kg}$ and 400 kg are placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle with sides 20 m . They are rearranged on the vertices of a bigger triangle of side 25 m and with the same centre. The work done in this process $\_\_\_\_$ J.
(Gravitational constant $\mathrm{G}=6.7 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{kg}^{2}$ )
Solution:
Work done by external agent :
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{w}_{\text {ext }}=\Delta \mathrm{U} \\
& \mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{i}}=-\frac{\mathrm{Gm}_{1} \mathrm{~m}_{2}}{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{i}}}-\frac{\mathrm{Gm}_{2} \mathrm{~m}_{3}}{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{i}}}-\frac{\mathrm{Gm}_{1} \mathrm{~m}_{3}}{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{i}}}: \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{i}}=20 \mathrm{~m} \\
& \mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{f}}=-\frac{\mathrm{Gm}_{1} \mathrm{~m}_{2}}{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{f}}}-\frac{\mathrm{Gm}_{2} \mathrm{~m}_{3}}{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{f}}}-\frac{\mathrm{Gm}_{1} \mathrm{~m}_{3}}{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{f}}}: \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{f}}=25 \mathrm{~m} \\
& \mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{i}}=\frac{-6.67 \times 10^{-11}}{20}[200 \times 300+300 \times 400+200 \times 400] \\
& =\frac{-6.67 \times 10^{-11}}{20} \times 26 \times 10^{4}=-86.71 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~J} \\
& \mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{f}}=\frac{-6.67 \times 10^{-11}}{0.25}[200 \times 300+300 \times 400+200 \times 400] \\
& =\frac{-6.67 \times 10^{-11}}{0.25} \times 26 \times 10^{4}=-693.68 \times 10^{-9} \\
& =-69.36 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~J} \\
& \Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{i}}=1.74 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~J}
\end{aligned}
$$

Question: There are three co-centric conducting spherical shells $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$ and C of radii $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}$ and c respectively. The potential of the spheres $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$ and C respectively, are :
Solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{\mathrm{Kq}_{1}}{\mathrm{a}}+\frac{\mathrm{Kq}_{2}}{\mathrm{~b}}+\frac{\mathrm{Kq}_{3}}{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{q}_{1}}{\mathrm{a}}+\frac{\mathrm{q}_{2}}{\mathrm{~b}}+\frac{\mathrm{q}_{3}}{\mathrm{c}}\right) \\
& \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\mathrm{Kq}_{1}}{\mathrm{~b}}+\frac{\mathrm{Kq}_{2}}{\mathrm{~b}}+\frac{\mathrm{Kq}_{3}}{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{q}_{1}+\mathrm{q}_{2}}{\mathrm{~b}}+\frac{\mathrm{q}_{3}}{\mathrm{c}}\right) \\
& \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{\mathrm{Kq}_{1}}{\mathrm{c}}+\frac{\mathrm{Kq}_{2}}{\mathrm{c}}+\frac{\mathrm{Kq}_{3}}{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{q}_{1}+\mathrm{q}_{2}+\mathrm{q}_{3}}{\mathrm{c}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Question:
A boy thrown a ball into air at $45^{\circ}$ from the horizontal to land it on a roof of a building of height H . If the ball attains maximum height in 2 s and lands on the building in 3 s after launch, then value of H is $\_\_\_\_$ m.
$\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right)$
Solution:
$\mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{y}}}{\mathrm{g}}=4$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{40}{2}=20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
$\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{y}} \Delta \mathrm{t}-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~g}(\Delta \mathrm{t})^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{H}=20 \times 3-5 \times 9$
$=60-45$
$=15 \mathrm{~m}$

Question: An unpolarised light is incident at an interface of two dielectric media having refractive indices of 2 (incident medium) and $2 \sqrt{3}$ (medium) respectively. To satisfy the condition that reflected and refracted rays are perpendicular to each other, the angle of incidence is $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
Brewester's law
$\tan \theta=\mu_{\text {rel }}=\sqrt{3}$
$\theta=60^{\circ}$

Question:
A point charge $\mathrm{q}=1 \mu \mathrm{C}$ is located at a distance 2 cm from one end of a thin insulating wire of length 10 cm having a charge $Q=24 \mu \mathrm{C}$, distributed uniformly along its length, as shown in figure. Force between q and wire is $\_\_\_\_$ N.
(Use $\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}=9 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~N} . \mathrm{m}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}$ )
Solution:
$\mathrm{F}=\int \mathrm{dF}=\int_{2 \mathrm{~cm}}^{12 \mathrm{~cm}} \frac{\mathrm{kq} \lambda \mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}=\mathrm{kq} \lambda\left(\frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-2}}-\frac{1}{12 \times 10^{-2}}\right)$
$\mathrm{F}=\left(9 \times 10^{9}\right)\left(10^{-6}\right)\left(\frac{24 \times 10^{-6}}{10^{-1}}\right)\left(\frac{5}{12}\right) \times 10^{2}$
$=9 \times 24 \times \frac{5}{12}=90 \mathrm{~N}$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Electric field due to standard bodies

Question: In case of vertical circular motion of a particle by a thread of length r if the tension in the thread is zero at an angle $30^{\circ}$ shown in figure, the velocity at the bottom point (A) of the circular path is (g = gravitational acceleration)
Solution:
$\mathrm{T}+\mathrm{mg} \cos 60^{\circ}=\frac{\mathrm{mV}^{2}}{\ell}$
$\mathrm{T}=0$
$\mathrm{V}^{2}=\frac{\mathrm{g} \ell}{2}$ here V is the speed at point A
M.E.C.
$\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mu}^{2}=\mathrm{mg}\left(\ell+\ell \cos 60^{\circ}\right)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mV}^{2}$
$\mathrm{u}^{2}=3 \mathrm{~g} \ell+\frac{\mathrm{g} \ell}{2}$
$\mathrm{u}=\sqrt{\frac{7 \mathrm{~g} \ell}{2}}$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Vertical circular motion involving strings and similar cases

Question: When 300 J of heat given to an ideal gas with $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{p}}=\frac{7}{2} \mathrm{R}$ its temperature raises from $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ keeping its volume constant. The mass of the gas is (approximately) $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{g} .(\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol} . \mathrm{K})$.
Solution:
$C_{v}=C_{P}-R=\frac{5}{2} R$
$\Delta \mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{nC}_{\mathrm{V}} \Delta \mathrm{T}$
$300=\mathrm{n} \times \frac{5}{2} \times 8.314 \times 30$
$\mathrm{n}=0.48$
$\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{M}}=0.48$
We cannot find mass (m) because molar mass (M) not given. NTA gave ans. 481.

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Heat supplied to the gas in a process

Question:
A uniform solid cylinder of length $L$ and radius $R$ has moment of inertia about its axis equal to $I_{1}$. A small co-centric cylinder of length $L / 2$ and radius $\mathrm{R} / 3$ carved from this cylinder has moment of inertia about its axis equals to $I_{2}$. The ratio $I_{1} / I_{2}$ is
$\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
Original mass (M)
The removed mass (m)
$\mathrm{m}=\rho \times \pi\left(\frac{\mathrm{R}}{3}\right)^{2} \times \frac{\mathrm{L}}{2}$
$=\frac{\rho \cdot \pi R^{2} L}{18}=\frac{M}{18}$
$\mathrm{I}^{\prime}=\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{M}}{18} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{R}^{2}}{9}=\frac{1}{324} \mathrm{MR}^{2}$
$\frac{\mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{I}^{\prime}}=\frac{\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{MR}^{2}}{\frac{1}{324} \mathrm{MR}^{2}}=162$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Moment of inertia of standard bodies

Question: Two identical circular loops P and Q each of radius $r$ are lying in parallel planes such that they have common axis. The current through P and Q are I and 4I respectively in clockwise direction as seen from O . The net magnetic field at O is:
Solution:
$B_{n e t}=B_{1}-B_{2}$

$=\frac{4 \mu_{0} i R^{2}}{2\left(R^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}-\frac{\mu_{0} i R^{2}}{2\left(R^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}$
$=\frac{3 \mu_{0} \mathrm{i}}{4 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{R}}$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Magnetic field due to standard shaped objects

Question: In a meter bridge experiment to determine the value of unknown resistance, first the resistances $2 \Omega$ and $3 \Omega$ are connected in the left and right gaps of the bridge and the null point is obtained at a distance $l \mathrm{~cm}$ from the left. Now when an unknown resistance $\mathrm{x} \Omega$ is connected in parallel to $3 \Omega$ resistance, the null point is shifted by 10 cm to the right of wire. The value of unknown resistance $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$.
Solution:
In case I
$$
\begin{equation*}
\frac{2}{3}=\frac{\ell}{(100-\ell)} \tag{1}
\end{equation*}
$$
$\ell=40 \mathrm{~cm}$
In case II
$\frac{2}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{\ell+10}{100-(\ell+10)}$
Put $\ell=40 \mathrm{~cm} \&$ solve
$\mathrm{R}=2 \Omega$
$\therefore \frac{3 \mathrm{x}}{3+\mathrm{x}}=2$
$\mathrm{x}=6 \Omega$

Question: A thin uniform rod $(X)$ of mass $M$ and length $L$ is pivoted at a height $\left(\frac{\mathrm{L}}{3}\right)$ as shown in the figure. The rod is allowed to fall from a vertical position and lie horizontally on the table. The angular velocity of this rod when it hits the table top, is $\_\_\_\_$ . ( $\mathrm{g}=$ gravitational acceleration)
Solution:
$\operatorname{mg} \frac{\ell}{6}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I} \omega^{2}$
Here $I=\frac{m \ell^{2}}{12}+\frac{m \ell^{2}}{36}=\frac{m \ell^{2}}{9}$
$\mathrm{mg} \frac{\ell}{6}=\frac{\mathrm{m} \ell^{2}}{18} \omega^{2} \Rightarrow \omega^{2}=\frac{3 \mathrm{~g}}{\ell}$
$\omega=\sqrt{\frac{3 \mathrm{~g}}{\ell}}$

Question: The fifth harmonic of a closed organ pipe is found to be in unison with the first harmonic of an open pipe. The ratio of lengths of closed pipe to that of the open pipe is $5 / x$. The value of $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$\quad \mathrm{f}_{5 \text { closed }}=\mathrm{f}_{1 \text { open }}$
$\frac{5 \mathrm{v}}{4 \mathrm{~L}_{\text {closed }}}=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{2 \mathrm{~L}_{\text {open }}}$
$\frac{\mathrm{L}_{\text {closed }}}{\mathrm{L}_{\text {open }}}=\frac{5}{2}$
$\mathrm{x}=2$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Organ pipes closed at one end

Question:
A soap bubble of surface tension $0.04 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$ is blown to a diameter of 7 cm . If $(15000-\mathrm{x}) \mu \mathrm{J}$ of work is done in blowing it further to make its diameter 14 cm , then the value of x is $\_\_\_\_$ .
( $\pi=22 / 7$ )
Solution:
$\mathrm{W}=\Delta \mathrm{u}$
$=\mathrm{S} \times\left(8 \pi \mathrm{r}_{2}^{2}-8 \pi \mathrm{r}_{1}^{2}\right)$
$=0.04 \times 2 \times \frac{22}{7}(147) \times 10^{-4}$
$\mathrm{W}=3696 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~J}$
$3696=15000-x$
$\mathrm{x}=11304 \mu \mathrm{~J}$

Question:
When a light of a given wavelength falls on a metallic surface the stopping potential for photoelectrons is 3.2 V . If a second light having wavelength twice of first light is used, the stopping potential drops to 0.7 V . The wavelength of first light is $\_\_\_\_$ m.
( $\mathrm{h}=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J} . \mathrm{s}, \mathrm{e}=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{c}=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ )
Solution:
$\mathrm{q} .(3.2)=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}-\phi$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\mathrm{q}(0.7)=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{2 \lambda}-\phi \tag{1}
\end{equation*}
$$

Eq. (1) - Eq. (2)
$\mathrm{q} \cdot(2.5)=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{2 \lambda}$
$2.5=\left(\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\mathrm{e}}\right)\left(\frac{1}{2 \lambda}\right)$
$$
2.5=\frac{12400}{2(\lambda)}
$$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
Tags.Topic: Photoelectric effect and Einstein's photoelectric equation

Question: Three parallel plate capacitors each with area A and separation d are filled with two dielectric ( $\mathrm{k}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{k}_{2}$ ) in the following fashion. Which of the following is true? $\left(\mathrm{k}_{1}>\mathrm{k}_{2}\right)$
Solution:
For $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{A}}$ :

Let $\frac{\varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d}}=\mathrm{C}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\therefore & \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{\mathrm{K}_{1} \mathrm{C}}{2}+\frac{\mathrm{K}_{1} \mathrm{~K}_{2} \mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{~K}_{1}+\mathrm{K}_{2}} \\
& =\mathrm{K}_{1} \mathrm{C}\left[\frac{\mathrm{~K}_{1}+2 \mathrm{~K}_{2}}{2\left(\mathrm{~K}_{1}+\mathrm{K}_{2}\right)}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$
For $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{B}}$ :

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{B}} & =\frac{\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{C}}{2}+\frac{\mathrm{K}_{1} \mathrm{~K}_{2} \mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{~K}_{1}+\mathrm{K}_{2}} \\
& =\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{C}\left[\frac{\mathrm{~K}_{1}+2 \mathrm{~K}_{2}}{2\left(\mathrm{~K}_{1}+\mathrm{K}_{2}\right)}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$
For $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{C}}$ :

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~K}_{1} \mathrm{~K}_{2} \mathrm{C}}{\left(\mathrm{~K}_{1}+\mathrm{K}_{2}\right)} \\
& \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{A}}>\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{C}}>\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{B}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Question: A regular hexagon is formed by six wires each of resistance $\mathrm{r} \Omega$ and the corners are joined to centre by wires of same resistance. If the current enters at one corner and leaves at the opposite corner, the equivalent resistance of the hexagon between the two opposite corners will be
Solution: $\mathrm{R}_{\text {eq }}=\frac{2 \mathrm{R} \times \frac{4 \mathrm{R}}{3}}{2 \mathrm{R}+\frac{4 \mathrm{R}}{3}}=\frac{8 \mathrm{R}^{2}}{10 \mathrm{R}}=\frac{4}{5} \mathrm{R}$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Calculation of equivalent resistance of a circuit

Question: Distance between an object and three times magnified real image is 40 cm . The focal length of the mirror used is $\_\_\_\_$ cm.
Solution:
$\mathrm{m}=-3=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{u}}$
$\mathrm{v}=-3 \mathrm{u}$
$|\mathrm{v}|-|\mathrm{u}|=40$
$\mathrm{u}=20 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\mathrm{v}=60 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\therefore \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}}$
$\frac{1}{-60}+\frac{1}{-20}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}}$
$\mathrm{f}=-15 \mathrm{~cm}$

Question: Five persons $\mathrm{P}_{1}, \mathrm{P}_{2}, \mathrm{P}_{3}, \mathrm{P}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{P}_{5}$ recorded object distance (u) and image distance (v) using same convex lens having power +5 D as $(25,96),(30,62)$, $(35,37),(45,35)$ and $(50,32)$ respectively. Identify correct statement
Options: Readings recorded by all persons are correct
Reading recorded by $P_{3}$ persons are incorrect
Reading recorded by $\mathrm{P}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{P}_{2}$ persons are incorrect
Reading recorded by $\mathrm{P}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{P}_{5}$ persons are incorrect
Solution:
$\mathrm{P}=+5 \mathrm{D}$
$$
\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}}=5 \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}=20 \mathrm{~cm}
$$
⇒ If object is between f & 2f image will be beyond 2f & magnified.
⇒ If object is beyond 2f, image will be between f & 2f & diminished.
Hence reading of $\mathrm{P}_{3}$ are incorrect.

Question: A moving coil galvanometer of resistance $100 \Omega$ shows a full scale deflection for a current of 1 mA . The value of resistance required to convert this galvanometer into an ammeter, showing full scale deflection for a current of 5 mA , is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$
Solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{G}=100 \Omega \\
& \mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{g}}=1 \mathrm{~mA} \\
& \mathrm{i}=5 \mathrm{~mA} \\
& \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{s}}=\frac{\mathrm{G}}{\left(\frac{\mathrm{i}}{\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{g}}}-1\right)} \\
& =\frac{100}{\left(\frac{5}{1}-1\right)}=25 \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

Question: A cubical block of density $\rho_{\mathrm{b}}=600 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$ floats in a liquid of density $\rho_{\mathrm{e}}=900 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$. If the height of block is $H=8.0 \mathrm{~cm}$ then height of the submerged part is $\_\_\_\_$ cm .
Solution:
$\mathrm{Mg}=\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{b}}$
$\mathrm{dAHg}=\rho \mathrm{Ahg}$
$600 \times 8 \mathrm{~cm}=900 \times \mathrm{h}$
$\mathrm{h}=\frac{16}{3} \mathrm{~cm}$
$\mathrm{h}=5.3 \mathrm{~cm}$

Question: A flexible chain of mass $m$ hangs between two fixed points at the same level. The inclination of the chain with the horizontal at the two points of support is $30^{\circ}$. Considering the equilibrium of each half of the chain, the tension of the chain at the lowest point is $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{T} \sin 30^{\circ}=\frac{\mathrm{m}}{2} \mathrm{~g} \\
& \mathrm{~T} \cos 30^{\circ}=\mathrm{T}_{0} \\
& \tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{\mathrm{mg}}{2 \mathrm{~T}_{0}} \\
& \mathrm{~T}_{0}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \mathrm{mg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Question: Identify the correct truth table of the given logical circuit.

$=\mathrm{A} \cdot(\overline{\mathrm{A}}+\overline{\mathrm{B}})=0+\mathrm{A} \overline{\mathrm{B}}$
ABY

Question: In the Young's double slit experiment the intensity produced by each one of the individual slits is $I_{0}$. The distance between two slits is 2 mm . The distance of screen from slits is 10 m . The wavelength of light is \[6000\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^ \circ  \]. The intensity of light on the screen in front of one of the slits is $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$\mathrm{d}=2 \mathrm{~mm}$
$\mathrm{D}=10 \mathrm{~m}$
\[\lambda  = 6000\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^ \circ  \]
$y=\frac{d}{2}($ in front of one slit $)$
$I=4 I_{0} \cos ^{2}\left(\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{y}{D} d\right)$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\mathrm{I}_{0}$

Question: A point source is kept at the center of a spherically enclosed detector. If the volume of the detector increased by 8 times, the intensity will
Solution:
$\mathrm{V} \rightarrow 8 \mathrm{~V} \Rightarrow \mathrm{R} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{R}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{A} \rightarrow 4 \mathrm{~A}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{I} \rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{I}_{0}}{4}$

Question: The velocity (v) - Distance (x) graph is shown in figure. Which graph represents acceleration (a) versus distance ( x ) variation of this system?
Solution:
Eq. of V vs x from graph
$\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{C}_{1}-\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{x}$
$\mathrm{a}=\mathrm{V} \frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dx}}$
$=\left(\mathrm{C}_{1}-\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{x}\right) \times-\mathrm{C}_{2}$
$\mathrm{a}=\mathrm{C}_{2}^{2} \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{C}_{1} \mathrm{C}_{2}$
∴ graph is straight line + ve solve - ve intercept

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Graphs of motion in a straight line

Question: In a vernier callipers, 50 vernier scale divisions are equal to 48 main scale divisions. If one main scale division $=0.05 \mathrm{~mm}$, then the least count of the vernier callipers is $\_\_\_\_$ mm .
Solution:
$\quad \mathrm{LC}=1 \mathrm{MSD}-1 \mathrm{MSD}=1 \mathrm{MSD}-\frac{48}{50} \mathrm{MSD}$
$$
=\frac{2}{50} \mathrm{MSD}=\frac{2}{50} \times .05 \mathrm{~mm}=0.002 \mathrm{~mm}
$$

Question: 10 mole of an ideal gas is undergoing the process shown in the figure. The heat involved in the process from $\mathrm{P}_{1}$ to $\mathrm{P}_{2}$ is $\alpha$ Joule $\left(\mathrm{P}_{1}=21.7 \mathrm{~Pa}\right.$ and $\left.\mathrm{P}_{2}=30 \mathrm{~Pa}, \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}}=21 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{K} . \mathrm{mol}, R=8.3 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol} . \mathrm{K}\right)$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$。
Solution:
$\Delta \mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{nC}_{\mathrm{v}} \Delta \mathrm{T}$ (isochoric)
$=\frac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}}}{\mathrm{R}} \cdot \mathrm{nR} \Delta \mathrm{T}=\frac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}}}{\mathrm{R}}\left(\mathrm{P}_{2}-\mathrm{P}_{1}\right) \mathrm{V}$
$=\frac{21}{8.3} \times(30-21.7) \times 1=21 \mathrm{~J}$

Question: The reading of the ammeter (A) in steady state in the following circuit (assuming negligible internal resistance of the ammeter) is $\_\_\_\_$ A.
Solution:
In steady state

$$
\mathrm{I}=2 \mathrm{~A}
$$

Ammeter reading is 1A.

Question:
The binding energy for the following nuclear reactions are expressed in MeV .
${ }_{2} \mathrm{He}^{3}+{ }_{0} \mathrm{n}^{1} \rightarrow{ }_{2} \mathrm{He}^{4}+20 \mathrm{MeV}$
${ }_{2} \mathrm{He}^{4}+{ }_{0} \mathrm{n}^{1} \rightarrow{ }_{2} \mathrm{He}^{5}-0.9 \mathrm{MeV}$
If $\mathrm{X}_{3}, \mathrm{X}_{4}, \mathrm{X}_{5}$ denote the stability of ${ }_{2} \mathrm{He}^{3},{ }_{2} \mathrm{He}^{4}$ and ${ }_{2} \mathrm{He}^{5}$, respectively, then the correct order is :
Solution:
$\mathrm{BE}_{\mathrm{He}^{4}}-\mathrm{BE}_{\mathrm{He}^{3}}=20 \mathrm{MeV}$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\mathrm{BE}_{\mathrm{He}^{5}}-\mathrm{BE}_{\mathrm{He}^{4}}=-0.9 \mathrm{MeV} \tag{1}
\end{equation*}
$$
From eq (1) & (2)
$\mathrm{BE}_{\mathrm{He}^{4}}>\mathrm{BE}_{\mathrm{He}^{5}}>\mathrm{BE}_{\mathrm{He}^{3}}$
$X_{4}>X_{5}>X_{3}$

Question:
Let
$A=\{z \in \mathbb{C}:|z-2| \leq 4\}$ and
$\mathrm{B}=\{\mathrm{z} \in \mathbb{C}:|\mathrm{z}-2|+|\mathrm{z}+2|=5\}$.
Then the max $\left\{\left|\mathrm{z}_{1}-\mathrm{z}_{2}\right|: \mathrm{z}_{1} \in \mathrm{~A}\right.$ and $\left.\mathrm{z}_{2} \in \mathrm{~B}\right\}$ is
Solution:
$|z-2| \leq 4 \Rightarrow(x-2)+y^{2} \leq 16$

Question: Let A be the focus of the parabola $\mathrm{y}^{2}=8 \mathrm{x}$. Let the line $\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{mx}+\mathrm{c}$ intersect the parabola at two distinct points B and C . If the centroid of the triangle ABC is $\left(\frac{7}{3}, \frac{4}{3}\right)$, then $(\mathrm{BC})^{2}$ is equal to :
Solution:
Coordinates of centroid of triangle ABC are
$\frac{2}{3}\left(\mathrm{t}_{1}^{2}+\mathrm{t}_{2}^{2}+1\right)=\frac{7}{3} \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}_{1}^{2}+\mathrm{t}_{2}^{2}=\frac{5}{2}$
$\frac{4}{3}\left(\mathrm{t}_{1}+\mathrm{t}_{2}\right)=\frac{4}{3} \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}_{1}+\mathrm{t}_{2}=1$
$\left(\mathrm{t}_{1}+\mathrm{t}_{2}\right)^{2}=\mathrm{t}_{1}^{2}+\mathrm{t}_{2}^{2}+2 \mathrm{t}_{1} \mathrm{t}_{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}_{1} \mathrm{t}_{2}=\frac{-3}{4}$
$\left(t_{1}-t_{2}\right)^{2}=\left(t_{1}+t_{2}\right)^{2}-4 t_{1} t_{2}=4$
$(B C)^{2}=4\left(t_{1}^{2}-t_{2}^{2}\right)^{2}+16\left(t_{1}-t_{2}\right)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow(\mathrm{BC})^{2}=80$

Question: Let $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\int \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}^{\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)}+2 \mathrm{x}^{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)}}$ be such that $\mathrm{f}(0)=-26+24 \log _{\mathrm{e}}(2)$. If $\mathrm{f}(1)=\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b} \log _{\mathrm{e}}(3)$, where $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b} \in \mathbf{Z}$, then $\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}$ is equal to:
Solution:
$f(x)=\int \frac{d x}{x^{2 / 3}+2 x^{1 / 2}}$
Put $x=t^{6} \Rightarrow d x=6 t^{5} d t$
$=\int \frac{6 t^{5} d t}{t^{4}+2 t^{3}}=6 \int \frac{\left(t^{2}-4\right)+4}{t+2} d t$
$=6\left[\int(\mathrm{t}-2) \mathrm{dt}+4 \int \frac{1}{\mathrm{t}+2} \mathrm{dt}\right]$
$=6\left[\frac{t^{2}}{2}-2 t+4 \ell n(t+2)\right]+C$
$=3 \mathrm{x}^{1 / 3}-12 \mathrm{x}^{1 / 6}+24 \ell \mathrm{n}\left(\mathrm{x}^{1 / 6}+2\right)+\mathrm{C}$
$\mathrm{f}(0)=24 \ell \mathrm{n} 2+\mathrm{C}=-26+24 \ell \mathrm{n} 2$ (given)
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{C}=-26$
Now
$\mathrm{f}(1)=-35+24 \ell \mathrm{n} 3=\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b} \ell \mathrm{n} 3$ (as given in ques.)
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=-35 \& \mathrm{~b}=24$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}=-11$

Let $f(x)=\lim _{\theta \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{\cos \pi x-x^{\left(\frac{2}{\theta}\right)} \sin (x-1)}{1+x^{\left(\frac{2}{\theta}\right)}(x-1)}\right), x \in R$.
Consider the following two statements :
(I) $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ is discontinous at $\mathrm{x}=1$.
(II) $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ is continous at $\mathrm{x}=-1$.
Then,
Solution:
$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}\cos \pi x & x \rightarrow 1^{-} \\ \frac{-\sin (x-1)}{(x-1)} & x \rightarrow 1^{+}\end{array}\right.$
$\mathrm{RHL}=\lim _{\mathrm{x} \rightarrow 1} \frac{-\sin (\mathrm{x}-1)}{(\mathrm{x}-1)}=-1$
$\mathrm{LHL}=\lim _{\mathrm{x} \rightarrow 1} \cos \pi \mathrm{x}=-1, \mathrm{f}(1)=-1$
$\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ is continuous at $\mathrm{x}=1$
$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}\frac{-\sin (x-1)}{-(x-1)} & x \rightarrow-1^{-} \\ \cos \pi x & x \rightarrow-1^{+}\end{array}\right.$
$\mathrm{RHL}=\lim _{\mathrm{x} \rightarrow-1} \cos \pi \mathrm{x}=-1$
$\mathrm{LHL}=\lim _{\mathrm{x} \rightarrow-1} \frac{-\sin (\mathrm{x}-1)}{(\mathrm{x}-1)}=\frac{\sin 2}{-2}$
$\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ is discontinuous at $\mathrm{x}=-1$

Question: If $\sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{25}\left(\frac{\mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{r}^{4}+\mathrm{r}^{2}+1}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{p}}{\mathrm{q}}$, where p and q are positive integers such that $\operatorname{gcd}(p, q)=1$, then $p+q$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$。
Solution:
$S=\sum \frac{r}{\left(r^{2}+r+1\right)\left(r^{2}-r+1\right)}$
$=\frac{1}{2} \sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{25}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}^{2}-\mathrm{r}+1}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}^{2}+\mathrm{r}+1}\right)$
$=\frac{1}{2}\left[\left(\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{3}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{7}\right)+\ldots+\left(\frac{1}{601}-\frac{1}{651}\right)\right]$
$=\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{651}\right]$
$=\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{650}{651}\right]=\frac{325}{651}$
$\frac{\mathrm{p}}{\mathrm{q}}=\frac{325}{651} \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}+\mathrm{q}=976$

Question:
The probability distribution of a random variable X is given below :

$X$$4k$$\frac{{30}}{7}k$$\frac{{32}}{7}k$$\frac{{34}}{7}k$$\frac{{36}}{7}k$$\frac{{38}}{7}k$$\frac{{40}}{7}k$$6k$
$P\left( X \right)$$\frac{2}{{15}}$$\frac{1}{{15}}$$\frac{2}{{15}}$$\frac{1}{5}$$\frac{1}{{15}}$$\frac{2}{{15}}$$\frac{1}{5}$$\frac{1}{{15}}$


If $\mathrm{E}(\mathrm{X})=\frac{263}{15}$, then $\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{X}<20)$ is equal to :
Solution:
$\mathrm{E}(\mathrm{X})=\sum \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{i}}\right)=\frac{526 \mathrm{k}}{15 \times 7}=\frac{263}{15} \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=\frac{7}{2}$

$X$1415161718192021
$P\left( X \right)$$\frac{2}{{15}}$$\frac{1}{{15}}$$\frac{2}{{15}}$$\frac{1}{5}$$\frac{1}{{15}}$$\frac{2}{{15}}$$\frac{1}{5}$$\frac{1}{{15}}$


$\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{X}<20)=\sum_{\mathrm{X}=14}^{19} \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{X})=\frac{11}{15}$

Question:
Let the arithmetic mean of $\frac{1}{\mathrm{a}}$ and $\frac{1}{\mathrm{~b}}$ be $\frac{5}{16}, \mathrm{a}>2$.
If $\alpha$ is such that $\mathrm{a}, 4, \alpha, \mathrm{~b}$ are in A.P., then the equation $\alpha x^{2}-a x+2(\alpha-2 b)=0$ has:
Options: One root in $(1,4)$ and another in $(-2,0)$
One root in $(0,2)$ and another in $(-4,-2)$
Complex roots of magnitude less than 2
Solution:
$\quad \mathrm{a}=4-\mathrm{d}, \alpha=4+\mathrm{d}, \mathrm{b}=4+2 \mathrm{~d}$
$\Rightarrow(4+\mathrm{d}) \mathrm{x}^{2}-(4-\mathrm{d}) \mathrm{x}+2(4+\mathrm{d}-8-4 \mathrm{~d})=0$
$\Rightarrow(4+\mathrm{d}) \mathrm{x}^{2}-(4-\mathrm{d}) \mathrm{x}+2(-4-3 \mathrm{~d})=0$
Also $\frac{\frac{1}{\mathrm{a}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{~b}}}{2}=\frac{5}{16}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\frac{1}{4-\mathrm{d}}+\frac{1}{4+2 \mathrm{~d}}}{2}=\frac{5}{16}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{d}=2$
Equation becomes
$6 \mathrm{x}^{2}-2 \mathrm{x}-20=0$
$3 \mathrm{x}^{2}-\mathrm{x}-10=0$
$\mathrm{x}=2, \frac{-5}{3}$

Question:
Let f be a differentiable function satisfying
$\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=1-2 \mathrm{x}+\int_{0}^{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{e}^{(\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{t})} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{t}) \mathrm{dt}, \mathrm{x} \in \mathbf{R}$ and let
$g(x)=\int_{0}^{x}(f(t)+2)^{15}(t-4)^{6}(t+12)^{17} d t, x \in \mathbf{R}$.
If p and q are respectively the points of local minima and local maxima of g , then the value of $|\mathrm{p}+\mathrm{q}|$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$。
Solution:
$f(x)=1-2 x+e^{x} \int_{0}^{x} e^{-t} f(t) d t$
$e^{-x} f(x)=(1-2 x) e^{-x}+\int_{0}^{x} e^{-t} f(t) d t$
$e^{-x} f^{\prime}(x)-e^{-x} f(x)=-2 e^{-x}+(1-2 x) e^{-x}(-1)+e^{-x} f(x)$
$\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})-2 \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=2 \mathrm{x}-3$
$\frac{d y}{d x}-2 y=2 x-3$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{y} . \mathrm{e}^{-2 \mathrm{x}}=\int \mathrm{e}^{-2 \mathrm{x}}(2 \mathrm{x}-3) \mathrm{dx}$
On solving we get
$\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=1-\mathrm{x}$
$g(x)=\int_{0}^{x}(3-t)^{15}(t-4)^{6}(t+12)^{17} d t$
$g^{\prime}(x)=(3-x)^{15}(x-4)^{6}(x+12)^{17}$
$=-(x-3)^{15}(x-4)^{6}(x+12)^{17}$

Local maxima $\Rightarrow \mathrm{q}=3$
Local minima $\Rightarrow \mathrm{p}=-12=|\mathrm{p}+\mathrm{q}|=9$

Question: Considering the principal values of inverse trigonometric functions, the value of the expression $\tan \left(2 \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{13}}\right)-2 \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{\sqrt{10}}\right)\right)$ is equal to :
Solution:
Let $\sin ^{-1} \frac{2}{\sqrt{13}}=\theta \& \cos ^{-1} \frac{3}{\sqrt{10}}=\phi$
$\sin \theta=\frac{2}{\sqrt{13}} \& \cos \phi=\frac{3}{\sqrt{10}}$
$\tan (2 \theta-2 \phi)=\frac{\tan 2 \theta-\tan 2 \phi}{1+\tan 2 \theta \tan 2 \phi}$
$\left(\because \tan 2 \theta=\frac{2 \tan \theta}{1-\tan ^{2} \theta}\right)$
$=\frac{\frac{12}{5}-\frac{3}{4}}{1+\frac{12}{5} \cdot \frac{3}{4}}$
$=\frac{33}{56}$

Question: Let P be a point in the plane of the vector $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AB}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AC}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$ such that P is equidistant from the lines AB and AC . $\therefore \operatorname{Area}(\triangle \mathrm{APB})=\frac{1}{2} \times \sqrt{11} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{5}}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{6}{11}}=\frac{\sqrt{30}}{4}$

Question:
Given below two statements :
Statement I : $25^{13}+20^{13}+8^{13}+3^{13}$ is divisible by 7 .
Statement II : The integral part of $(7+4 \sqrt{3})^{25}$ is an odd number.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
Options: Both Statement I and Statement II are false.
Both Statement I and Statement II are true.
Statement I is false but Statement II is true.
Statement I is true but Statement II is false.
Solution:
Statement I :

Statement II : $\mathrm{R}=(7+4 \sqrt{3})^{25}=\mathrm{I}+\mathrm{f}$
$\mathrm{R}^{\prime}=(7-4 \sqrt{3})^{25}=\mathrm{f}^{\prime}$

Question: An ellipse has its center at $(1,-2)$, one focus at $(3,-2)$ and one vertex at (5,-2). Then the length of its latus rectum is :
Solution:
$\mathrm{CA}_{1}=\mathrm{a}=4$

Question: Let $A=\left[\begin{array}{ll}3 & -4 \\ 1 & -1\end{array}\right]$ and $B$ be two matrices such that $A^{100}=100 B+I$. Then the sum of all the elements of $\mathrm{B}^{100}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$\quad \mathrm{A}=\mathrm{I}+\left[\begin{array}{ll}2 & -4 \\ 1 & -2\end{array}\right]$, let $\mathrm{M}=\left[\begin{array}{ll}2 & -4 \\ 1 & -2\end{array}\right]$
$\mathrm{M}^{2}=\left[\begin{array}{ll}0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0\end{array}\right]=\mathrm{M}^{3}=\mathrm{M}^{4}=\ldots=\mathrm{M}^{100}$
$A^{100}=(I+M)^{100}=\sum_{r=0}^{100}\binom{100}{r} M^{r} . I$
$A^{100}=I+100 M=I+100 B$
$\therefore \mathrm{M}=\mathrm{B} \Rightarrow \mathrm{M}^{100}=\mathrm{B}^{100}=\left[\begin{array}{ll}0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0\end{array}\right]$

Question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I: The function $\mathrm{f}: \mathbf{R} \rightarrow \mathbf{R}$ defined by $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\frac{\mathrm{x}}{1+|\mathrm{x}|}$ is one-one.
Statement II : The function $\mathrm{f}: \mathbf{R} \rightarrow \mathbf{R}$ defined by $f(x)=\frac{x^{2}+4 x-30}{x^{2}-8 x+18}$ is many-one.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
Options: Both Statement I and Statement II are false.
Both Statement I and Statement II are true.
Statement I is false but Statement II is true .
Statement I is true but Statement II is false.
Solution:
Statement 1: $f(x)=\frac{x}{1+|x|}$
$\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})= \begin{cases}\frac{\mathrm{x}}{1+\mathrm{x}} & \mathrm{x} \geq 0 \\ \frac{\mathrm{x}}{1-\mathrm{x}} & \mathrm{x}<0\end{cases}$

$\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ is one-one
Statement 2: $f(x)=\frac{x^{2}+4 x-30}{x^{2}-8 x+18}, f(0)=\frac{-30}{18}=\frac{-5}{3}$
$\frac{-5}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}+4 \mathrm{x}-30}{\mathrm{x}^{2}-8 \mathrm{x}+18}$
On solving $\mathrm{x}=0,-1$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(0)=\mathrm{f}(-1)=\frac{-5}{3}$
$\therefore \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ is many-one

Question: Let $P_{1}: y=4 x^{2}$ and $P_{2}: y=x^{2}+27$ be two parabolas. If the area of the bounded region enclosed between $\mathrm{P}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{P}_{2}$ is six times the area of the bounded region enclosed between the line $\mathrm{y}=\alpha \mathrm{x}, \alpha>0$ and $\mathrm{P}_{1}$, then $\alpha$ is equal to:
Solution:
Area bounded between $P_{1} \& P_{2}$ is
$\int_{-3}^{3}\left(\left(x^{2}+27\right)-\left(4 x^{2}\right)\right) d x$
(P.O.I. of $P_{1} \& P_{2}$ is $x= \pm 3$ )
$=2 \int_{0}^{3}\left(27-3 \mathrm{x}^{2}\right) \mathrm{dx}=2\left[27 \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x}^{3}\right]_{0}^{3}$
$=2[81-27]=108$
$\therefore $ Area bounded between $\mathrm{P}_{1} \& \mathrm{~L}$ is 18 sq. units
(Area between $\mathrm{x}^{2}=4ay$ & line $\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{my}$ ) is $\frac{8 \mathrm{a}^{2}}{3 \mathrm{~m}^{3}}$
$\therefore $ Area between $\mathrm{x}^{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}}{4} \& \mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\alpha}$ is
$\frac{8 \cdot\left(\frac{1}{16}\right)^{2}}{3 \cdot\left(\frac{1}{\alpha}\right)^{3}}=18$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\frac{8}{16.16}}{\frac{3}{\alpha^{3}}}=18 \Rightarrow \alpha^{3}=2^{6} .3^{3}$
$\Rightarrow \alpha=12$

Question: Let the ellipse $\mathrm{E}: \frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{144}+\frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}}{169}=1$ and the hyperbola $H: \frac{x^{2}}{16}-\frac{y^{2}}{\lambda^{2}}=-1$ have the same foci. If $e$ and $L$ respectively denote the eccentricity and the length of the latus rectum of H , then the value of $24(\mathrm{e}+\mathrm{L})$ is :
Solution:
Equation of hyperbola : $\frac{y^{2}}{\lambda^{2}}-\frac{x^{2}}{16}=1$
Equation of ellipse : $\frac{x^{2}}{144}+\frac{y^{2}}{169}=1$
$e^{\prime}=\sqrt{1-\frac{144}{169}}=\frac{5}{13}$
focus $\Rightarrow(0,5)$
$\Rightarrow \lambda \sqrt{1+\frac{16}{\lambda^{2}}}=5$
$\Rightarrow \lambda^{2}+16=25$
$\lambda=3$
Eccentricity of hyperbola $=\sqrt{1+\frac{16}{\lambda^{2}}}=\frac{5}{3}$
Length of latus rectum of hyperbola $=\frac{2(16)}{3}=\frac{32}{3}$
$24(\mathrm{e}+\ell)=24\left[\frac{5}{3}+\frac{32}{3}\right]=8 \times 37=296$

Question: Let [ • ] denote the greatest integer function. Then $\int_{-\frac{\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}\left(\frac{12(3+[x])}{3+[\sin x]+[\cos x]}\right) d x$ is equal to:
Solution:
$I=\int_{-\frac{\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{12(3+[x]) d x}{3+[\sin x]+[\cos x]}$
$\mathrm{I}=\int_{-\frac{\pi}{2}}^{-1} \frac{12(1) \mathrm{dx}}{2}+\int_{-1}^{0} \frac{12(2) \mathrm{dx}}{2}+\int_{0}^{1} \frac{12(3) \mathrm{dx}}{3}+\int_{1}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{12(4) \mathrm{dx}}{3}$
$\mathrm{I}=6\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-1\right)+12(0+1)+12(1-0)+16\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-1\right)$
$\mathrm{I}=3 \pi-6+12+12+8 \pi-16$
$\mathrm{I}=11 \pi+2$

Question: Let the circle $x^{2}+y^{2}=4$ intersect $x$-axis at the points $\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{a}, 0), \mathrm{a}>0$ and $\mathrm{B}(\mathrm{b}, 0)$. Let $\mathrm{P}(2 \cos \alpha$, $2 \sin \alpha), 0<\alpha<\frac{\pi}{2}$ and $\mathrm{Q}(2 \cos \beta, 2 \sin \beta)$ be two points such that $(\alpha-\beta)=\frac{\pi}{2}$. Then the point of intersection of AQ and BP lies on :
Solution:
Let point of intersection $\mathrm{R}(\mathrm{h}, \mathrm{k})$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& m_{B R}=m_{B P} \Rightarrow \frac{k}{h+2}=\frac{2 \sin \alpha}{2 \cos \alpha+2} \Rightarrow \frac{k}{h+2}=\tan \frac{\alpha}{2} \\
& m_{A R}=m_{A Q} \Rightarrow \frac{k}{h-2}=\frac{2 \sin \beta}{2 \cos \beta-2}=\frac{\sin \beta}{\cos \beta-1}=-\cot \frac{\beta}{2} \\
& \frac{\alpha}{2}-\frac{\beta}{2}=\frac{\pi}{4} \\
& \tan \left(\frac{\alpha}{2}-\frac{\beta}{2}\right)=\tan \frac{\pi}{4}=1 \\
& \frac{\tan \frac{\alpha}{2}-\tan \frac{\beta}{2}}{1+\tan \frac{\alpha}{2} \tan \frac{\beta}{2}}=1 \\
& \frac{\frac{k}{h+2}+\frac{h-2}{k}}{1+\left(\frac{k}{h+2}\right)\left(\frac{2-h}{k}\right)}=1 \Rightarrow \frac{k^{2}+h^{2}-4}{\frac{k(h+2)}{4}}{(h+2)}=1 \\
& \frac{h^{2}+k^{2}-4}{4 k}=1 \\
& x^{2}+y^{2}-4 y-4=0
\end{aligned}
$$

Question:
The sum of all the elements in the range of $f(x)=\operatorname{Sgn}(\sin x)+\operatorname{Sgn}(\cos x)+\operatorname{Sgn}(\tan x)+\operatorname{Sgn}(\cot x)$, $\mathrm{x} \neq \frac{\mathrm{n} \pi}{2}, \mathrm{n} \in \mathbf{Z}$,
where $\operatorname{Sgn}(t)=\left\{\begin{array}{lll}1, & \text { if } & t>0 \\ -1 & \text { if } & t<0\end{array}\right.$, is
Solution:
$x \in(0, \pi / 2) \Rightarrow y=1+1+1+1=4$
$\mathrm{x} \in(\pi / 2, \pi) \Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=1-1-1-1=-2$
$\mathrm{x} \in(\pi, 3 \pi / 2) \Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=-1-1+1+1=0$
$\mathrm{x} \in(3 \pi / 2,2 \pi) \Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=-1+1-1-1=-2$
$\therefore $ Range of $y$ is $\{-2,0,4\}$
Required sum $=-2+0+4=2$

Question:
Let $\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{y}(\mathrm{x})$ be the solution of the differential equation $x \frac{d y}{d x}-y=x^{2} \cot x, x \in(0, \pi)$.
If $y\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=\frac{\pi}{2}$, then $6 y\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)-8 y\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)$ is equal to :
Solution:
$x d y-y d x=x^{2} \cot x d x$
$x^{2} d\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=x^{2} \cot x d x$
$\mathrm{d}\left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)=\cot \mathrm{x} \mathrm{dx}$
$\int d\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\int \cot x d x$
$\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}=\log _{\mathrm{e}} \sin \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}$
given $\mathrm{y}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=\frac{\pi}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=1$
$\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{x}\left(\log _{\mathrm{e}} \sin \mathrm{x}+1\right)$
$y\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=\frac{\pi}{6}\left[-\log _{e} 2+1\right]$
$\mathrm{y}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\frac{\pi}{4}\left[-\frac{1}{2} \log _{\mathrm{e}} 2+1\right]$
$6 y\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)-8 y\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)$
$=\pi\left[\left(-\log _{\mathrm{e}} 2+1\right)+2\left(\frac{1}{2} \log _{\mathrm{e}} 2-1\right)\right]$
$=\pi[1-2]=-\pi$

Question: If the distance of the point $\mathrm{P}(43, \alpha, \beta), \beta<0$, from the line $\vec{r}=4 \hat{i}-\hat{k}+\mu(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{k}), \mu \in \mathbf{R}$ along a line with direction ratios $3,-1,0$ is $13 \sqrt{10}$, then $\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$\quad \frac{x-43}{3}=\frac{y-\alpha}{-1}=\frac{z-\beta}{0} \Rightarrow P_{1}(43+3 \lambda, \alpha-\lambda, \beta)$
$\frac{\mathrm{x}-4}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}}{0}=\frac{\mathrm{z}+1}{3} \Rightarrow \mathrm{P}_{1}(2 \mu+4,0,3 \mu-1)$
$\therefore \mu=\frac{3 \lambda+39}{2}, \alpha=\lambda, \beta=\frac{9 \lambda-115}{2}$
$P(43, \alpha, \beta), P_{1}(43+3 \alpha, 0, \beta)$
$\left(\mathrm{PP}_{1}\right)^{2}=1690=10 \alpha^{2}, \therefore \alpha=13, \beta=1$
$\therefore \alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}=170$

Question: In the potentiometer, when the cell in the secondary circuit is shunted with $4 \Omega$ resistance, the balance is obtained at the length 120 cm of wire. Now when the same cell in shunted with $12 \Omega$ resistance, the balance is shifted to a length of 180 cm . The internal resistance of cell is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$.
Solution:
Let E is emf and r is internal resistance of cell.

$\frac{\mathrm{E} \cdot 4}{\mathrm{r}+4}=120 \mathrm{~K}$
$\frac{\mathrm{E} \cdot 12}{\mathrm{r}+12}=180 \mathrm{~K}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{3} \frac{\mathrm{r}+12}{\mathrm{r}+4}=\frac{2}{3}$
$\mathrm{r}+12=2(\mathrm{r}+4)$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{r}=4$

Question:
Two point charges of 1 nC and 2 nC are placed at the two corners of equilateral triangle of side 3 cm . The work done in bringing a charge of 3 nC from infinity to the third corner of the triangle is
$\_\_\_\_$ $\mu \mathrm{J}$.
$$
\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}=9 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~N} . \mathrm{m}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}
$$
Solution:
$\mathrm{W}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{kq}_{1}}{\ell}+\frac{\mathrm{kq}_{2}}{\ell}\right) \mathrm{q}_{3}$
$=\frac{9 \times 10^{9}}{3 \times 10^{-2}}\left(3 \times 10^{-9}\right) \times 3 \times 10^{-9}$
$=27 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~J}=2.7 \mu \mathrm{~J}$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Electric potential energy of a system of charges

Question: The electric current in the circuit is given as $i=i_{\mathrm{o}}(t / T)$. The r.m.s current for the period $t=0$ to $t=T$ is $\_\_\_\_$
Solution:
$\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{rms}}^{2}=\frac{\int_{0}^{\mathrm{T}}\left(\mathrm{i}_{0}^{2} \mathrm{t}^{2} / \mathrm{T}^{2}\right) \mathrm{dt}}{\int_{0}^{\mathrm{T}} \mathrm{dt}}=\frac{\mathrm{i}_{0}^{2}}{\mathrm{~T}^{3}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{~T}^{3}}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{i}_{0}^{2}}{3}$
$\mathrm{i}_{\text {rms }}=\frac{\mathrm{i}_{0}}{\sqrt{3}}$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Average and RMS value of a function

Question: The magnetic field at the centre of a current carrying circular loop of radius $R$ is $16 \mu \mathrm{~T}$. The magnetic field at a distance $x=\sqrt{3} R$ on its axis from the centre is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mu \mathrm{T}$.
Solution:
$\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 R}=16 \mu \mathrm{~T}$
$\frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{IR}^{2}}{2\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{R}^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}=\frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{IR}^{2}}{2 \times 8 \mathrm{R}^{3}}=2 \mu \mathrm{~T}$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Magnetic field due to standard shaped objects

Question:
10 kg of ice at $-10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is added to 100 kg of water to lower its temperature from $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Consider no heat exchange to surroundings. The decrement to the temperature of water is $\_\_\_\_$ ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.
(specific heat of ice $=2100 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{Kg} .{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, specific heat of water $=4200 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{Kg} .{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, latent heat of fusion of ice $=3.36 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{Kg}$ )
Solution:
$10 \times 3.36 \times 10^{5}+10 \times 2100 \times 10+10 \times 4200 \times(\mathrm{T}-0) =100 \times 4200 \times(25-\mathrm{T})$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{T}=15^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
$\Delta \mathrm{T}=25-15=10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Problems based on mixing of liquids

Question: When both jaws of vernier callipers touch each other, zero mark of the vernier scale is right to zero mark of main scale, $4^{\text {th }}$ mark on vernier scale coincides with certain mark on the main scale. While measuring the length of a cylinder, observer observes 15 divisions on main scale and $5^{\text {th }}$ division of vernier scale coincides with a main scale division. Measured length of cylinder is $\_\_\_\_$ mm . (Least count of Vernier calliper $=0.1 \mathrm{~mm}$ )
Solution:
Reading $=\mathrm{MSR}+(\mathrm{VSR} \times \mathrm{LC})-$ (zero Error) $=15 \mathrm{~mm}+(5 \times 0.1 \mathrm{~mm})-(4 \times 0.1 \mathrm{~mm})$ Reading = 15.1 mm
$$
\therefore \ell=15.1 \mathrm{~mm}
$$

Question:
A convex lens of refractive index 1.5 and focal length $f=18 \mathrm{~cm}$ is immersed in water. The difference in focal lengths of the given lens when it is in water and in air is $\alpha \times \mathrm{f}$. The value of $\alpha$ is
$\_\_\_\_$ .
(refractive index of water = 4/3)
Solution:
$\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}_{\text {Air }}}=\left(\frac{1.5-1}{1}\right)\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{1}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}\right)$
$\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}_{\text {water }}}=\left(\frac{1.5-4 / 3}{4 / 3}\right)\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{1}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{f}_{\text {water }}}{\mathrm{f}_{\text {air }}}=\frac{0.5}{0.5 / 4}=4$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}_{\text {water }}-\mathrm{f}_{\text {air }}=3 \mathrm{f}$

Question:
The electric field of an electromagnetic wave travelling through a medium is given by
$\vec{E}(x, t)=25 \sin \left(2.0 \times 10^{15} t-10^{7} x\right) \hat{n}$
then the refractive index of the medium is $\_\_\_\_$。
(All given measurement are in SI units)
Solution:
$\omega=2 \times 10^{15} \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$
$\mathrm{k}=10^{7} \mathrm{~m}^{-1}$
$\mathrm{V}=\frac{2 \pi}{\mathrm{k}} \cdot \frac{\omega}{2 \pi}=\frac{\omega}{\mathrm{k}}=\frac{2 \times 10^{15}}{10^{7}}=2 \times 10^{8}=\frac{\mathrm{C}}{1.5}$
$\Rightarrow \mu=1.5$

Question: A solid sphere of radius 10 cm is rotating about an axis which is at a distance 15 cm from its centre. The radius of gyration about this axis is $\sqrt{n} \mathrm{~cm}$. The value of $n$ is
Solution:
Let radius of gyration is k
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{mk}^{2}=\frac{2}{3} \mathrm{mR}^{2}+\mathrm{md}^{2} \\
& \mathrm{k}^{2}=\frac{2}{3} \times 10^{2}+15^{2}=265 \\
& (\sqrt{\mathrm{n}})^{2}=265 \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=265
\end{aligned}
$$

Question: An atom ${ }_{3}^{8} X$ is bombarded by shower of fundamental particles and in 10 s this atom absorbed 10 electrons, 10 protons and 9 neutrons. The percentage growth in the surface area of the nucleons is recorded by :
Solution:
Surface area $\mathrm{x} \propto \mathrm{A}^{2 / 3}$
$\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{i}}=8^{2 / 3} \mathrm{~K}=4 \mathrm{~K}$
$\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{f}}=(8+10+9)^{2 / 3} \mathrm{~K}=9 \mathrm{~K}$
% increase in surface area of nucleus
$x_{i}=\frac{9 K-4 K}{4 K} \times 100=125 \%$

NTA gave option c correct, we are getting 125 percent.

Question:
The ratio of de Broglie wavelength of a deutron with kinetic energy $E$ to that of an alpha particle with kinetic energy $2 E$, is $n: 1$. The value of $n$ is
$\_\_\_\_$ .
(Assume mass of proton $=$ mass of neutron) :
Solution:
$\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~m} \cdot \mathrm{KE}}}$
$\frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{d}}}{\lambda_{\alpha}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\alpha} \cdot \mathrm{KE}_{\alpha}}{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{d}} \cdot \mathrm{KE}_{\mathrm{d}}}}=\sqrt{\frac{4 \mathrm{~m} \cdot 2 \mathrm{E}}{2 \mathrm{~m} \cdot \mathrm{E}}}=2: 1$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

Question:
Water drops fall from a tap on the floor, 5 m below, at regular intervals of time, the first drop strikes the floor when the sixth drop begins to fall. The height at which the fourth drop will be from ground, at the instant when the first drop strikes the ground is $\_\_\_\_$ m.
$\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right)$
Solution:
Time to reach ground $=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{~h}}{\mathrm{~g}}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5}{10}}=1 \mathrm{sec}$
Five drops per second
Time between each drop $=0.2 \mathrm{sec}$.
Time of fall for $4^{\text {th }}$ drop is $1-0.6=0.4 \mathrm{sec}$
Height fall of $4^{\text {th }}$ drop is $=\frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 0.4^{2}=0.8 \mathrm{~m}$
Height from ground $=5-0.8=4.2 \mathrm{~m}$

Question:
The displacement of a particle, executing simple harmonic motion with time period $T$, is expressed as $x(t)=\mathrm{A} \sin \omega \mathrm{t}$, where $A$ is the amplitude. The maximum value of potential energy of this oscillator is found at $t=T / 2 \beta$. The value of $\beta$ is
$\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution: Potential energy is maximum at extreme position The particle starting at mean position reaches extreme position in time $\frac{\mathrm{T}}{4}$.

Question:
Two circular discs of radius each 10 cm are joined at their centres by a rod of length 30 cm and mass 600 gm as shown in figure.
If the mass of each disc is 600 gm and applied torque between two discs is $43 \times 10^{5}$ dyne cm , the angular acceleration of the discs about the given axis $A B$ is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$.
Solution:
$\alpha=\frac{\tau}{\mathrm{I}}$
$\mathrm{I}=\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{mR}^{2}+\mathrm{mR}^{2}+\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{mR}^{2}+\mathrm{m}(2 \mathrm{R})^{2}+\frac{\mathrm{m}(3 \mathrm{R})^{2}}{12}+\mathrm{m}\left(\frac{\mathrm{R}}{2}\right)^{2}$
$=\left(\frac{3}{2}+4+1\right) \mathrm{mR}^{2}=\frac{13}{2} \mathrm{mR}^{2}=\frac{13}{2} \times 600 \times 10^{2}=39 \times 10^{4}$
$\alpha=\frac{43 \times 10^{5}}{39 \times 10^{4}} \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}^{2}=\frac{430}{39} \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}^{2} \approx 11 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$

Question: A particle of mass m falls from rest through a resistive medium having resistive force, $\mathrm{F}=-\mathrm{kv}$, where v is the velocity of the particle and k is a constant. Which of the following graphs represents velocity (v) versus time(t) ?
Solution:
$\mathrm{m} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{mg}-\mathrm{kv}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \int_{0}^{\mathrm{v}} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{mg}-\mathrm{kv}}=\int_{0}^{\mathrm{t}} \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{~m}} \\
& \frac{-1}{\mathrm{k}} \ln \left(\frac{\mathrm{mg}-\mathrm{kv}}{\mathrm{mg}}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{~m}} \\
& \mathrm{v}=\frac{\mathrm{mg}}{\mathrm{k}}\left(1-\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{kt} / \mathrm{m}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Question: For the two cells having same EMF E and internal resistance $r$, the current passing through the external resistor $6 \Omega$ is same when both the cells are connected either in parallel or in series. The value of internal resistance $r$ is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$.
Solution:
In series, $i_{i}=\frac{2 E}{6+2 r}$
In parallel, $i_{2}=\frac{E}{6+\frac{r}{2}}$
$\mathrm{i}_{1}=\mathrm{i}_{2} \Rightarrow \frac{2 \mathrm{E}}{6+2 \mathrm{r}}=\frac{\mathrm{E}}{6+\frac{\mathrm{r}}{2}}$
$12+\mathrm{r}=6+2 \mathrm{r}$
$\mathrm{r}=6 \Omega$

Question: In the following $p-V$ diagram the equation of state along the curved path is given by $(V-2)^{2}=4 a p$ where $a$ is a constant. The total work done in the closed path is
Solution:
$\mathrm{w}=$ Area of parabola
$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{2}{3}(\text { Area of rectangle } \mathrm{AC} 31 \mathrm{~A}) \\
& =\frac{2}{3} \mathrm{P}_{0}(3-1)=\frac{4 \mathrm{P}_{0}}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$
When $\mathrm{V}=1$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& (1-2)^{2}=4 \mathrm{aP}_{0} \\
& \mathrm{P}_{0}=\frac{1}{4 \mathrm{a}} \\
& \mathrm{~W}=\frac{4}{3} \mathrm{P}_{0}=\frac{4}{3} \frac{1}{4 \mathrm{a}}=\frac{1}{3 \mathrm{a}} \\
& \mathrm{~W}_{\text {gas }}=\frac{-1}{3 \mathrm{a}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Question: The magnitudes of power of a biconvex lens (refractive index 1.5) and that of a plano-concave lens (refractive index $=1.7$ ) are same. If the curvature of plano-concave lens exactly matches with the curvature of back surface of the biconvex lens, then ratio of radius of curvature of front and back surface of the biconvex lens is $\_\_\_\_$。
Solution:
$\left|\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}\right|=\left|\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}\right|$
$0.5\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{1}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}\right)=\frac{0.7}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}$
$\frac{5}{\mathrm{R}_{1}}=\frac{2}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}$
$\frac{\mathrm{R}_{1}}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}=\frac{5}{2}$

Question:
Assuming in forward bias condition there is a voltage drop of 0.7 V across a silicon diode, the current through diode $\mathrm{D}_{1}$ in the circuit is
$\_\_\_\_$ mA . (Assume all diodes in the given circuit are identical)
Solution:
$\quad 12-0.3 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{I}-0.7=0$
$\frac{11.3}{0.3 \times 10^{3}}=\mathrm{I}$
$37.66 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~A}=\mathrm{I}$
Current through diode $\mathrm{D}_{1}, \mathrm{I}_{1}=\mathrm{I} / 2$
$\mathrm{I}_{1}=18.83 \mathrm{~mA}$

Tags.Chapter: Semiconductors, Electronic Devices and Logic Gates
Tags.Topic: p-n junction as an ideal diode

Question:
Three long straight wires carrying current are arranged mutually parallel as shown in the figure. The force experienced by 15 cm length of wire $Q$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .

( $\mu_{0}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~T} . \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{A}$ )
Solution:
$F_{n e t}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} I_{0}\left(\frac{I_{1}}{d_{1}}+\frac{I_{2}}{d_{2}}\right) \ell$
$\mathrm{F}_{\text {net }}=2 \times 10^{-7} \times 1\left(\frac{3}{3}+\frac{2}{2}\right) \times \frac{15 \times 10^{-2}}{10^{-2}}$
$=4 \times 15 \times 10^{-7}$
$\mathrm{F}_{\text {net }}=6 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~N}$

Question:
The equivalent resistance between the points $A$ and $B$ in the following circuit is $\frac{x}{5} \Omega$. The value of $x$ is
$\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$6 \mathrm{i}_{1}+3\left(2 \mathrm{i}_{1}-\mathrm{i}\right)=3\left(\mathrm{i}-\mathrm{i}_{1}\right)$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\Rightarrow 15 \mathrm{i}_{1}=6 \mathrm{i} \Rightarrow \mathrm{i}_{1}=\frac{2}{5} \mathrm{i} \tag{$[$}
\end{equation*}
$$
$3\left(\mathrm{i}-\mathrm{i}_{1}\right)+6 \mathrm{i}_{1}=1$
$3 \mathrm{i}+3 \mathrm{i}_{1}=1$
$\left(3+\frac{6}{5}\right) \mathrm{i}=1$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{i}=\frac{5}{21} \mathrm{~A}=\frac{1 \mathrm{~V}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq}}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{21}{5} \Omega$

Question: Which of the following best represents the temperature versus heat supplied graph for water, in the range of $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $120^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ?

Given below are two statements :
Statement-I : A plane wave after passing through prism remains as plane wave but passing through small pin hole may become spherical wave.
Statement-II : The curvature of a spherical wave emerging from a slit will increase for increasing slit width.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
Options: Both Statement-I and Statement-II are false.
Both Statement-I and Statement-II are true.
Statement-I is true but Statement-II is false.
Statement-I is false but Statement-II is true.
Solution: Increasing the slit width 'a' decreases the diffraction angle ( $\theta=\lambda / a$ ) and reduces the spreading of the wave. A narrower slit produces a more pronounced spherical wave (high curvature) while a wider slit leads to a flatter, less curved wave.

Question: A block of mass 5 kg is moving on an inclined plane which makes an angle of $30^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. Friction coefficient between the block and inclined plane surface is $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. The force to be applied on the block so that the block will move down without acceleration is $\_\_\_\_$ N.
Solution:
$\mathrm{mg} \sin 30^{\circ}=\mathrm{F}+\mu \mathrm{mg} \cos 30^{\circ}$
$\mathrm{F}=5 \times 10 \times \frac{1}{2}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times 5 \times 10 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$
$\mathrm{F}=25-\frac{75}{2}=25-37.5$
$\mathrm{F}=-12.5 \mathrm{~N}$
∴ force will be downward on incline of magnitude 12.5 N

Question:
Two wires $A$ and $B$ made of different materials of length 6.0 cm and 5.4 cm , respectively and area of cross sections $3.0 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^{2}$ and $4.5 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}^{2}$, respectively are stretched by the same magnitude under a given load. The ratio of the Young's modulus of $A$ to that of $B$ is $\mathrm{x}: 3$. The value of x is
$\_\_\_\_$。
Solution:
$T=\frac{F / A}{\Delta \ell / \ell} \Rightarrow Y=\frac{F \ell}{A \Delta \ell}$
$\frac{\mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{\ell_{\mathrm{A}}}{\ell_{\mathrm{B}}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{A}}}\right)$
$=\frac{6}{5.4}\left(\frac{4.5 \times 10^{-5}}{3 \times 10^{-5}}\right)=\frac{9}{5.4}=\frac{5}{3} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{x}}{3}=\frac{5}{3}$
$\mathrm{x}=5$

Question: Let the mean and variance of 7 observations $2,4,10, x, 12,14, y, x>y$, be 8 and 16 respectively. Two numbers are chosen from $\{1,2,3, \mathrm{x}-4, \mathrm{y}, 5\}$ one after another without replacement, then the probability, that the smaller number among the two chosen numbers is less than 4 , is:
Solution:
Mean $(\overline{\mathrm{x}})=8$ (Given)
$\Rightarrow \frac{2+4+10+x+12+14+y}{7}=8$
$\Rightarrow x+y=14 \ldots .(1) \ldots$.
Variance $\left(\sigma^{2}\right)=16$ (Given)
$\Rightarrow 16=\frac{2^{2}+4^{2}+10^{2}+x^{2}+12^{2}+14^{2}+y^{2}}{7}-8^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}=100 \quad \ldots \ldots(2) \ldots$.
$\because(x+y)^{2}=x^{2}+y^{2}+2 x y$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{xy}=48$ (sum is 14 product is 48 )
Since problem states $x>y$
$\therefore \mathrm{x}=8$ and $\mathrm{y}=6$
Now set $X=\{1,2,3,4,6,5\}$
Now we choose two numbers one after other without replacement total outcomes $=6 \times 5=30$

We want the prob. That the smaller number among the two is less than 4
$\mathrm{P}($ smaller $<4)=1-\mathrm{P}($ smaller $\geq 4)$
$$
=1-\frac{6}{30}=\frac{4}{5}
$$

Question:
Let $(\alpha, \beta, \gamma)$ be the co-ordinates of the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point $(5,4,2)$ on the line $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=(-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})+\lambda(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}})$.
Then the length of the projection of the vector $\alpha \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\beta \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\gamma \hat{\mathrm{k}}$ on the vector $6 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$ is :
Solution:
$\overline{\mathrm{r}}=(-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})+\lambda(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}})$
$\frac{\mathrm{x}+1}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-3}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-1}{-1}=\lambda$
Any general point P on the line is
$(2 \lambda-1,3 \lambda+3,-\lambda+1)$
Let the given point is $\mathrm{A}(5,4,2)$
$\overline{\mathrm{AP}}=(2 \lambda-6) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(3 \lambda-1) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(-\lambda-1) \hat{\mathrm{k}}$
$\because \overline{\mathrm{AP}} \perp^{\mathrm{r}}$ Line $(\mathrm{L})$
$\therefore \overline{\mathrm{AP}} \cdot(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}})=0$
$2(2 \lambda-6)+3(3 \lambda-1)-1(-\lambda-1)=0$
$\Rightarrow \lambda=1$
$\therefore \alpha=1$
$\beta=6$
$\gamma=0$
Let the vector $\overline{\mathrm{u}}=\alpha \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\beta \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\gamma \hat{\mathrm{k}}$
$$
\overline{\mathrm{u}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\mathrm{O} \hat{\mathrm{k}}
$$
$\& \overline{\mathrm{w}}=6 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$
So projection $=\frac{|\overline{\mathrm{u}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{w}}|}{|\overline{\mathrm{w}}|}=\frac{18}{7}$

Tags.Subject: Math
Tags.Topic: Equation of lines in 3D and vector form

Question: Let the foci of hyperbola coincide with the foci of the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{36}+\frac{y^{2}}{16}=1$. If the eccentricity of the hyperbola is 5 , then the length of its latus rectum is:
Solution:
Let $\mathrm{e}_{1}$ be eccentricity of ellipse
$\Rightarrow e_{1}=\sqrt{1-\frac{16}{36}}=\sqrt{1-\frac{4}{9}}=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{3}$
So $\mathrm{ae}_{1}=6 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{5}}{3}=2 \sqrt{5}$
Now H : $\frac{x^{2}}{p^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{q^{2}}=1$
p.e $=\mathrm{ae}_{1}$
p. $5=2 \sqrt{5}$
$\mathrm{p}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{2}=1+\frac{\mathrm{q}^{2}}{\mathrm{p}^{2}} \Rightarrow 25=1+\frac{5 \mathrm{q}^{2}}{4} \Rightarrow \mathrm{q}^{2}=\frac{96}{5}$
So length of $L R \frac{2 q^{2}}{p}=\frac{96}{\sqrt{5}}$

Question: The value of $\int_{-\pi / 6}^{\pi / 6}\left(\frac{\pi+4 x^{11}}{1-\sin (|x|+\pi / 6)}\right) d x$ is equal to
Solution:
$=2 \pi \int_{0}^{\pi / 6} \frac{1}{1-\sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)} d x$ let $x+\frac{x}{6}=t, d x=d t$
$=2 \pi \int_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3} \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{1-\sin \mathrm{t}}=2 \pi \int_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3} \frac{1+\sin \mathrm{t}}{\cos ^{2} \mathrm{t}} \mathrm{dt}$
$=2 \pi\left[\int_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3} \sec ^{2} \mathrm{tdt}+\int_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3} \sec \mathrm{t} \tan \mathrm{tdt}\right]$
$=2 \pi\left[(\tan \mathrm{t})_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3}+(\sec \mathrm{t})_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3}\right]$
$=2 \pi\left[\left(\sqrt{3}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)+\left(2-\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}\right)\right]$
$=2 \pi[\sqrt{3}+2-\sqrt{3}]=4 \pi$

Question: The value of $\operatorname{cosec} 10^{\circ}-\sqrt{3} \sec 10^{\circ}$ is equal to:
Solution:
$=\frac{1}{\sin 10^{\circ}}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{\cos 10^{\circ}}$
$=\frac{\cos 10^{\circ}-\sqrt{3} \sin 10^{\circ}}{\sin 10^{\circ} \cos 10^{\circ}}$
$=4\left[\frac{\frac{1}{2} \cos 10^{\circ}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \sin 10^{\circ}}{2 \sin 10^{\circ} \cos 10^{\circ}}\right]$
$=4\left[\frac{\sin \left(30^{\circ}-10^{\circ}\right)}{\sin 20^{\circ}}\right]$
$=4$

Question: The sum of all the roots of the equation $(\mathrm{x}-1)^{2}-5|\mathrm{x}-1|+6=0$, is:
Solution:
Let $|\mathrm{x}-1|=\mathrm{t}$
$\mathrm{t}^{2}-5 \mathrm{t}+6=0$
$\mathrm{t}=2 \& \mathrm{t}=3$
$|\mathrm{x}-1|=2 \&|\mathrm{x}-1|=3$
$\mathrm{x}-1 \pm 2 \& \mathrm{x}-1= \pm 3$
$\mathrm{x}=1 \pm 2 \& \mathrm{x}=1 \pm 3$
$\therefore $ $\operatorname{root}=3,-1,4,-2$
$\therefore $ Sum of root $=3+(-1)+4+(-2)=4$.

Question: If the coefficient of $x$ in the expansion of $\left(a x^{2}+b x+c\right)(1-2 x)^{26}$ is -56 and the coefficients of $\mathrm{x}^{2}$ and $\mathrm{x}^{3}$ are both zero, then $\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}$ is equal to
Solution:
$\left(a x^{2}+b c+c\right) \sum_{\mathrm{r}=0}^{26}{ }^{26} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}(-2 \mathrm{x})^{\mathrm{r}}$
Coeff. of $\mathrm{x}^{2}: \mathrm{a} .{ }^{26} \mathrm{C}_{0}(-2)^{0}+\mathrm{b} .{ }^{26} \mathrm{C}_{1}(-2)+$ c. ${ }^{26} \mathrm{C}_{2}(-2)^{2}=0$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\Rightarrow a-52 b+1300 c=0 \tag{1}
\end{equation*}
$$
Coeff. of $\mathrm{x}^{3}: \mathrm{a} .{ }^{26} \mathrm{C}_{1}(-2)+\mathrm{b} .{ }^{26} \mathrm{C}_{2}(-2)^{2}+$ c. ${ }^{26} \mathrm{C}_{3}(-2)^{3}=0$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\Rightarrow-52 a+1300 b-20800 c=0 \tag{2}
\end{equation*}
$$
Coeff. of $x=-56$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{b} .{ }^{26} \mathrm{C}_{0}(-2)^{0}+\mathrm{c} .{ }^{26} \mathrm{C}_{1}(-2)^{1}=-56$
$$
\begin{equation*}
b-52 c=-56 \tag{3}
\end{equation*}
$$
After solving (1), (2) & (3)
$\mathrm{a}=1300, \mathrm{~b}=100, \mathrm{c}=3$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}=1403$

Question: If $x^{2}+x+1=0$, then the value of $\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{4}+\left(x^{2}+\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)^{4}+\left(x^{3}+\frac{1}{x^{3}}\right)^{4}+\ldots $ $+\left(x^{25}+\frac{1}{x^{25}}\right)^{4}$ is :
Solution:
$x^{2}+x+1=0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\omega$ or $\omega^{2}$
$\therefore \alpha=\omega \& \beta=\omega^{2}$
$=\left(\omega+\omega^{2}\right)^{4}+\left(\omega^{2}+\omega^{4}\right)^{4}+\left(\omega^{3}+\omega^{6}\right)^{4}+\ldots+ \left(\omega^{25}+\omega^{50}\right)^{4}$
$=\left[\left(\omega+\omega^{2}\right)^{4}+\left(\omega^{2}+\omega^{4}\right)^{4}+\left(\omega^{4}+\omega^{8}\right)^{4}+\ldots .+\right. \left.\left(\omega^{25}+\omega^{50}\right)^{4}\right]+\left[\left(\omega^{3}+\omega^{6}\right)^{4}+\left(\omega^{6}+\omega^{12}\right)^{4}+\right. \left.\left(\omega^{9}+\omega^{18}\right)^{4}+\ldots+\left(\omega^{24}+\omega^{48}\right)^{4}\right]$
$=\underbrace{[1+1+1 \ldots \ldots+1]}_{17 \text { times }}+\underbrace{\left[(1+1)^{4}+(1+1)^{4}+\ldots . .(1+1)^{4}\right]}_{8 \text { times }}$
$=17+128$
$=145$

Given circle

Now
$\mathrm{OR}^{2}=(\mathrm{h}-2)^{2}+(\mathrm{k}-3)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow 3\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}\right)-12 \mathrm{x}-18 \mathrm{y}-25=0$

Question:
Let $f: \mathrm{R} \rightarrow(0, \infty)$ be a twice differentiable function such that $\mathrm{f}(3)=18, \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(3)=0$ and $\mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(3)=4$.
Then $\lim _{x \rightarrow 1}\left(\log _{e}\left(\frac{f(2+x)}{f(3)}\right)^{\frac{18}{(x-1)^{2}}}\right)$ is equal to :
Solution:
Let $T=\lim _{x \rightarrow 1}\left(\frac{f(x+2)}{f(3)}\right)^{\frac{18}{(x-1)^{2}}} ; 1^{\infty}$ form
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{T}=\mathrm{e}^{\lim _{\mathrm{x} \rightarrow 1} \frac{18}{(\mathrm{x}-1)^{2}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}+2)-\mathrm{f}(3)}{\mathrm{f}(3)}\right)}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{T}=\mathrm{e}^{\lim _{\mathrm{x} \rightarrow 1} \frac{18}{(\mathrm{x}-1)^{2}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}+2)-\mathrm{f}(3)}{18}\right)}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{T}=\mathrm{e}^{\lim _{\mathrm{x} \rightarrow 1}\left(\frac{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}+2)-\mathrm{f}(3)}{(\mathrm{x}-1)^{2}}\right) \frac{0}{0} \text { form }}$ apply L' pital
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{T}=\mathrm{e}^{\lim _{\mathrm{x} \rightarrow 1} \frac{\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}+2)}{2(\mathrm{x}-1)} ; \frac{0}{0} \text { form }} \quad$ apply L' pital
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{T}=\mathrm{e}^{\lim _{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{\mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(\mathrm{x}+2)}{2}} ;=\mathrm{e}^{\frac{4}{2}}=\mathrm{e}^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \log _{\mathrm{e}}(\mathrm{T})=2$

Question: For some $\alpha, \beta \in R$, let $A=\left[\begin{array}{ll}\alpha & 2 \\ 1 & 2\end{array}\right]$ and $B=\left[\begin{array}{ll}1 & 1 \\ 1 & \beta\end{array}\right]$ be such that $\mathrm{A}^{2}-4 \mathrm{~A}+2 \mathrm{I}=\mathrm{B}^{2}-3 \mathrm{~B}+\mathrm{I}=\mathrm{O}$. Then $\left(\operatorname{det}\left(\operatorname{adj}\left(\mathrm{A}^{3}-\mathrm{B}^{3}\right)\right)\right)^{2}$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$
Solution:
$\operatorname{Tr}(\mathrm{A})=4 \Rightarrow \alpha+2=4 \Rightarrow \alpha=2$
$\operatorname{Tr}(\mathrm{B})=3 \Rightarrow \beta+1=3 \Rightarrow \beta=2$
$A^{2}-4 A+2 I=O$
$A^{3}=4 A^{2}-2 A=16 A-8 I-2 A$
14A-8I
$=\left[\begin{array}{cc}28 & 28 \\ 14 & 28\end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{cc}-8 & 0 \\ 0 & -8\end{array}\right]$
$=\mathrm{A}^{3}=\left[\begin{array}{ll}20 & 28 \\ 14 & 20\end{array}\right]$
$\mathrm{B}^{2}-3 \mathrm{~B}+\mathrm{I}=\mathrm{O}$
$\mathrm{B}^{2}=3 \mathrm{~B}-\mathrm{I}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{B}^{3}=3 \mathrm{~B}^{2}-\mathrm{B}=3(3 \mathrm{~B}-\mathrm{I})-\mathrm{B}=8 \mathrm{~B}-3 \mathrm{I}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{B}^{3}=\left(\begin{array}{cc}
8 & 8 \\
8 & 16
\end{array}\right)+\left(\begin{array}{cc}
-3 & 0 \\
0 & -3
\end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{cc}
5 & 8 \\
8 & 13
\end{array}\right) \\
& \mathrm{A}^{3}-\mathrm{B}^{3}=\left(\begin{array}{ll}
20 & 28 \\
14 & 20
\end{array}\right)-\left(\begin{array}{cc}
5 & 8 \\
8 & 13
\end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{cc}
15 & 20 \\
6 & 7
\end{array}\right) \\
& \Rightarrow\left|\mathrm{A}^{3}-\mathrm{B}^{3}\right|=105-120=-15 \\
& \Rightarrow\left|\operatorname{adj}\left(\mathrm{~A}^{3}-\mathrm{B}^{3}\right)\right|=\left|\mathrm{A}^{3}-\mathrm{B}^{3}\right|=-15 \\
& \Rightarrow\left|\operatorname{adj}\left(\mathrm{~A}^{3}-\mathrm{B}^{3}\right)\right|^{2}=225
\end{aligned}
$$

Question:
Let $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}$ be vectors such that $|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}|=\sqrt{29}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}} \times(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}})=(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}$. If $\lambda_{1}, \lambda_{2}\left(\lambda_{1}>\lambda_{2}\right)$ are the possible values of $(\vec{c}+\vec{d}) \cdot(-7 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})$, then the equation
$\mathrm{K}^{2} \mathrm{x}^{2}+\left(\mathrm{K}^{2}-5 \mathrm{~K}+\lambda_{1}\right) \mathrm{xy}+\left(3 \mathrm{~K}+\frac{\lambda_{2}}{2}\right) \mathrm{y}^{2}$ $-8 \mathrm{x}+12 \mathrm{y}+\lambda_{2}=0$ represents a circle, for k equal to :
Solution:
$|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}|=\sqrt{29}$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}=\lambda((2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}})$
$\lambda= \pm 1$
$\lambda(-14+6+12)=4 \lambda, \lambda_{1}=4, \lambda_{2}=-4$
$k^{2} x^{2}+\left(k^{2}-5 k+4\right) x y+(3 k-2) y^{2}-8 x+12 y-4 =0$
is circle
$\mathrm{k}^{2}-5 \mathrm{k}+4=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=1,4$
$\mathrm{k}^{2}=3 \mathrm{k}-2 \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=1,2$
$\mathrm{k}=1$

Question: $\quad 6 \int_{0}^{\pi}|(\sin 3 \mathrm{x}+\sin 2 \mathrm{x}+\sin \mathrm{x})| \mathrm{dx}$ is equal to.......
Solution:
$\quad 6 \int_{0}^{\pi}|2 \sin 2 \mathrm{x} \cos \mathrm{x}+\sin 2 \mathrm{x}| \mathrm{dx}$
$6 \int_{0}^{\pi}\left|4 \sin x \cos ^{2} x+2 \sin x \cos x\right| d x$
$I=12 \int_{0}^{\pi} \sin x\left|2 \cos ^{2} x+\cos x\right|$
Put $\cos \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{t},-\sin \mathrm{x} \mathrm{dx}=\mathrm{dt}$
$I=-12 \int_{1}^{-1}\left|2 t^{2}+t\right| d t$
$\mathrm{I}=$
$12\left(\int_{-1}^{-1 / 2}\left(2 t^{2}+t\right) d t+\int_{-1 / 2}^{0}-\left(2 t^{2}+t\right) d t+\int_{0}^{1}\left(2 t^{2}+t\right) d t\right)$
$\mathrm{I}=17$

Question: Let $y=y(x)$ be the solution curve of the differential equation $\left(1+x^{2}\right) d y+\left(y-\tan ^{-1} x\right) d x=0$, $y(0)=1$. Then the value of $y(1)$ is :
Solution:
$\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}=\frac{\tan ^{-1} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}$
I.f. $=e^{\tan ^{-1} x}$
$y \times e^{\tan ^{-1} x}=\int e^{\tan ^{-1} x} \cdot \frac{\tan ^{-1} x}{1+x^{2}} d x$
$\mathrm{y} \times \mathrm{e}^{\tan ^{-1} \mathrm{x}}=\tan ^{-1} \mathrm{x}\left(\mathrm{e}^{\tan ^{-1} \mathrm{x}}\right)-\mathrm{e}^{\tan ^{-1} \mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{c}$
$y(o)=1 \Rightarrow c=2$
$\mathrm{y}(1)=\frac{2}{\mathrm{e}^{\pi / 4}}+\frac{\pi}{4}-1$

Question:
Let $\mathrm{a}_{1}=1$ and for $\mathrm{n} \geq 1, \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}+1}$
$=\frac{1}{2} a_{n}+\frac{n^{2}-2 n-1}{n^{2}(n+1)^{2}}$. Then $\left|\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\left(a_{n}-\frac{2}{n^{2}}\right)\right|$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$a_{n+1}-\frac{1}{2} a_{n}=\frac{n^{2}-2 n-1}{n^{2}(n+1)^{2}}=\frac{2 n^{2}-(n+1)^{3}}{n^{2}(n+1)^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}+1}-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{2}{(\mathrm{n}+1)^{2}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}^{2}}$
$\mathrm{n}=1 \mathrm{a}_{2}-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{a}_{1}=\frac{2}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{1^{2}}$
$2\left[\mathrm{a}_{3}-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{a}_{2}=\frac{2}{3^{2}}-\frac{1}{2^{2}}\right]$
$2^{2}\left[\mathrm{a}_{4}-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{a}_{3}=\frac{2}{4^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right]$
$ \vdots $
$2^{n-2}\left[a_{n}-\frac{1}{2} a_{n-1}=\frac{2}{n^{2}}-\frac{1}{(n-1)^{2}}\right]$
$2^{n-1}\left[a_{n+1}-\frac{1}{2} a_{n}=\frac{2}{(n+1)^{2}}-\frac{1}{n^{2}}\right]$
Adding
$\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}+1}=\frac{2}{(\mathrm{n}+1)^{2}}-\frac{1}{2^{\mathrm{n}}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{2}{\mathrm{n}^{2}}-\frac{1}{2^{\mathrm{n}-1}}$
$\Rightarrow\left|\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\left(a_{n}-\frac{2}{n^{2}}\right)\right| \Rightarrow\left|\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}-\frac{1}{2^{n-1}}\right|=2$

Question: Let $\mathrm{S}=\{(\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{n}): \mathrm{m}, \mathrm{n} \in\{1,2,3, \ldots . ., 50\}\}$. If the number of elements ( $\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{n}$ ) in S such that $6^{\mathrm{m}}+9^{\mathrm{n}}$ is a multiple of 5 is p and the number of elements $(\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{n})$ in S such that $\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}$ is a square of a prime number is q , then $\mathrm{p}+\mathrm{q}$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$
Solution:
$\mathrm{S}=\{1,2,3, \ldots .50\}$
$\mathrm{p}=\left(6^{\mathrm{m}}+9^{\mathrm{n}}\right)$ is divisible by 5
No. of ways
$6^{\mathrm{m}}=(5 \lambda+1)^{\mathrm{m}}=5 \mathrm{k}+1$
$9^{\mathrm{n}}=(10-1)^{\mathrm{n}}=10 \mu-1$ if n is odd
$ \Rightarrow $n must be odd
$10 \mu+1$ if n is even
$ \Rightarrow $ No. of ways $=50 \times 25=1250$
$\mathrm{q} \Rightarrow(\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n})$ is square of a prime
$m + n = 4$$m + n = 9$$m + n = 25$$m + n = 49$
No. of$ \downarrow $$ \downarrow $$ \downarrow $$ \downarrow $
ways382448


$\mathrm{q}=3+8+24+48=83$
$=\mathrm{p}+\mathrm{q}=1250+83=1333$

Question: Let $f: R \rightarrow R$ be a twice differentiable function such that the quadratic equation $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{m}^{2}-2 \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{m}+\mathrm{f}^{{\prime}{\prime}}(\mathrm{x})=0$ in m , has two equal roots for every $\mathrm{x} \in \mathrm{R}$. If $\mathrm{f}(0)=1$, $\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(0)=2$ and $(\alpha, \beta)$ is the largest interval in which the function $f\left(\log _{e} x-x\right)$ is increasing, then $\alpha+\beta$ is equal to
Solution:
Given quadratic equation has equal roots, thus
$\mathrm{D}=0 \Rightarrow\left(\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})\right)^{2}=\mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(\mathrm{x}) \cdot \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$
$\frac{\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})}{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})}=\frac{\mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(\mathrm{x})}{\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})}$
Integrate
$\ell \mathrm{n}(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}))=\ell \mathrm{n}\left(\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})\right)+\ell \mathrm{nC} \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{c} . \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})$
Put $=\mathrm{x}=0$
$1=\mathrm{c} .2 \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=\frac{1}{2}$
Now $2 \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})}{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})}=2$
Integrate
$\ell \mathrm{n}(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}))=2 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{d}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{d}=0$
$\Rightarrow \ln (\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}))=2 \mathrm{x} \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}}$
Now let $\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{f}(\ln \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{e}^{2(\operatorname{lnx} \mathrm{-x})}$
$\therefore \mathrm{g}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=2 \mathrm{e}^{2(\ell \mathrm{n} \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x})}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}-1\right) \geq 3$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1-x}{x} \geq 0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \in(0,1]$
$\Rightarrow \alpha=0, \beta=1$
$\alpha+\beta=1$.

Question: If the area of the region $\left\{(x, y): 1-2 x \leq y \leq 4-x^{2}\right.$, $x \geq 0, y \geq 0\}$ is $\frac{\alpha}{\beta}, \alpha, \beta, \in N, \operatorname{gcd}(\alpha, \beta)=1$, then the value of $(\alpha+\beta)$ is :
Solution:
Required area $=\frac{2}{3} \times 8-\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2} \times 1$
$=\frac{16}{3}-\frac{1}{4}=\frac{61}{12}=\frac{\alpha}{\beta}$
$\Rightarrow \alpha+\beta=73$

Question:
A random variable $X$ takes values $0,1,2,3$ with probabilities $\frac{2 \mathrm{a}+1}{30}, \frac{8 \mathrm{a}-1}{30}, \frac{4 \mathrm{a}+1}{30}$, b respectively, where $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b} \in \mathbf{R}$. Let $\mu$ and $\sigma$ respectively be the mean and standard deviation of X such that $\sigma^{2}+\mu^{2}=2$.
Then $\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{b}}$ is equal to :
Solution:
$x$0123
$p\left( x \right)$$\frac{{2a + 1}}{{30}}$$\frac{{8a - 1}}{{30}}$$\frac{{4a + 1}}{{30}}$$b$


$\sigma^{2}=\sum \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}^{2} \mathrm{p}\left(\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}\right)-\mu^{2}$
$\sigma^{2}+\mu^{2}=\sum \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}^{2} \mathrm{p}\left(\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}\right)$
$=0+1\left(\frac{8 a-1}{30}\right)+4\left(\frac{4 a+1}{30}\right)+9 b$
$\Rightarrow \frac{24 \mathrm{a}+270 \mathrm{~b}+3}{30}=2$
$24 \mathrm{a}+270 \mathrm{~b}=57$
$$
\begin{equation*}
8 \mathrm{a}+90 \mathrm{~b}=19 \tag{1}
\end{equation*}
$$
Also
$\sum \mathrm{p}(\mathrm{i})=1$
$\frac{2 a+1}{30}+\frac{8 a-1}{30}+\frac{4 a+1}{30}+b=1$
$$
\begin{equation*}
14 \mathrm{a}+30 \mathrm{~b}=29 \tag{2}
\end{equation*}
$$
Solving (1) & (2)
$\mathrm{a}=2, \quad \mathrm{~b}=\frac{1}{30}, \frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{b}}=60$

Question: For a triangle $A B C$, let $\vec{p}=\overrightarrow{B C}, \vec{q}=\overrightarrow{C A}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{BA}}$. If $|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}|=2 \sqrt{3},|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}|=2$ and $\cos \theta=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$, where $\theta$ is the angle between $\vec{p}$ and $\vec{q}$, then $|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \times(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}-3 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}})|^{2}+3|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}|^{2}$ is equal to:
Solution:
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}$
$\cos (\pi-\theta)=\frac{|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}|^{2}+|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}|^{2}-|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}|^{2}}{2|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \| \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}|}$
$\frac{-1}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{12+4-|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}|^{2}}{2 \cdot 2 \sqrt{3} \cdot 2}$
$|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}|^{2}=24$
$\therefore|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \times(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}-3 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}})|^{2}+3|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}|^{2}$
$=|\vec{p} \times(\vec{q}-3 \vec{p}-3 \vec{q})|^{2}+72$
$=|\vec{p} \times(-3 \vec{p}-2 \vec{q})|^{2}+72$
$=|-2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}|^{2}+72$
$=4|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}|^{2}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}|^{2} \times \sin ^{2} \theta+72$
$=4 \cdot 12.4 \cdot \frac{2}{3}+72$
$=200$

Question: For the matrices $\mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{ll}3 & -4 \\ 1 & -1\end{array}\right]$ and $\mathrm{B}=\left[\begin{array}{ll}-29 & 49 \\ -13 & 18\end{array}\right]$, if $\left(A^{15}+B\right)\left[\begin{array}{l}x \\ y\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l}0 \\ 0\end{array}\right]$, then among the following which one is true?
Solution:
Here $\mathrm{A}^{\mathrm{n}}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}2 \mathrm{n}+1 & -4 \mathrm{n} \\ \mathrm{n} & -2 \mathrm{n}+1\end{array}\right]$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{A}^{15}=\left[\begin{array}{ll}31 & -60 \\ 15 & -29\end{array}\right]$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{A}^{15}+\mathrm{B}=\left[\begin{array}{ll}2 & -11 \\ 2 & -11\end{array}\right]$
Now $\left(A^{15}+B\right)\left[\begin{array}{l}x \\ y\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l}0 \\ 0\end{array}\right]$
$\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{cc}2 & -11 \\ 2 & -11\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l}\mathrm{x} \\ \mathrm{y}\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l}0 \\ 0\end{array}\right]$
$\Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{x}-11 \mathrm{y}=0$

Question: Let $\mathrm{a}_{1}, \frac{\mathrm{a}_{2}}{2}, \frac{\mathrm{a}_{3}}{2^{2}}, \ldots, \frac{\mathrm{a}_{10}}{2^{9}}$ be a G.P. of common ratio $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$. If $\mathrm{a}_{1}+\mathrm{a}_{2}+\ldots+\mathrm{a}_{10}=62$, then $\mathrm{a}_{1}$ is equal to :
Solution:
$\frac{\mathrm{a}_{2}}{2 \mathrm{a}_{1}}=\frac{\mathrm{a}_{3}}{2 \mathrm{a}_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{a}_{4}}{2 \mathrm{a}_{3}}=\ldots .=\frac{\mathrm{a}_{10}}{2 \mathrm{a}_{9}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$
$\therefore \mathrm{a}_{1}, \mathrm{a}_{2}, \mathrm{a}_{3}, ., \mathrm{a}_{10}$ are in G.P. with common ratio $\sqrt{2}$.
$\sum_{i=1}^{10} a_{i}=\frac{a_{1}\left((\sqrt{2})^{10}-1\right)}{\sqrt{2}-1}=62$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}_{1}=2(\sqrt{2}-1)$

Question: Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be the roots of equation $x^{2}+2 a x+(3 a+10) =0$ such that $\alpha<1<\beta$. Then the set of all possible values of $a$ is :
Solution:
$\because \alpha<1<\beta$
$\mathrm{f}(1)<0$
