$\Rightarrow 5 \mathrm{a}+11<0$
$\mathrm{a}<\frac{-11}{5}$
$\therefore \mathrm{a} \in\left(-\infty, \frac{-11}{5}\right)$

Question: If P is a point on the circle $\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}=4, \mathrm{Q}$ is a point on the straight line $5 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}+2=0$ and $\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}+1=0$ is the perpendicular bisector of PQ , then 13 times the sum of abscissa of all such point P is $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
Mid point of PQ lies on $x-y+1=0$
$\frac{2 \cos \theta+\alpha}{2}-\frac{2 \sin \theta-5 \alpha-2}{2}+1=0$
$2 \cos \theta+\alpha-2 \sin \theta+5 \alpha+2+2=0$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\cos \theta-\sin \theta+3 \alpha+2=0 \tag{1}
\end{equation*}
$$
∵ Slope of PQ is -1
$\frac{2 \sin \theta+5 \alpha+2}{2 \cos \theta-\alpha}=-1$
$2 \sin \theta+5 \alpha+2=-2 \cos \theta+\alpha$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\sin \theta+\cos \theta+2 \alpha+1=0 \tag{2}
\end{equation*}
$$
eliminate $\alpha$ from (1) and (2)
$\Rightarrow \cos \theta+5 \sin \theta=1, \theta \in[0,2 \pi]$
$\Rightarrow 5 \times 2 \sin \frac{\theta}{2} \cos \frac{\theta}{2}=2 \sin ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}$
$\therefore \sin \frac{\theta}{2}=0 \Rightarrow \cos \theta=1$
or
$\sin \frac{\theta}{2}=5 \Rightarrow \cos \theta=-\frac{12}{13}$
Sum of all possible values of abscissa of point P is
$=2 \times 1+2\left(\frac{-12}{13}\right)=\frac{2}{13}$
$\therefore $ 13 times sum of all possible values of abscissa of point P is 2 .

Question: Let the line $L_{1}$ be parallel to the vector $-3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}$ and pass through the point $(2,6,7)$ and the line $L_{2}$ be parallel to the vector $2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$ and pass through the point $(4,3,5)$. If the line $\mathrm{L}_{3}$ is parallel to the vector $-3 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+16 \hat{k}$ and intersects the lines $\mathrm{L}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{L}_{2}$ at the points C and D , respectively, then $|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{CD}}|^{2}$ is equal to :
Solution:
$\quad L_{1}: \frac{x-2}{-3}=\frac{y-6}{2}=\frac{z-7}{4}$
Point C on $\mathrm{L}_{1}:\left(-3 \lambda_{1}+2,2 \lambda_{1}+6,4 \lambda_{1}+7\right)$
$\mathrm{L}_{2}: \frac{\mathrm{x}-4}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-3}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-5}{3}$
Point D on $\mathrm{L}_{2}:\left(2 \lambda_{2}+4, \lambda_{2}+3,3 \lambda_{2}+5\right)$
Dr's of line $L_{3}$ :
$\mathrm{L}_{3}: \frac{2 \lambda_{2}+3 \lambda_{1}+2}{-3}=\frac{\lambda_{2}-2 \lambda_{1}-3}{5}=\frac{3 \lambda_{2}-4 \lambda_{1}-2}{16}$
$\lambda_{1}=-3, \lambda_{2}=2$
C (11, 0, -5)
D (8, 5, 11)
$|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{CD}}|^{2}=3^{2}+5^{2}+16^{2}=290$

Question: The largest $\mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{N}$, for which $7^{\mathrm{n}}$ divides 101!, is :
Solution:
Exponent of 7 in 101!
$=\left[\frac{101}{7}\right]+\left[\frac{101}{7^{2}}\right]+\left[\frac{101}{7^{3}}\right]+\ldots \ldots$
$=14+2=16$

Question:
If
$\left(\frac{1}{{ }^{15} \mathrm{C}_{0}}+\frac{1}{{ }^{15} \mathrm{C}_{1}}\right)\left(\frac{1}{{ }^{15} \mathrm{C}_{1}}+\frac{1}{{ }^{15} \mathrm{C}_{2}}\right) \ldots\left(\frac{1}{{ }^{15} \mathrm{C}_{12}}+\frac{1}{{ }^{15} \mathrm{C}_{13}}\right) =$ $\frac{\alpha^{13}}{{ }^{14} \mathrm{C}_{0}{ }^{14} \mathrm{C}_{1} \ldots{ }^{14} \mathrm{C}_{12}}$, then $30 \alpha$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$\quad \prod_{\mathrm{r}=0}^{12}\left(\frac{1}{{ }^{15} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}}+\frac{1}{{ }^{15} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}+1}}\right)=\prod_{\mathrm{r}=0}^{12} \frac{\frac{16}{\mathrm{r}+1} \cdot{ }^{15} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}}{{ }^{15} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}} \cdot{ }^{15} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}+1}}$
$=\prod_{\mathrm{r}=0}^{12} \frac{16}{(\mathrm{r}+1) \cdot \frac{15}{\mathrm{r}+1} \cdot{ }^{14} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}}=\prod_{\mathrm{r}=0}^{12} \frac{\left(\frac{16}{15}\right)}{{ }^{14} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}}$
$=\frac{\left(\frac{16}{15}\right)^{13}}{{ }^{14} \mathrm{C}_{0} \cdot{ }^{14} \mathrm{C}_{1} \quad{ }^{14} \mathrm{C}_{12}} \Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{16}{15}$
$\Rightarrow 30 \alpha=32$

Question: Let the line L pass through the point $(-3,5,2)$ and make equal angles with the positive coordinate axes. If the distance of L from the point ( $-2, \mathrm{r}, 1$ ) is $\sqrt{\frac{14}{3}}$, then the sum of all possible values of $r$ is :
Solution:
Equation line is : $\frac{x+3}{1}=\frac{y-5}{1}=\frac{z-2}{1}=\lambda$
$\therefore $ General point R on line is $\mathrm{R}(\lambda-3, \lambda+5, \lambda+2)$

$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PR}} \equiv(\lambda-1, \lambda+5-\mathrm{r}, \lambda+1)$
Now $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PR}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}=0$
$\Rightarrow(\lambda-1) 1+(\lambda+5-\mathrm{r}) 1+(\lambda+1) 1=0$
$\Rightarrow 3 \lambda-\mathrm{r}+5=0$
$\Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{\mathrm{r}-5}{3}$
$\therefore \mathrm{R} \equiv\left(\frac{\mathrm{r}-5}{3}-3, \frac{\mathrm{r}-5}{3}+5, \frac{\mathrm{r}-5}{3}+2\right)$
$\mathrm{R} \equiv\left(\frac{\mathrm{r}-14}{3}, \frac{\mathrm{r}+10}{3}, \frac{\mathrm{r}+1}{3}\right)$
Now
$\mathrm{PR}=\sqrt{\frac{14}{3}} \Rightarrow(\mathrm{PR})^{2}=\frac{14}{3}$
$\Rightarrow\left(\frac{\mathrm{r}-14}{3}+2\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\mathrm{r}+10}{3}-\mathrm{r}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\mathrm{r}+1}{3}-1\right)^{2}=\frac{14}{3}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{(\mathrm{r}-8)^{2}}{9}+\frac{(10-2 \mathrm{r})^{2}}{9}+\frac{(\mathrm{r}-2)^{2}}{9}=\frac{14}{3}$
$\Rightarrow\left(r^{2}-16 r+64\right)+\left(100+4 r^{2}-40 r\right)+\left(r^{2}-4 r+4\right)=42$
$\Rightarrow 6 \mathrm{r}^{2}-60 \mathrm{r}+126=0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{r}^{2}-10 \mathrm{r}+21=0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{r}=3,7$
sum of possible value of r is $=10$

Question:
Let the maximum value of $\left(\sin ^{-1} \mathrm{x}\right)^{2}+\left(\cos ^{-1} \mathrm{x}\right)^{2}$ for $x \in\left[-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right]$ be $\frac{m}{n} \pi^{2}$, where $\operatorname{gcd}(m, n)=1$.
Then $\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$\left(\sin ^{-1} \mathrm{x}\right)^{2}+\left(\cos ^{-1} \mathrm{x}\right)^{2}$
$=\left(\sin ^{-1} \mathrm{x}+\cos ^{-1} \mathrm{x}\right)^{2}-2 \sin ^{-1} \mathrm{x} \cos ^{-1} \mathrm{x}$
$=\frac{\pi^{2}}{4}-2\left(\sin ^{-1} \mathrm{x}\right)\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\sin ^{-1} \mathrm{x}\right)$
$=2\left(\sin ^{-1} \mathrm{x}-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)^{2}+\frac{\pi^{2}}{8} \quad$ where $\sin ^{-1} \mathrm{x} \in\left[\frac{-\pi}{3}, \frac{\pi}{4}\right]$
Then max value occurs at $\sin ^{-1} \mathrm{x}=\frac{-\pi}{3}$
Which is $2\left(\frac{\pi}{3}+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)^{2}+\frac{\pi^{2}}{8}=\frac{29 \pi^{2}}{36}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{m}=29$ and $\mathrm{n}=36$
$\therefore \mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}=65$

Question: Let one end of a focal chord of the parabola $y^{2}=16 x$ be (16, 16). If $\mathrm{P}(\alpha, \beta)$ divides this focal chord internally in the ratio $5: 2$, then the minimum value of $\alpha+\beta$ is equal to :
Solution:
$\mathrm{y}^{2}=16 \mathrm{x}$

$\because $ parameter of point A is $\mathrm{t}=2$
⇒ Parameter of point $B$ is $t=-\frac{1}{2}$
⇒ Coordinates of B is $(1,-4)$
Case 1:

$\alpha=\frac{5+32}{7}=\frac{37}{7}$
$\beta=\frac{-20+32}{7}=\frac{12}{7}$
$\Rightarrow \alpha+\beta=7$

Case 2:

$\alpha=\frac{2+80}{7}$,
$\beta=\frac{-8+80}{7}$
$\alpha+\beta=22$
So minimum value of $\alpha+\beta=7$

Question: If $\int_{0}^{1} 4 \cot ^{-1}\left(1-2 \mathrm{x}+4 \mathrm{x}^{2}\right) \mathrm{dx}=\mathrm{a} \tan ^{-1}(2)-\operatorname{b log}_{\mathrm{e}}(5)$, where $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b} \in \mathbf{N}$, then $(2 \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b})$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
Let $\mathrm{I}=\int_{0}^{1} \cot ^{-1}\left(1-2 \mathrm{x}+4 \mathrm{x}^{2}\right) \mathrm{dx}$
$$
\begin{equation*}
I=\int_{0}^{1}\left(\cot ^{-1}(2 x-1)-\cot ^{-1}(2 x)\right) d x \tag{1}
\end{equation*}
$$
Applying king
$$
\begin{equation*}
I=\int_{0}^{1}\left(-\cot ^{-1}(2 x-1)+\cot ^{-1}(2 x-2)\right) d x \tag{2}
\end{equation*}
$$
From (1) & (2)
$2 \mathrm{I}=\int_{0}^{1}\left(\cot ^{-1}(2 \mathrm{x}-2)-\cot ^{-1}(2 \mathrm{x})\right) \mathrm{dx}$
$=\int_{0}^{1} \cot ^{-1}(2 \mathrm{x}-2) \mathrm{dx}-\int_{0}^{1} \cot ^{-1}(2 \mathrm{x}) \mathrm{dx}$
Applying King
$=\int_{0}^{1} \cot ^{-1}(-2 x) d x-\int_{0}^{1} \cot ^{-1}(2 x) d x$
$=\int_{0}^{1}\left(\pi-\cot ^{-1}(2 \mathrm{x})\right) \mathrm{dx}-\int_{0}^{1} \cot ^{-1}(2 \mathrm{x}) \mathrm{dx}$
$=\int_{0}^{1}\left(\pi-2 \cot ^{-1}(2 x)\right) d x$
$=\pi-2 \int_{0}^{1}\left(\cot ^{-1} 2 \mathrm{x}\right) \cdot 1 \mathrm{dx}$
By parts
$=\pi-2\left[\left(\mathrm{x}_{\left.\left.\cot ^{-1} 2 \mathrm{x}\right)_{0}^{1}+\int_{0}^{1} \frac{2 \mathrm{x}}{1+4 \mathrm{x}^{2}} \mathrm{dx}\right]}\right.\right.$
Let $1+4 \mathrm{x}^{2}=\mathrm{t}$
$8 \mathrm{xdx}=\mathrm{dt}$
$=\pi-2\left[\cot ^{-1} 2+\frac{1}{4} \int_{1}^{5} \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{t}}\right]$
$=\pi-2 \cot ^{-1} 2-\frac{1}{2} \ell \mathrm{n} 5$
$2 \mathrm{I}=2 \tan ^{-1} 2-\frac{1}{2} \ln 5$
$\Rightarrow 4 \mathrm{I}=4 \tan ^{-1} 2-\ell \mathrm{n} 5$
$\therefore 2 \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}=8+1=9$

Question: Let $\mathrm{y}^{2}=12 \mathrm{x}$ be the parabola with its vertex at O . Let P be a point on the parabola and A be a point on the x -axis such that $\angle \mathrm{OPA}=90^{\circ}$. Then the locus of the centroid of such triangles OPA is :
Solution:
$\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{AP}}=\frac{-\mathrm{t}}{2}$
Equation of AP is
$y-6 t=\frac{-t}{2}\left(x-3 t^{2}\right)$
Put $\mathrm{y}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=12+3 \mathrm{t}^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{A}\left(12+3 \mathrm{t}^{2}, 0\right)$
Let centroid of $\triangle$ OPA be $\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{h}, \mathrm{k})$
$\Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{~h}=0+3 \mathrm{t}^{2}+12+3 \mathrm{t}^{2}$
$3 \mathrm{k}=0+6 \mathrm{t}+0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=\frac{\mathrm{k}}{2}, \mathrm{~h}=2 \mathrm{t}^{2}+4$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{h}=2 \frac{\mathrm{k}^{2}}{4}+4$
⇒ Locus of $(\mathrm{h}, \mathrm{k})$ is
$y^{2}=2 x-8$

Question: Let [•]denote the greatest integer function and $f(\mathrm{x})=\lim _{\mathrm{n} \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{\mathrm{n}^{3}} \sum_{\mathrm{k}=1}^{\mathrm{n}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{k}^{2}}{3^{\mathrm{x}}}\right]$. Then $12 \sum_{\mathrm{j}=1}^{\infty} f(\mathrm{j})$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$\sum_{\mathrm{k}=1}^{\mathrm{n}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{k}^{2}}{3^{\mathrm{x}}}-1\right)<\sum_{\mathrm{k}=1}^{\mathrm{n}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{k}^{2}}{3^{\mathrm{x}}}\right] \leq \sum_{\mathrm{k}=1}^{\mathrm{n}} \frac{\mathrm{k}^{2}}{3^{\mathrm{x}}}$
$\frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+1)(2 \mathrm{n}+1)}{6.3^{\mathrm{x}}}<\sum_{\mathrm{k}=1}^{\mathrm{n}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{k}^{2}}{3^{\mathrm{x}}}\right] \leq \frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+1)(2 \mathrm{n}+1)}{6.3^{\mathrm{x}}}$
$\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{n(n+1)(2 n+1)}{6 n^{3} \cdot 3^{x}}<\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{n^{3}} \sum_{k=1}^{n}\left[\frac{k^{2}}{3^{x}}\right] \leq \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{n(n+1)(2 n+1)}{6 \cdot 3^{x} \cdot n^{3}}$
$\frac{1}{3^{\mathrm{x}+1}}<\lim _{\mathrm{n} \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{\mathrm{n}^{3}} \sum_{\mathrm{k}=1}^{\mathrm{n}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{k}^{2}}{3^{\mathrm{x}}}\right] \leq \frac{1}{3^{\mathrm{x}+1}}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\frac{1}{3^{\mathrm{x}+1}}$
$\Rightarrow 12 \sum_{\mathrm{j}=1}^{\infty} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{j})=12 \sum_{\mathrm{j}=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{3^{\mathrm{j}+1}}=12\left[\frac{1}{9}+\frac{1}{27}+-\infty\right]$
$=12 \cdot\left(\frac{\frac{1}{9}}{1-\frac{1}{3}}\right)=2$

Question: In the line $\alpha x+4 y=\sqrt{7}$, where $\alpha \in R$, touches the ellipse $3 x^{2}+4 y^{2}=1$ at the point $P$ in the first quadrant, then one of the focal distances of P is :
Solution:
$\alpha x+4 y-\sqrt{7}=0$ touches $3 x^{2}+4 y^{2}=1$
$\therefore \mathrm{c}^{2}=\mathrm{a}^{2} \mathrm{~m}^{2}+\mathrm{b}^{2}$
$\frac{7}{16}=\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{\alpha^{2}}{16}+\frac{1}{4} \Rightarrow \alpha=3,-3$
Tangent is $3 x+4 y-\sqrt{7}=0$
Let the point of contact is $\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{x}_{1} \mathrm{y}_{1}\right)$
$\therefore $ Tangent is $3 \mathrm{xx}_{1}+4 \mathrm{yy}_{1}=1$
$\therefore \frac{3 \mathrm{x}_{1}}{3}=\frac{4 \mathrm{y}_{1}}{4}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{7}} \quad \therefore \mathrm{P}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{7}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{7}}\right)$
$\mathrm{e}=\sqrt{1-\frac{3}{4}}=\frac{1}{2}$

$\mathrm{PS}=\mathrm{e}(\mathrm{PM})$
$=e\left(\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{e}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{7}}\right)$
$=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{7}}\right)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{7}}$
$\mathrm{PS}^{\prime}=\mathrm{e}\left(\mathrm{PM}^{\prime}\right)=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{e}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{7}}\right)=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{7}}+\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}\right)$
$=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}+\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{7}}$

Question:
Let $z$ be the complex number satisfying $|z-5| \leq 3$ and having maximum positive principal argument.
Then $34\left|\frac{5 z-12}{5 i z+16}\right|^{2}$ is equal to:
Solution:
$|\mathrm{z}-5| \leq 3$
For $\arg (\mathrm{z})$ to be maximum, z lies at P .
$\mathrm{z} \equiv(4 \cos \theta, 4 \sin \theta)$
$\equiv\left(4 \cdot\left(\frac{4}{5}\right), 4\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)\right)=\left(\frac{16}{5}, \frac{12}{5}\right)=\frac{16}{5}+\frac{12 \mathrm{i}}{5}$
Now, $34\left|\frac{5 \mathrm{z}-12}{5 \mathrm{iz}+16}\right|^{2}=34\left|\frac{(16+12 \mathrm{i})-12}{(16 \mathrm{i}-12)+16}\right|^{2}$
$=34\left|\frac{4+12 \mathrm{i}}{16 \mathrm{i}+4}\right|^{2}$
$=34\left(\frac{16+144}{256+16}\right)=34\left(\frac{160}{272}\right)=20$

Question:
Let $f: \mathbf{R} \rightarrow \mathbf{R}$ be a twice differentiable function such that $f^{\prime \prime}(\mathrm{x})>0$ for all $\mathrm{x} \in \mathbf{R}$ and $f^{\prime}(\mathrm{a}-1)=0$, where a is real number. Let $\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x})=f\left(\tan ^{2} \mathrm{x}-2 \tan \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{a}\right)$, $0<\mathrm{x}<\frac{\pi}{2}$.
Consider the following two statements :
(I) g is increasing in $\left(0, \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$
(II) g is decreasing in $\left(\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$
Then,
Solution:
$\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x})=f\left((\tan \mathrm{x}-1)^{2}+\mathrm{a}-1\right)$
$\mathrm{g}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=f^{\prime}\left((\tan \mathrm{x}-1)^{2}+\mathrm{a}-1\right) \cdot 2(\tan \mathrm{x}-1) \sec ^{2} \mathrm{x}$
$\because \quad f^{\prime}(\mathrm{a}-1)=0$ and $f^{\prime \prime}(\mathrm{x})>0$
$\therefore f^{\prime}\left((\tan \mathrm{x}-1)^{2}+\mathrm{a}-1\right)>0$
$\mathrm{g}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})>0$ if $(\tan \mathrm{x}-1)>0$
g is increasing in $\mathrm{x} \in\left(\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$
$\mathrm{g}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})<0$ if $\tan \mathrm{x}-1<0$
g is decreasing in $\mathrm{x} \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$

Question:
If the system of equations
$3 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}+4 \mathrm{z}=3$
$2 \mathrm{x}+\alpha \mathrm{y}-\mathrm{z}=-3$
$\mathrm{x}+2 \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{z}=4$
has no solution, then the value of $\alpha$ is equal to:
Solution:
for no solution $\Delta=0$
$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}3 & 1 & 4 \\ 2 & \alpha & -1 \\ 1 & 2 & 1\end{array}\right|=0$
$\Rightarrow 3(\mathrm{a}+2)+1(-1-2)+4(4-\alpha)=0$
$\Rightarrow 19-\alpha=0 \Rightarrow \alpha=19$
& for $\alpha=19$
$\Delta_{\mathrm{x}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}3 & 1 & 4 \\ -3 & 19 & -1 \\ 4 & 2 & 1\end{array}\right|=3(21)+1(-1)+4(-82)$
$\neq 0$
$\therefore $ no solution for $\alpha=19$

Question:
The positive integer n , for which the solutions of the equation
$\mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}+2)+(\mathrm{x}+2)(\mathrm{x}+4)+\ldots .+(\mathrm{x}+2 \mathrm{n}-2)(\mathrm{x}+2 \mathrm{n})$
$=\frac{8 n}{3}$
are two consecutive even integers, is :-
Solution:
$x(x+2)+(x+2)(x+4)+\ldots .+(x+2 n-2)(x+2 n)=\frac{8 n}{3}$
$\Rightarrow \sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{\mathrm{n}}(\mathrm{x}+2 \mathrm{r}-2)(\mathrm{x}+2 \mathrm{r})=\frac{8 \mathrm{n}}{3}$
$n x^{2}+2 x \sum_{r=1}^{n}(2 r-1)+4 \sum_{r=1}^{n} r(r-1)=\frac{8 n}{3}$
$n x^{2}+2 x \cdot n^{2}+\frac{4 n\left(n^{2}-1\right)}{3}-\frac{8 n}{3}=0$
$x^{2}+2 n x+\frac{4\left(n^{2}-1\right)}{3}-\frac{8}{3}=0<_{\beta}^{\alpha}$
$\because|\alpha-\beta|=2 \Rightarrow \frac{\sqrt{\mathrm{D}}}{|\mathrm{a}|}=2 \Rightarrow \mathrm{D}=4$
$\Rightarrow 4 \mathrm{n}^{2}-4\left(4 \frac{\left(\mathrm{n}^{2}-1\right)}{3}-\frac{8}{3}\right)=4$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}^{2}-\frac{4 \mathrm{n}^{2}}{3}=-3$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}^{2}=9$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=3$

Question: Let $\mathrm{A}=\{2,3,5,7,9\}$. Let R be the relation on A defined by x Ry if and only if $2 \mathrm{x} \leq 3 \mathrm{y}$. Let $\ell$ be the number of elements in R , and m be the minimum number of elements required to be added in R to make it a symmetric relation. Then $\ell+\mathrm{m}$ is equal to:
Solution:
$\quad \mathrm{A}=\{2,3,5,7,9\}$
$\mathrm{y} \geq \frac{2 \mathrm{x}}{3}$
$\left.\begin{array}{ll}\mathrm{x}=2, & \mathrm{y}=2,3,5,7,9 \\ \mathrm{x}=3, & \mathrm{y}=2,3,5,7,9 \\ \mathrm{x}=5, & \mathrm{y}=5,7,9 \\ \mathrm{x}=7, & \mathrm{y}=5,7,9 \\ \mathrm{x}=9 & \mathrm{y}=7,9\end{array}\right] \rightarrow \ell=18$
to make it symmetric elements to be added are $\{(5,2),(7,2),(9,2),(5,3),(7,3),(9,3),(9,5)\}$
$\mathrm{m}=7$
$\therefore \quad \ell+\mathrm{m}=25$

Question: Let $y=y(x)$ be the solution of the differential equation $\sec x \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}-2 \mathrm{y}=2+3 \sin \mathrm{x}, \mathrm{x} \in\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$, $y(0)=-\frac{7}{4}$. Then $y\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$ is equal to:
Solution:
$\frac{d y}{d x}-2 y \cos x=2 \cos x+3 \sin x \cdot \cos x$
I.F. $=\mathrm{e}^{-2 \sin \mathrm{x}}$
$e^{-2 \sin x} \cdot y=\int e^{-2 \sin x}(3 \sin x \cos x+2 \cos x) d x$
$y . e^{-2 \sin x}=e^{-2 \sin x}\left(-\frac{3}{2} \sin x-\frac{7}{4}\right)+C$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=-\frac{3}{2} \sin \mathrm{x}-\frac{7}{4}+\mathrm{C} . \mathrm{e}^{2 \sin \mathrm{x}}$
$\because \mathrm{y}(0)=-\frac{7}{4} \Rightarrow \mathrm{C}=0$
$y\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=\frac{-3}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2}-\frac{7}{4}=\frac{-5}{2}$

Question: Let $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{x}^{3}+\mathrm{x}^{2} f^{\prime}(1)+2 \mathrm{x} f^{\prime \prime}(2)+f^{\prime \prime \prime}(3)$, $\mathrm{x} \in \mathrm{R}$. Then the value of $f^{\prime}(5)$ is :
Solution:
$\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=3 \mathrm{x}^{2}+2 \mathrm{xf}^{\prime}(1)+2 \mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(2)$
$\mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(\mathrm{x})=6 \mathrm{x}+2 \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)$
$\mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(2)=12+2 \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)$
$\therefore \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=3 \mathrm{x}^{2}+2 \mathrm{xf}^{\prime}(1)+2\left(12+2 \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)\right)$
$\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=3 \mathrm{x}^{2}+2(\mathrm{x}+2) \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)+24$
Putting, $\mathrm{x}=1$
$\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)=3+6 \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1)+24$
$-5 f^{\prime}(1)=27 \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(1)=\frac{-27}{5}$
$\therefore \mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(2)=12+2\left(\frac{-27}{5}\right)=12-\frac{54}{5}=\frac{6}{5}$
$\therefore \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=3 \mathrm{x}^{2}-\frac{54}{5} \mathrm{x}+\frac{12}{5}$
$\therefore \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(5)=75-54+\frac{12}{5}=\frac{117}{5}$

Question:
The Boolean expression \[Y = A\bar BC + \bar A\bar C\]can be realised with which of the following gate configurations.
A. One 3-input AND gate, 3 NOT gates and one 2 input OR gate, One 2-input AND gate,
B. One 3-input AND gate, 1 NOT gate, One 2 input NOR gate and one 2-input OR gate
C. 3-input OR gate, 3 NOT gates and one 2-input AND gate
Choose the correct answer from the options given below
Solution:
(A)
(B)
\[\because \,\,\bar A.\bar C + \overline {A + C} \equiv NOR\,gate\]

Question:
The helium and argon are put in the flask at the same room temperature (300 K). The ratio of average kinetic energies (per molecule) of helium and argon is :
(Give : Molar mass of helium = 4 g/mol, Molar mass of argon = 40 g/ mol )
Solution:
\[K.E = \frac{f}{2}KT\]
For He and Ar f = 3
\[\frac{{K.{E_{HE}}}}{{K.{E_{Ar}}}} = \frac{1}{1}\]

Question:
Given below are two statements :
1 M aqueous solution of each of Cu(NO3)2, AgNO3, Hg2(NO3)2; Mg(NO3)2 are electrolysed using inert electrodes,
\[Given:\;E_{A{g^ + }/Ag}^\theta = 0.80V,\;E_{Hg_2^{2 + }/Hg}^\theta = 0.79V,\]
\[E_{C{u^{2 + }}/Cu}^\theta = 0.24V\;and\;E_{M{g^{2 + }}/Mg}^\theta = - 2.37V\]
Statement (I) : With increasing voltage, the sequence of deposition of metals on the cathode will be Ag, Hg and Cu
Statement (II) : Magnesium will not be deposited at cathode instead oxygen gas will be evolved at the cathode.
In the light of the above statement, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
Options: Both statement I and statement II are incorrect
Statement I is correct but statement II is incorrect
Both statement I and statement II are correct
Statement I is incorrect but statement II is correct
Solution: Statement-II ⇒ At cathode, instead of Mg, H2O(l) will reduce & evolve H2 gas.

Question:
For A2 + B2 ⇌ 2AB
Ea for forward and backward reaction are 180 and 200 kJ mol–1 respectively
If catalyst lowers Ea for both reaction by 100 kJ mol–1.
Which of the following statement is correct?
Options: Catalyst does not alter the Gibbs energy change of a reaction.
Catalyst can cause non-spontaneous reactions to occur.
The enthalpy change for the reaction is +20 kJ mol<sup>–1</sup>.
The enthalpy change for the catalysed reaction is different from that of uncatalysed reaction.
Solution:
A2 + B2 ⇌ 2AB
Ef = 180 kJ mol–1
Eb = 200 kJ mol–1
∆ H = Ef – Eb = –20 kJ mol–1
In presence of catalyst :
Ef = 180 – 100 = 80 kJ mol–1
Eb = 200 – 100 = 100 kJ mol–1
Catalyst does not change ∆ H or ∆ G of a reaction.

Question:
Consider the following statements related to temperature dependence of rate constants. Identify the correct statements,
A. The Arrhenius equation holds true only for an elementary homogenous reaction.
B. The unit of A is same as that of k in Arrhenius equation.
C. At a given temperature, a low activation energy means a fast reaction.
D. A and Ea as used in Arrhenius equation depend on temperature.
E. When Ea >> RT. A and Ea become interdependent.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
Solution: Arrhenious equation hold true for elementary as well as complex reactions. Unit of A is same as unit of k. Rate of reaction is high if activation energy is low, A and Ea are temperature independent.

Question: Pre-exponential factors of two different reactions of same order are identical. Let activation energy of first reaction exceeds the activation energy of second reaction by $20 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. If $\mathrm{k}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{k}_{2}$ are the rate constants of first and second reaction respectively at 300 K , then In $\frac{\mathrm{k}_{2}}{\mathrm{k}_{1}}$ will be ___________ (nearest integer) $\left[\mathrm{R}=8.3 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right]$
Solution:
$\mathrm{A} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{RX}^{\mathrm{n}}(1)}$ product $\mathrm{E}_{1}$
$\mathrm{B} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{RX}^{\mathrm{n}}(2)}$ product $\mathrm{E}_{2}$
Assuming 'A' same for both reaction.
$\ln \mathrm{k}_{1}=\ln \mathrm{A}-\frac{\mathrm{E}_{1}}{300 \mathrm{R}}$
$\ln \mathrm{k}_{1}=\ln \mathrm{A}-\frac{\mathrm{E}_{2}}{300 \mathrm{R}}$
$\ln \left(\frac{\mathrm{k}_{2}}{\mathrm{k}_{1}}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{E}_{1}-\mathrm{E}_{2}}{300 \mathrm{R}}=\frac{20 \times 1000}{300 \mathrm{R}}$
$=8.032$

Question:
$ 0.53 \mathrm{~g}$ of an organic compound ( x ) when heated with excess of nitric acid (concentrated) and then with silver nitrate gave 0.75 g of silver bromide precipitate. 1.0 g of $(\mathrm{x})$ gave 1.32 g of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ gas on combustion. The percentage of hydrogen in the compound ( x ) is __________ %. [Nearest Integer]
[Given : Molar mass in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{H}: 1, \mathrm{C}: 12, \mathrm{Br}:$ 80, Ag : 108, O : 16; Compound (x) : $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{Br}_{\mathrm{z}}$ ]
Solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \underset{1 \mathrm{~g}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{Br}_{\mathrm{z}}} \rightarrow \underset{1.32 \mathrm{~g}}{\mathrm{CO}_{2}} \\
& \% \mathrm{C}=\frac{1.32 \times 12}{44 \times 1} \times 100=36 \% \\
& \underset{0.53 \mathrm{~g}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{Br}_{\mathrm{z}}} \rightarrow \underset{0.75 \mathrm{~g}}{\mathrm{AgBr}} \\
& \% \mathrm{Br}=\frac{0.75 \times 80}{188 \times 0.53} \times 100=60.2 \% \\
& \% \mathrm{H}=100-(36+60.2) \\
& \% \mathrm{H} \simeq 4 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

Question:
On complete combustion 1.0 g of an organic compound (X) gave 1.46 g of CO2 and 0.567 g of H2O. The empirical formula mass of compound (X) is -------- g.
(Given molar mass in g mol–1 C : 12, H : 1, O : 16)

On combustion 0.210 g of an organic compound containing C, H and O gave 0.127 g H<sub>2</sub>O and 0.307 g CO<sub>2</sub>. The percentages of hydrogen and oxygen in the given organic compound respectively are:
Solution:
In the combustion of organic compound, all “C” in CO2 and all “H” in H2O comes from organic compound
\[{C_x}{H_y}{O_z} + {O_2} \to \mathop {C{O_2}}\limits_{0.307\,\,gm} + \mathop {{H_2}O}\limits_{0.127\,\,gm} \]
Weight of “C” in CO2 = \[\frac{{12}}{{44}} \times 0.307\]
\[ = 0.0837\,gm\]
Weight of “H” in H2O = \[\frac{2}{{18}} \times 0.127 = 0.041\,g\]
% ‘H’ in compound = \[\frac{{0.0141}}{{0.21}} \times 100 = 6.719\% \]
= 6.72%
Weight of “O” in compound
= 0.210 – (0.0837 + 0.0141)
= 0.1122
% of “O” in compound = \[\frac{{0.1122}}{{0.21}} \times 100\]
= 53.41%

Question: The wavelength of photon ' $A$ ' is 400 nm . The frequency of photon ' B ' is $10^{16} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. The wave number of photon ' C ' is $10^{4} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$. The correct order of energy of these photons is :
Solution:
(1) Wavelength of $\mathrm{A}=400 \mathrm{~nm}$.
(2) Wavelength of $\mathrm{B}(\lambda)=\frac{\mathrm{C}}{v}=\frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{10^{16}} =3 \times 10^{-8}=30 \times 10^{-9}=30 \mathrm{~nm}$.
(3) Wavelength of $\mathrm{C}(\lambda)=\frac{1}{\bar{v}}=\frac{1}{10^{4}}=10^{-4} \mathrm{~cm} =10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}=1000 \mathrm{~nm}$
Here $\lambda_{\mathrm{C}}>\lambda_{\mathrm{A}}>\lambda_{\mathrm{B}}$
Energy $(\mathrm{E}) \propto \frac{1}{\lambda}$
So $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{B}}>\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{A}}>\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{C}}$
Ans. (2) is correct.

Question:
The energy required by electrons, present in the first Bohr orbit of hydrogen atom to be excited to second Bohr orbit is __________ J mol–1.
Given : $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}}=2.18 \times 10^{-11}$ ergs.
Solution:
$E_{n}=-R_{H} \times \frac{Z^{2}}{n^{2}}$
$\Delta \mathrm{E}=2.18 \times 10^{-11} \times 10^{-7} \times 1^{2}\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{2^{2}}\right]$
$=1.635 \times 10^{-18}$ Joule/atom
$=1.635 \times 10^{-18} \times 6.02 \times 10^{23}$ Joule $/$ mole
$=9.835 \times 10^{5}$ Joule $/$ mole

For charging of lead storage battery cell reaction is 2PbSO4(s) + 2H2O(l) → Pb(s) + PbO2(s) + 2H2SO4(aq) At anode PbSO4 reduced back to Pb and at cathode PbSO4 oxidised back to PbO2.
\[\because \] x1 = +2, y1 = 0
x2 = + 2, y2 = 4

The molar conductance of an infinitely dilute solution of ammonium chloride was found to be 185 S cm<sup>2</sup> mol<sup>–1</sup> and the ionic conductance of hydroxyl and chloride ions are 170 and 70 S cm<sup>2</sup> mol<sup>–1</sup>, respectively. If molar conductance of 0.02 M solution of ammonium hydroxide is 85.5 S cm<sup>2</sup> mol<sup>–1</sup>, its degree of dissociation is given by x × 10<sup>–1</sup>. The value of x is _____. (Nearest integer)
Solution:
\[\lambda _m^{\text{o}}\,{\text{of}}\,N{H_4}Cl = 185\]
\[{\left( {\lambda _m^{\text{o}}} \right)_{NH_4^ + }} + {\left( {\lambda _m^o} \right)_{C{l^ - }}} = 185\]
\[{\left( {\lambda _m^{\text{o}}} \right)_{NH_4^ + }} = 185 - 70 = 115\,{\text{Sc}}{{\text{m}}^2}\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}\]
\[{\left( {\lambda _m^{\text{o}}} \right)_{N{H_4}OH}} = {\left( {\lambda _m^{\text{o}}} \right)_{NH_4^ + }} + {\left( {\lambda _m^o} \right)_{O{H^ - }}}\]
\[ = 115 + 170\]
\[{\left( {\lambda _m^{\text{o}}} \right)_{N{H_4}OH}} = 285\,Sc{m^2}\,mo{l^{ - 1}}\]
degree of dissociation \[ = \frac{{{{\left( {{\lambda _m}} \right)}_{N{H_4}OH}}}}{{{{\left( {\lambda _m^{\text{o}}} \right)}_{N{H_4}OH}}}}\]
\[ = \frac{{85.5}}{{285}}\]
\[ = 0.3\]
\[ = 3 \times {10^{ - 1}}\]

Question: If the mean and the variance of 6, 4, a, 8, b, 12, 10, 13 are 9 and 9.25 respectively, then a + b + ab is equal to :
Solution:
\[\therefore \]mean = 9
\[\therefore 53 + a + b = 72\]
\[ \Rightarrow a + b = 19\]

\[ \Rightarrow 81 + \frac{{37}}{4} = \frac{{529 + {a^2} + {b^2}}}{8}\]

\[ \Rightarrow {a^2} + {b^2} = 193\]

Question: Let a random variable X take values 0, 1, 2, 3 with P(X = 0) = P(X = 1) = p, P(X = 2) = P(X = 3) and E(X<sup>2</sup>) = 2E(X). Then the value of 8p – 1 is :
Solution:
\[2p + 2q = \frac{1}{2}\]
p + q
\[E\left( {{x^2}} \right) = \sum\limits_{i = 0}^3 {x_i^2p\left( {{x_i}} \right) = 0.p + 1.p + 4.q + 9q} \]
= p + 13q
\[E\left( x \right) = \sum\limits_{i = 0}^3 {x_i^2p\left( {{x_i}} \right) = 0.p + 1.p + 2q + 3q} = p + 5q\]
p + 13q = 2 (p + 5q)
p = 3q
\[So,\;q = \frac{1}{8}\;\& \;p = \frac{3}{8}\]
So, 8p – 1 = 2

Question:

($\mathrm{R}=$ Rydberg constant)

Balmer series line $\Rightarrow \bar{v}=\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}} \mathrm{z}^{2}\left[\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}^{2}}\right]$
if $\mathrm{n}=3 \Rightarrow \overline{\mathrm{v}}=\mathrm{R}(1)^{2}\left[\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right]=\frac{5 \mathrm{R}}{36}$
if $\mathrm{n}=4 \Rightarrow \bar{v}=\frac{3 \mathrm{R}}{16}$
if $\mathrm{n}=5 \Rightarrow \overline{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{21 \mathrm{R}}{100}$

Question: At 298 K , the mole percentage of $\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ in air is $80 \%$. Water is in equilibrium with air at a pressure of 10 atm . What is the mole fraction of $\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ in water at 298 K ? ( $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{H}}$ for $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ is $6.5 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg}$ )
Solution:
$\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{H}} \cdot \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}$
$\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}=0.8 \times 10=8 \mathrm{~atm}$
$8 \times 760=6.5 \times 10^{7} \times \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}$
$\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}=\frac{8 \times 760}{6.5 \times 10^{7}}$
$\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}=9.35 \times 10^{-5}$.

Question:
Let $\mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}0 & 2 & -3 \\ -2 & 0 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 & 0\end{array}\right]$ and B be a matrix such that
$\mathrm{B}(\mathrm{I}-\mathrm{A})=\mathrm{I}+\mathrm{A}$. Then the sum of the diagonal elements of $B^{T} B$ is equal to
Solution:
$A^{T}=-A$
$\mathrm{B}=(\mathrm{I}+\mathrm{A})(\mathrm{I}-\mathrm{A})^{-1}$
$\mathrm{B}^{\mathrm{T}}=\left((\mathrm{I}-\mathrm{A})^{-1}\right)^{\mathrm{T}}(\mathrm{I}+\mathrm{A})^{\mathrm{T}}$
$B^{T}=\left(I-A^{T}\right)^{-1}\left(I+A^{T}\right)$
$\mathrm{B}^{\mathrm{T}}=(\mathrm{I}+\mathrm{A})^{-1}(\mathrm{I}+\mathrm{A})$
$\mathrm{B}^{\mathrm{T}} \mathrm{B}=(\mathrm{I}+\mathrm{A})^{-1}(\mathrm{I}-\mathrm{A})(\mathrm{I}+\mathrm{A})(\mathrm{I}-\mathrm{A})^{-1}$
$=(\mathrm{I}+\mathrm{A})^{-1}(\mathrm{I}+\mathrm{A})(\mathrm{I}-\mathrm{A})(\mathrm{I}-\mathrm{A})^{-1}$
$=\mathrm{I}$
$\operatorname{tr}\left(\mathrm{B}^{\mathrm{T}} \mathrm{B}\right)=3$

Solution:
For $\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{s})={ }^{10} \mathrm{C}_{4}=210$
$(5,4,1,1,1) \quad(5,2,2,1,1)$
For $\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{p})=\frac{5!}{3!}+\frac{5!}{2!2!}=50$
$\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{p})=210+50=260$

Question: Choose the correct set of reagents for the following conversion.

Let z be a complex number such that $|\mathrm{z}-6|=5$ and $|z+2-6 i|=5$.
Then the value of $z^{3}+3 z^{2}-15 z+141$ is equal to
Solution:
Center of first circle $\mathrm{C}_{1}(6,0), \mathrm{r}_{1}=5$
Center of second circle $\mathrm{C}_{2}(-2,6), \mathrm{r}_{2}=5$
$\because \mathrm{C}_{1} \mathrm{C}_{2}=\mathrm{r}_{1}+\mathrm{r}_{2}$
$\therefore $ common point Z is mid point of $\mathrm{C}_{1} \& \mathrm{C}_{2}$

$\therefore z^{3}+3 z^{2}-15 z+141=50$

Question: For electron in '2s' and '2p' orbitals, the orbital angular momentum values, respectively are :
Solution:
Orbital angular momentum \[ = \sqrt {l\left( {l + 1} \right)} \;\frac{h}{{2\pi }}\]
∴ For 2s orbital : l = 0
Orbital angular momentum = 0
∴ For 2p orbital : l = 1
Orbital angular momentum \[ = \sqrt {l\left( {l + 1} \right)} \;\frac{h}{{2\pi }}\]
\[ = \sqrt 2 \;\frac{h}{{2\pi }}\]

Question: A solution is made by mixing one mole of volatile liquid A with 3 moles of volatile liquid B. The vapour pressure of pure A is 200 mm Hg and that of the solution is 500 mm Hg. The vapour pressure of pure B and the least volatile component of the solution, respectively, are :
Solution:
\[{P_S} = P_A^0.{X_A} + P_B^0\]
\[500 = 200 \times \frac{1}{4} + P_B^o.\frac{3}{4}\]
\[P_B^o = 600\,mm\,\,Hg\]
\[As\,\,P_A^o < P_B^o\]⇒ A is least volatile.

Question: If S and S' are the foci of the ellipse \[\frac{{{x^2}}}{{18}} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{9} = 1\] and P be a point on the ellipse, then min(SP.S'P) + max(SP.S'P) is equal to :
Solution:
\[PS + PS' = 2 \times 3\sqrt 2 \]
\[{b^2} = {a^2}(1 - {e^2}) \Rightarrow 9 = 18(1 - {e^2})\]
Directrix \[x = \frac{a}{e} = \frac{{3\sqrt 2 }}{{\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}}} = 6\]
PS.PS’= \[\left| {\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\left( {3\sqrt 2 \cos \theta - 6} \right)\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}(3\sqrt 2 \cos \theta + 6)} \right|\]
\[ = \frac{1}{2}\left| {18{{\cos }^2}\theta - 36} \right|\]
(PS·PS')max = 18 ; (PS·PS')min = 9
sum = 27

Question:
Let a ∈ R and A be a matrix of order 3×3 such that
det (A) = − 4 and A + I = \[\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  1&a&1 \\
  2&1&0 \\
  a&1&2
\end{array}} \right]\], where I is the identify matrix of order 3 × 3
If det ((a + 1)adj((a–1)A)) is 2m3n, m, n ∈ {0,1,2,…..20}, then m + n is equal to :
Solution:
\[A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  1&a&1 \\
  2&1&0 \\
  a&1&2
\end{array}} \right] = - I = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  0&a&1 \\
  2&0&0 \\
  a&1&1
\end{array}} \right]\]
|A| = – 4 ⇒ 2 – 2a = – 4 ⇒ a = 3
|(a + 1) adj (a – 1)A| = |4 adj 3A|
 = 43|adj 3A|
= 43 × |3A|3–1 = 64|3A|2
= 64 × (33)2 |A|2
= 26 × 36 × 16
2m × 3n = 210 × 36
∴ m = 10, n = 6
⇒ m + n = 16

Question: Let A be the set of all functions f : Z →  Z and R be a relation on A such that R = {(f, g) : f (0) = g(1) and f (1) = g(0)}. Then R is:
Options: Symmetric and transitive but not reflective
Symmetric but neither reflective nor transitive
Reflexive but neither symmetric nor transitive
Transitive but neither reflexive nor symmetric
Solution:
R = {(f, g) : f(0) = g(1) and f(1) = g(0)} Reflexive: (f, f) ∈ R
= f(0) = f(1) and f(1) = f(0) → must hold
⇒ but this is not true for all function so not reflexive
Symmetric: If (f, g) ∈ R ⇒ (g, f) ∈ R
Now, g(0) = f(1) and g(1) = f(0) → true
∴ symmetric
Transitive : If(f, g) ∈ R and (g, h) ∈ R
⇒ (f, h) ∈ R
Now (f, g) ∈ R ⇒ f(0) = g(1) and f(1) = g(0)
(g, h) ∈ R ⇒ g(0) = h(1) and g(1) = h(0)
For (f, h) ∈ R we need f(0) = h(1) and f(1) = h(0)
Now f(0) = g(1) = h(0) and f(1) = g(0) = h(1)
Hence not transitive

Question: If \[\vec a\]is nonzero vector such that its projections on the vectors \[2\hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k,\,\,\hat i + 2\hat j - 2\hat k\] and \[\hat k\] are equal, then a unit vector along \[\vec a\] is
Solution:
Let \[\bar a = {a_1}\hat i + {a_2}\hat j + {a_3}\hat k\]
\[a_1^2 + a_2^2 + a_3^2 = 1\]
Let \[\bar b = 2\vec i - - \hat j + 2\hat k,\bar c = \vec i - - 2\hat j - 2\hat k\]
\[\bar d = \hat k\]
\[\frac{{\bar a.\bar b}}{{|b|}} = \frac{{\bar a.\bar c}}{{|c|}} = \frac{{\bar a.\bar d}}{{|d|}}\]
\[\frac{{2{a_1} - {a_2} + 2{a_3}}}{3} = \frac{{{a_1} + 2{a_2} - 2{a_3}}}{3} = {a_3}\]
By solving
\[{a_1} = \frac{7}{{\sqrt {155} }},{a_2} = \frac{9}{{\sqrt {155} }},{a_3} = \frac{5}{{\sqrt {155} }}\]

Question:
Let ƒ : R → R be a thrice differentiable odd function satisfying
ƒ'(x) ≥ 0, ƒ'(x) = ƒ(x), ƒ(0) = 0, ƒ'(0) = 3. Then $9ƒ(log_{e}3)$ is equal to _____.
Solution:
f ''(x) = f(x)
⇒ f '(x) . f ''(x) = f '(x)·f(x)
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{{{(f'(x))}^2}}}{2} = \frac{{{{(f(x))}^2}}}{2} + C\]
\[ \Rightarrow {(f'(x))^2} = {(f(x))^2} + C'\]
\[f(0) = 0,\,f'(0) = 3\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, \Rightarrow C' = 9\]
\[\therefore \,\,(f'(x) = \sqrt {{{(f(x))}^2} + 9} \]
\[(f'(x) = \sqrt {{{(f(x))}^2} + 9} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\because f'(x) \geqslant 0\]
\[\int {\frac{{dy}}{{\sqrt {{y^2} + 9} }} = \int {dx \Rightarrow \ell n} \left| {y + \sqrt {{y^2} + 9} } \right|} = x + C\]
\[ \Rightarrow f(0) = 0 \Rightarrow C = \ell n3\]
\[ \Rightarrow y + \sqrt {{y^2} + 9} = 3{e^x}\]
\[at\,\,x = \ell n3;\,y = 4'\]
\[\therefore \,\,9f(\ell n3) = 36\]

Question:
Let f : R → R be a twice differentiable function such that (sin x cos y)(f(2x + 2y) – f(2x – 2y)) = (cos x sin y)(f(2x + 2y) + f(2x – 2y)), for all x, y ∈ R.

f(2x + 2y)(sin(x–y)) = f(2x – 2y)sin(x + y)
$\frac{{{\text{f}}\left( {2{\text{x}} + 2{\text{y}}} \right)}}{{\sin \left( {{\text{x}} + {\text{y}}} \right)}} = \frac{{{\text{f}}\left( {2{\text{x}} - 2{\text{y}}} \right)}}{{\sin \left( {{\text{x}} - {\text{y}}} \right)}}$
Put 2x + 2y = m, 2x – 2y = n
$\frac{{{\text{f}}\left( {\text{m}} \right)}}{{\sin \left( {\frac{{\text{m}}}{2}} \right)}} = \frac{{{\text{f}}\left( {\text{n}} \right)}}{{\sin \left( {\frac{{\text{n}}}{2}} \right)}} = {\text{K}}$
$ \Rightarrow {\text{f}}({\text{m}}) = {\text{K}}\sin \left( {\frac{{\text{m}}}{2}} \right)$
$\therefore \quad {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = {\text{Ksin}}\left( {\frac{{\text{x}}}{2}} \right)$
Put ${\text{x}} = 0;\frac{1}{2} = \frac{{\text{K}}}{2} \Rightarrow {\text{K}} = 1$
${\text{f'}}({\text{x}}) = \frac{1}{2}{\text{cos}}\frac{{\text{x}}}{2}$

\[ = \frac{{ - 24}}{8} = - 3\]

Question: If the function \[f(x) = 2{x^3} - 9a{x^2} + 12{a^2}x + 1,\]where a > 0, attains its local maximum and local minimum values at p and q, respectively, such that p<sup>2</sup> = q, then f(3) is equal to:
Solution:
f'(x) = 6x2 – 18ax + 12a2
f'(x) = 6(x2 – 3ax + 2a2)
roots are a, 2a
p2 = q ⇒ a2 = 2a
a = 2
f(x) = 2x3 – 18x2 + 48x + 1
f(3) = 37

Question: The absolute difference between the squares of the radii of the two circles passing through the point (–9, 4) and touching the lines x + y = 3 and x – y = 3, is equal to _____.
Solution:
Centre (a, 0)
${\mathbf{r}} = \left| {\frac{{{\mathbf{a}} - 0 - 3}}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right|$
Circle (x - a)2 + y2 = ${\left( {\frac{{{\mathbf{a}} - 3}}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right)^2}$
passes through (–9, 4)
2(a2 + 18a + 81 + 16) = (a2 – 6a + 9)
a2 + 42a + 185 = 0
(a + 37) (a + 5) = 0
⇒ a = –37, – 5
${{\text{r}}_1} = \left| {\frac{{ - 37 - 3}}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right| = 20\sqrt 2 $
${{\mathbf{r}}_2} = \left| {\frac{{ - 5 - 3}}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right| = 4\sqrt 2 $
\[\left| {r_1^2 - r_2^2} \right| = \left| {800 - 32} \right| = 768\]

Question: If $\lim _{x \rightarrow 2} \frac{\sin \left(x^{3}-5 x^{2}+a x+b\right)}{(\sqrt{x-1}-1) \log _{e}(x-1)}=m$, then $a+b+m$ is equal to:
Solution:
$\because$ Denominator $=0$ at $\mathrm{x}=2$
⇒ Numerator $=0$ at $\mathrm{x}=2$
$\Rightarrow 2^{3}-5(2)^{2}+\mathrm{a}(2)+\mathrm{b}=0$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}=12 \tag{1}
\end{equation*}
$$
$\mathrm{m}=\underset{\mathrm{x} \rightarrow 2}{\operatorname{Lim}} \frac{\frac{\sin \left(\mathrm{x}^{3}-5 \mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{ax}+\mathrm{b}\right)}{\left(\mathrm{x}^{3}-5 \mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{ax}+\mathrm{b}\right)} \cdot\left(\mathrm{x}^{3}-5 \mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{ax}+\mathrm{b}\right)}{\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}-1-1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{x}-1}+1}\right) \cdot \frac{\log _{\mathrm{e}}(1+(\mathrm{x}-2))}{(\mathrm{x}-2)} \cdot(\mathrm{x}-2)}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{m}=\operatorname{Lim}_{\mathrm{x} \rightarrow 2} 2\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}^{3}-5 \mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{ax}+\mathrm{b}}{(\mathrm{x}-2)^{2}}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{m}=\operatorname{Lim}_{\mathrm{x} \rightarrow 2} 2\left(\frac{3 \mathrm{x}^{2}-10 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{a}}{2(\mathrm{x}-2)}\right)$
∵ Denominator $=0$ at $\mathrm{x}=2$
⇒ Numerator $=0$ at $\mathrm{x}=2$
$\Rightarrow 3(2)^{2}-10(2)+\mathrm{a}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=8$
put $\mathrm{a}=8$ in (1), we get $\mathrm{b}=-4$
$\therefore \mathrm{m}=\operatorname{Lim}_{\mathrm{x} \rightarrow 2} 2\left(\frac{6 \mathrm{x}-10}{2}\right) \Rightarrow \mathrm{m}=2$
$\therefore \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{m}=6$

Question: Let a line L passing through the point $(1,1,1)$ be perpendicular to both the vectors $2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}$ and $\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}$. If $\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c})$ is the foot of perpendicular from the origin on the line L , then the value of $34(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c})$ is :
Solution:
Direction vector of line is
$=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 1\end{array}\right|=-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& (2 \lambda+1,-3 \lambda+1,2 \lambda+1) \\
\frac{\mathrm{x}-1}{2}= & \frac{\mathrm{y}-1}{-3}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-1}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$
So equation of line is
$\frac{\mathrm{x}-1}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-1}{-3}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-1}{2}=\lambda$ let
let ( $2 \lambda+1,-3 \lambda+1,2 \lambda+1$ ) be the foot of perpendicular from $(0,0,0)$ upon given line
$\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OM}} \cdot(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \mathrm{j}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})=0$
$\Rightarrow 2(2 \lambda+1)-3(-3 \lambda+1)+2(2 \lambda+1)=0$
$\Rightarrow \lambda=-\frac{1}{17}$
So $(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}) \equiv\left(\frac{-2}{17}+1, \frac{3}{17}+1, \frac{-2}{17}+1\right)$
$\Rightarrow 34(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c})=100$

Question: If $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ are two vectors such that $|\vec{a}|=2$ and $|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}|=3$, then the maximum value of $3|(3 \vec{a}+2 \vec{b})|+4|(3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b})|$ is :
Solution:
$E=3 \sqrt{9 a^{2}+4 b^{2}+12 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}+4 \sqrt{9 a^{2}+4 b^{2}-12 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}$
$=3 \sqrt{36+36+12 \times 6 \cos \theta}+4 \sqrt{36+36-72 \cos \theta}$
$=3 \sqrt{72+72 \cos \theta}+4 \sqrt{72-72 \cos \theta}$
$=18 \sqrt{2} \sqrt{1+\cos \theta}+24 \sqrt{2} \sqrt{1-\cos \theta}$
$=18 \sqrt{2} \sqrt{2} \cos \frac{\theta}{2}+24 \sqrt{2}\left(\sqrt{2} \sin \frac{\theta}{2}\right)$
$=36\left(\cos \frac{\theta}{2}\right)+48 \sin \left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$
$\mathrm{E}_{\text {max }}=\sqrt{(36)^{2}+(48)^{2}}=\sqrt{6^{4}+6^{2} \times 8^{2}}=60$

Question: If the point of intersection of the lines $\frac{x+1}{3}= \frac{y+a}{5}=\frac{z+b+1}{7}$ and $\frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y-b}{4}=\frac{z-2 a}{7}$ lies on $x y$-plane, then the value of $a+b$ is :
Solution:
Line $\left(\mathrm{L}_{1}\right)$
$\frac{\mathrm{x}+1}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{y}+\mathrm{a}}{5}=\frac{\mathrm{z}+\mathrm{b}+1}{7}=\mathrm{r}_{1}$
⇒ General point P on $\mathrm{L}_{1}$ is
$\left(3 \mathrm{r}_{1}-1,5 \mathrm{r}_{1}-\mathrm{a}, 7 \mathrm{r}_{1}-\mathrm{b}-1\right)$
Line ( $\mathrm{L}_{2}$ )
$\frac{\mathrm{x}-2}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-\mathrm{b}}{4}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-2 \mathrm{a}}{7}=\mathrm{r}_{2}$
⇒ General point Q on $\mathrm{L}_{2}$ is
$\left(\mathrm{r}_{2}+2,4 \mathrm{r}_{2}+\mathrm{b}, 7 \mathrm{r}_{2}+2 \mathrm{a}\right)$
For point of intersection
$$
\begin{equation*}
3 \mathrm{r}_{1}-1=\mathrm{r}_{2}+2 \Rightarrow \mathrm{r}_{2}=3 \mathrm{r}_{1}-3 \tag{1}
\end{equation*}
$$
$$
\begin{equation*}
5 \mathrm{r}_{1}-\mathrm{a}=4 \mathrm{r}_{2}+\mathrm{b} \tag{2}
\end{equation*}
$$
$$
\begin{equation*}
7 \mathrm{r}_{1}-\mathrm{b}-1=7 \mathrm{r}_{2}+2 \mathrm{a} \tag{3}
\end{equation*}
$$
Since point lies on XY plane
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{z}-$ coordinate $=0$
From $\mathrm{L}_{1}: 7 \mathrm{r}_{1}-\mathrm{b}-1=0 \Rightarrow 7 \mathrm{r}_{1}=\mathrm{b}+1$
From $\mathrm{L}_{2}: 7 \mathrm{r}_{2}+2 \mathrm{a}=0 \Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{a}=-7 \mathrm{r}_{2}$
Substitute $\mathrm{r}_{2}=3 \mathrm{r}_{1}-3$
$2 \mathrm{a}=-7\left(3 \mathrm{r}_{1}-3\right)$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=\frac{-2 \mathrm{lr}_{1}+21}{2}$
Put in equation (2)
$5 \mathrm{r}_{1}-\mathrm{a}=4 \mathrm{r}_{2}+\mathrm{b}$
$5 \mathrm{r}_{1}-\left(\frac{-21 \mathrm{r}_{1}+21}{2}\right)=4\left(3 \mathrm{r}_{1}-3\right)+\left(7 \mathrm{r}_{1}-1\right)$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{r}_{1}=\frac{5}{7}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{b}=7 \mathrm{r}_{1}-1=4$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=3 \therefore \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}=7$

Question:
Let $\alpha, \alpha+2, \alpha \in \mathbf{Z}$, be the roots of the quadratic equation $\mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}+2)+(\mathrm{x}+1)(\mathrm{x}+3)+(\mathrm{x}+2)(\mathrm{x}+4) +\ldots . .$ $+(\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{n}-1)(\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{n}+1)=4 \mathrm{n}$ for some $\mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{N}$.
Then $(\mathrm{n}+\alpha)$ is equal to :
Solution:
$\mathrm{nx}^{2}+\mathrm{x}(2+4+6+\ldots . .+2 \mathrm{n})+(1.3+\ldots . .+(\mathrm{n}-1) (\mathrm{n}+1))=4 \mathrm{n}$
$\mathrm{nx}^{2}+\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+1) \mathrm{x}+\frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}-1)(2 \mathrm{n}+5)}{6}=4 \mathrm{n}$
$\mathrm{x}^{2}+(\mathrm{n}+1) \mathrm{x}+\frac{(\mathrm{n}-1)(2 \mathrm{n}+5)}{6}=4$
D must be a perfect square
$\mathrm{D}=\frac{122-2 \mathrm{n}^{2}}{6}=20-\left(\frac{\mathrm{n}^{2}-1}{3}\right)$
If make perfect square $\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{n}^{2}-1}{3}=16 \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=7$
So Equation is
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}^{2}+8 \mathrm{x}+\frac{8 \times 15}{6}-5=0$
$\mathrm{x}^{2}+8 \mathrm{x}+15=0$
$\mathrm{x}=-3,-5$
$\alpha=-5, \alpha+2=-3$
$\alpha+\mathrm{n}=7-5=2$

Question: If the curve $\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ passes through the point ( $1, \mathrm{e}$ ) and satisfies the differential equation $d y=y\left(2+\log _{e} x\right) d x, x>0$, then $f(e)$ is equal to :
Solution:
$\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{y}(2+\ell \mathrm{nx})$
$\int \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{y}}=\int(2+\ell \mathrm{nx}) \mathrm{dx}$
$\ell \mathrm{ny}=2 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{x} \ell \mathrm{nx}-\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C}$
$\ell \mathrm{ny}=\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{x} \ell \mathrm{nx}+\mathrm{C}$
Since it passes through $(1, \mathrm{e})$
$1=1+0+C \Rightarrow C=0$
$\ell \mathrm{ny}=\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{x} \ell \mathrm{nx}$
$f(x)=y=e^{x+x \ln x}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{e})=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{e+} \mathrm{e}}=\mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{e}}$
